8244708.transcript: Now of course adding this header was relatively simple and straightforward because it's just a very simple component. Now things are going to get a bit more complex because now I want to display my meals. And for that, I'll add another new component here in the Components folder, which I'll name Meals.jsx. And the idea here of course is that in the end, we load that meal data from that dummy backend. There will have that meals endpoint which handles GET requests and which then will return us this dummy meal data which lives on that dummy backend. So we'll need to send a request to that from inside the Meals component and then output the meal items. For that in Meals.jsx, I'll start by exporting that Meals component function. And then here I'll return an unordered list. An unordered list with an id of meals, because again, if you take a look at the index.css file, in there you will find such a rule that's looking for meals and that's indeed intended to be used on that list that will display the meals. Now of course, the list alone isn't too helpful. We also need items in that list. And for that in turn, we need data. So we need to send such a HTTP request to the backend. Now, as I just mentioned, we'll need to send a GET request to /meals on the backend and therefore, we'll need to write some code to do that. In this course, you learned that you can send an HTTP request by using the builtin fetch function. This function takes the URL of the server to which you want to send a request as an input. And here that's http://localhost:3000. And then it's /meals because again that is the endpoint we have to target. Now, you could configure this request and for example change the request method, but GET is already the default and we also don't need to send any other information and therefore I'll just send a request like this. As you also learned in this course, fetch then returns a promise and you can use the then method to define a function that will be executed when that promise resolves when you gut back a response. Alternatively, you could use async await, but you must not convert your component function into a async function because that's not allowed by React. Therefore, in order to handle this request, we could use then or wrap this into a separate function which we could name fetchMeals. This can now be an async function because it's now a standard function inside of the component function. And if we now move that code in here, we can, therefore, use await to get our response like this. Now that response then might be an error response because maybe the request failed, maybe something went wrong on the server and therefore, we should check if the response is not OK, if it has a 400-ish or 500 ish status code in which case we probably should do something. And we also probably should do something if sending the request fails, for example because there is no internet connection. So we might wanna wrap this with try catch. Though, we'll do that a little bit later because for the moment, I want to keep it simple and just send this request and expect that we do get back a valid response. That's also why we'll add this code here later. So to use that data, which we expect to get back, we can and should now call response.json because this dummy backend here, will send back its data in JSON format. So using this JSON data format and therefore, to extract that data and convert it to JavaScript objects and values, we must use the JSON method on that response object. And since this also yields a promise as you can see here we can and should also await this so that we in the end get back our response data which of course should then be those meals. So we could also name that meals here, because again if we take another look at that dummy backend, you see that it's in the end just the meals loaded from some file that are being sent back to the front end. And with that, we'll have our meals. The next step now is to update the UI with that meals data. And of course, since we're awaiting a response and the extraction of the data, it makes sense that the meals data will not be available instantly when this component function is executed. Instead, there will very likely be some delay even if it's just a couple of milliseconds. Therefore, the meals will not be there initially and therefore, we should manage them as some state so that we initially have no meals and we render a UI without meals data. And then once the meals data arrived, we update the UI with that new data. And hence we should use the useState hook here in this Meals component and import useState from React. And then here, start with an empty array. Since this should be that Meals state or that loadedMeals state to make it very clear that that meals data will not be there initially. Initially it's always this empty array but then then here in line 13 we'll have the meals. And therefore, after loading those meals, we can update the loadedMeals state by calling setLoadedMeals to set the loadedMeals equal to the meals we got back from the backend. And with that, we'll update this state and we can now output the meal data down there. And for the moment, to keep this relatively simple, I'll just use this loadedMeals state and map every meals item into a list item which gets a key that should be meal.id because every dummy meal I'm providing on the backend will have an ID which we can access. And then every meal also has a name, a price, a description and an image. And for now I'll just output the name. So back here in Meals.jsx, between those list item tags I'll output meal.name just so that we can see whether this works or not. Now, at the moment, it'll definitely not work though because we have all this logic for fetching the meals and for updating the state inside of this fetchMeals function here, which we never call anywhere. And therefore, at the moment, this code will never get executed. Now of course we could now call it here in the component function, but you learned before in this course that, this is not the best idea. It's not a great idea because if we call this function here in the component function, obviously this code here will be executed. And the problem with that is that in this code, we're updating the state. Now what happens if you update the state? Write the component function to which this state belongs is executed again. So therefore, the Meals component function would be executed again, this line of code here would be executed again and therefore, of course this code would run again, the state would be set again and we'd end up in an infinite loop, which we definitely don't want because that would crash our application. And that's why for situations like this, where we have a side effect that would cause an infinite loop, we should use the useEffect hook about which you also learned earlier in the course. This hook allows us to run side effects after the component rendered, and it also allows us to define a dependencies array that controls when exactly the side effect function will run. So, therefore, we can now import useEffect from React and define this effect function here, and then we can move this fetchMeals function and the call to this function into this effect function. Now, we could also leave this fetchMeals function outside of this effect function, but then we would have to add it as a dependency because we're now using something in the effect That's defined outside of the effect. And we then might have to use useCallback here to avoid that disk changes every time the component function is re-executed. And it's just a lot of unnecessary work since this fetchMeals function is only used in this effect function anyways. So moving it into this effect function is the simplest way of dealing with this. And therefore, now we also execute this code here after the component function executed. But if we got no dependencies or if we got dependencies that never change, this code will never execute again and therefore, we won't have an infinite loop. Now indeed here, we don't have to add any dependencies because this effect function here, is now not using any external props or state or any other values that could change across renders. The only external thing it's using, is the set loadedMeals function, which is provided by the useState hook and which is guaranteed by React to never change. Therefore, this should work like this and we should successfully load our meals. To now see this an action, we of course have to go to the App component and in there, add our Meals component which must be imported from the Meals.jsx file. And if we then save this and reload our page, we should see that meals data here. Obviously, at the moment, just the names because at the moment we're not outputting anything else but this proves that fetching this data works. And that's of course a huge step into the right direction.

8244716.title: Consuming the Context
8244716.section: React's Context API & useReducer - Advanced State Management
8244716.transcript: So now that we're providing the context, let's consume it. And we could, for example, consume it in the Cart component, because there I need the Cart items, which is why currently I'm getting them through props, so that those items can be output here or this fallback text here can be shown if we have no items in the cart. Now that we added this context feature and this context we created here has a value that does have an, at the moment, empty items array, we can and should consume this context to get hold of these items. And we'll soon make sure that those items aren't always empty, of course. Now to do that, we must import this CartContext from our store folder and there the shopping-cart-context.jsx file. And then here in this file where we're also importing CartContext, so where we want to access this context, we also have to import something from react and that something is one of two hooks that help us with getting hold of that context. And the first hook we can use is the useContext hook. This hook does what the name implies. You can use it to consume some context or to access some context from inside a component. And you would use this hook by calling it inside of the component function. And by then passing this context object, this CartContext in this case, to it as an argument. And you'll get back a value from useContext, which you can store in a constant or a variable. Here I'll store it in a constant called cartCtx. And that will be the value that is in the end provided by this context and we'll work with that value throughout the next minutes and lectures. But that's just one way of getting hold of the context. There also is another hook you could use instead and that would be the use hook. So it's just called use, nothing else, and you would use it like the useContext hook, essentially. So it's shorter, but besides that, it is used in pretty much the same way. And indeed, it will also give you that context value as a return value and you can therefore then use it as I'm using it here. In this case, I'm storing it in a constant. Now why do we have two hooks for the same purpose? Well, the use hook is a bit more flexible than the useContext hook. Because the use hook, whilst it is a React hook and whilst it can only be used in React components or other hooks, can for example be used inside of an if block. So this code here would be valid when using the use hook. Now normally, you're not allowed to use React hooks in if blocks or for loops. So normally, this would not work, but with the use hook, it will work. So whenever you have a situation like this, you could simply use the use hook to have valid code. If you were using useContext, this would not be allowed. This would be an incorrect way of using a React hook. And of course, useContext is a bit longer than use, so using the use hook simply is a bit more convenient, you could say. But the use hook is only available if you're using React version 19 or higher. In older React versions, like React 18, it's not available. There, you have to use useContext. And therefore, since I want to show you code that is backwards compatible, so that would also work with older React versions and since I don't need to access my context conditionally here, I will use useContext instead of use. And by the way, the useContext hook is also still available if you are using React 19 or higher. So you can use that hook without problems there as well. I just mentioned the use hook so that you are aware of this alternative and of course, you could use it if you are using at least React version 19. But here in this course for backwards compatibility reasons, I'll use the useContext hook instead. And therefore, this value should have a items property because we added a items property here in this initial value. Hence, we cannot use CartContext here and use cart context, or cartCtx, as I named it, .items.length here in this check. And then do the same here for this second check. And then also use cartCtx.items here when we map through these items. So that we're no longer using this prop and we therefore can get rid of that prop and we should get rid of it. And also use cartCtx.items up here therefore where I basically derive the totalPrice from those items. And with that, if we save this, we'll get an empty website. And we can see why it's empty if we open the developer tools because here we learn that we did not add the required value prop to our Context.Provider component. And that's indeed one thing we also have to do here. Even though we're setting a default value here, you also must add a value prop to your CartContext.Provider component. And here I'll set the value prop to an object that also has such an empty items array. So we do need to set that value prop and also provide our context value here. And I'll get back to why this default value here can still be useful, in a couple of seconds. But you need to add this value prop and provide your context value here as well, in this case, as a dynamic kind of hard-coded value at the moment. And if you now save that, you'll see that the website is there again. And if I now open the cart, I get no items in the cart and I got no error here and this now proves that this context feature is working because I'm getting this no items in cart message because at the moment, my context items, of course, always is an empty array. But I'm not getting an error or anything else and therefore, we can clearly see that we are able to read this context value. Now why is setting this default value here useful? Because if you do set this, you get better auto-completion. Here if I type cartCtx., it for example now suggests that I might want to access the items property and I'm getting that because I set that default value. So setting this default context value can make your life as a developer easier. And speaking of making your life easier, we can, of course, also destructure this context object, and for example, pull out the items like this. And we even get auto-completion here if I press Control + Space between these destructuring curly braces and then we could go back to just items.reduce, items.length here and here, and items.map and therefore write less code. But that's how we can now access the context and its value. How can we now link this context to state though?

8246374.title: Creating & Providing The Context
8246374.section: React's Context API & useReducer - Advanced State Management
8246374.transcript: So let's add some context to this demo app here, to this demo shop. And for that I'll start by adding a new folder in my src folder and I'll name that folder store. Now this is not required, this is not a technical requirement, this is just a convention which you'll see in many React apps, that those context values are stored in files that are stored in a folder called store, because it's your data and state store for the entire application or for multiple components at least. That's the idea behind this name, but the name is up to you, it's not set in stone. Now in that store folder, I'll create a new file now and I'll name that file shopping-cart-context.jsx. Though of course this file name also is totally up to you. You could also just name it shopping-cart without -context or anything you want. But here I'll name it -context to make it very clear that in this file I'll be managing some React context value. Now you can now create such a context value by importing a function from React. And that function that must be imported is called createContext. As a next step, you then call this function and it will then create such a context value for you, and you can then store that value in any variable or constant of your choice, for example, a constant called CartContext. Now the name is up to you, but I'm deliberately starting with an uppercase character, an uppercase C here, because this value that is produced by createContext will actually be an object that contains a React component which we'll need later. So I'll store this context in this constant. And as a next step, you can also pass a value to createContext that will be used as an initial value that can be provided to multiple components in your React app, to all the components that will be wrapped by this context. And I'll show you how to wrap this context around components and how to provide it to those components soon. Now, the value that will be provided to components via this context feature can be any value you want. It can be a number, it can be a string, and it can also be an object or array of course. And here I'll go for an object and I'll go for an object that has a items property, which initially will be an empty array and that will later store my shopping cart items. So that's the first thing I'll add here. And I'm using an object that contains this property instead of just passing an array as a value, which we could also do because I will soon add more to this context object. But with that we got the context set up. The next step now is to provide it to this application and to our components. We need to provide this context to our application and we need to wrap it around parts of that application, parts of our component tree, so to say, so that those wrapped components can access this value which we're providing here. And you do provide your context by first of all, exporting it so that we can use it outside of this file because we need to use it outside of this file. And then as a next step, you should go to a component that contains all the other components that will need to use this context. For example here, our app component. There we got all these components here, the header and the shop, and the product component. And it's for example, the product component that will later need to change the value that's stored in our context because it needs to update that items array. And it's some nested component in the header component, to be precise, it's the CartComponent there that will need to read the items to output them, and it then also needs to update them. So therefore the app component is a great place to wrap header and shop with our context so that those components or their child components can use that context. And therefore I'll start by importing this CartContext, which we just created from this store folder, and there the shopping-cart-context.jsx file. So now with that imported here, we can use that as a wrapper around header and shop. And that's why I chose a name that starts with an uppercase character because we're now going to use this context as a component. We'll wrap it around the content that should be able to access this context. Now it's worth noting here that using the context as a component like this will work if you are using React 19 or higher, but will not work with older versions of React. Instead there when using older versions of React, you would have to access a nested provider property on that CartContext object. And this also still works with React 19 by the way. So you can use this approach even if you are using React 19, but you need to use it if you're using an older version. And that's why I will use this notation, this code here in this course, so that you can follow along and get something out of the course no matter on which kind of projects and with which React versions you might work in the future. Now, this of course is a notation that you might have never seen before, having a component name that contains a . like this. But in the end, what we're doing here is we're simply accessing a nested property on the CartContext object. And that property turns out to hold a React component and therefore this overall code can be used like a React component. So it's not just components like header or shop that can be used like components as JSX elements like this, but it's also nested components like this that can be used in JSX. That's just something to keep in mind. So I'll use CartContext.Provider here as a wrapper around header and shop. And it's worth noting of course that this provider property is not a property created by us. Instead, my context value here just has a items property, but there is a difference between the object that's created by createContext, which I'm storing in this CartContext constant here. And my object, which I'm passing as a value to createContext. My value will be accessible through that context, but the context itself, this context object here, is created by React and for example, has this provider component. And with that we're providing that context. The next step now is to consume it in the components that need it, to output some data.

8246378.title: Prop Drilling: Component Composition as a Solution
8246378.section: React's Context API & useReducer - Advanced State Management
8246378.transcript: Now one possible solution for this drilling problem is to embrace component composition. And I will say right away that this can be part of the overall solution, that this can solve prop drilling in certain situations, but that in most cases, you'll also need that second solution we'll take a look at later. But for now, let's explore this possible solution here. And to explore this concept of component composition, here in this project I prepared for you in the shop component, we could say that outputting that list of products here could be moved from the shop component directly to the app component. Which would then have the advantage that this pointer at this handleAddItemToCart function would not have to be passed to the shop component but could directly be passed to the products. Now, I don't want to delete the shop component. Instead, I want to embrace component composition by refactoring this shop component to a wrapper around a list of products. And to show you what I mean, let's grab this code here where I'm mapping through my dummy products to render these list items. And let's cut it from the shop component and instead go to the app component. And then there, actually change this shop component from a self-closing component to a component that has a opening and closing tag. And move that code to output that list here into the app component between the opening and closing tags of the shop component. Now if you make that change, you also have to import those dummy products from the dummy products JS file into the app component. But thankfully I'm already doing that here. We can remove that import from the shop JSX file though, and we can now also remove the product component import from that shop component file and instead import that component here in the app JSX file. So import that product component from ./components/Product.jsx. And with that import added here, we can now output that list of products down here. Now we of course just have to make sure that we now pass the right function to this onAddToCart prop. This product component is expecting. And the function we wanna pass here is this handleAddItemToCart function here. So we can now copy that and use that as a value for onAddToCart. And with that, we still have the shop component as a wrapper but we have the logic for outputting the products and the usage of that product component in the app component now. Which now means that we no longer have to pass a pointer at this handleAddItemToCart function here to the shop component. Instead there we can now get rid of this prop because in the shop component, it's not needed anymore. We can also get rid of it here because we no longer render the products in here. Instead, what we should now do in the shop component is that we should accept this special children prop you learned about, so that we can use this custom component as a wrapper with opening and closing tags around this product list. So that we output our children here in this unordered list. And with that, we're embracing component composition, and we're using the shop component around the wrapper of that list of products. And we therefore kind of got rid of one layer of component nesting, and we're instead outputting the list of products here. And therefore, now if I save that and I reload my app here, I can still add items to my cart and I can still view and edit that cart. But now we at least got rid of parts of that prop drilling problem. Though, and that's the downside of this solution, you typically don't want to use this solution for all your component layers. Because it would mean that in the end, all your components just end up in the app component and all the other components are just wrapper components. Which you could do, but which would of course lead to a pretty bloated app component. And that's why we're now also going to explore another possible solution.

8246380.title: Outsourcing Validation Logic
8246380.transcript: Now, reusing code and simplifying code doesn't stop at the custom component level because adding such a custom input component can definitely help, but it's not the only code that could be outsourced because what about this validation logic here, for example? Obviously these are rather basic checks here, but still this is logic which we might need in different places of the app. For example, if we would add all validation manually instead of using browser based in features, we would also have to check the password and the email in the signup component. And therefore, you might want to make that validation logic code reusable as well. And for that, in this starting project, I actually already created a util folder which contains a validation.js file with some example validation functions that you can consider using in your own projects. And, of course, you can also enhance them for those projects, but which we most importantly can also use now in this project here. For example, isEmail to define this check for whether something is an email or not once in this project and then use it as often and anywhere I need it. For example, in StateLogin.jsx, I can now import from this util folder and their validation.js and I can import the isEmail, isNotEmpty, and hasMinLength validation functions defined there. And we could say that the email is invalid if it is not an email. And for that, I have to pass my email value to isEmail. And if it is not, not empty, so if it is empty. That's how we could enhance this validation by outsourcing and reusing the validation logic. And we can do the same for the password here. I can check whether we did edit the password and then if it has a min length and hasMinLength, then once the value which should be checked, and as a second argument, the minLength we wanna have. And therefore here, I can pass my password as a first value and then let's say six if I require at least six characters as a second value. And then here we need an exclamation mark because here I wanna check for invalidity, so if this condition is not met because then the password is invalid. And with that, we got this validation code out of this component. And therefore again, we got the same behavior as before, but now again achieved by outsourcing and reusing logic.

And if the timer did expire, the next question should be presented and this question should be skipped. So if the user does not answer in time, no answer will be registered. And to implement this, we will now use useEffect. I'll add a question timer component file called QuestionTimer.jsx. And in that file, I wanna export a new component function QuestionTimer. And here the goal in this file then is to display this progress bar, so the default progress element. Now, I will actually start by giving this an id, question time, which is there for styling purposes. But then to add some logic, we also wanna set a timer that expires after sometime, and we can do that with the built-in setTimeout function as you learned before. We then just have to define after how much time this should expire. And here, I think it would be great if this question timer would be a configurable component, so that the exact time is not hard coded in this component, but can be defined in the component where this question timer component is being used. So that in here, in this timer, I call onTimeout. And we can actually shorten this code and just set onTimeout as the function that should be called by the browser once this timeout expired. Now, I'm not using useEffect here at this point because even though this is a side effect, it's at the moment not an effect that would require the usage of useEffect, because I'm not facing the danger of an infinite loop here, because I'm not updating any component state here, and I'm also not trying to interact with an element that wouldn't be available yet. So this code at the moment is fine as it is, but of course that's not all I wanna do here. Instead, I also wanna make sure that I update this progress bar, and therefore we also need an interval that executes some code every couple of milliseconds so that we can update this timer. Therefore, we of course also need some state in this component so that this progress bar is rerendered every couple of milliseconds whenever that state changes. So I'll import useState from React here and then the state that is registered here is in the end the remaining time, so remainingTime and setRemainingTime. The initial value for the state should be this timeout value because that is the remaining time of this timer when this component is rendered. But then I wanna update this every 10 milliseconds or something like this. And therefore we of course need set interval as you learned, because here we can define that every 10 or 100 or whatever milliseconds, this function here should execute. And in this function, I wanna update my remaining time by deducting 100 milliseconds or whichever frequency you chose here from it. So here we need the function form since we update the state based on the previously stored state value. And the new value is standard previously remaining time minus 100, so minus my frequency here. And this would now of course create an infinite loop, because we're updating the state here. This would re-execute this component function. We would create a new interval where we would also update the state again and we would quickly have multiple intervals up and running which all would call this component function. And that's of course definitely not what we want and that's therefore a use case for useEffect because now we can wrap this code with useEffect here by passing this effect function to useEffect and this dependencies array about which you learned. And then we should move this set interval code into useEffect to make sure that this is not re executed all the time, but instead only when those dependencies change. And in here, we don't have any dependencies that would need to be added here, because you basically only need to add props and state values, and we're using neither of those in this effect function. But we should now of course also wrap this timeout code with useEffect, because otherwise when we update the remaining time, which we will do every 100 milliseconds, this component function executes again and this timer would be recreated, and we therefore would quickly have multiple timers up and running. Therefore, we should add another useEffect call here and move this code into this useEffect function. And here we now do need to add a dependency, because we actually have two dependencies that are used in this effect function. Because we're using two props in there, the timeout prop and the onTimeout prop, which is a function, but still a prop. So here we should add timeout and onTimeout as dependencies to make sure that this effect function gets re-executed if one of those dependencies changes. And that of course makes sense because of the parent component should decide that the question timer timeout should change, we, of course, also wanna reset the timer and set it again. And with all that, we're not done yet. We will tweak the code later, but we get a good starting state. And we can now use this remaining time here to update this progress bar. There we wanna set the max value to this timeout duration we're getting through the timeout prop, and the current value then should be the remaining time. Because keep in mind, this remaining time initially is this timeout value, but it's then getting less and less as this time expires. With that, we can go back to the quiz component and there import this question timer component we just worked on from question timer JSX and use that component here. And I wanna display it right above my question text here inside of that question div. That's where I wanna output the question timer. And now we of course must set both the timeout and the onTimeout props. Now, timeout should be the duration of the timer in milliseconds. And to get a ten second timer, for example, we should therefore enter 10,000 here, because that's 10 seconds in milliseconds. And on timeout, of course, should be a function that should be executed once the timer expired. And here we could execute handleSelectAnswer, but pass null as a value. So that we add a new entry to this user answers array in this function, but the value we add is null, so that it's not actually an answer from the available answers, but instead a placeholder that basically tells us that no answer was chosen for this question. And with that, if we save that and reload, we see this timer here expiring and as it expires, nothing happens. Or actually something happens after a while, but not instantly as it expired. Instead, as you see, if you reload, this timer expires and then we have to wait until at some point of time we do move on to the next question. And here we then of course have no timer as you see, instead it stays empty instead of resetting. 8294724.title: Handling Form Submission & Validation
8294724.section: Practice Project: Building a Food Order App
8294724.transcript: So now that we have this checkout form in place here, it's time to also deal with the actual form submission so that thereafter we can send that request to the backend and submit the order to the backend. And of course, here we already have a form element, so if we wanna handle the submission of this form, we can either do that manually by adding the onSubmit prop and setting up our own function that's triggered when that submit event occurs, or we could use a form action. Now, here, I'll start by using this onSubmit prop and I'll handle the form submission manually. I'll not use this form actions feature that's offered by React, but later, at the end of this section, we'll actually migrate this project to use form actions so that you see both approaches in action. So therefore here, I'll start by adding this onSubmit prop to this form element. And then we can set up a function, handleSubmit could be the name, which we connect to that prop, so which we pass as a value to that prop as we always do. Now, as you learned in the forms course section, the thing with forum submissions just is that out of the box, when using a regular button in a regular form, the browser will go ahead and create an HTTTP request for you and send it for you, but unfortunately not to the backend we want it to be sent to because the browser doesn't know about that. It's a standalone process and it would be a standalone server on the internet. Instead, the browser would send the request to this development server that's serving this site. So our front end. But this server and this site is not prepared and equipped to handle this request. Therefore, what we need to do is we need to prevent that default. And as you learned, we can do this by calling preventDefault on that standard event object, which we automatically receive in our event handling function. It's passed in by React in the end. And by calling this method here, preventDefault, we make sure that this request, which otherwise would get created and sent is not getting created and sent. Therefore now we have a chance to control what should happen when that form is submitted. And what should happen here is that A, the input values should get validated so that an empty form, for example, can't be submitted. And B, I wanna make sure that I get hold of the entered values. Now here to validate the input, I added the required prop on those input fields. And as a result, if we save that and we go to the Checkout page and we try to submit an empty form, we get some nice error messages here. So that's pretty good. We don't have to worry about this problem here. Though, as mentioned before, you could of course also implement custom Java script-driven validation as you also learned it in the forms section. But here, I'll keep it like this and therefore the validation part is already done. Now, when it comes to extracting the values, we also got multiple options. We could add a state value for every input or a combined state object for all the inputs and use the onChange prop to update the state on every keystroke. We could do that, and for some validation approaches where we wanna validate on every keystroke, we typically would have to do that. Alternatively, we could work with refs. Now, since I have a custom component here, the input component, in order to pass a ref to that component, you might need to use forward ref and wrap that component function with forward ref if you're using a React version lower than 19. If you are using React version 19 or higher, you could simply pass a ref to this component and use it in there. But here again, I instead prefer using the built-in features the browser offers to us. We can create such a FormData object and pass the event target, which is the form element in the end, the underlying object that's managed by the browser to be precise. Pass that to the FormData constructor function. And what we'll get back is an object that makes it very easy for us to get hold of those user entered values. The the only important thing here is that we got this name prop on our inputs because this is required to then access those different input fields by their name and to extract the values entered by the user. Because as you learned, we can now use the get method, for example, to get the full name value. So the value that was entered into this field. Or as you also learned in the forms section, we can actually convert this FormData object to a simpler JavaScript object where those form inputs are basically represented by their name as property names, and the entered values are then values for those properties, by using object fromEntries and passing FormData entries like this to it. And this will essentially give us an object where we, for example, have an email property with the value entered by the user, and of course key value pairs for all the other input fields as well. And therefore, this is how we can easily extract the data entered by the user. And that's then the data which we in the end wanna send to the backend, combined with the cart data, because of course the order which we're sending to the backend should also include details about what was ordered. And therefore that's now the last step here. We need to send such an HTTP request to the backend. So let's do that as a next step.

8294726.title: Getting Started with Cart Context & Reducer
8294726.section: Practice Project: Building a Food Order App
8294726.transcript: So my next goal is to make this Add to Cart button work and to manage such a shopping cart behind the scenes, which then can also be viewed once we click this Cart button in the header. Now of course, this shopping cart data therefore will be required in many areas of this application. We'll need to be able to edit it from inside every meal item, by clicking this button. So therefore, it sounds like this cart data should not be data that should be managed in a single component. But managing the cart data in the app component of course would mean that we would have to bloat this component, that we would have to add a lot of code to it, and that we would probably have to do a lot of prop drilling later on in this app to get that cart data into all the components in our component tree that need it. And since that's typically not what you want, I'll instead use React's context feature to manage this cart data in a more general, centralized way. And for that, I'll add a new folder in my source folder, which I'll name store. As you learned earlier in the course, this name of course is up to you, but it is a name that's commonly chosen for context management. In this store folder, I'll add a CartContext.jsx file where I wanna manage this cart data and this CartContext. Because as you learned, this context feature offered by React in the end allows us to spread data into all components that need it in a very easy and reusable way. So therefore, now here in this CartContext file, I'll import createContext from React and then call createContext to create such a new context object, my CartContext here. Now you can pass a default value to the context here and as you learned before in this course, we don't really need that value because we'll set it in a different way anyways, but it still makes sense to set it up here to get better auto completion. And it also helps us plan this context object in advance. And here I'd say that my CartContext should definitely manage a list of cart items, so an array of items. And we'll then also need some functions, some methods in this object to edit those items, to add or remove items. And therefore here I'll also add a addItem function where I expect to get a item, a meal item that should be added to the cart and where we will then update the cart. Though the actual logic will not go into this dummy placeholder function here, but instead later in a separate function. And will also need a removeItem function where we might expect to get the ID of some meal and where we then wanna remove that item from the items array. That could be the shape of our CartContext. Now, as you also learn before in this course as a next step, you typically also want to define such a ContextProvider component, which then can be wrapped around our components to make this context available to them and which will do the actual data management and the actual state management. Because the context feature on its own, standalone without anything else, is not about managing any state. It's not about changing any values and triggering any component updates. Instead it's just about spreading data to components. But of course that data that is being spread can be stateful and therefore it is this CartContextProvider component that should manage that changing data. So therefore here in this CartContextProvider component, we wanna manage some state and eventually, I wanna return my CartContext.Provider component. Or if you are using React 19 or higher, you can actually just use CartContext as a component. Because since React 19, this object that you create with help of createContext will be a component that can be wrapped around other components and other content in general so that this wrapped content, these wrapped components can reach out to this context and interact with it. Prior to React 19, this CartContext object here would not be a wrappable component and instead there you would have to access the nested .Provider property, which then would be that component that can be wrapped to grant access to the context. Now it's up to you which approach you prefer. You can of course use the more concise modern syntax if you know that you're using React 19. Here for this course, I'll use the more backward compatible syntax that would also work with older React versions. Now here my idea is to wrap my custom component around those components and therefore here we should accept the children prop and pass that as a value between CartContext.Provider. Now, we should also export this function, maybe as a default and also export this context here. And here I'll actually not export this function as a default now that I think about it, but instead I'll export the CartContext object itself as a default. But both things should be exported so that both this component and this context object can be used outside of this file. And with that, we can start adding some logic to this component function here, some stateful logic to manage these items and to add or remove items. And we could of course do that with useState, that would be absolutely fine and you could pass state values through context to components. But since the state we're going to manage here will be at least a little bit more complex, I'd rather use the useReducer hook, which also is provided by React and which also allows us to manage state, but which typically makes it simpler to manage more complex state, and which also makes it easier to move that state management logic out of this component function. Because this useReducer hook, which of course must be imported from React, needs a reducer function as an input. And for that I'll add a new function, which I'll name cartReducer, which will get a state and an action as parameters. And these parameter values will be passed into function automatically by React because it will be React that's executing this function later. And the goal of this cartReducer is to return an updated state. And it's this action object that will tell this function how to update this state. And here it's quite common to receive an object as a value for action, to be precise, an object that also has a type property or some other identifier property, but type is a commonly chosen name. And the idea simply is that we can take a look at this type that's part of the incoming action and then run different code for different action types. For example, here we could have a type of ADD_ITEM, and that's just some unique identifier I just came up with. This is all totally up to you because you will be the person dispatching actions in your code later. So here I expect to get an action of type ADD_ITEM and in here we then wanna update the state to add a meal item. We'll add that logic soon, but before we do that, I also want to handle another action type. And that would be the REMOVE_ITEM type, which should do the opposite, it should remove an item from the state. And then of course we wanna return the updated state and if we make it to the end of this reducer without making it into any if block, I simply wanna return the unchanged state like this. But now let's make sure that items can be added here. And for this, in this first if block, I'll start by adding an item to this items array. So I expect that my state I'm getting here in the end will have such an items array, and I can expect that because I'm the developer building this, so it's totally up to me to define how this state object should look like. Because indeed it will be us here in the component function when we call useReducer that define how the state should look like. Because you use useReducer by passing your reducer function as a first parameter. You are not calling it, instead you're just passing a pointer at this function to useReducer. And then you pass the initial state value. So the state that should be assumed when this component renders for the first time as a second argument to useReducer. And that's what I mean, it is up to you to define how this should look like. And here we can then add our items array to this state, for example, and therefore now work with that items array here in our reducer because this incoming state object will have this shape here. So therefore here in ADD_ITEM, we can of course simply reach out to state.items and push a new item onto this array. And that could be an item which we expect to get on this action, maybe on a item property. Again, we will dispatch those actions so we can definitely add an item property to the action when we dispatch it. But updating this state like this would not be a good idea. It would not be a good idea for two main reasons. The first reason, and arguably the more important one, is that you should never mutate the existing state like this because push will edit the existing items array, which is already stored in memory, and therefore it will change the state value before this cartReducer is done executing. And if you then had some code that would, for example, return some other state under certain conditions, you would still have changed the items. So you make a change before you're done executing the code and that's not a good idea. The other reason why this is not a good idea to update the state like this, is that I don't always want to add a new item to the items array. Because if I click on this Add to Cart button on a meal multiple times, I don't want to add this meal 10 times to this array, instead I want to add it once and then just increase some quantity property that's connected to that meal, so that when we later view our cart, we don't have a list of 10 times Margherita pizza but instead we only have one Margherita pizza in there with a quantity of 10. And therefore I'll not update a state like this, but instead, I'll check if we already have that item in our item array by adding an existingCartItemIndex constant where I reach out to my state items. So to this existing array of items and where I'll then call findIndex, a method built into JavaScript. This method takes a function as an input, a function that will execute on every item in this items array and a function that should return true if we found the item we're looking for and false otherwise. And then once we found an item, we'll automatically get the index of that item, that's what findIndex will return and we'll need that index later to update that item. So here I wanna return true if the ID of the item I'm currently looking at here is equal to the ID of the item I'm receiving on my action. With that information, as a next step, we can check if existingCartItemIndex is greater than minus one. Because findIndex will return minus one as a value if it did not find an item. So if that index is greater than minus one, we know that the item already exists in this item's array. Otherwise, in the else case it doesn't. So therefore, in this else case I indeed simply want to add this item, this incoming action item here to this item's array, but again, not by simply pushing into state.items for the reasons I mentioned before. Instead I'll add a new constant here, updatedItems, where I'll create a new array and therefore also a new array object in memory into which I spread my existing items. So with that, I create a copy of that old array. It's worth noting though that the item objects still will be the old item objects, but that won't be a problem here. Because in the else case, I can now use this new copy and therefore this new array object to push this new action item onto this array. So that's the case that we didn't have an item in that array yet. If we did have it in that array, I wanna update some quantity property, which I expect to exist on every cart item. For this we can add a new updatedItem here, which is a new JavaScript object into which I spread the properties of the old existing item. For that we can use the spread operator and then reach out to state.items for this existingCartItemIndex. So that will then give us that existing item in that state.items array. And I'll take all the properties of that existing meal item or off that existing cart item, which is a meal item in the end though, and I'll spread it into this new object. And then in addition, I'll add a quantity property here to this object where I wanna update the existing item quantity and add one to it. So therefore, again, I'll reach out to that existing item and then the quantity property and add one to it. And since I'm using this code twice now, we can, of course, also outsource it now and get our existing item here by accessing existingCartItemIndex on state.items and then using just existingItem here and also here to make that code a bit more readable. Of course, now we also must make sure that we do have such a quantity property on items that we're adding as new items to that array though because otherwise accessing this quantity property as I'm doing it here will fail in the future. So therefore, we need to tweak this code here in line 24. When a new item is added, we should create a new object here where we spread all the meal item data into this object, but where we also add a quantity property and set it to one initially. So that new cart items start with a quantity of one, but then if we update them later, we continue adding one to that quantity. Of course, in that updating case, we now must reinsert this updatedItem into our updatedItems array, and therefore here we should go to updatedItems, access the existingCartItemIndex and override the item at that index with the updatedItem because it's that item at that index that we wanna update so we do that by overriding it with that new item. And that's how we can update this state in a immutable way, so without changing the existing state in memory. And now that we went through all these steps, here, still in that main if block of the ADD_ITEM check, but outside of this inner if block, we of course wanna return the updated state for this reducer because that's the main thing of this reducer, it should return an updated state. And for that I'll return a new object because again, we don't want to edit the existing state and I'll spread my existing state data into this object. But if we would add other data to that state later it makes sense to paste that old, unchanged data into this new state object and then just update the parts of the state that need updating. And for that here, I'll then override my items array with the updatedItems like this. 8294732.title: Using useEffect for Handling (Some) Side Effects
8294732.section: Handling Side Effects & Working with the useEffect() Hook
8294732.transcript: So, how can we use "React useEffect Hook" to get rid of this problem? So, to handle this side effect in a better way. Well, first of all, we have to import it. useEffect from React. And with it imported like all hooks, this hook must be executed in the component function, in the app component function. Now, useEffect, unlike useState or useRef does not return a value, though. Instead, useEffect needs two arguments. And the first argument is a function that should wrap your side effect code. So, in this case here, I'm creating an anonymous function and I move my location fetching state updating code into this anonymous function. That's the first step, but that's not all we have to do. Instead, you should also pass second argument to useEffect. So, after this anonymous function, and that second argument is an array of dependencies of that effect function. And I'll get back to these dependencies later. For now, just add an empty array. Now, if you do that, if you change the code to look like this. You will not run into this infinite loop problem. And why is that? Because the idea behind useEffect is that this function which you pass as a first argument to useEffect will be executed by React after very important. After every component execution. So, if the app starts and the app component function executes, this code here will not be executed right away. Instead, it's only after the app component function execution finished. So, after this JSX code here has been returned. That this side effect function you passed to useEffect will be executed by React. So, React will execute that after the component function is done executing. Now, if you then update the state here, the component function executes again as you learned. And in theory this effect function would execute again. But that's where this dependencies array comes into play. If you define this dependencies array. So, if you do not omit it, but you define it. Then, React will actually take a look at the dependencies specified there. And it will only execute this effect function again. If the dependency values changed. Now, this might sound a bit abstract but I'll show you a use case. Where we have a dependency a little bit later. Here, where we have no dependencies. Those dependencies also can't change because we have none. So, they obviously never change. Therefore, React actually never re-executes this effect function. Instead, it only executes it once after this app component function was executed for the first time. But then this effect function is never executed again. If you would omit this dependencies array, this effect function would be executed after every app component render cycle. And therefore, we would still have an infinite loop. But with an empty dependencies array, that will not be the case. And therefore, with this out of the way. We will now set our available places to the sorted available places. Once we have to users' location without creating an infinite loop. And now, I'll just tweak the application a little bit. And down here on this places component, I'll add the fallback text prop, which is supported by this component and set it to Sorting places by distance. Which is simply some fallback text that will be shown during the time where we don't have any places yet. Because we're still looking for the user's location. Because initially of course, we don't have any places here. We start with an empty array. With all that, If you save that and you reload, you should see a little pop-up here, that asks you for permission to get your location. And if you click block, you'll break the app. So, here, I'll click allow. And with that it'll fetch my location which can take some time as I mentioned. But then, it'll present me these places sorted by distance to my location. Now of course, the order will depend on where you are located. For me, it looks pretty accurate. I'd say, I haven't checked all locations here. And it's just some dummy data anyways, but it looks correct. There also is no error in the console. And therefore, this code now works. And we're handling this side effect in a way that does not crash our app. Which I guess is always a good thing to have.

8298056.title: Outsourcing Context & State Into a Separate Provider Component
8298056.section: React's Context API & useReducer - Advanced State Management
8298056.transcript: Now, as you probably can tell, this context feature can be very useful for sharing data across multiple components in your application. But at the moment, the way we're using it, we still have a pretty bloated app component. Because we're in the end, setting up the value that should be shared through our context in that component here. And whilst this works, you might wanna avoid something like this because if your app would become more complex, you might have multiple contexts in the same app, which is possible. And you might have different state values that should be shared through those different contexts. And you would therefore end up with a lot of logic in your app component since that is typically your root component and has access to all the components that might want to use a context. And therefore there is an alternative approach an alternative pattern, which you'll see in many React projects, which allows you to get all this context related data management out of the app component into a separate context component. And we'll use this pattern here now starting in this shopping cart context file. Because there besides creating and sharing that context, we can also create and share a component function, a CartContextPovider component function. Though the name, of course, as always is up to you, but you, of course, wanna use descriptive component function names. And since this component function will be all about managing context data and providing that data to your application and it will be all about context related to the shopping cart. This sounds like a good name for this component function. And now the idea simply is to, in the end, grab all that state management and context value management code from inside the app component. So starting here where we create the state with useState, including all these functions that added to the state, all the way down to where I construct this context value, I'll cut all of that from the app component hence making it much leaner. And I'll move that into that CartContextProvider component function like this so that I'm managing my state in here. Now for this to work, we must import useState in this file here. And then with this imported here in this case, we also must import Dummy Products. So here I'll import Dummy Products from going up one level and then there it's this dummyproducts.js file here in this starting product. And with that, we're managing this entire state and we're constructing this context value here in this component function. But it's not a real component function yet, we can't use it as such because we're not returning anything renderable yet. That of course needs to change. And what I wanna return here is my CartContext provider component and the value prop here should still be set to this context value. But now this CartContext provider component here should be wrapped around any value. This custom CartContextProvider component here will be wrapped around. So here we should accept and destructure the children prop and then use that down here in our return JSX code to in the end make sure that we wrap this CartContext provider with that value around any JSX code around any other components. Therefore, this shopping CartContextProvider component here will be wrapped around. So therefore we can now use this CartContextProvider component in our app component. And there import this from shopping cart context JSX this CartContextProvider component instead of that context object. And we can get rid of useState here because I'm not managing any state in here anymore. And we can and should now use this custom component here as a wrapper around header and shop like this. So with that, we're still setting up the wrapper here but we of course got rid of all that state management logic here. And with that, if you save that and you reload this app still works as it did before. I still have my cart here and I can change this cart but now it works through context. And we outsourced all this context and state management code into a separate component so that if you had multiple contexts and multiple pieces of independent data in the same app you could simply create multiple context files and therefore keep your app component pretty lean. Because there you would just wrap your components with that context provider component.

15700440.title: Starting Project & Why You Would Replace Redux

And in addition, as I already covered throughout the course, I don't want to pass all the state through multiple layers of components, just so that I have it everywhere where I need it. And Redux is really convenient for such cases, it's a great state management tool when you have some global state. And a list of products, and favorited products would be a great example if you're building a more complete online shop, for example. Now in this relatively simple project, which of course is simple so that we can focus on Redux and how to replace it, there I used the traditional Redux approach I showed you in the course, here I have my product reducer with some initial state, which initializes my products, every product has an ID, a title, a description, and a favorite status. And then in the reducer, I watch for one action, where I basically switch that favorite status off the product and invert it and assign a new state, thereafter. That is connected here to the products.js file, which is our products page. The shop slice, which we create in index.js here by combining our reducers or, well our one reducer here. In the favorites.js file, I also select something with useSelector from my store. I select all products that are a favorite so that I can output them there. And last, but not least, in the productitem.js file, there is use the useDispatch hook provided by react-redux, so that when we click that favorite button here, we dispatch this toggleFav action, which is defined in the actions folder of our store folder. That's how this app works, and the first important thing I really want to emphasize is that there's nothing wrong with that approach. You don't need to switch away from Redux at all. But maybe you want to. And why would you? Well, maybe you want to stay in the react-only world, so that you don't need to learn Redux. Arguably it's not that hard to learn Redux, but still, maybe you want to use only the tools react gives you. Maybe you also simply don't want to add that extra Redux and react-redux library to your project. Hence, you would end up with a smaller bundle, because if you don't have these extra libraries included, obviously you ship less code when you deploy your application. And that could be a good argument because you can certainly shrink your bundle a bit. Though I will also say, if you have a really large application, it might not matter that much if it's included or not. Yet, that could be a reason for why you don't want to use Redux. Or you simply want to explore how you can manage your state globally without passing everything through props, but still without using Redux. 15700464.title: Context API Summary (and why NOT to use it instead of Redux)
15700464.transcript: Well, one huge downside is this comment here by a React team member. The statement here is clear, the context is great for low-frequency updates but not for high-frequency ones. That means if you have something which changes rarely, let's say the user authentication status or just the example I used in the React Hooks module, that is fine to be covered with the context API because this will not change all the time, not that often. The same would be the case if you have something like theme-ing on a page, or something which doesn't change all the time. For a favorite status like this, you could already argue that it's quite a high-frequency update. Now, obviously users might not be tapping these buttons every second but still switching the favorite status of something will very likely occur way more often than logging in or changing the theme of a page and therefore I would argue that this is more on the high-frequency side, just like maybe managing a shopping cart. Basically anything which changes rather often. The context API of course works in that case but regarding the performance it's simply not optimized for that. The way the react context API works is such that whenever something changes in your context it has no way of cleverly figuring out which component that uses this context really is concerned and which component is not. Which means that every component that uses use context will rebuild, will re-render when you switch something in that context. No matter if it's directly effected or not. And in general the react context API is simply not optimized and not meant to be your Global State Management tool in your app. It's meant for some states, like authentication status, like the theme but not for all your states because of these missing optimizations and all of this missing intent behind the context API. Hence it is great for some things like authentication which I mentioned a couple of times but not for all use cases not for all scenarios and something like your products and the favorite status is probably not what you want to manage with the context API in larger projects. But there still is a non redux alternative for that and that's what we'll have a look at next.

25595340.title: What is React.js? And Why Would You Use It?
25595340.transcript: So what is React, and why exactly would you use it? Well, if we visit the official webpage, react.dev, we get an answer. It's a library for web and native user interfaces. So put in other words, React is a library for building user interfaces. That's what React is all about. And as it turns out, it's a JavaScript library for building user interfaces. But that of course, still doesn't answer the other question. Why would you use it? And the answer can be found if you visit a webpage that uses React, like, for example, Netflix. As you navigate around on such a website, you'll see that all these transitions are super smooth, pretty much instant, and you never have this effect where you seem to load a new page. So where you seem to wait for a new page to be sent back by the server. To some extent, it therefore feels like a mobile app, because in mobile apps, getting instant feedback and having smooth transitions is pretty much the standard. And it's libraries like React that help us build such user experiences, such user interfaces for the web as well. Because React, since it's a JavaScript library, uses JavaScript in the browser to update the page and the user interface we see on that page without reloading the page. JavaScript can do that, because JavaScript in the end runs kind of in the background of a website, and JavaScript is able to read and manipulate the webpage after it was loaded. So JavaScript can, for example, make sure that as I click on the movies tab, it fetches the movie's data behind the scenes in the background without reloading or leaving the page, and it then updates what I see on the screen with that movie's data, leading to that smooth transition between those pages. But wait a second. Since this is all JavaScript in the end, why do we need this React library for JavaScript? Because as you'll see in the next lecture where I'll compare using just JavaScript to using libraries like React, working with just JavaScript, of course, is an option, but it isn't a great one. It's cumbersome, it means more work for you, it can be more error-prone, and especially if you're building more complex user interfaces and more complex applications like this Netflix web app, then using just vanilla JavaScript, so without any library like React, would just be a huge pain, and wouldn't be a very realistic option, to be honest.

25599684.title: Module Introduction
25599684.section: An Alternative Way Of Building Components: Class-based Components
25599684.transcript: In this course section, we are going to learn how we may build components in React. Well, wait a second! Isn't that something we've learned in-depth until now already? You can build Class-based Components. But I get one important note, right away, before you feel overwhelmed. This is 100% optional, and in most modern React projects, you are probably not going to use this approach. You can therefore skip this section, but I wanna emphasize that you will see a lot of Class-based Components out there, in the wild, in third-party libraries or in projects you might be working on, because traditionally, not too long ago, you had to use Class-based Components in certain cases. This is no longer the case, but I will explain all about that throughout this section. I will explain what exactly Class-based Components are and why they exist. We will learn how we can build Class-based Components and how we can work with Class-based Components, and, we're then also going to have a look at Error Boundaries, and at least this is something you should at least take a mental note to come back to, because Error Boundaries, right now, require Class-based Components to be built. That being said, you will not build a lot of Error Boundaries. But still, this module holds important information about React's past, about an alternative way of building components, and most importantly of course, why that way exists and how it works.

25599690.title: What & Why
25599690.section: An Alternative Way Of Building Components: Class-based Components
25599690.transcript: Now as I mentioned in the last lecture already, Class-Based Components exist and they are an alternative to functions. Up to this point, when we built Components, they looked something like this. Of course, instead of the function keyword, we might have used const and stored an arrow function in there. But either way, we simply built a function. That is what we did. And we built a function that receives props and then returns the JSX code that should be rendered to the screen. This is what Components are in React, and they are simply regular JavaScript functions which return some renderable results, typically JSX. This is what we refer to as Functional Components, which is a term I also already used a couple of times. Now this term exists because it's not the only way of building Components. If it would be the only way, we could just call it Components, right? But we call it Functional Components because there is an alternative way of defining Components. Not of using them. You always use them as custom HTML elements in your JSX code. But for defining them, there is this alternative. You can define a Component by creating a class, which is a default JavaScript feature. Classes are not a React feature, classes exist in modern JavaScript. And you can define a class with a render method and that is a reserved name, render. It has to be named like this. And then React will call that render method to evaluate what should be rendered to the screen. So this is an alternative way of defining Components. And that is what is referred to as class Components or Class-Based Components. Now, I will emphasize the left approach, using Functional Components, is the default and most modern approach of building Components these days. And in most modern React projects, you will therefore pretty much work with Functional Components only. You might never build a single class-based Component there because nowadays with the exception of error boundaries, which we'll have a look at later, there is no reason to go for Class-Based Components. Except for personal preferences, of course. You can simply prefer Class-Based Components, and that would be absolutely fine. You can build anything you can build with Functional Components with Class-Based Components as well. It just requires a different mental model in some regards and some aspects. Now, the Class-Based Components exist because they were required in the past. In the past, which means prior to React 16.8, there were scenarios and use cases where you needed Class-Based Components. Specifically when dealing with State and with side effects, you had to use Class-Based Components. Traditionally, prior to React 16.8, you could not change State in Functional Components and you couldn't really handle side effects there either. That changed with React 16.8 because that React version introduced a concept called React Hooks, which are all these Hooks, these functions starting with use. UseState and useEffect and so on. These are functions for Functional Components, which bring features to Functional Components, which previously were reserved for Class-Based Components. And indeed, Class-Based Components can't use React Hooks. That's also important. That's really important. What you learned about react Hooks up to this point, it does not matter at all when working with Class-Based Components. But now in the next lectures, I'll show you how you can convert Functional Components to Class-Based Components, how you can manage State there, and also how you translate useEffect and useContext and useReducer to the Class-Based Component world.

It, for example, is rendered to the DOM or remove from the DOM, but there are specific methods you can add two class-based components to run code at different points in that life cycle. And the first, most important life cycle method you can add to class-based components, is to componentDidMount() method like render() that's a built in method, which you can use as soon as you extend the built in component which you import from React. And then you can add this method and React. We'll call it for you when the component was just mounted but more on that in a second. And when that happens, when that state in that data store is updated, subscribing components are notified, so that they can update their UI. That's how Redux works. Now of course that's all nice. That's great theory. But how does that look like in reality then. How does the code we have to write look like? Well that's what we're going to explore now.
