So in here we could, for example, check if identifier is equal to, let's say, title. And if that's the case, we update the title state by calling setEnteredTitle and setting this equal to the new value. Else if it's not title, we could check if the identifier is maybe equal to the date here, so if it's equal to date, and then update the EnteredDate with the value. And else it must be the amount, and therefore we could then call setEnteredAmount and set as equal to value. So with that, I am calling all these state updating functions, but I'm now doing this all in one shared change handler function. Now we can't use this change handler function as a input for onChange now though. That won't work because as you learned, React will, in the end, call this function for us and will pass such an event object to it and it will definitely not pass an identifier and value to it. We're not going to get those data points when React calls this function. But we need them for this function to work correctly. Now what we can do here to work around this, is we can go to this onChange function and instead of passing a pointer to inputChangeHandler as a value to onChange, we can pass an arrow function to it. A new anonymous arrow function which we create down there where we need it. With that, it's this arrow function that will be called by React whenever this input changes. Therefore this arrow function will receive this event object. And in the body of this arrow function we can now manually execute inputChangeHandler. So execute this function here, which I just added. We manually execute it by adding parentheses. And now this code in here will not be executed when this line of code here is parsed, but instead this here will only be executed when this arrow function here is executed. And that will only be the case when this change event occurs. But now that we have this wrapper arrow function here we have full control over how inputChangeHandler will be executed. And we can therefore now pass our identifier as a first argument. So for example, title here, so that for this title input here, we pass title as an identifier, so that we make it into this if branch here. And as a second argument, we pass the value that changed. So in this case here, event.target.value, just as before, but now in this arrow function. And with that, we as a developer, control how this function here will be called by wrapping it in such a arrow function, which is the actual function that's passed as a value to the onChange prop. But then we control how this inner function will be executed when the change event occurs, and we can then use this generic handler function because now we could use this same code here, or almost the same code, on the amount input value, for example, and pass amount as an identifier. But other than that, it's the same code and the function that's being executed is always the same. And this approach simply is an alternative to having multiple specialized handler functions. As mentioned, there's nothing wrong with having these specialized handler functions, but as an alternative, you could consider using such shared functions. With that, I'll get rid of that though and move back to the code we had before with the titleChangeHandler and the amountChangeHandler here. But I definitely wanted to share this alternative since it is something you might see in some React projects. And since it is something you might want to use yourself in your React projects.

38345450.title: Derived / Computed State
38345450.section: [BONUS / OPTIONAL] React State & Working with Events
38345450.transcript: So at this point in the section, you already learned quite a bit about state, and events, and a lot about React in general. Now, before we come to an end and conclude this section, I wanna dive into another state related very important concept, and that's the concept of derived or computed state, or derived or computed values as you could also say. And to explore this concept, let's say that below the "expenses filter" but above the "expense item" elements here, I wanna show a little text in a paragraph. And let's say that here, I wanna say, "Data for years 2019, 2021, and 2022 is hidden," if the filter is set to 2020. If it's set to 2021, I of course wanna say "2019, 2020, and 2022." So, I basically wanna list all the years which are currently not selected. Now this is actually a feature which I'll soon remove because we don't actually need it here in this app, but it is a feature that allows me to explore this important concept of derived values. Because one way of implementing this here would be to register a new state, which we could name, "filter info text," with a "set filter info text" state updating function. And the initial value here could be this string here maybe, where we say, "2019, 2021, 2022," and then we dynamically output "filter info text" down there. And now, we could update this whenever the filter changes. Then we could check "if selected year is equal to 2019," and if that's the case, we call "set filter info text," and set this to "2020, '21 and '22." "Else if selected year is 2020," we "set filter info text" to "2019, '21 and '22," and so on. We also would of course add the condition for '21, where we set the filter info text like this. And "else" for the last case, we would exclude '22. That's how we could implement this. If we save this, we have this text here. It's a bit hard to read because it's not styled because I'll delete it soon anyways. But you can see that currently 2020 is excluded because that's the year I selected. If I switch this to 2021 on the other hand, this updates and '21 is now excluded in this list here, and instead all the other years are mentioned. Also, please keep in mind that the filter doesn't work yet which is why we see all these years. But, this text updates as expected. Nonetheless, this is typically not how you should implement this. Whilst it works, you are managing an additional piece of state which in the end is redundant. Because this state here is in the end based on this state. The year we exclude is the year we're managing in this state. So we're managing two pieces of state here, even though they both depend on the same source so to say. One state would be enough here. Instead of being managed as a standalone state which updates whenever the other state updates, we should manage the info text as a computed value. And that's what that derived state or computed state concept is all about. If you are setting a state that in the end is directly related to another state, you typically don't wanna manage this as a separate state. Instead you just want to add a new variable, a good old variable in your component function. And that could now be the "filter info text" which initially is this text here with the year 2020 excluded. And then we can extract this "if" check here out of this handler function, and also put it directly in this component function. So that there, we now check our filtered year. So this state on which this value in the end depends, we use this state in our "if" condition here, and we simply set our variable to those different pieces of text. So instead of updating a separate state, we just dynamically compute the value of this variable, and the value will be different depending on which state was selected. This condition now also is redundant here, since the value for this check here is our default anyways. So, we can even get rid of that. And now we have this leaner computed code here, which works because when the state is updated, when this function here is called, this component function will re-execute again anyways. So all this code here will be executed again, and will derive a new value for "filter info text" therefore. Hence, now I save everything, we get exactly the same behavior as before. If I switch my state here, my filter, this value updates. Even though the value itself is not managed as state, but it's part of the component that's re-executed when the state changes, and it then depends on the state that changes. So this here is a computed value, a value which we derive based on this state. And whilst you could also manage multiple states, and it wouldn't be a huge problem using derived values like this, it's typically the more elegant and recommended approach. Here, I'll get rid of that though because we don't need that info text. But knowing about this concept of derived state or derived values as it's also called, is important.

Here, we can then target all these different CSS properties, for example, textAlign, which I'll simply set to center here, and with that, with that inline styling added here, this fallback text is now centered, but that's the only new style I want to add. With that, if you save this and reload, you should get the same look as before, but now, we put the header-related styles into this CSS module file, and, of course, I'll do the same for the rest of this app as well. For that, I'll continue with the user-input-related styles by grabbing this form rule here, the input-group rule, and all these other rules that are related to the form, including the button rules like this, cut them, and add them here to UserInput.module.css, and here, this should actually be .button:hover, and with that in UserInput.js, we, of course, also have to import classes from ./UserInput.module.css, and now, here, on the form, we apply classes.form like this. 39465488.title: Module Introduction
39465488.transcript: In this course section here, we will explore how we can animate React apps, how we can bring them to life because whilst static apps that don't have any animations of course also might work, animated apps often look and feel better, and therefore may also drive user engagement with your website. Take this website here as an example and indeed this is the example we'll be working on throughout this section. This website here has a starting page, and then it has a challenges page where website users can create and manage challenges and every challenge has a title, an image, and so on. And users can then also mark challenges as completed or failed and switch between different tabs to view those different challenges. Now, this website works fine and looks decent, I'd argue but it can be improved by adding animations. For example, here's exactly the same page now enhanced with extra animations that have been added to it. And you'll see tiny little animations in basically all areas of this website and therefore, I'd argue this website looks and feels nicer and website visitors might therefore be motivated to spend more time with your web app, which ultimately of course is probably the goal of every web app you're building. That's why in this course section we'll explore how you can animate React apps and we'll actually start by taking a look at just CSS because you might not even know which features are built into CSS that can be used to animate parts of your website. But as you'll also see in this section CSS animations might be enough in certain use cases but they also have certain limitations. And that's why for the majority of this section we'll explore a third party package called Framer Motion which is an amazing package for building and controlling more complex animations. And you will learn how to use that package, how to for example, use it to animate elements on the screen in and out to animate lists. And also how to build scroll based animations. So animations that depend on the user scrolling on the website as you can see it on the starting page of that finished website, for example. So plenty of things in this section. Let's get started.

And then this challenges area here where you got these tabs and where you can also add a new challenge like learn react from the ground up, set a date, pick an image, add it and then view it here, expand the details and so on. 39471858.title: Animating with CSS Transitions
39471858.transcript: Now, as I mentioned before, we'll actually not start by diving into Framer Motion right away, even though we'll spend the majority of this section with that package. But we will actually start with just CSS because you might not need an animation library like Framer Motion, instead CSS Transitions and Animations, features that are built right into vanilla CSS, might be enough for some use cases. And therefore you don't necessarily need to install and learn a new library just to animate some things. For example here in this demo app, let's take a look at this view details button here. When I press it, this expands, and I can view some details here, but you might notice that this icon here never changes. And it would be nice if this would basically just rotate and point down if the details are expanded and point up if they're not. And as it turns out this is something you can animate with just CSS. To do that, let's go to that project folder and then there you'll find an index CSS file which is rather large because it contains all the styles for this entire project here, for this entire website just to have one single CSS file and not have a bunch of CSS files flying around here. And in that file, we can find this button here or some style that allows us to target that button. And to now add some CSS code here to animate this button, let's first go to the JSX code that's responsible for this button and this code can be found here in the components folder, in the ChallengeItem.jsx file, .jsx as a file extension here because this is Vite project where you need to add .jsx instead of just .js at the end. And then in this file here, you got the view details text, and then here this is the icon. So this entire span here is the icon that should be animated. And as you see, this icon has a CSS className and we can just copy that className and then search for that className here in this CSS file. And once we found it, we can animate it because as you can see, there are two CSS rules that seem to involve this icon. And even if you don't know CSS, which you absolutely don't have to for this section, you should be able to find these two rules and you will be able to follow along with the changes we're about to make. Because now that we identified these two rules, we can add some animations here. And indeed, as you can see there already is the transform CSS property being set to this rotate value, so it looks like we're already trying to at least rotate the icon, even if it's not animated yet. But for this to be activated, we must make sure that the expanded CSS class is added to some element above this icon element that has a class of challenge-item-details. So let's go back to the ChallengeItem.jsx file and there it's this challenge-item-details div that needs this expanded class for this icon to be rotated. So we wanna conditionally add this expanded class here if the details are visible and as you can see here in this component there is isExpanded prop which we're receiving, which then tells us whether this item here is currently expanded, whether the details of this item are expanded. So with that, we can just go here and actually replace this className here with a dynamic value where we can use the template literal syntax with two backticks to construct a string with that because it's this backtick syntax that then makes it easy for us to inject dynamic values into this string. And here I want to inject a dynamic value depending on whether is expanded is true or not. If it's true, I want to add the expanded class here to this list of classes. Otherwise I'll add an empty string, so I will not add any extra classes. This is one way of dynamically adding a class to a class list. And with that change made, if we save this, we can now at least see that this button rotates. But of course it's not animated yet and that's the next step because animating this button now means that we need to tell CSS that this change to this rotation should be animated. And this can be done with help of a feature called CSS Transitions. CSS Transitions can be used by adding the transition property to a rule and here it's to the base rule, which is always active on this icon, so not the rule that's just active if the parent is expanded, but instead the rule that's always active. And here we can tell CSS for which future changes to which CSS properties we want to animate those changes. And here in this example, where we want to animate this transformation, we could tell CSS that we wanna animate all transitions for the transform property. So whenever that property changes, for example from no value to this value, we wanna animate that change, we want to have CSS animate that change, that's what we're saying here. You could also say all if you want to animate all CSS properties that could change on this element, but here it's just a transform property. Next, you can set a duration over which the animation should be played. For example, 0.3 seconds, you could also write this as 300 milliseconds, and it's of course up to you which duration you wanna pick here. And then you can also add a easing function which basically controls the acceleration of the animation and therefore the look and feel of it. For example here, we could use ease out or ease in or any other easing function value, here I'll go for ease out. And with that added, if we save this you will see that now this arrow here is animated, even though we have not added any extra library, instead just by using a feature that's built into CSS. And you should therefore absolutely be aware of features like this because often the built-in animation support provided by CSS might be all you need.

39471864.title: Animating with CSS Animations
39471864.transcript: Now CSS transitions as we're using them here to animate this icon are nice, but sometimes not enough. For example, here, if we open this modal, it might be nice if we would play a little animation. As this modal opens, maybe it should slide up from the bottom and also fade in whilst it's doing that. And this can also still be achieved with just CSS but not with a CSS transition, at least not here the way this application is built. Because if we take a look at our component code and there at the Header.jsx file, we can see that this new challenge component which in the end is the component that also uses this modal that this new challenge component in the header file is added to the DOM conditionally based on this IsCreatingNewChallenge state. So it's not always part of the DOM. And you can of course also see that if you inspect this modal here, there if you inspect the body and then this div with the ID modal, you find the backdrop behind the modal and the modal dialogue here but as I close the modal, both are gone. So they're not part of the DOM at all if the modal is not open. Now, this also means that we can't use a CSS transition because we can't transition and animate from any initial value as this modal opens because there is no initial value because the modal element is not there right at the start. But we can still animate it as you will see by using another feature that's built into CSS, CSS animations. The idea behind CSS animations is that we define a animation, so a sequence of steps on our own by using the special at key frames syntax here. Then you can give your animation any name of your choice. For example, slide up, fade in, and then you have curly braces to define the different steps your animation should go through. And this allows you to set an initial state, even if the element was not part of the DOM before and an end state and as many in-between states as you want. You can do this with from and to or with percentage. So 0% means that will be the state at the beginning. 100% means that this will be the end state. And then between those curly braces that belong to those states, you set up your CSS properties. So for example, here for the modal, we might want to push it down a little bit initially so that it can slide up as this animation plays. We can use the transform property for this again and then use the translateY property to push it down. And here we could, for example, push it down by 30 pixels. In addition, we might wanna set the opacity to zero so that initially it's invisible, even though it's part of the DOM. And then we can copy this and set our end state to have a translateY value of zero so that it's not pushed down anymore and an opacity of one so that it's not invisible anymore. And now with that custom animation defined here in CSS, we can go to the selector where we wanna attach the animation so to the elements the animation should be attached to, in this case, this modal. And then use the special animation CSS property to attach an animation to this element. And this animation will then automatically be triggered by CSS whenever this element appears in the DOM. So here we then refer to our animation name so this name here in my case of course. Then we also set a duration, which of course is again up to you, also if you want a easing function. And then here I'll also add a special keyword, forwards, at the end, which simply means that I want to keep the end state once the animation is done. So once this duration here is over. Now you could configure more and you can, of course check out the MDN article on CSS animations if you wanna learn all about them. This of course, is not a CSS course but these are the most important configuration items of the CSS animation feature that you wanna be aware of. And with that set, you'll notice that if I go back and I click add challenge, now this modal does indeed slide up from the bottom and fade in whilst doing that. And that's therefore another way of animating things you should be aware of. Again, using just CSS and with CSS animations and CSS transitions which I showed you before, you have two extremely powerful features that often might be all you need.

39471868.title: Introducing Framer Motion
39471868.transcript: Now CSS transitions and animations are really powerful but they also have limitations. For example, one limitation is that you can use CSS animations to animate the appearance of an element in the DOM, but not the disappearance. Because whenever this element, this modal here, for example, is removed from the DOM, it's removed instantly and there is no chance for us to animate that, at least not with just CSS. That's one limitation. Another limitation would be more complex animations, like for example, if this bar here should be animated as it switches between those different tabs. With just CSS, this would technically certainly be possible in some way but it would be really complex and not easy at all. And another thing that would be pretty complex to achieve with just CSS, is a realistic look and feel of certain animations. For example, here, animating this arrow works and definitely looks all right, but take a look at this animation instead in comparison. It might not look like much, but here on the right side, it looks more natural, more physically correct, you could say. And it's for reasons like this, hard limitations like not being able to animate the disappearance of an element or this look and feel aspect or the complexity you would have to go through to build certain animations that you might wanna reach for extra libraries like Framer Motion, which is an extremely powerful animation library for React to which I'll introduce you throughout the rest of this section. Now, in order to use Framer Motion in your project, you first of all have to install it. Now locally, that means that you should stop the development server and run npm install framer-motion and this will install this package and make it available in this project. And thereafter, you can restart that development server. On CodeSandbox it's in this dependencies area here on the left where you should search for Framer Motion and then select it and this will add it as a dependency and make it available in this project. And with it added, you are ready to get started with it. Now, of course, you can absolutely also explore the official documentation, and I would recommend that you do that after going through this section here, because Framer Motion is a pretty complex and powerful library, and you therefore might want to dive deeper into it and explore all its configuration options and more advanced features after you went through the introduction you'll get in this section here. And speaking of an introduction, in order to get you started with Framer Motion, I prepared a little playground which we can use to get started with the basics of Framer Motion and attach to therefore find this playground both as a local and a CodeSandbox version. And in both versions, Framer Motion is already installed so you don't need to do that here. And of course, we will switch back to this project here, to the challenges project later in this section, but in order to get started with Framer Motion and learn about its basics, we'll use this playground here.

39471870.title: Framer Motion Basics & Fundamentals
39471870.transcript: So here in this playground, we got this box here and we got a couple of input fields. And my idea now is to allow you to enter values here into these input fields and to then animate this box based on those input fields. For example, if you enter five here into the input X this block here should be moved to the right by five pixels. That's the idea. And of course, this should be achieved with Framer Motion so that this change in position or rotation is animated. Now for that, we can go into the app.jsx file in this playground project. And as you see there we already have some state management going on here to get the values entered by the user essentially. And here we got this box. Now to animate it with Framer Motion all you have to do is import motion from Framer Motion and you have to replace this div or whichever element you wanna animate with motion.div. This will then still render a div on the page but a div with extra capabilities that can be controlled by Framer Motion to animate it in a highly performant way because that's important about this Framer Motion package. It's really built to give you high performance animations not animations that cause unnecessary component re-render cycles or that slow down the application but instead animations that are as performant as possible. That's why you have to use special elements. And there is not just motion div, of course, instead if you press the dot after motion, you see that you basically got all HTML elements as motion versions so that you can really animate anything. But here is a div I want. Now with this special motion component here being used we can now add a special prop to this component, the animate prop which is available because we're using this motion version of the div. And Animate now in its basic form wants an object as a value where we describe the animation we wanna play. And here you can animate a broad variety of things because Framer Motion is really powerful. You can, for example, animate the text color or the background color of an element. You can animate the rotation, you can animate the X and Y position and a lot of other things as well. Simply play around with this prop and all the things you can set and take a look at the official documentation to learn about all the things you can animate. Now, one of the most common things to animate is the position of an element though because often the elements should move around on the website and we can, for example animate the movement along the horizontal axis by animating the X coordinate of the element. And we can set this here for example, to this X. So this X is now what I'm feeding as a value to this X here and therefore whenever this state here updates the animation should play again because that's how Framer Motion works. When you set this animate prop it will re-trigger the animation and animate this element whenever the value you want to animate to changes. So therefore, here, if we save this, if I type 100 here you see that moved to the right. If I type minus 300, you see it moves to the left. And as you can also see it's also animated whilst it moves around and it indeed has animation that looks and feels really realistic. If you can say that, it has a realistic look and feel because it bounces a little bit it feels like if you would really be pushing a block around and that's another feature built into Framer Motion. It gives you this natural look and feel for your animations, at least if you want it. Now, when using Framer Motion, the animate prop is one of your most important props because it's this prop that's used to declaratively animate an element but another important prop is the transition prop which is a prop that can be used to configure the animation that will be played. You do that by passing an object to transition and then here in this object you can set a broad variety of things. For example, the duration over which this change in this value will be animated. For example, we could set this to three which would mean three seconds, with that, if I saved this and I changed this X value you now see that the animation plays much slower because I decreased that duration. It now takes three seconds to go to the end state, but the duration isn't everything, though I'll set it back to a much lower value so that the animation plays quicker. Another thing you can set here, for example is the type property. The type property allows you to control which type of animation should be played. For example, this spring type, which was the default as long as this transition prop wasn't added is this type that has this natural look and feel with this bouncing and so on. And you can indeed also control how much it bounces by adding the bounce property here. If you set bounce to one you got a lot of bouncing going on as you can see and if you set it to zero, you got no bouncing. And of course you can set it to any value between zero and one to control how much it bounces but you can also switch to a different type. For example, if you change to tween you'll have a simple animation between the starting and end value without any physics simulation as you do get it when using spring. So if I change to tween here you'll see that now there is no bouncing no matter which bounce factor you set. Instead, you have a simple animation between the start and the end value. Now, sometimes this might be the look you want but often it's this spring type you want because especially when animating the position or size of elements using this physics-based approach often looks and feels best for users. So that's why I'll stick to spring here and why I will now also make sure that we can also animate movement on the Y axis by picking up that Y state and binding that to our animate prop and also rotations. This can be achieved by setting the rotate property on this object we're passing to animate, and since here property and value variable names are equal. We can also use the JavaScript shortcut of just using this name once, and it'll automatically be expanded to the longer version behind the scenes by JavaScript. This is not Framer Motion or React specific. Instead that's a default JavaScript feature. But with that, if we now save that, we can also rotate this for example, by setting it to 90 or 130 and we can of course also move it on the Y axis. There you also see that we can play smooth animations by changing this Y value.

39471878.title: Adding Entry Animations
39471878.transcript: As a next step, I also wanna migrate this entry animation of this modal here from just CSS to Framer Motion. And I wanna do this for two reasons. And then here, instead of using the standard dialogue, I wanna use the motion dialogue so that this element can be animated with Framer Motion. Then as a next step, as before, we can add the animate prop to define the value we want to animate to. But here's something tricky now. Inside of this modal component, this dialogue will always be part of the screen, and there won't be any props or any state that would change inside of this component here that would tell us that this modal did now appear. So animating to a different value will be tricky here because we got no indicator for whether this modal was just added or if it's been visible for a minute now. So unlike in challenge-item, where we had this isExpanded prop that could be used to switch between different values. We have no such prop or state in here. But we also don't need it, because these motion components also give us access to another pretty useful prop that we can set on them. We can set the initial prop, and this initial prop allows us to define an initial state for the to-be-performed animations that will be assumed immediately after this element has been added to the DOM. So initial allows us to set the starting state of this element, so to say. And if that state then deviates from the animate state, Framer Motion will automatically play that animation to get to that target animate state. So therefore, here, in initial, we can set the opacity to zero, for example, to make it invisible initially when it occurs in the DOM, and set the y value to minus 30 to push it down by 30 pixels. And then in animate, I wanna set the opacity to 1, so that will be the target value for the opacity and y to 0, so that it's not pushed down anymore. And that's how we can use initial and animate to play an initial animation from these values to these values. And that's there for already all again. If we save that, and I click Add Challenge, it's coming down from the top, because of course this should be a positive number here to push it down. So this must be plus 30, not minus 30. But with that, again, if I click Add Challenge, this is now coming up from the bottom. And it also has a slight bounce effect, which you might see here, and therefore feels a bit more natural and snappy, which is great. But as a next step, let's now also make sure that we can animate the disappearance of this modal, because as mentioned before, that's not really possible with just CSS.

39471884.title: Animating Element Disappearances / Removal
39471884.transcript: To animate the disappearance of an element in the DOM, we can add another useful prop to these motion components. And that's the exit prop which also takes such a configuration object here. And you can probably see a pattern here. We have the initial prop for the starting state when the element appears in the DOM. Then animate to define the state we want to animate to right after it did appear. And then exit, which allows us to define the state that we want to animate to when the element is removed from the DOM. And here I'll just copy and paste the initial state as my exit state but of course you could configure this however you want and Framer Motion would automatically figure out how to get from here to here. But with that, if you save that, you'll notice something strange. If I opened this modal and then close it, for example, by clicking on the backdrop, no animation is played. It still disappears instantly. And the reason for that is that just by using this motion component here, you are not changing how React handles elements. And React handles elements such that if they're not returned by a component function anymore; so the hetero component here, if new challenge which includes that modal is not part of that returned value anymore because IsCreatingNewChallenge turned false, if that's the case, it's instantly removed from the DOM. And React doesn't ask whether you wanna do that or not. It does just do that. It does instantly remove it. But Framer Motion gives you a way of working around that. It gives you a special component that you can wrap around code like this to disable or control this functionality. For that Here in the Header.jsx file, you must import something from Framer Motion and that something is not motion but instead a component provided by a Framer Motion that's called animate presence. This component is meant to be used as a wrapper around code that conditionally displays or removes elements if you want to animate those elements, to be precise, if you want to animate the disappearance of those elements. So for that, we can use animate presence as a wrapper here. And just by doing that, Framer Motion will make sure that if this code here executes the elements that are rendered by that code, so also that modal are not removed instantly, but instead it will check if there's some element that has an exit animation. And if it finds such an element, it will play that exit animation first before removing this and all the other elements. And therefore, if you save that, you'll now see that opening plays this animation but so does closing. If I click this backdrop, now we got this nice closing animation, this exit animation where this modal is pushed down and fades out. And this now is the case because of this animate presence component that's used as a wrapper around the code that ultimately is responsible for rendering this modal where we have this motion component that has this exit prop. That's how those things are working together.

39471898.title: Making Elements "Pop" With Hover Animations
39471898.transcript: Now let's stay in this header component for now, because in that header, there's one other element I'd like to animate with help of Framer Motion, and that would be this button because you might wanna make sure that this button pops a bit more as users hover over it, maybe by increasing the scale and making it bounce a little bit. And this is pretty easy to do with Framer Motion since it gives us these realistic natural feeling animations. Therefore, here in the header component, I will now actually also import motion from Framer Motion so that we can turn this standard button here into a motion button and unlock these special features. And here I now wanna do something we haven't done before because here I don't wanna set the animate prop because whilst we could do that to change, for example, the scale of this button and make it pop as a user hovers over it, trying to animate this button with the animate prop here would mean that we also would have to listen to the onHover event here to make sure that we change this scale as the user hovers over this button. And as the user does, stop hovering over this button. And whilst this would be possible, it's a lot of unnecessary work. Instead, since it is fairly common that you wanna animate elements as users click them or hover over them, Framer Motion gives you some special props you can add to those framework components which start with while at the beginning. And then you get special props like whileHover or whileTap, which allow you to define animation states that should only apply whilst the user is tapping or hovering and so on or whilst the element is in view. So whilst it can be seen on the page, for example. So here, I'll use whileHover and herein whileHover, we can then set an animation object as we would add it on the animate prop to, for example change the scale to 1.1. So increase the scale by 10%, and that's all with that done, if we save this, this button now pops a little more, but as you can see out of the box, it just increases in scale and does not really bounce. The reason for that is that here, by default, Framer Motion does not apply a spring animation but this can thankfully be changed by setting the transition here and then setting the type to spring. And I showed you this transition prop before. And the great thing about this prop is that it will control all animations applied on this element no matter if you set them with animate, with exit, or with whileHover and so on. So here, we can change this to spring and then for example, also configure something called the stiffness and set this to 500. And this will also have an impact on the bounce effect and it's best if you simply play around with that value and also with the mass value here, and set these properties to different values to get a feeling for their impact. They do play together with this spring animation type though, and therefore with that added here on this button now you'll see that as I hover on this button and also as I hover away, we got this nice bounce effect and it definitely now pops more than it did before. So that's now a nicer look I'd say. And we therefore now don't just have this modal entry and exit animation but also a nice popping button to open this modal.

39471902.title: Reusing Animation States
39471902.transcript:
With that button finished here. Let's go back to this modal.
Here in this modal, we previously added the initial animate and exit animation states. And as you might notice, the initial and exit animation, of course, is the same here. Now we could therefore of course, add a constant here where we have our hidden animation state or however you wanna call it and store this object in this constant. And then just reuse this constant here so that if we ever wanna make changes here, we just have to make those changes in one place instead of two places. And with that, you will see that we still got this entry and access animation, but there also is another way of reusing a certain animation value or animation state. And for that, you can add the variance prop to your motion component. And I will say right away that the main idea behind variance is a different one which we'll explore in the next lecture. But nonetheless, this variance prop indeed also useful for defining and reusing certain animation states. For this variance takes an object. But now it's not such an animation object but instead an object where you set any keys of your choice. For example, a key named hidden. And then the value for that key will again be such an animation object. And with that, we're defining a custom identifier and then the animation state that should be applied if this identifier is targeted. And you will soon learn how to target such an identifier. Now here, besides hidden, I'll also add another variant called visible. Though as mentioned, these names are up to you. And that will be this animation state here for me. And you can now target these different variants and apply those animation states here by using these variant identifiers as values for initial animate or exit. So here for initial, I can set this to hidden. For animate, I can set this to visible. And for exit, I can again set this to hidden. And of course, these keys here have to be the keys you're using here. So you have to make sure that you don't have any typos here. But with that, you've got an easy way of defining those variants once and of then reusing them. And you can also reuse them with while hover and so on. So this would also be possible, though of course, this here makes no sense for this modal since now it would disappear whenever I hover over it, but it would work and you therefore see how those variants work. But I'll get rid of while hover now, of course. And with that, you can see that this modal still has this entry and exit animation. It works as shown before, but now powered by variants. Though as mentioned before, the true power of variants comes from another feature which we'll explore in the next lecture.

39471904.title: Nested Animations & Variants
39471904.transcript: Variants cannot just be used for defining and reusing animation states, but instead they can also be used to trigger animations deep inside of a component tree by just setting an animation to a certain variant on an ancestor component. And this of course, probably sounds pretty abstract, so let me show you what I mean. Keep in mind that here, we're in the modal component, and this modal component, this dialog here, will in the end wrap whatever is passed between the opening and closing tags of this modal component. And that therefore, here in my case, will be this New Challenge component, to be precise here, this form, because this form is passed as a child between the modal opening and closing tag here. Now the idea behind variants is that we can now set a certain animation state to a variant in such a wrapper or a parent component, like this modal component here, and inside of the wrapped or child component, like this form here and all the elements we're using in here. We can now define the same variants, so using the same names, hidden and visible, in my case, but we don't have to set them there to activate them. Instead, if they activate in the parent or wrapping component in this modal component here, they will automatically be activated in the child component. And this can be very useful because here in this modal, we could, for example, animate the appearance of those images here. And of course, I want to animate those as this modal is opened, as this component here is loaded therefore. Now thanks to this variants feature, we can go to this unordered list and there to these list items which are these images, and we can change this list item here to a motion list item so that we can animate it with help of variants. To do that, of course, we first must import motion from framer-motion as always, and then go down to that list item again and replace it with motion.li. But other than that, it's of course still a regular list item. And now to play some animation when this appears, we don't have to add animate and initial, instead we just have to define a hidden and visible variant with the variants prop here, because those variants will then automatically be activated by the parent wrapping component, this modal component, because there we are setting this hidden variant as a value for the initial state and divisible variant as a value for the animate state. And as mentioned, this information, which variant should be active at which point of time, will be passed down by a Framer Motion to any wrapped or child components, so also to this list item here, and therefore here, we just have to define a hidden and a visible variant and those variants will be activated automatically when they activate on the parent component. And here on those images, I want my images to be transparent initially and maybe be a bit smaller so that they basically grow in size as they become visible, and therefore, in the visible state, I want to have an opacity of 1 and a scale of 1, let's say. Now in addition, I'll also add the transition prop, which we of course can also do on such a child animation element, and here I'll make sure that we use this spring animation type. Now with that, if we save that, you'll see that if I open this, those images do bounce in. You'll also note, though, that as I close this modal, we have a pretty strange effect. It takes some time for the backdrop to disappear. And we got this behavior here because in my modal, I'm setting this exit prop here to the hidden state, to the hidden variant. Therefore, this will also be activated on the child components and therefore those images will actually be animated out there in this child component. And we don't really see this here as I close this, though you can see it briefly. If you take a close look at those images, you might see them shrink right before the modal disappears and Framer Motion actually waits for that image animation to finish before it then finally removes this element from the DOM and also this backdrop from the DOM. And that's of course not the behavior I want here. Now, one way to fix or change this behavior is to go to this child component, this list item component here, and to there, set the exit prop and set it to visible here. So with that, I'm overriding the variant that should be activated when we enter this exit state on this list item, and I'm setting it to the visible variant just for this component, not for any other component. So for the parent component for this modal, the exit state will still be set to the hidden variant but for this child component, this nested list item here, the exit state will be set to the visible variant, and therefore no animation will be played here because we already are in this visible state. So now with this exit prop set to visible here, you will see that as you close this modal, indeed now the backdrop does disappear right after the modal closes. But you also will notice that now the entry animation of these images is gone. If I open the modal, those images are not animated anymore. And we got this behavior because at least at the point of time where I'm recording this, if you set one of those animation props like initial, animate, or exit on some child components when using that variants feature to activate animations as we're doing it here, you must not use one of your variant names here. Instead, you should copy the value that's assigned to a certain variant, the visible variant in this case, and use stat in stat. This might be fixed and changed in the future but at the point of time I'm recording this, this is the workaround that's needed to make sure that we're still using the variants feature to automatically activate those animations whilst also making sure that we're not causing this delay in the removal of this modal here. With this workaround applied, if you save everything, if we open the modal, you see this animation for these images is back and it still closes fine. So now we got the best of both worlds. We got those entry animations, we got a nice closing modal, and we're using variants to activate animations in a child component from inside a parent component.

39471910.title: Animating Staggered Lists
39471910.transcript: So now that we're animating the appearance of those images, we can also explore another nice feature offered by Framer Motion that helps with animating list items like this list of images here. Because often when rendering lists you don't want all list items to appear and be animated at exactly the same point of time. But instead, you might want to have them animated, step-by-step, after each other. And this is a technique that's called staggering. You want to stagger that animation of those list items. So you wanna play them after each other instead of simultaneously. And this is thankfully something that's pretty easy to achieve with Framer Motion. For this, you have to go to the parent element of the list items that you do want to stagger. So in this case, this unordered list element and replace it with a motion unordered list element, of course also the closing tag. And with that done here, since I'm using variance to activate these child animations, we should also add the variance prop here to this unordered list component. Now here for my variants, I wanna set the visible variant, so this variant here. But here I'm not setting it to animate the opacity or scale of this unordered list because I am happy with that default look and behavior of that list. I don't wanna animate the list as a whole I just want to animate the list items. And therefore I'll not set any of those properties but instead, when using variance you can also set the special transition property here, which allows you to apply the same configuration you are applying with the transition prop to the entire component and all the animations on that component to a specific animation. And this, by the way, is not just possible for variants because whilst often you might want to globally configure all animations on a component, you can always add this transition property here to control just the exit animation and to use different settings for the exit animation than you're using for all the other animations on this component, for example. So that's always possible, not just when using variants, but when using variants I can set this transition on my visible variant on this parent component of those list items to set a special configuration option here. And that's the staggered children configuration option. And this option can be used to control the delay by which these child elements in this parent element will start their animations. By default, they will all start simultaneously, but if you set stagger children to any value that's not zero, so for example to 0.05, then this will be the delay between all those different child items. So now the second item will start its animation 50 milliseconds after the first item and the third item will start its animation 50 milliseconds after the second item and so on. That's what this setting does, and you can of course play around with this time setting to see its impact. And by setting this, if we save this, if we open this, you now see that those images don't appear simultaneously but instead after each other. And this of course, is even easier to see if I use a bigger value here to add more delay between those different child item animations. Now you see they animate in really slowly after each other and therefore it's of course up to you which value you want to use. I think it looks best if we use a very low value so that we have this staggering effect, but overall still animate everything pretty quickly. And that's how you can easily place such a staggering animation for list items when using Framer Motion.

39471914.title: Animating Colors & Working with Keyframes
39471914.transcript: Up to this point in this section here, we pretty much only used numbers as VLUs for these different animation properties, so for opacity, scale, and so on. And that's not uncommon because opacity, scale, X and Y are amongst thee most important properties you might wanna animate. But it's worth mentioning that you're not limited to simple numbers like this, for example, in this header component, where we get this button, this button here that pops when we hover over it, we could also animate its color, for example, which we don't set through a number, but through a hex code instead. This could be achieved by going to the whileHover property here and then for example, setting the background color property here, so that we don't just animate the scale, but also the background color and as a color, you can then set a string that contains the hex code of the color you wanna animate to. So for example, here we could define the hex code 8b11f0 to animate to a nice purple background color and if we save that, you'll see that as I hover over this, indeed the color also gets animated and please note that the color also kind of bounces, you could say, so that the transition settings set here are also applied to the color here. And it's just important to be aware of the fact that you're not limited to animating just the scale, opacity, or position with numbers, but that you can also animate more complex things like colors with Framer Motion. Now, with that, I'll actually get rid of the color here because I don't wanna animate it, but it's important to understand that you can. And there's also some other kind of VLU you should be aware of and for that, I'm back in the NewChallenge.jsx file and here on this list item which I'm animating, these staggered list items here. Here on the list item in the visible variant, I'm setting the scale to 1 and with that, I make sure that in the end we animate from a scale of .5 to 1, so from half the size I wanna have to the size this element should have. But instead of setting a single VLU here, we can actually also set an array as a VLU here and if you do that, if you use an array VLU as a value for a property you're animating, you're creating an array of key frames through which Framer Motion will go as it animates this item, so it allows you to define multiple steps the animation should go through instead of just having an initial VLU and a final VLU. And therefore, here for example, for this visible state of this list item, we could add a bunch of numbers here and for example, first go to a size of 80% of the size I wanna have, then go up to 1.3, so 30% bigger than it should be, and then to 1 at the end, so that we go from 50% scale to 80% to 130 before we reach the final scale of 1 which you wanna have, so the size the element should have. And with that, if we save that, if I click Add Challenge here, those images are again popping in and staggering in, but they're now actually bouncing slightly differently according to my key frames here. And that's therefore another feature you should be aware of, you can set key frames as VLUs for the properties you're animating.

39471916.title: Imperative Animations
39471916.transcript: So at this point you already know a lot about using Framer Motion with those motion components to animate things. And up to this point in this section we used the default approach of working with Framer Motion by using these motion components and by defining all the animations and all the animation settings declaratively in our JSX code through props. That's the default and that's how you will define and control most of your animations with Framer Motion. But sometimes you encounter scenarios or use cases where you maybe wanna start an animation imperatively instead of declaratively. And therefore I'll also show you how that could be done. And for that, let's assume that if the user submits an invalid form here, so if the input fields are empty, or at least one input field is empty, for example, that we wanna shake all these input fields. That could be a little animation we might wanna play if a user tries to submit this without entering valid values in those input fields. And whilst we could definitely also achieve this goal in a declarative way, here I'll show you how you could do it in a imperative way because Framer Motion also supports this approach. For that, we first of all have to import a hook from Framer Motion. We have to import the useAnimate hook. This hook can then be called in our component function like all hooks. And this hook then returns an array that contains two important elements: a scope element, and an animate element. The names of course are up to you, but the first element will be a ref, which you can add to elements and I'll explain why and where to add it soon. And the second element, animate, will be a function you can use in your code to imperatively trigger a certain animation. Now let's start with that function here actually. And for that, let's go to the place where the animation should be triggered, which is inside of this if statement. Here, I'm checking if any of the inputs contains an invalid value, and if that's the case at the moment, I just return. Now I instead want to call animate here to trigger some animation and shake those input and textarea elements. To achieve this, you can pass a string to animate, where you use a CSS selector to target the elements that should be animated. For example, all input and textarea elements like this. You could also look for CSS classes if you had them. But here I'll just use the input and textarea tags. As a second argument to animate, you can then pass an object where you describe the animation that should be played. And that's the same kind of object you would pass to exit or initial or animate or to your variance down there. So it's an object like this where you define which properties should be animated to which values. So for example here, we could animate the exposition to move the elements along the X axis and then define some key frames here, so that maybe we first move them to the left by 10 pixels, then back to the middle, then to the right by 10 pixels, and then back to the middle, so that we shake every element from left to right. And of course you could animate more, the opacity, whatever you want. But here for me it's just the X position that I want to animate. As a third argument you can then also pass a configuration object to animate that allows you to configure how the animation will be played. That would be the equivalent to this transition prop, or this transition property, you can add to your variance. So in there, in this last object, you can, for example, make sure that it's a spring animation that's being played. You can control the duration, for example, set this to 0.2 seconds. And you can also do something fancy here, you can add a delay property here which allows you to set a delay after which the animation will start. Now that on its own is not that fancy, even though it sometimes can be helpful, but it gets fancy if we combine it with another imperative function we can use from Framer Motion because from there we can also import a stagger function. And stagger might sound familiar to you, we used the staggerChildren setting before to make sure that the animations on the child items of this list get staggered so that delays are inserted between the different animations of the different items. And this stagger function simply allows us to do the same thing if we're setting up an imperative animation, so if we're using the animate function. Because then we can set delay here to stagger and then our stagger amount like 0.05. And this will then achieve the same as staggerChildren did before here on this unordered list. It will add delays between the different animations for the different elements that are targeted by this selector, so that we don't shake them simultaneously but after each other. And that's how we can trigger an animation imperatively from inside our code. But that alone won't do the trick. You might recall that here we also had this scope element in that array that was returned by useAnimate. And this scope thing here is in the end a ref as we could create it ourselves with useRef that should be connected to a JSX element that's scopes the selector we're setting up here in the animate function. So that we're not selecting input and textarea elements from all over the page, but from a selected area. For that, we should add the ref here to this form and pass this scope ref as a value for this ref prop to this form. And now animate will only select inputs and text areas inside of this form and not anywhere else on the page. And with that, we're using useAnimate to trigger our animation imperatively, and therefore here, if I click Add Challenge with invalid inputs, you see that those inputs are shaking and they're not shaking simultaneously, but instead in this staggered manner after each other.

39471920.title: Animating Layout Changes
39471920.transcript: So at this point, I am quite happy with this modal here. And of course we can now use it as we always could to add new challenges to our list of challenges here. And that's indeed the part I wanna work on next because here we got these different tabs, and at the moment we don't really have any animations in those tabs. We have no animation as we switch tabs and we got no animation if we have multiple items here and we, for example move one item to a different tab by marking it as completed it just disappears here and appears here. But this list updates without any animation which also of course could be improved. And that's what we'll do next. And therefore here in this project I'll switch to the challenges JSX file which is where I have my challenges tab and this list of challenge items. And here we could, for example start by animating these challenge items as we mark them as completed, to be precise, what I'd like to achieve is that as those items are removed the list does not instantly update and snap into place like this. But instead, the item that remains here should be animated smoothly as the item before it was removed. And this can easily be achieved by going to this challenge item component where I have this list item that makes up such a challenge item in my list by replacing this list item with a motion list item. Now I'm already importing motion here in challenge item so we just have to Convert LI to motion.li, also the closing tag of course. Now when we do that out of the box, nothing happens. These items still move instantly and the list updates instantly. So nothing changed. But now that this is a motion component we can add a special property here. The layout property, if you add this prop to a motion component, Framer Motion will automatically try to animate layout changes in that component. So in that list item here, in this case, which means for example, that if we have a list with two items and we move the first item by marking it as failed the second item, this item in this case here, of course remains, but it now moves to the top. And this move to the top would then be animated by Framer Motion. And therefore, now with layout added here to this motion component, if I marked this first item as failed and its therefore gets removed from this list you see the second item moves up there. Now, of course, we have no animation. If I mark this as failed as well because now it's not moving to a different place. It's just gone here, it just reappears if I go to this tab. But if I have a scenario where an element needs to move to a new position, like here if I mark the first item as completed, now this is animated. If you add this layout prop to the element that is moving.

39471924.title: Orchestrating Multi-Element Animations
39471924.transcript: Now in this list here, which we're animating, there also are other things we could animate besides the remaining item moving around. For example, we could also animate the disappearance of the item. So not just the item that remains, that moves to a new position, but also the item that disappears. And we already learned before at the example of the modal here how we can animate the disappearance of an element. We need that AnimatePresence component. And here in this case, for this list, we need it in the Challenges.jsx file because that is where I'm rendering this list of items. So that's in the end also the code that's controlling how many items are displayed and it's also this code that updates the list if this array of items here changes. Therefore it's in this Challenges.jsx file where we should import AnimatePresence from Framer Motion. Now with AnimatePresence imported here in Challenges.jsx we can now wrap it here around this code that is responsible for rendering this list of challenge items. So here we wrap AnimatePresence around challenge item and inside of the challenge item component file here on this list item, which already is a motion component, we can add our exit prop to define the animation we wanna play as an item is removed. And here we could, for example, set exit to an animation where the Y position, so the position along the vertical axis should change by minus 30 pixels. So moving the item up by 30 pixels and the opacity should change to zero. Now whenever we remove an item from the list, this is the animation that should be played. The item should become transparent and be moved up. Now if I save that and I go here to my list with two items and important, it must contain at least two items. If I click mark as completed which will remove this first item, you see it moves up and then only once it's gone, does the other item move into its place. So that works, but it does not work if I remove the last item, that still disappears instantly and this item still disappears instantly because if we go back to Challenges.jsx and we take a look at this code here, we can see that actually this entire ordered list is removed from the DOM, if displayedChallenges, so this array of challenges that should be displayed on the screen is an empty array. So AnimatePresence has no effect then because this is all removed because it's part of this conditional rendering block here. So what we would have to do here to make sure that the last item also kind of is animated is we would have to wrap this entire list with AnimatePresence as well and then convert this ordered list to a motion ordered list. Of course, also the closing tag. Now at the moment, I'm not importing motion here so I have to do that, import motion from Framer Motion. And then with that imported here on this motion order list which is now wrapped by AnimatePresence, we can also set an exit animation. Because I'm deriving it up here by filtering it and by changing lists, I'm simply ending up with a list that then is empty. Therefore, since after switching tabs displayedChallenges sometimes may be empty, this entire list is removed and the exit animation is played. And that's of course not necessarily the behavior we might want to have. And we can fix this behavior by wrapping AnimatePresence not just around that list, but actually also around this fallback paragraph that's shown if we have no items in the list for this selected tab. Though, that alone is not enough because as you'll see, if I empty this list again, it still does not behave the way I want it to behave. Instead now it suddenly disappears instantly which is not what I want. Where is that exit animation we had before? Well, to bring back this exit animation of this ordered list here again, we now have to tweak this ordered list and this paragraph. But whenever you have more than one, you should add a key to every element here in AnimatePresence to help Framer Motion tell them apart. And here, this can be a hardcoded key. This ordered list can get a key off list and this fallback paragraph here can get a key of fallback, for example. And to get rid of this behavior, we now have to do one last thing. We have to configure this AnimatePresence that does wrap our ordered list and this paragraph. There we have to set the mode prop and set it to wait. The default is sync, which means it plays all the animations inside of this component here simultaneously. So if one element disappears like this list and one element appears like this paragraph, it plays both animations at the same point of time. Now here we actually want it to wait, and hence we have to set the mode to wait for this first element to disappear before it plays any appearance animations. The second important hook is to use transport hook, which will allow us to transform values to values we can use in animations. For example, values you get from scrolling to values we can use to animate the movement along the y axis. So we need these two hooks, and I'll now show you how to use them. We'll start by using use scroll. This will return an object with a couple of useful properties. For example, the scrollY property to tell us by how many pixels the user scrolled along the vertical axis. We also have scrollX for the horizontal axis, and we have progress properties to get a relative scrolling value. So a value between zero and one, for example, where one would mean that the user scrolled all the way to the bottom and zero means the user didn't scroll at all. But here, I want to get the absolute pixels with scrollY. And now these pixels should be translated to values we can use for the animation. And let's maybe start by animating some opacity before we start moving elements. Because starting with opacity and transporting this scroll value to opacity values makes understanding this use transform hook a little bit easier, in my experience. So let's say that we want to make this city image here more and more transparent as the user scrolls along, so then after awhile it is totally transparent. To achieve this, you must set the animate prop here, and then the opacity, and now we need a value of zero once we scrolled far enough and a value of one initially, so that it's visible. Hence, we need to transform scrollY to one and zero and all the values in between as we are scrolling. And that's what use transform does for us. Use transform takes a value that should be transformed, so scrollY in this case, the number of pixels the user scrolled. Then as a second value, it will take an array of break points, you could say, and as a third value, an array of values. And then maybe a opacity of .5, so that it has a transparency of 50%. It's 50% transparent after we scroll 200 pixels. And Framer Motion will automatically interpolate between those values. And that's how use transform is used to transform these pixel values to opacity values. You're also not limited to two values here. We could add a third break point of 300 and a fourth and last break point of 500. Now, we also should add some matching values here in this transformation array. And we could say that after 300 pixels, we still want to have an opacity of .5. So as the user continues scrolling after 200 pixels of scrolling down, the opacity will not change. However, if we then add a last value of zero here, this means that after 300 pixels all the way up to 500 pixels of scrolling, the opacity will change from .5 to zero, which means that the item will be transparent. That's how we can transform values and use transform then returns the transformed value. Though it actually wraps this in a so-called motion value object so that it does not re-render this component function every time we scroll, but instead manages this transformed value behind the scenes without causing the component function to re-render. So that this entire animation does not impact the component render cycle. That's really important to keep in mind. So here we get the opacity for our city. And this opacity city value can now be used to update the animation of this image. However, you actually don't do that with hubble key anime prop now. Instead, those motion components all to support the style prop, which regular components also support, but it's now an enhanced style prop. This is a style prop that will be watched by Framer Motion, and as value is applied to style prop change, Framer Motion will automatically play an animation to get to that new value. And you need to use this style prop with the results you get back from use transform because those values, as I mentioned before, are managed behind the scenes by Framer Motion. This component function will not be re-executed as this value changes. And therefore, animate wouldn't work with this special kind of value. This special style prop here however will. And there, you can now, for example, set your opacity to opacity city. And behind the scenes, Framer Motion will detect changes in that value and will smoothly animate this component, this image component, even though it's overall welcome page component was not re-executed. So it will lay this animation in a highly performing way. And therefore, now with that, if you save that and reload the page, if you scroll down, you'll see that this city image gets more and more transparent. And at some point, if you scroll down far enough, it disappears. You can of course also see that if you inspect it with the div tools. This image here, as you scroll, you see the opacity here, and you see it changes as I continue scrolling. Now there is pause at .5 because that's where we have this scrolling range where we don't change the opacity. But after that range, after some time here, after we continue scrolling, it continues updating and decreases until it reaches zero. And that's how you can update styles and get Framer Motion to play animations based on scrolling values after transforming those values to the special values that can be used on a style prop. And now with that, we're of course not limited to just animating the opacity of this city image. We can now, for example, also animate the y position of this city image, so the position along the vertical axis. For that, we can use transform again to again transform this scrolling value, maybe based on a range of zero to 200 to a movement on the y axis between zero, so the original position, and minus 100, where the item gets pushed up a little bit. And you can simply play around with different values here to see their impact. With that, we can use Y city and set it as a value for Y here on this style prop on this city image. And if you do that, you'll see that now we got a nice parallax effect already because the city image in the background moves at a different speed than the superhero image and the text above it. Because we're mapping it to a different a range, to different pixels, it should animate between as the user scrolls. And this approach can now be repeated to also animate the superhero image, so that we have a y hero and an opacity hero constant here. And for y hero, I'll use the same range as for y city, but I'll map it to a different movement range, let's say zero to -150, so that the hero gets pushed up further as we scroll than the city does. And for opacity hero, I'll set a range of zero, 300, 500. And keep that hero fully opaque, so fully visible, until after 300 pixels, and then I'll fade it out until we scroll 500 pixels down. And last but not least, I also want to scale my text by transforming my scrolling position along the y axis for a range between zero and 300 from a value of one, which is the initial size, to a value of 1.5. So that it's 50% bigger after we scroll to 300 pixels down. And now we can assign all these values. For example, on this motion div here, onset the style prop, the special style prop here, and set the scale to scale text. And now actually I'll move the text along the y axis. So for that, I'll also add another constant here, y text, and use transform to transform scrollY for a range of zero, 200, 300, 500 to a movement range of zero, 50, 50, 300. So between 200 and 300 pixels, the text will be stuck pushed down by 50 pixels from its original position, and then we'll push it down further to 300 pixels at 500 pixels of scrolling. And therefore, here I'll also set y to y text on this div here. And now here on my hero image, I'll also add this style prop here, and set y to y hero and opacity to opacity hero. With that, we can save that. And if we now reload this page here, we get a nice parallax effect here where the hero moves at a slightly different speed than the city and this text gets bigger and bigger and moves as well until we push it down like this and we're right above the upper text here. And of course, you can tweak those values, animate other things, play around with use transform and different properties you can animate. 39486888.title: Module Introduction
39486888.section: React Query / Tanstack Query: Handling HTTP Requests With Ease
39486888.transcript: In this section, we're going to work on yet another new project, and whilst working on this project, you will learn how to use Tanstack Query, a third-party React library, formerly known as React Query, so that's its old name, the new name is Tanstack Query, and it is a library that helps you with sending HTTP requests from inside your React app. So it helps you with connecting your React frontend to a backend. Now, of course, in this course, you already learned how to do that, for example, with useEffect and using the built-in fetch function that's provide by the browser, but in this section here, you will learn what exactly Tanstack Query is, and most importantly, why you would use it instead of using useEffect and fetch for example. And you will then learn how to use this Tanstack Query library to fetch data and to mutate data, so how to send, get, and post, and put, and delete requests, and all these things. You will learn how to configure and efficiently use this library, and we will also explore many more advanced concepts like working with the cache that's provided by that library, how it works, and how you can invalidate and change it. We'll explore the topic of optimistic updating, what that is, and how you would implement it with Tanstack Query, and much more.
