39486894.title: React Query: What & Why?
39486894.section: React Query / Tanstack Query: Handling HTTP Requests With Ease
39486894.transcript: So before we'll start using Tanstack Query in this project here, let's make sure we all understand what exactly Tanstack Query is and why you might want to use it. And I already mentioned it in the first video of this section, Tanstack Query is a library that helps you with sending HTTP requests and keeping your frontend user interface in sync with your backend data. That's the core idea. And it's important to understand that you don't need Tanstack Query to do that. You could do it with useEffect and the fetch function, for example. That's how you learned to do it earlier in this course, and using this approach is also fine in general. But Tanstack Query can vastly simplify your code and therefore also your life as a developer, especially also because it comes with many built in more advanced features which might be needed by more complex React apps and which are relatively difficult and challenging to build on your own. Now, if we dive into this starting project I prepared for you, and there in the src components and then the Events folder, you'll find a New Events section component. And in this component file here you will find the useEffect hook being used to then in the end contain some code that will send an HTTP request to the backend and fetch some events data. And you'll see that in there we're managing some state, the data, which we'll eventually have, a possible error we might be facing and a is loading state to update the UI accordingly. So that we show a loading indicator whilst we're still waiting for the data. That we show an error block if we got an error or that we output that list of events if we got the data. And here I'm just using a custom event item component which the end simply is responsible for outputting these items here. So that's what we're doing here in this component. And what we're doing here, of course, does not need any third party library because we're using features like useState and useEffect that are built into React, and the fetch function that's built into the browser. But whilst this approach here works fine, and as mentioned is fine to use, it has a couple of potential flaws. For example, it's quite a lot of code that we have to write here. We have to write all this code here in the useEffect hook to make sure that we fetch the data and then trigger and set our different states. We have to manage all these states, and we have to do that, basically, in every component that wants to send HTTP requests. Now to be fair, we could simplify this code and reuse code by building a custom hook, though. We could build a custom use HTTP hook for example that in the end contains this code and can then be reused. And that's also something about which you learned earlier in the course already, how you can build custom hooks. But even if we would build such a custom hook this code would still have some problems or missing features to be precise. For example, one feature we might want in this app here, but which we don't have yet, is that if I'm on this website and I then switch to a different tab to do something there and I then come back to this website I might want to trigger a refetch so that behind the scenes we're fetching new data and we're updating the UI if we found out that the data we're currently displaying is outdated. Now, out of the box with the code I have here we don't have that behavior. And it would take us quite a bit of code to write to implement this behavior in a good way. Another feature we might want in this app here could be caching so that the data once fetched is cached, stored in memory, and we can then reuse that data if we need it again. So that for example, if I go to a different page, the View Details page, which currently doesn't work yet, but we'll make it work throughout this section, and I then go back to view all events, we don't have to refetch all that data but instead we maybe use the data we already have in memory and fetch updated data behind the scenes. It's subtle, small features like this which can make an application much better and much more user-friendly. And building all these things from scratch on your own and getting those things right is definitely challenging and can be difficult. And that's where Tanstack Query comes into play because with this library we'll not just be able to get rid of a bunch of code, like this state management here and some other code as well, but we'll also get some advanced features like caching behind the scenes, data fetching, and all those things I just described to make our app better and therefore, let's now get started with Tanstack Query.

And you'll learn more about this later but this is of course a super important mechanism because this makes sure that data can be shown to the user quicker if you already have it because it doesn't need to be refetched all the time. 39499428.title: Understanding & Configuring Query Behaviors - Cache & Stale Data
39499428.section: React Query / Tanstack Query: Handling HTTP Requests With Ease
39499428.transcript: Now, I mentioned before, that React Query offers certain advantages and I showed you one advantage at the end of the last lecture. But I now wanna explore another super important feature of Tanstack Query together with you, before we're going to write more code. And that would be Caching. Because React Query caches response data. And as a result, if we're on our website, we can, for example, go to a different page by clicking View Details. And if we then go back by clicking View All Events, the events here are available instantly. Now, this might seem obvious, but it is not, because before, when we used useEffect, and our own fetching logic, this was not the case. There, if we went to a different page and came back, a brand new request was sent and all the data was fetched again. Now here with React Query, the data is available instantly. Though, if you take a look at the Network Tab, you will actually see that as I click on View All Events, there is another request being sent. And nonetheless, the data is available instantly. Now, of course, these requests here are all pretty fast, because we're working locally, but we would even see that data instantly if the request were a bit slower. And we can easily simulate this by again, going to the Network Tab in the Developer Tools, and by then, Throttling this a little bit and going to Slow 3G, which means network requests will take a bit longer, because we're Throttling the network. With that enabled, you'll see that still, if I go back to View All Events, the data is available instantly. Just the images were not available instantly, because those of course, still had to be re-fetched. These are not fetched by useQuery and not cached by React Query. Instead, these are simply included through links and fetched by the browser. But you saw that despite these images being re-fetched the other data was available instantly, and now even the images were cached. And that's really important to understand. There is another request being sent Behind the Scenes here, but this is not the request that's needed to display data instantly. Instead, React Query caches the response data you are getting back from your requests and it will reuse that data whenever it encounters a never useQuery execution with the same Query Key. So for example, if we go back to this page and therefore, this component function executes again, React Query will see that this Query Key has been used before and that it did already cache data for that key. And it will then instantly yield that data, but at the same time, also send this request again Behind the Scenes to see if updated data is available. And then it will kind of silently replace that data with the updated data so that after a couple of seconds or however long it takes to fetch that data, we do have the updated data on the screen. So that we get the best of both worlds. Instant results, but still updated data, once this Behind the Scenes request is done. And with that here I'll disable Throttling, so that we have the normal speed again. Now as a developer, when using React Query, you can of course, control if this is the behavior you want. For example, by setting a staleTime on your queries. This controls after which time React Query will send such a Behind the Scenes request to get updated data if it found data in your cache. And the default is zero, which means it will use data from the cache, but it will then always also send such a Behind the Scenes request to get updated data. If you set this to 5,000, for example, it will wait for 5,000 milliseconds before sending another request. So if this component was rendered and therefore this request was sent, and within five seconds this component is rendered again, and the same request would need to be sent, React Query would not send it if the staleTime is set to 5,000. And you can see this in your application, if you go back here to this page. You reload this page and you then click View Details, and within five seconds you click on View All Events. Now, no Events Request was sent here. If I wait for more than five seconds though, and I go somewhere else and come back, this Events Request is being sent. So that's the staleTime with which you can make sure that no unnecessary requests are sent. As mentioned, the default is zero, but you can set this to whatever you want. Another value you can set here is the gcTime, the Garbage Collection Time. This controls how long the data and the cache will be kept around. And the default here are five minutes. We could of course, also reduce this, for example, to half a minute, with 30,000 milliseconds. This would mean that the cached data would only be kept around for half a minute and thereafter, it would be discarded. So thereafter, if this component needs to render again, there would be no cached data, and therefore, React Query would always need to send a new request to get some data before it can show anything. So if I reduce this to one second, for example, as a result, if I load this page, we need to send the request and wait for the data. But if I then go somewhere else and come back, the data was not there, which becomes even clearer if I, again, Throttle this. So first I reload this, then I'll Throttle this, and now I'll go somewhere else and then come back. And now you see, there's this loading spinner. Because now no data was there and we did not just send some Behind the Scenes request, instead, this request was now actually needed to show anything at all, because the cache was cleared because of this setting. Which is why I'm not setting this setting here, but you should be aware of it. You can control how long data is kept around in cache. And I'm exploring these topics in great depth here, because this is one of the main features of React Query. Being able to control how long data is kept around and when new requests will be sent. And therefore, with this out of the way now, we're now ready to dive deeper into React Query and use it in more places of this demo application.

39499460.title: The Query Configuration Object & Aborting Requests
39499460.section: React Query / Tanstack Query: Handling HTTP Requests With Ease
39499460.transcript: So why is search term set to some strange value, as we can see in this one request here, when the new events section component tries to fetch events with this useQuery usage? Well, because React Query and the useQuery hook actually passes some default data to this Query function you're defining here and we can see that data if we simply console log search term here. If we do that, we should see two logs in the console because we got two components, the find events section and the new events section components that do use the fetch events function with React Query. An object which I never passed to fetch events in no place of this app. Instead, as I mentioned in the last lecture, React Query by default passes some data to your Query function. So to fetch events in this case here, when assigned to Query FN like this and the data it passes in is an object that gives us information about the Query key that was used for that Query and a signal. And that signal is required for aborting that request. If you, for example, navigate away from this page before the request was finished because React Query thankfully can do that for you, it can abort requests and it does that with help of that signal. So for that reason to give you that signal and to also give you that Query key, which you might want to use in your data fetching function. For that reason, React Query passes an object to that function you defined as a Query function. For that we can use this signal and pass it to the built-in fetch function by adding a second argument to fetch, a configuration object, which takes a signal property and wants a signal of that shape as React Query gives it to us so that the browser then can use that aboard signal internally to stop this request if it receives that signal. So that's one thing we can do here but that's not all I wanna do here because of course I still wanna make sure that I do forward the search term. This makes sure that this search term property exists here when it's needed. Now to also forward that signal here, we can simply accept this object here in this anonymous function because that's now the function that will actually be called by React Query and therefore we'll get the signal here. I even get auto completion here and can see that I get the signal here. We can then simply set it as a key value pair in this object here as well. And therefore now we have the highest degree of flexibility we can have. We can wrap fetch events with an anonymous function to pass any data we want via that object to fetch events and still get that data that's provided to us by React Query, or we set this function directly as a value for Query event if we're happy with the default object we're getting by React Query, which is the case here in New Events section where I don't need to pass any custom data to my data fetching function. 39499464.title: Enabled & Disabled Queries
39499464.section: React Query / Tanstack Query: Handling HTTP Requests With Ease

Because in that case I see all those events down there, which kind of makes sense because no search term was entered and therefore of course we have no criteria to narrow those events down. But of course, as a result, I'm seeing the same events down here as I do up here. And of course, as a result, if we take a look at the network tab, we're sending two requests to events, one from every section. And we also can't change this with stale time or garbage collection time because we're using different Query keys here. So the data from the Query in the first component, the new events section component wouldn't be reused here anyways, as I also explained before. And it's a feature that's pretty easy to implement when using React Query because this use Query configuration object takes an enabled property and if you set this two false, the Query is disabled and will not be sent, so the request will not be sent. If you send it to true it will be sent and that's the default. But now I wanna set it to false if we did not enter a search term. Now to achieve this, we could set enabled to search term unequal empty string, which means it's true if the user did enter anything and false otherwise, but this would actually not achieve what I wanna achieve here because if I'm doing that and I reload, as a result I would not be seeing any results here. I would see the loading indicator instead but we'll fix this soon. But if I now enter, I would see results. But if I go back to no search term, I would see that loading indicator again. But maybe if I did enter something and I then go back to no search term, I wanna see all events, I just don't wanna see all those events initially. So there should be a difference between this input being empty initially or being empty because the user cleared it. At least that's the behavior I'd like to have in this app. If the user did enter something else and then clears this input, I wanna show all events because the user probably looked for all events but initially I wanna show no event. And to achieve this, I'll simply make sure that my search term state initially is undefined by not passing any value at all to use state. And therefore then here I can check that this should only be enabled if search term is not equal to undefined. So if it is undefined, which is the initial value, this Query will be disabled. But if search term is anything else including an empty string, which would be the case if the user did clear the input field manually, it will be enabled and the request will be sent. And with that, I can now make sure that initially I don't have any results but if I search something, I get that result. And if I then clear that and search, I get all events which is exactly the behavior I want. Now I just also want to get rid of that loading spinner which we see initially and we're seeing that loading spinner because when a Query is disabled, which initially here in this component will be the case, React Query treats it as pending because we don't have any data. And instead we're waiting for data to arrive, which can only happen once the Query is enabled. But that's why initially it treats it as is pending because it kind of waits for this Query to be enabled. Now of course here, that's not really the behavior I want. I don't want a loading spinner if we're waiting for the user to enter something here. And that's why React Query gives us an alternative to is pending. You can also use is loading Boolean. The difference between is loading and is pending is that is loading will not be true if this Query is just disabled. So therefore, if I switch from is pending to is loading here in this if check and I save this now, we start with this info text for the user and I can then enter City to briefly get that loading spinner and then my result and I can clear that to go back to all the search results. And I therefore now got exactly the behavior I want with help of this Query enabling and disabling feature. And this is loading property.

39499474.title: Acting on Mutation Success & Invalidating Queries
39499474.section: React Query / Tanstack Query: Handling HTTP Requests With Ease
39499474.transcript: So at the moment, whenever we create a new event, nothing happens because as I mentioned at the end of the last lecture, we don't have any code that would define what should happen after we mutate. Now we could navigate away programmatically, for example, with help of the UseNavigate hook provided by React Router. Here in Handle Submit, we could call Navigate and go back to slash events for example, to go back to the starting page essentially, we could do that here, but we also might want to wait for this mutation to be finished until we do that so that we don't close this screen whilst the request is still on its way. And we could achieve that by adding another property to this useMutation configuration object, the on success property, which in the end wants a function as a value. So which is a method that should be added here, a function that will be executed once this mutation succeeded. This also makes sure that this code will only execute if the mutation did succeed. If we instead would navigate away here in handleSubmit we would always do that no matter if the mutation succeeds or fails. So if it fails and an error message should be displayed, we would never see that because we instantly navigate away. If we instead do that in on success, we'll stay on this screen until the mutation did really succeed. So any errors would be shown to us. And therefore it's now in here where I want to call Navigate and go to slash events. But that's actually not all I want to do. Instead, if you would go back here and you would submit such a new event here by clicking Create, you would see that it is submitted but it's not showing up here under my recently added events which right now are basically all events I have here. It's not showing up here until I, for example, switch to a different page and come back because as you learned, this triggers React Query to refetch data behind the scenes. But of course, if I know that the data just changed because I added a new event, for example, I want React Query to immediately refetch data. I wanted to immediately update my data here, put in other words, here in this new events section for this Query, which is responsible for fetching those events here, I wanna make sure that the data that was fetched here is marked as stale and a refetch is triggered. And we can achieve this by calling a method that's provided by React Query that allows us to invalidate one or more queries. So that allows us to tell React Query that the data that's connected to some queries is outdated and that it should be refetched. And to achieve this, we should first of all go to the app JSX file where we create this Query client because it's with help of that Query client. So with help of this object here, that we'll be able to force this invalidation of a Query. Therefore we should cut this from this file and remove this import and instead add it to some other file from which we can then import it into multiple files. In my case here, I'll just add it to this HTDP JS file. There at the very top I'll export this Query client and of course for that we have to import this Query client class that's being instantiated here. Now with that exported here in HTDP JS we should go back to App JSX and import this Query client from this HTDP JS file, which we find in the UTIL folder. Because we still need that Query client object here to pass it to the Query client provider. But now we can use that same Query client in the new event JSX file here in On Success because there, before navigating away, I now want to use this Query client. So we should import Query client from going up two levels and then UTIL and then HTDP JS and then in on success, so at a point of time where we know that it worked, I want to call Query client invalidate queries which does what its name implies. It in the end tells React Query that the data fetched by certain queries is outdated now, that it should be marked as stale and that an immediate refetch should be triggered if the Query belongs to a component that's currently visible on the screen. So for example, if I'm creating a new event, of course the page that shows all events is still visible on the screen. It's below this modal after all. So the Query that was responsible for this section here should be reexecuted. And for that to target specific queries, invalidate queries takes an object as an input where we have to define the Query key which we wanna target. And this should of course be a Query key in the same format as we're using it for all those queries. So an array which then, for example, could include the word events. And this will then invalidate all queries that include this key. It does not have to be exactly the same key. So for example, in find events section I have a Query key that includes events and then also this object, this Query here with this key will also be invalidated because it includes events and I'm invalidating any Query key that does include events with that code. We could work around that by also setting the exact property on this object that we're passing to invalidate queries to true and now only queries with exactly that key would be invalidated. But since you should build your Query keys such that they kind of describe the data you are fetching, it makes sense to invalidate all queries that include events because all those queries would otherwise be dealing with old data. For example, here in find events section where I'm looking for events based on a search term entered by the user, I of course don't want to ignore new events that have been added. So I wanna invalidate this Query as well, so that if a new event was added, that is met by this search term entered by the user it's presented as a result for this search term, automatically by React Query. And that's why by default all queries that include this key will be invalidated and why this is also typically the behavior you want. And therefore, now with that added, with this invalidate queries call added here, if we save that and I now create a new event with some dummy text here just to have something on the screen quickly, and I click Create, you see it shows up down here basically instantly because it's refetched behind the scenes immediately because I'm invalidating queries here. And that's why this here is often an important step when performing mutations because this guarantees that all queries that use a certain key operate on recent data again.

39499480.title: Disabling Automatic Refetching After Invalidations
39499480.section: React Query / Tanstack Query: Handling HTTP Requests With Ease
39499480.transcript: So I did implement this data fetching and deletion logic, but as mentioned in the last lecture, if I delete an event and we're navigated back, I also get such a 404 request here. Now, if we take a closer look at this request, we see that it actually was for one specific event with a specific ID. Now, why was this request even sent here If we actually navigated back to this page that shows all events, not just one single specific event? Well, because for deleting an event, we were on that details page here and when then after deleting an event, we invalidated all event related queries. We still were on that page. And therefore, technically, since we invalidated all queries. React Query went ahead and immediately triggered a refetch for this details query here. Now, to avoid this behavior, we should go back to invalidate queries and add a second property to this configuration object for invalidate queries. Here, you can set the re fetch type to none, which makes sure that when you call invalidate queries, these existing queries will not automatically be triggered again immediately. Instead, they will just be invalidated and the next time they are required, they will run again. But they will not be re-triggered immediately which otherwise would be the default behavior. And here, that's what I want because this makes sure that this event details query of this page on which I'm currently at is not triggered again. But if we then go back to this all events page here, the queries on this page will be triggered again because this component re-rendered, again, this entire page component and all the nested components, but the query on the page on which we triggered the deletion, where this component for this page was not re rendered, will not be triggered just because we called invalidate queries. So that's the behavior I want here. And with that, if we make that change and we save that and we reload and open the developer tools if I click delete, we see that request, we are navigated back to the starting page and we see no failing request anymore. And that's therefore the behavior we now want to have.

39499488.title: Optimistic Updating
39499488.section: React Query / Tanstack Query: Handling HTTP Requests With Ease
39499488.transcript: So as I showed you in the last lecture, our current updating approach has the problem that whilst it works, we're not reflecting the updated state on the page we're coming from unless we reload it. Now, earlier in this course you learned that one way of making this work and of fixing this would be to call query client invalidate queries here and invalidate all the queries that need that data. And that would work here as well. But in that case, you should also wait with navigating away until this mutation is done and you should show a loading spinner on this added page whilst you are waiting for the mutation to succeed. And that's not how I want to build this here. Instead here I want to do something which is called optimistic updating. If I confirm and update here, by pressing this update button, I want to update this UI here instantly without waiting for the response of my backend. And if my backend then turns out to fail, if the update fails for whatever reason, I simply want to roll back the optimistic update I performed. And this is a pattern or an approach that's relatively easy to implement with React Query. And it all starts by adding a new property to this use mutation configuration object. And that's the on mutate property, which wants a function as a value. On mutate, so this function here, will be executed right when you call mutate. So before this process is done, before you got back a response. And it's here in on mutate where I essentially wanna update the data that's cached by React Query where I wanna update this detail data that's stored behind the scenes, so to say. And this can be done by importing query client from http.js, because as you already saw before in this section through this query client you can essentially interact with React Query and tell it to invalidate queries or as you will see now, to change the cached data. And therefore, here in this on mutate function we should start by getting the currently stored data so that we can then manipulate and edit it. And therefore, here in this on mutate function, we can use query client and then call set query data to manipulate that already stored data without waiting for a response. Normally it's manipulated by React Query whenever you got a new response that's being cached. But you can also manipulate that stored data yourself by calling set query data. And that's what we're doing here. Now, set query data then needs two arguments. And the first argument is the key of the query that you do want to edit. In this case it's events params ID because it was a specific event that I edited it. It is therefore the data that's stored for that event by React Query which I wanna manipulate here without waiting for the response. The second argument then is the new data you wanna store under that query key. And here in this scenario, in this component, that would simply be that data which I also sent to my backend, this updated event data. Now thankfully getting this here in on mutate is pretty simple because React Query actually passes this data, which you passed to mutate, as a value to on mutate. So you automatically get that data which you did submit to the backend as input here. Now that data, which I did submit, had an ID property and then the updated event. And it's this updated event I'm interested here, so therefore, my new event, which I also sent to the backend, is data.event accessing this property here. So that's the new data I wanna set for this query key. So this will manipulate the data behind the scenes without waiting for a response. But that's not all I want to do here in on mutate. Another thing you should typically do when performing optimistic updating, like we are doing it here, is that you should also use to query client to cancel all active queries for a specific key by passing an object to cancel queries, and then setting a query key for which you want to cancel queries. And in my case, it's that same query key. With this line of code we're making sure that if we had any outgoing queries for that key, those queries would be canceled and we would not have clashing response data from those queries and our optimistically updated query data because if those ongoing queries finished before the updating request was done, we would've fetched old data again and we don't wanna do that. Now this actually returns a promise which we want to await. And in order to use await here we should add async here in front of this function. So with that, we're canceling ongoing queries for this data and we're setting our own data. By the way, cancel queries will not cancel the mutation it will really only cancel queries triggered with use query. And with that, if you do that, if you save this and you edit this and I remove those exclamation marks and I click update, you see they're gone here immediately because we edited that query data behind the scenes and that therefore was reflected automatically by React Query in the UI. But that's not all we should do when performing optimistic updating because of course the updating process on the backend could fail. And in that case, we would now have outdated data here. For example, if I delete the entire title, now it's updated here, but if I reload it's actually back because my backend code blocks this, my backend code does require an input for every field. So we wanna make sure that we roll back our optimistic update if it does fail on the backend. And currently that's not happening. So to make sure that we can roll back, we also need to get the old data and store that old data somewhere so that we can roll back to that old data. And we should do that before we update the data. Now, query client also has a solution for that. It also has a get query data method which gives us the currently stored query data, which we of course want to execute before we set it to some new data. Now get query data simply wants the key of the query for which we want to get the data. And with that here I get my previous event. So the old event data. That's what I get with get query data. And now we wanna roll back to that event if our update mutation failed. Now to do that, we can add another property to this use mutation configuration object and that's the on error property, which also wants a function which will be executed if this update mutation errors, so if it fails. Now on error conveniently receives a couple of inputs that are passed in automatically by React Query. It receives the error object with which it failed, the data which was submitted to the mutation, and a context object which will become important because it's this context object which can contain this previous event. Now, in order for this previous event to be part of this context, we should return an object here in this on mutate function because it's actually this object that will be this context here. So in here, we can then add a previous event key and store this previous event under this key and hence use this shortcut. And now context will be this object, essentially, and will contain this key most importantly, which we can therefore use to access this previous event inside of this on error function, so if we have an error. And then here in on error we can call query client set query data, again, to again manually update the stored data for this query with this key. But now we're of course setting it back to that old event which was previously stored with context.previous event. And with that, we're rolling back this optimistic update if the mutation fails. So if we now save this, and I reload and I edit this and I try to update this by removing the title, you see this did not work. It very briefly went to a state without a title but it's almost impossible to see because it's so fast. But then it reinserts the title. It's a bit easier to see if you, again, throttle this. Now, if I delete this, you see the title is gone but then it's rolled back, it's re-added because we have the rollback process in place. Now, in addition is one other last thing which you should also do when performing optimistic updating. And that's another property that should be added to this use mutation configuration object, the unsettled property, which also wants a function as a value. Now unsettled will simply be called whenever this mutation is done, no matter if it failed or succeeded. And in that case, just to be sure that you really got the same data in your front end as you have on your backend. You should also, again use query client to invalidate your queries. Of course, not all of them, but in this case simply all the events queries or maybe all the events queries that use this specific parameter, this specific ID. So all the event queries for this specific ID. And with this line and this property added here you simply make sure that whenever this mutation finished, even though you did perform this optimistic updating and you rolled back if things went wrong, you still make sure that you fetched the latest data from the backend so that if the backend did something different and the data would be out of sync between backend and front end right now it gets back into sync by forcing React Query to refetch the data behind the scenes. And with that, you got a good working optimistic updating strategy in place and you can now provide this nice user experience to your users where they don't have to stare at some loading spinner, but instead any changes they make are instantly reflected on your page.

And with that, just to be clear, we'll not be performing this optimistic updating anymore because all this code will soon not be executed anymore. This here, what we're doing here in the action function is instead simply an alternative approach that embraces those React Router features with all advantages and disadvantages. So here will not be performing optimistic updating anymore. We would have to write our own logic in the submit method, for example, if we would want to do that. And once this is all done we can finally return here and return a redirect, which is now a function that is provided by React Router. So redirect is imported from React Router DOM and this is simply a utility function which will make sure that we navigate to a different page. In this case, for example, simply to the parent page, the event details page we were coming from. With that, we should now tweak the component as mentioned and make sure that in handleSubmit we no longer call mutate and navigate like this. Instead here we should make sure that the form is actually submitted so that this action function here is triggered. Because that's how React Router works. It needs a form submission to trigger those action functions. Now we can programmatically submit a form here by using another hook provided by React Router. They useSubmit hook, which gives us a submit function like this. This submit function can now be used in this handleSubmit method here instead of mutate and navigate to submit this form. And then as a second argument, pass a configuration object where we set the method to anything but GET. And therefore, of course this entire mutation code can now be removed from this component. And now the last step is to go back to App.jsx and there import this action from the EditEvent component file and maybe also give it an alias, editEventAction, and then assign it to this route. So just as we assign the loader, we now also must assign the action like this. And with that, if we go back and I edit this, if I click update, it takes a short while, but thereafter we're navigated back to this details page and we see this exclamation mark here. And of course it can be confusing that we have to wait for a short while where nothing happens. And we could therefore use another React Router feature here in EditEvent to give the user some feedback that the request is on its way. And this hook will return an object which we can structure where we, for example, get this state here that will tell us what the current navigation state of React Router is. For example, whether we're currently submitting data. So we can use this state to show some loading text whilst we're submitting data. Down here where we got the cancel and update buttons, we could check if state is equal to submitting, for example. With that, if I make a change here we show this text until we're done and we then navigate away. And of course it's also weird that If I click on edit, it takes a short while until something happens. To provide a better user experience here we can use another hook provided by React Query. And I'll do that in this header component, which is simply this header that's always visible on all those pages. Here I got this div prepared where I wanna show a little loading progress bar, which is a standard HTML element if React Query is currently fetching data. And to find out if React Query is currently fetching data anywhere in this application, we can use a special hook provided by React Query. And that hook is called useIsFetching. Now, fetching will be a number that's zero if React Query is not fetching any data at this point of time anywhere in the application, or a higher number if React Query is fetching data. And we can use this to conditionally show this progress element. To only show it if fetching is greater than zero so that if we are fetching somewhere in this app this progress bar is shown. And with that, if I save this, you'll see that if I click edit, this progress bar appears and we see this screen. And we now see this whenever we're fetching data anywhere in this application. And this allows you to show some global data fetching indicator whenever React Query is fetching anywhere in this application. Now as our last and final step here, I also wanna make sure that we avoid redundant HTTP requests when using React Router in conjunction with React Query. And for that, let's take a look at the network tab. 39648976.title: Module Introduction
39648976.section: React Essentials - Components, JSX, Props, State & More
39648976.transcript: In this React Essentials Course Section, we are going to build this beautiful demo web application, with React and from the ground up. And as you can see, this website has both static as well as dynamic interactive content. And therefore, along the way, whilst building this website, you will learn about the most important React features, the essentials you'll always need, no matter which React app of whichever complexity you're going to build. And we'll get started by taking a look at one of the most important React concepts you can learn. We'll get started by exploring Components, and a couple of related concepts like JSX, working with Props, and what all these things are, and how to work with them as a React developer. You will also learn how to work with data in your React apps, how to output data, for example. And you will learn how to make your React apps interactive by reacting to user events like button clicks, and by diving into another crucial React concept called State, which will be super important for turning static websites into dynamic interactive websites. And therefore, by the end of this section, you'll know about all these crucial fundamental React concepts and how to use them. You'll be able to build basic React apps, both static and dynamic interactive ones, of course, and you need no prior React knowledge to get started, you just need that basic JavaScript knowledge because that's, of course, assumed for this entire course.

39648984.title: It's All About Components! [Core Concept]
39648984.section: React Essentials - Components, JSX, Props, State & More
39648984.transcript: React and its ecosystem provide dozens of useful and important features and concepts, which, of course, are all going to be covered in depth throughout this course. But if you were forced to identify only one core concept which you will need for all React apps, it would be the concept of working with Components. Because Components are potentially reusable building blocks which you, as a React developer, can create, and which you then combine to build the overall user interface. So React apps are in the end built by combining Components. And whilst a couple of additional features and concepts would be required to create truly interactive user interfaces, Components are the one concept every React app, no matter its complexity, will use. And indeed, the idea of thinking of user interfaces as combinations of Components is not unique or exclusive to React. If you visit any website of your choice, you'll quickly be able to identify a couple of key building blocks that make up the overall site or app. For example, in this demo website we're going to work on throughout this section, you could identify the header area, the key concept items, and the interactive tabs section as Components. You could also identify more or less Components because as you will also learn in this section and this course, it will come down to the developers who are working on an app to define how big or small a component should be. But the idea behind those Components really just is that they wrap HTML and possibly also CSS code, as well as any related JavaScript logic that might be needed. And together, those languages and code pieces then define and control a part of the UI. This allows you, the developer, to split up complex user interfaces into smaller, more manageable parts, which, at least potentially, may also be reused in different places of the UI. For example, this core concept component is used four times, just configured with different data, but using the same HTML markup and styling and JavaScript logic under the hood. And that's one of the main reasons why this Components approach is so powerful and popular. Without Components, you would quickly end up with rather large and complex HTML files that may become difficult to navigate and work with. In addition, change in your code might need to be applied in different parts of your application, hence, increasing the likelihood of errors. When using this Components approach, you are instead reusing code and you only need to make such a change in one place. Another big advantage of this Components idea is that related code can be stored together. Because without Components, you would typically have your markup in the HTML file and your JavaScript code in one of your JavaScript files. If you needed to make a change to the JavaScript code, you would therefore have to switch back and forth between those files all the time. And since you are likely working in different files and it's quite possible that you're not seeing all the related code together all the time, it's also quite likely that you might accidentally break your code because you may be changed something in your JavaScript code that then would've also required a change in the HtML code. On the other hand, when working with Components, related code is typically kept closely together, hence, simplifying the development process. And the last major advantage of working with Components is that you follow a design and development principle, which is almost always a good idea. You have a separation of concerns. Different Components do different things and handle different parts of the UI. For example, in the demo project of this section, we have Components that are responsible for outputting information and we have other Components that are responsible for switching between tabs and handling user input. And that's, of course, just a basic app. The more complex your projects become, the more useful this pattern becomes, as it also simplifies the process of working on the same project with multiple developers, where every developer works on their own feature area, for example. And that's why this idea of working with Components is embraced by React and why as a React developer, you will create and use hundreds and thousands of such Components, also in this course. And just as a side note, it's not just React. This concept is so popular and useful that you'll also find it in other popular front-end frameworks, like Angular, Vue, or Svelte. And you'll also find it outside the world of web development. For example, in mobile development frameworks like Flutter. So let's therefore now take a look at our first React component in code and then, of course, also create our first component.

39648994.title: JSX & React Components [Core Concept]
39648994.section: React Essentials - Components, JSX, Props, State & More
39648994.transcript: So now with that starting project up and running, you'll find a couple of files and folders in that Project folder. For example, you'll find an index.html file in that folder. But if you open that file, you'll see that it's pretty empty, though. It only contains some basic markup, but it definitely does not contain the HTML code that's responsible for displaying the content we're seeing on the screen, the image, the title, and so on. And the reason for that, of course, is that React is rendering this content on the screen, that React is responsible for bringing something onto the screen. And we can see in that index.html file, that it does indeed load a JavaScript file. It includes this index.jsx file. Now, let's ignore that strange extension .jsx for now, and instead take a look at that file. As we can see here, we find it in this src, this source folder. And if we open it, we do indeed find some JavaScript code in that file. But we still don't see anything that seems to be related to that image or title we're seeing on the website. Instead, besides other things which we will explore later, this file imports something from the App.jsx file. And this file finally does contain the markup for the content we're seeing on the screen. Though this file actually contains code, that might be a bit confusing if you take a closer look because it seems to contain HTML code inside of a JavaScript function, so inside of JavaScript code. And it also got this strange extension .jsx. Well, this file, as well as the index.jsx file, has this extension because it is a JavaScript file that uses non-standard JavaScript syntax. To be precise, it uses a JavaScript syntax extension called JSX which conveniently simply stands for JavaScript Syntax Extension. And this extension allows developers to describe and create HTML elements by writing HTML markup code inside of JavaScript files. And since React will be about describing and creating user interfaces and parts of user interfaces, this is a very useful feature. But whilst it is a nice feature and therefore used in basically all React projects, and also many non-React projects, it is a feature that's not supported by browsers. So the code you see in the index.jsx and App.jsx files would not work in browsers. Instead, the code you write as a React developer is transformed behind the scenes by the development server, in this case here, before it reaches the browser. It's transformed to code that does work in the browser. But as a developer, thanks to this JSX extension, you have this more convenient way of describing the user interface. But what's really important about this App.jsx file is that what we can see here is a React Component. It is such a Component I mentioned in previous lectures. And as you can see, in React, a Component is really just a JavaScript function. Though in order to be recognized and used as a Component by React, it's a function that must follow two important rules. The function name must start with an uppercase character and the function must return a renderable value, typically the to-be-rendered HTML markup. So this JSX code you see here. And that's really all. This is how Components are created in React. You create a regular JavaScript function, give it a suitable name that starts with an uppercase character and returns some JSX code. And with that, let's now finally create our first custom Component.

Just make sure that it starts with an uppercase character. For example, we could pick header as a name because my goal here is to take this header markup and put it into a separate component. As a next, and in this case also already final step, we should then return a value inside of this newly created header function. And the returned value should be the content that should be displayed on the screen by this component. So in this case, this header markup here, this HTML code you can cut this from the app component and paste it here into the header component. Now, when returning a multi-line JSX code snippet as I have it here, you must wrap it with parentheses to in the end tell JavaScript that all this code here belongs together. So you always need that forward slash also for your own custom components. But with that, if you save the app JS file and you reload your preview window you should see the header again this image title and subtitle, which of course isn't an impressive change necessarily because that's the content we saw before as well. 39649006.title: How React Handles Components & How It Builds A "Component Tree" [Core Concept]
39649006.section: React Essentials - Components, JSX, Props, State & More
39649006.transcript: So now that we built a first custom component, it's important to fully understand how that component's content ended up on the website and our screen. So as a first step, let's go to the website and let's inspect the source code of that website. And it also shouldn't be too surprising that we see the index.jsx file being loaded here in the actual website source code because that's also what we see here in our project, in the index.html file. And in the end, it is this index.html file that's being served to the website visitors. Now, as you learned before, this index.jsx file does import something from the App.jsx file. It imports the App component to be precise because it's that App component that's being exported in that App.jsx file. By the way, this export keyword and this import keyword and this entire import-export feature is a regular JavaScript feature. It's not React-specific. So it's this App component function that's being imported into the index.jsx file. And in this file, it's being used as JSX code here. Now as you can see, we have no React component here though. This JSX code is not getting returned by some function. Instead, it's getting used as a value, as an argument for some other method that's being called here, the render method. And indeed, as a React developer, you will almost exclusively use JSX code inside of component functions. What we see here in the index.jsx file is the one important exception because this file, this index.jsx file, acts as the main entry point of our React app since it is the first file to be loaded by the HTML file. And it's in this place where the React app boots up, you could say. To be precise, it's this special React DOM library, which in the end belongs to the overall React library from which we're importing here which ultimately renders this App component. So, which is responsible for outputting the App component's content on the screen, you could say. And this App component is rendered by passing JSX code to this render method. This render method, however, is being called on an object that's created with another method, the createRoot method. This method takes an existing HTML element as an input, so an element that's not being created by React but that instead is part of the index.html file already. In this case, that would be this div here with the id root, since that's what we're selecting here with getElementById, that's what's getting passed to this createRoot method. And then with that element selected and set as a root for the React project, React goes ahead and injects a React component, the App component in this case, into this element, you could say. It renders this component and all its contents, including any nested components it may contain and their content into this div. That's what this render method does. And that's why if you open the developer tools and there the elements tab, you see more than just the initial HTML structure. Instead, you see all the elements that are indeed visible on the screen and you see that they are inside of that div here with the id root. So the header and the main section, which is in the end simply the content defined in the App component and our custom Header component are inside of that div with the id root. So the createRoot and render methods are responsible for rendering a single root component, the App component in this case, which then in turn may contain as many nested components as needed. And those nested components, it may include like this Header component in this case, could then contain even more child components. And with that, ultimately you end up with a component hierarchy, which is often called a tree of components, a structure of components, which is then rendered to the screen via React. But what's important to understand about this tree of components and components in general is that your custom components are not showing up in the actual rendered DOM though. There you only find default HTML elements, for example, here, the header with the lowercase h, which is the built-in header element, not our custom component, which had an uppercase H. We also don't see the App component in here. So your tree of components is, in the end, just analyzed by React. And React then combines all the JSX code from all those components to generate the overall DOM, the elements that are showing up on the screen. But as a developer, you have the convenience of working with those individual building blocks instead of a single potentially huge file that contains all the markup. And you can now also see why your component function names must start with an uppercase character. All built-in elements like div, image, or header start with lowercase characters. Custom components on the other hand, so components built by you or other developers must start with an uppercase character to tell React that it's not a built-in component. So that's how React tells custom components apart from built-in components. This does not just prevent potential name clashes, like for example here where header with a lowercase h, would clash with the builtin header. But it also changes how React handles a component. Built-in components like header, image, div, and so on are rendered as DOM nodes by React. On the other hand, custom components like our Header here are just functions and are therefore executed as functions by React. And it then just takes a look at them, takes the returned value, the returned JSX code, and starts analyzing that code until it ends up with only built-in elements at a certain point of time, which are then rendered to the screen. And that's how React works, how it uses those components, and how it ultimately renders them onto the screen.

39649022.title: Making Components Reusable with Props [Core Concept]

And the goal, of course, is to use that multiple times, but every time I'm using it I wanna pass in different data. And thanks to this props concept, that's super easy to do. you can simply add custom attributes to your custom components, and since these are your components it's also entirely up to you which attributes you wanna set on those components, so those attribute names are also entirely up to you. And here I'll start by adding a title attribute, and I'll set it equal to a value of components here for this first usage of core concept. And I'll add a description attribute, let's say, though, in React we actually call that a prop because that concept of configuring components is called props. And for this description prop here we could say the core UI building block, for example. And last but not least, we can add an image here, and here I actually wanna set this to a dynamic value because I want to import that image, just as I showed it to you in the last lectures. So all your custom attributes as keys and the values for these attributes as values grouped into it. So one object with these key value pairs, that's what you'll get here for this props parameter. 39649190.title: Best Practice: Storing Components in Files & Using a Good Project Structure
39649190.section: React Essentials - Components, JSX, Props, State & More
