So now back here in examples, JSX, we could try to use this tabs component here. And for that of course, we also must add this import where we import tabs from tabs.JSX. And then here we can take our tab content, which is managed here in this examples component and which I wanna manage here, not inside of the tabs component, because the idea behind this component is that we could use it for all kinds of tabs with all kinds of content in different places of the app. So I don't wanna manage the content in here, it should instead be managed in the place where the tabs component is used. And then also some unselect tab prop, for example, which is triggered whenever some tab button is clicked. But adding these extra props does not just add some extra complexity here, but also makes tabs less reusable because now I couldn't use the component in a place where I'm not managing topics but something totally different. So this is not a great solution. I don't wanna accept a bunch of props here just to make these buttons work. Instead, what would be better would be to leave those buttons here in the examples JSX file, but to pass them as JSX code to the tabs element just as we're passing this dynamic content to tabs. And that is something you can do in React because as I mentioned before in this course, JSX code, JSX elements are in the end just regular values that can be used like values in your code. So here we can grab all those tab buttons and cut them and use them as a value here for buttons. The only problem we'll have with that here is that now we got a bunch of sibling elements being used as a value here, and that's simply not allowed with JSX. As you learned before, whenever you are using JSX code as a value in some place, no matter if that place is the value being returned in a component or a value being stored in a variable, you have to make sure that there's only one root element in that place. And for that, we could add a div as a wrapper around these tap buttons like this. Or since we don't really need a div here, simply this special fragment about what you learned. And with that, we're now setting these buttons as a special prop, which might look weird but which is a common pattern indeed because now with that pattern being used, we're able to basically set two different slots on this tabs component, the main content slot, which is set with help of children. And then this buttons slot, which is set with help of our custom buttons prop. And of course, we could accept more props and set more slots if our component would need extra slots for extra JSX content that should be set. But here I only want to have my buttons and my children and the buttons are wrapped by the menu element then. And as you can see if you reload the app, everything still works as it did before. And whilst this pattern might maybe look a bit redundant for this basic app here, it is a crucial pattern. 39659756.title: Setting Component Types Dynamically
39659756.transcript: Now with that Tabs component added here, there's one other thing I wanna do with that component and one other concept and pattern I wanna explore together with you. And this extra concept is related to this menu element here in this case. In this Tabs component, we might wanna wrap our buttons with a menu, but maybe we wanna make this Tabs component a bit more flexible. And if it is being used in different parts of the application, which is not the case here, but which could be the case in bigger applications, we might want to be able to use different wrapper elements around our buttons in those different places in the application. Now, one way, of course, would be to simply get rid of the menu here and to then use the elements that we wanna use as a wrapper here in the place where we're setting the buttons. Now, this would be one way of solving this but a different way, which is maybe more elegant is to keep using this wrapper element here because this also ensures that this separation between our buttons and the content is always enforced. But we might want to allow the developer to choose which wrapper element should be used if the Tabs component is used in different places of the application. And for that, we could accept an additional prop that could be named buttonsContainer. Though the name, of course, is up to you. But the idea here behind this prop is to get the element that should be used as a wrapper so that in Examples.jsx, I can go to the Tabs component and set this buttonsContainer prop, which I just added in my destructuring code in the Tabs.jsx file. And then maybe here, set this to menu if I wanna use the menu element or ul if I wanna use the unordered list element instead, or div if I wanna use a div or maybe even a custom component, like the Section component if I would want to use this. So I basically want to be able to use the identifier of a component as a value for this prop, not the component itself with those angle brackets but just the identifier, like for example, menu. And there is an important difference between built-in and custom components, which you could already see here. Custom components must be passed as a dynamic value so to say like this. So with curly braces, and then your function name like this. But for built-in elements like menu, you're not doing it like this because that would look for a variable named menu in your code and try to pass the value stored in such a variable as a value for this buttonsContainer prop. And I don't have a variable named menu in my code here but instead for built-in elements like menu or div, you simply pass the identifier as a string like this here. So that's an important difference here. But how can we now use this buttonsContainer that contains this component identifier inside of the Tabs component? Well, we could try to simply replace the hard-coded menu element identifier between those angle brackets here with buttonsContainer, so with that prop name here, but this wouldn't work. Instead, this would be looking for a built-in element called buttonsContainer. And I'm talking about a built-in component here because it's starting with a lowercase character, but of course, there is no built-in component called buttonsContainer. Instead, we wanna use the value that's received on this buttonsContainer prop here as an identifier. So what we have to do here to make sure that instead of looking for a built-in component named buttonsContainer, it takes a look at the value inside of this prop and treats that as a custom component, which should be rendered. We have to add a special const or a variable here, which could be named Buttons or ButtonContainer like this, but which starts with a capital case character, that's important. And then here in this ButtonsContainer, we could store this buttonsContainer. So with that, we're basically creating a variable or constant in this case, which can be used as a custom component because it starts with an uppercase character and which can therefore be used here like this. And now what React will do here is it will take a look at the value stored in buttonsContainer and that will, of course, be the value received on this buttonsContainer prop. And it will then either see that it's a string and in that case, it will try to look for a built-in component that can be identified by that string value. Or if we would pass in a custom component like section here, React would also recognize that and see that we're not trying to output some built-in component here, but that we're instead trying to display some custom component here and it would then render that component. It would call that component function, so to say. But in this case, I wanna stick to this string identifier. And with that, if we save that, if I reload, this all works snd if we inspect our tabs here, we can also still see that there is this menu element being used. And we could now, on the other hand, also replace menu here with div. And if I save that, we don't just see that the styling is broken because the menu element was actually used in some CSS rules here but we also see that indeed, here we have a div as a wrapper around these buttons. And with that, we're now able to dynamically set the wrapper that should be used around these buttons when we're using this Tabs component. Here I'll go back to using a menu but knowing about this pattern where you essentially pass a component identifier as a value for a prop and you're then using this identifier inside of a component to dynamically render different HTML elements is another crucial concept you should be aware of. And of course, here we could have also taken some shortcut and instead of remapping this prop to some constant, we could have simply accepted a prop that starts with an uppercase character. We then just have to make sure that we also set this prop with an uppercase character here in the place where we're using this component. And with that, we would achieve the same result as before. So now here I got the menu back, for example. But this is a crucial pattern. And whilst it might be confusing at first, it's, in the end, simply about receiving a component identifier as a value for a prop. And the only two important things to remember here is that that prop then must be usable as a custom component in the receiving component. So the tabs component in this case. So it must start with an uppercase character or it must be remapped to a constant or a variable that starts with an uppercase character. And the other important thing is that when it comes to these identifiers, you use string names for the built-in elements like menu, ul or div and you use your component functions like this here if you wanna use a custom component. You're not calling the function and you're not using it with angle brackets like this. Instead, you're just using the function name if you wanna pass the identifier for a custom component as a prop value. But here it's this string identifier because I wanna use the built-in menu component.

39659768.title: Concept Repetition: Splitting Components & Building Reusable Components

39659774.title: Component Instances Work In Isolation!
39659774.transcript: Now, before we're going to continue working on this player component and this editing functionality, there's one important thing I wanna point out here. As you can see in this application here, if I reload if I click Edit here, we show the input field here but the other player component here does not change. Here we're still showing the player name instead. And that's a pretty important thing to note because in the end of course we are using the same component here, the player component. But whenever you are using or reusing a component, React will basically create a new isolated instance. So even though both these players here use the same player component, they work totally isolated from each other. If the state in this first player component instance here changes, the second player component instance does not care about that at all. It doesn't even know about that. So we have a shared component with the same logic inside of it, but then once you use that component, totally different isolated instances are created which only use the same logic but they then use it on their own. And that's a really important feature in React, and a feature which I absolutely wanna emphasize here because this is one of the most powerful React features. Having this isolation allows you to build super complex reusable components which don't interfere with each other. Because of course here it would not be great if we would click Edit here and the second player would get edited for some reason. That would be horrible, and this whole component concept would be useless if they would behave like this, but thankfully in React, they don't.

39659780.title: Best Practice: Updating State Based On Old State Correctly
39659780.transcript: In React, and that's now really important. In React, when updating your state based on the previous value of that state, you should not do it like this. Instead, when updating your state based on the previous value of that state, you should pass a function to that state updating function. This is a strong recommendation by the React team. It's a best practice in the React world and it is simply something you should memorize. When updating your state based on the previous value of that state, you should pass a function to that state updating function instead of that new state value you wanna have. Now, why do you pass a function though? Because this function which you pass here will be called by React and it will automatically get the current state value. So the value before this state update here as an input. So in this case here, we could accept a parameter that could be named editing. So this is again, true or false, just like isEditing. But this will dynamically be set and passed as a value by React when it calls this function and this function, which you pass to setIsEditing should then return the new state you wanna set. And here we can then again use this exclamation mark trick. Now with that, if you save that, you'll see that it still works as before. But of course, this code here now looks unnecessarily complex. What's wrong with just using this code, which also worked? Well, the problem with this code here is that React behind the scenes is, in the end, scheduling those state updates you're triggering with those state updating functions, like setIsEditing. So this state update here is not performed instantly. Instead, it's scheduled by React to be performed in the future. Now, the future will probably be in one or two milliseconds. So it's really fast, but it's not instant. That's important. Now, as a React developer, it's really important to understand that React behaves that way and that behavior also explains why when you copy this state updating function and you paste it in immediately after the first state updating function, you might not get the result you might expect because here you might expect that in line seven, we setIsEditing to the opposite of what it currently is. And since it's false by default, this should set it to true. And then, of course, in line eight since we setIsEditing to true in line seven, we might expect to set it back to false. So with this dummy code here, which I'm adding for demo purposes right now, you might expect that in the end, nothing happens because we setIsEditing to true and then immediately to false. So clicking the button shouldn't do anything. That would be the expectation. But if we save this and you go back to the website, you'll see that that's not the case. Instead, if I click this edit button, the input does appear and it disappears if I click save. So I got the same behavior as I had it without this second line because without that, we can also open and close the input. And that's exactly why you should use this function form because with this code here, React is scheduling these state updates and both are based on the current value of isEditing, which initially here is false. So both these lines here, lines seven and eight are based on isEditing being false because that's the value isEditing has initially when this component function gets executed for the first time. And since this inner function, of course, gets created as part of the component function execution, isEditing has that value it has initially here. Now, calling setIsEditing with the opposite of isEditing here does schedule a state update that wants to setIsEditing to true but does not, as I just mentioned, immediately change that state. Instead, in the next line thereafter, we still have that same old state as we had before because we're, of course, still in the same component function execution cycle so to say. So therefore, this line also does not change the state back to false, but schedules the same update to true and then those two schedule state updates will be executed after each other. Now, things will change if we use this function form for updating the state instead. If we do that in both cases, you will see that now if I save this and I click this button, nothing happens. And that's the case now because this first scheduled update does now automatically get the latest state value for this editing state from React at the point of time where this scheduled update is executed. And here since this component just rendered, that value will be false. But then the update that's scheduled thereafter will indeed also get that latest state value at the point of time where this scheduled update is executed. And since this second scheduled update will, of course, be executed after the first scheduled update, React will pass the updated state value, which will be true in this case here to that function when it is being executed by React. And that's why it's considered a good practice to use this function form here for updating the state instead of using this way of updating it because when using the function form, you simply have a guarantee by React that you'll always be working with the latest available state value. So again, even though it's not needed here, this is simply a best practice and something you should keep in mind if you are updating your state based on a previous state value, you should use this function form here of the state updating function. And with that, we can still open and close this input but we're now doing it in a best practice way.

But it will have a target property, which will refer to the element that did emit the event. So to this input element here. So that's how we get access to the value the user wanted to enter. And we're now setting this value as a new state for player name. And since we're then feeding player name back into the value prop of the input element, that entered value will now also be reflected in this input element. Because we're now updating that value that's displayed based on the value the user wanted to have. So with all of that, I'll get rid of the console lock because I don't really wanna look into this event object, but you can of course do that. But with all of that, if we save all files and reload, you'll see that we still get this initial value. But if I now replace this with MAX, this works, and that's the first new thing. And this is therefore an important new concept. This is how you can get access to values entered by a user into an input field with help of onChange, and managing a separate piece of state, and how you can make sure that this updated value is then reflected back into this input field and also in other parts of the application if needed. By the way, this way of listening to a change on the input and then feeding that updated value back into this input is also called two-way binding, because we're getting a value out of this input and we're feeding a value back into this input. Instead, just as you should use this state updating function when updating your state based on some previous state, it's also strongly recommended that if your state is an object or array, you update that state in an immutable way, which simply means you create a copy of the old state, so a new object or a new array first, and you then just change that copy instead of that existing object or array. And the reason for that recommendation is that if your state is an object or array you are dealing with a reference value in JavaScript. And I want to set that property of that name that's stored in this parameter to this new value which we're also getting as a parameter. And with that, we got a pretty generic handle change function which we now have to connect to our inputs. Now, as you learned before in the course, you can add the onChange prop to add a listener to the change event to this input field. And you can then define which function should get executed when that change event occurs. Now just pointing at handle change like this, so at this handle change function here won't work though because by default when the change event occurs, React will call this function and pass the event object to it. A default event object that is automatically generated by React. But whilst this event object will contain useful data, it will not hold the inputIdentifier and it will also just be one parameter instead of two, which we're expecting here. Therefore, we have to use a trick about which you learn earlier in the course where we create a new anonymous function here, which is then the actual function that's passed as a value to onChange. And that will be executed by React eventually once the change event occurs and inside of this function we therefore then have full control over how handle change will eventually be executed. And therefore, now we can make sure that we pass those two arguments to handle change. And the first argument should be the identifier Which as mentioned before, should be one of these four properties. And for this first input, which is about the initialInvestment, it will be this property here. So that will be my identifier passed to handle change as a string. Because it is this anonymous function that is passed as a value to onChange, and that will be called by React, therefore. Hence this anonymous arrow function in my case here, will get this event object. And this event object, as you learned before in the course, has a target property which will refer to this input. And this input has a value property, which will be the value entered by the user. And with that, we're now calling handle change with our identifier and the value entered by the user so that we should be able to update the state correctly. 39760388.title: Computing Values & Properly Handling Number Values
39760388.transcript: So let's now derive the results data. And for that, of course I'll use this calculate investment results function, which I'm providing to you in that investment.js file. Therefore, in here in Results.jsx, we can in the end derive our results from that input prop here, which in the end of course is that user input state. So we can derive our results from that state. And as you learned before, deriving values, computing values based on state is something you'll do a lot in React. Therefore, here in the results component, I'll use this calculate investment results function, which must be imported from the UTIL folder and DRD investment.js file like this. And to calculate investment results we should pass our input here because that input will be an object at least here in my app that has this shape I'm expecting here. If you are managing your state differently, or if you use different property names in your user input state, you of course first must transform your input object, or create a new user input object that then has this shape here. But here in my case, this user input state which I'm managing in the app component already has the right shape, and therefore I can just pass this prop value like this to calculate investment results. And with that, I'll get my results data here, and for the moment I'll just console.log this results data in this component so that we can take a look at those results. And if I save that and I open the console and I reload, in the developer tools I get an array with 10 elements starting at the index zero. And as you can see, these 10 elements are for the 10 different years. And then in there you can see that for every year I have the amount invested in that year, which of course is the same for every year since it's simply this amount here. But I then also got the interest earned in a given year, which is different for those different years. And then also the value of my investment at the end of the year, which is simply my initial investment, plus any annual investments, plus that interest earned. And of course also the interest earned in earlier years. So that's my investment data. And if I clear this and I, for example change the initial investment, you see that this gets recalculated, but you now also see that I seem to have a strange error in there. The interest amount looks pretty wrong and the value at the end of the year does as well. And then from a certain point on I'm getting not a number as an interest value. What's the problem here? Well, the problem here is that in JavaScript when you're getting the value of an input field, like here for this initial investment input field, but really for any input field, that value when extracted as we're doing it here will always be a string. Even if you set the type here to number this does not influence the type of value you're getting in JavaScript. Instead, you will always get a value of type string. And that is the problem here in our calculate investment results function, because in there I'm then in the end performing mathematical calculations with strings. And when using the plus operator in a calculation and one of those values is a string, JavaScript will not use the plus operator to add two numbers, but instead it will concatenate to two values as a new string. Which leads to these super long numbers here, which in the end are not numbers but simply concatenated strings. And you can see this here in year one, in the first year. Here, in the end that's simply the number 15,000, which is my initial investment combined with the number 2,100, which is my annual investment and this interest earned here. So these in the end are two numbers, not treated as numbers, but instead concatenated as a string. And that's the problem here. That's the bug in this application. And to fix this error, we should go back to the app component where we are storing the values entered by the user and we should simply add a plus in front of new value. Because simply adding this plus here will force a conversion of this string value to a number value. And with this simple fix added here if you now reload and you change that investment here, you'll see that this bug is gone and we now instead do get proper numbers here. And with that, we now therefore get our results in this results component and we can as a next step output these results in a table.

Or maybe, to be more specific, please enter a duration greater than 0. And I'll give this paragraph a class name of center, since this is a class, I defined in the index CSS file that will make sure that this looks nice. And with that if I reload, I still see my table and I will see my table as I change those inputs, and you see that the table dynamically recalculates as I change those inputs but if I change the duration to 1, it also still works, but if I change it to 0, you see it breaks, and if I change it to a negative value, it also breaks and I see this error message. But if I go back to a positive number again, this all works again. And that's now the finished app.

But, of course, as a React developer, you should know how you can generally apply CSS to your React Components, because as you will learn in this course section, there exist different options with different advantages and disadvantages when it comes to styling React absent Components. And therefore, in this section we'll take a look at styling React apps with Vanilla CSS, so with just CSS, without any special extra packages or features. But we're thereafter going to take a look at a couple of more advanced techniques and extra packages you can use for styling your Components. For example, you will learn how to scope your styles to certain Components with CSS modules, and you'll see what scoping means in this section as well. You will also learn about a popular CSS in JavaScript styling package called Styled Components, and you'll learn how that works and what the advantages of this approach are. And, of course, also about the disadvantages, as for all those approaches, and I'll introduce you to Tailwind CSS and to styling your React apps with Tailwind CSS, since this is also a super popular choice when it comes to styling React applications. And, of course, for all these different approaches, I will show you how to apply both static styles that never change and dynamic or conditional styles that should only be applied when certain circumstances are met. Now as basically for all the sections in this course, this course comes with a starting project, which you find attached, both a local version and a link to CodeSandbox if you wanna follow along there. As always, if you're using the local version, download and extract it, then run npm install once to install all dependencies, and then npm run dev thereafter to start the development server. This will not be required if you're using the CodeSandbox solution. And then you'll have a starting project that in the end renders this webpage here. Now, please note that this webpage here won't work, clicking Create a new account doesn't do anything. And if you do enter valid credentials up here, and you click Sign in, also nothing happens. But, for example, if you enter invalid credentials, and you try to sign in with those, you'll see that these invalid input fields get highlighted, that they get a red background color and a red border, so that we got some styles here that are applied conditionally. And, of course, in general, you can, of course, tell that this webpage already got some styles applied to it, even though it's just the starting project. But at the moment, this page is simply using Vanilla CSS, it's using one CSS file that then applies styles to the entire page, and that's what we'll get started with in the next lecture.

39835968.title: Splitting CSS Code Across Multiple Files
39835968.transcript: So as mentioned before, when it comes to styling React apps, you got many options. And one option definitely is to simply add a CSS file to your project like this index CSS file. Define all your CSS rules in there, using element selectors, using ID selectors, using class selectors. So any CSS rules you can define, define them in here, then import this CSS file. And as you learned earlier in this course already, in a typical React project, for example, in this project here, which uses Vite as an underlying development and build tool, you can import CSS files into JavaScript files like this. And the build tool, so Vite in this case here, will identify such imports and in the end simply make sure that the CSS file you are trying to import here gets injected into this webpage, which is served here. Therefore, if you open the developer tools on your website you will see that in the head section this style here was dynamically injected by Vite. So in here you find all the styles that are defined in index CSS, and this was added dynamically to the DOM here by Vite. So that's how that works and therefore you can include vanilla CSS files like this and you can of course include as many vanilla CSS files as you want. You don't have to go with a single CSS file. Instead you can add as many as you want and you could, for example, therefore also split this file into multiple files where you attach the individual files to the Components to which they belong. For example, here we could say that the header specific styles, so all these rules here in the end, should go into a separate header CSS file, which maybe is co-located next to this header JSX file. So we could add a header CSS file here and then just grab these header related styles, cut them from index CSS, and add them here in header CSS. And then with that in header JSX, we can of course also add such a import, which imports from header.CSS. And with those changes applied, if you save that and you reload, you'll see that the page still looks the same as it did before because of course now this import here is also resolved by Vite js, and the styles defined in the header CSS file are therefore also injected into this webpage. As you can still see if you open the developer tools, there in the head section we now got two injected style tags here, one which includes the header styles and then the other one which includes all the other styles from the index CSS file.

39835972.title: Styling React Apps with Vanilla CSS - Pros & Cons
39835972.transcript: So, as shown in the last lecture, you can use Vanilla CSS by simply adding CSS rules in CSS files and importing those CSS files into your JSX files. That all works and is really great if you want to work with Vanilla CSS, or if you've got another developer or designer who's working on those CSS files because that person can just deliver these files to you. You just import them, and therefore you can work on the components and the JSX code, and the other person can work on the CSS code. That's one advantage of using this Vanilla CSS approach. And of course, you can simply write CSS code as you know it and you don't have to follow any special conventions because it is just Vanilla CSS code. Therefore, when it comes to using Vanilla CSS for styling React apps, one advantage is that you get your CSS code out of the JSX code and you will soon see that this is not necessarily the case for all available styling options. Another advantage is that you do write Vanilla CSS code. So, CSS as you know, and maybe love it or maybe hate it. Depends on you. And of course, another advantage related to that decoupling from JSX code is that different people can work on that code so that you can work on a component without getting distracted or interrupted by the person who needs to add some styling. Of course, we also got some disadvantages with Vanilla CSS though. For example, you need to know CSS in order to write it unless you have a colleague who does that. And then, there's another which we'll take a closer look in the next lecture. Because when writing Vanilla CSS code, when using the solution I just showed you in the last lecture, then that code and those CSS rules are not scoped to components which means you can have styling clashes between different components if you are using CSS rules that may affect JSX code stored in different components. But we'll take a closer look at that disadvantage in the next lecture.

39835980.title: Vanilla CSS Styles Are NOT Scoped To Components!
39835980.transcript: Now, as mentioned in the previous lecture, one disadvantage of using Vanilla CSS, is that the CSS rules and your CSS code in general, is not scoped to the component to which you maybe want it to belong, and let me simply show you an example here. In that Header.css file, we got a couple of rules that should affect the styling of our header here. For example, also this rule here, which styles the paragraph that's shown in the header. Now since we're importing this Header.css file in the Header.jsx file, you could think that the rules defined in here only apply to the JSX code defined in this header component because it's that component file where we are importing the CSS file. But that's not the case. As I can show you, by removing this header part in front of the paragraph rule, so that now I'm just targeting any paragraph no matter if it's in a header or not. And if I then temporarily go to my Authinputs.jsx file here, and there maybe above my buttons here, I add a paragraph where I say, Some text, just as a demo. If I do that and I saved it, you'll see that this paragraph here, receives exactly the same styling as this paragraph. And, of course, I can easily prove that by temporarily changing the color from this gray color, which I want to have, to some red color in the Header.css file. If I do that, you will see that now this, and, of course, also this paragraph, both paragraphs are red. So this bottom paragraph here gets targeted by this rule, even though that bottom paragraph is defined in a different component in a different file. And therefore, it's really important to understand that even if you do split your CSS code in multiple files, and you then do import some of these files into certain component files, the CSS rules in those files will not be scoped to the components to which they belong, because they don't really belong to these components. Instead, if you open the developer tools again, keep in mind that all these styles are in the end just injected into the head section here by Vite, so the header styles and the other styles. And therefore, of course, they are applied globally to this page. And that's not necessarily a problem, but it is something to keep in mind. When using this Vanilla CSS approach, your styles are not scoped, and therefore here I'll revert this change here, and set the color back to the color I wanted to have. And I'll get rid of this paragraph in AuthInputs which I don't really need, but you should keep in mind that this rule, like all these rules, and all the rules in the index.css file, will not be scoped to any component.

39835986.title: Styling React Apps with Inline Styles
39835986.transcript: So we got this at least potential disadvantage that CSS code is not scoped to components when using vanilla CSS. So what can we do about that if we wanna achieve some scoping? Well, one solution would be to switch to inline styles. So to apply these paragraph styles inline to the paragraph to which they should apply, for example. And inline styles simply means that instead of defining CSS styles in a CSS file, you apply them right in your JSX code. And you can do that in React by setting the style prop on JSX elements, just as you can set the style attribute in vanilla HTML. The style prop does not take a string which then contains color red, for example, though, even though that is how you would use this attribute in just HTML. But if you would try to use that here in JSX, in your React app, you'll see that you get an error that the style prop expects a mapping of style properties to values, not a string. So this is not how you should use the style prop in JSX in React apps. Instead the style prop takes a dynamic value, hence we need the opening and closing curly braces, and then you pass an object as a value to the style prop. And that's important to understand here. This here is not some special double curly brace syntax you have to use for styling, instead this is the regular dynamic value syntax where you then just pass an object as a value instead of a number, or a boolean, for example. And it's now in this object where you set up the styles that should be applied to this paragraph as key-value pairs. So that for example, if you would want to give this paragraph a red color, you would add color as a key, and then a colon, since we are in a JavaScript object, and then the value as a string here, so red in this case. And with that, this would be applied as a inline style right here in the JSX code. And if we save this, you indeed see that now the paragraph is red again. And of course, this does not just work for colors, but you can set any CSS styles like this. For example, also the text alignment. For that you just have to keep in mind that text-align would be an invalid property name in JavaScript, and therefore you either have to wrap this with single quotes, or more common and recommended, you have to switch to the camel case notation where you omit the dash, and you start the next word in the word with an uppercase character. And then you can set this to left, for example. And with that here, it's actually still centered because it turns out that this styling here is actually not required, though you can see that it is applied at least, you also see that down here. In this app it just doesn't have an effect here for this paragraph because the header uses Flexbox, and automatically centers all its children. If you disable that, though, you see indeed that it is on the left now. But here I'll just reload and go back to the old styles, but this is how you could apply inline styling, and one advantage of this inline styling approach is of course that these styles here really only affect the JSX element on which you add them. And therefore using inline styles also has some advantages and disadvantages. And one advantage is that it's pretty easy to add them, as you saw, and that the styles you do add as inline styles only affect the element to which you add them, and no other element. Of course, this also has a disadvantage, though, because besides, of course, also having to know CSS when using inline styles, another disadvantage is that you have to style and target every element individually. If you use style files here, you can target all paragraphs with one rule, and you don't have to add this here to every paragraph on its own, which is the case with inline styles. If we had multiple paragraphs that should all have this style here, you would have to copy and paste this style prop with its object here to all the paragraphs that should get this style. And if you then wanna change the styling, you again have to do that for all paragraphs. So that's definitely a disadvantage here. And another potential disadvantage of using inline styles is that you got no separation between CSS and JSX code. Instead, all your CSS code lives in your JSX code, which means the person who writes the JSX code probably also needs to be the person who adds the styling, or you and your designer constantly need to switch working on that file since you both need to work on the JSX code. You for the structure and the logic, and your colleague for adding the styles, and that of course can be annoying. But inline styles also have one more advantage I wanna mention, which we'll take a closer look at in the next lecture. Because with inline styles, adding and setting styles dynamically, conditionally to be precise, is pretty simple as you will see.

39835994.title: Dynamic & Conditional Inline Styles
39835994.transcript: So let's stick to inline styling for now, though I will remove the inline styles from this paragraph because I don't really need them here anymore. But let's now switch to AuthInputs and there let's say we wanna conditionally style those inputs if they are invalid. And I am actually already doing this here by conditionally adding a class name. And I'll get back to this in just a second. For the moment, let's comment out this line of code here on both inputs and let's instead try to achieve this by adding inline styles. Now first, let's take a look at the index.css file and there at these invalid classes here, which are responsible for applying some invalid styles. And let's now try to set this background color. So this reddish color here with inline styles on this input here if it happens to be invalid. For that, of course, we can set background color. And now setting a value conditionally, so dynamically in the end, is pretty simple. We just have to check what our condition is and in this case, it's this emailNotValid constant, which contains true or false depending on the result of this code here. So depending on this condition here in the end. And we can therefore then just use emailNotValid here in a turnary expression to set the background color that should be applied if this is true. So if the email address is not valid here, and to define the color that should be applied if this is not true. So if the email is valid. So here, if email is not valid, I wanna apply this reddish color, otherwise I wanna apply my default background color, which is this background color here, this light grayish color. And that's therefore, the value we wanna use here in the else part of the turnary expression. And that's all. If we now save that, you'll see that if I click sign in here, this input here gets highlighted with a light red color thanks to my conditional styling. And this, of course, becomes even clearer if I use just red as a temporary value here, because now it's really red. So let's switch back to the other approach. And that's therefore how you can add dynamic and conditional styles when using inline styling. And we could, of course, also do that on the password input here, but I'm not going to do it here because the disadvantage with inline styling still is that you often end up with a lot of duplication and that all your CSS code goes into the JSX code, which also isn't ideal. And therefore, as a next step, let's take a look at this class name here and let's see how we can conditionally style elements when using vanilla CSS.

39836004.title: Dynamic & Conditional Styling with CSS Files & CSS Classes
39836004.transcript: Pretty much all React applications have parts in them that need to be styled dynamically like we're doing it here with the inline styles. But as mentioned, inline styles have certain disadvantages which I don't wanna have here in this app, and therefore, I'll switch back to using class names and using my standalone CSS files. Though, of course, as mentioned before, you will learn about some other styling options later in this section as well. But as you can already see here, you can, of course, also style elements conditionally when you using extra CSS files, typically achieved by adding a CSS class conditionally. Here, it's just important to note that you should then use ternary expression and add undefined as a class name if you don't wanna have this conditional class applied. Because if you would use this double ampersand shortcut here, which technically would work, the result would be if you reload the app that you get a warning here in the console because in the end, you now added false as a class name because with this trick here, if this condition here is not met, if it's false, you're now setting this here as a value for class name. And since this is a Boolean, it's basically an invalid kind of value for class name which is why we're getting this warning here. That's why when adding a class name conditionally, you should use this ternary expression approach for doing that. Now, of course, it's also possible that you maybe wanna add a class dynamically to an element that also already has a class that should always be applied. Let's say here on this email label, there we could add className and let's say this label here should always have a label class. It's not really needed in this project, but, of course, it could absolutely be the case that such a label class is needed. And this class should always be applied not just when a certain condition is met. But let's now say that in addition to this label class, we wanna add the invalid class to this label here if an invalid value was entered into this input here. Because indeed in the CSS file I provided to you in the index.css file, I got a rule that does change the color of the label if it has an invalid clause. And we could also change this rule to look for a label clause that then also has the invalid clause in addition, so that we do indeed need this label class as well. Now with that, if I save this, you see that now this label is red, but, of course, now it's always red because I'm always adding the invalid class. So to add this class conditionally and merge it with this class which is always required, you can simply set the value here on className dynamically and set it to a template literal by using back ticks. So not single quotes, but this special back tick character you find on your keyboard. This is another way of creating strings in JavaScript. And when using this approach for creating strings, you can use a special JavaScript syntax, this dollar sign followed by opening and closing curly braces to inject dynamic values into that string. And this allows you to construct a string that includes both hard-coded values like this label here as well as dynamically injected values. And you can inject as many values as you need to construct a longer string. And therefore here, we could then check if emailNotValid and if that's the case, inject the invalid class, and otherwise an empty string into this string here so that this overall string is always a string that includes the word label and then separated by a white space either the word invalid if this condition here is met or nothing else. And therefore, with that, you'll see that if I saved it and reload, by default, the label has this grayish look here. But if I click Sign In without entering a valid value, you see that it does get this invalid style and that's therefore how you can merge such styles and how you can add some classes conditionally and other classes permanently. And, of course, we can therefore also copy and paste this down here to the password label and add it there as well, so that now if you save this and you reload and click Sign In without entering valid values in either of these two fields, both labels are now highlighted, as you can tell, and that's how you can apply styles dynamically conditionally with vanilla CSS.

39836012.title: Scoping CSS Rules with CSS Modules
39836012.transcript: So using just CSS is definitely not a bad option. As you saw, it's pretty easy to separate code and to apply styles conditionally but we have this one potentially big disadvantage, that CSS code and CSS rules are not scoped to components, that you can accidentally write CSS code that affects more components that it should affect. And we can see this in our application here when we take a look at these labels, they are now centered and initially they weren't. But they are centered because these labels are inside of a paragraph, just as the input here. And therefore due to this rule in Header CSS, which I changed earlier in this section, where for all paragraphs I align my text in the center, I also got these centered labels, which is not the look I wanna have here. Now of course, the fix here is to bring back that header in front of the paragraph so that now I only target paragraphs that are inside of a header. But this of course also implies that I as a developer am forced to carefully think about my rules and my selectors here, which might be a good thing, but which also means some extra work. It would be nicer if this would instead be scoped. So if it couldn't affect any other component than the component that is defined in the file where I'm importing this. And that's exactly the behavior you can achieve with CSS Modules, which allows you to write Vanilla CSS code and rules that are then scoped. Now, CSS Modules is an approach, a solution you could say, that in the end needs to be implemented and enforced by the build process that's used in your React project. It's not a default browser or JavaScript feature. Instead, CSS Modules is an approach where the build tool will transform your CSS class names and only those two class names that are guaranteed to be unique per file. And to show you what I mean, let's switch from the P element selector here in Header CSS to the let's say paragraph class selector with this dot here in front of it. Now of course we can add that here with class name paragraph, and as a result, you will see that this still gets the same style as before. These labels now no longer get those styles because those don't have that class. But if I would add this class here to this paragraph that contains my label with class name paragraph, then you would see that with this added here, this is again centered because just by using a CSS class as a selector, these styles do not get scoped. But they will now get scoped if you now rename the header CSS file to header.module.CSS. This .module naming pattern here is basically a signal for the underlying build process. So for this Vite build tool, which is getting used in this project here to process this file here slightly differently because now we also have to import it differently, not just because we must change this name of course But also because now we must import something from this header.module file and that something will be a JavaScript object that will be generated by that underlying build process, which you could name classes or styles, whatever you want But classes is a fitting name because this object will contain your class names transformed to unique class names. For example, here for this paragraph class. Now with this import added, we can go to this paragraph and instead of adding paragraph as a text value for class name, we should now add a dynamic value and use this class's object that we are importing up here to access .paragraph here, .paragraph because I have a class named paragraph in there. If this would be named para, we would have to access .para here, but I chose paragraph as a name, so I'll use that in both files. With that, if you save that, you'll see the same result as before. But if you inspect this paragraph In the header now. you'll see something interesting there. If you take a look at the class that's actually applied In the rendered DOM, you see that it has this weird name here, which is definitely not the name we chose. It includes paragraph, but it also then includes more characters and words. And this strange class name here was in the end generated by the built build tool automatically. And if you take a look at the head section here, you'll see that in the injected style here, we got all the other element selectors we got before as well. But this paragraph class here was transformed. The name of it was transformed. It is that weird name we saw being applied to that paragraph a couple of seconds ago. And that's the idea behind CSS modules. This class name is now unique for this component file and therefore for this component, if I add paragraph as a text here in another file, in another component, you'll see that this label is not affected by it. Indeed, if I inspect this control here and this paragraph, it does have this paragraph class of course but this has no effect because this original paragraph class name I defined in the header.module CSS file get transformed by that build process, and therefore this build process in the end ensures that my class styles are scoped to the component file in which I'm importing them. And that's how you can achieve CSS rule scoping with almost Vanilla CSS. And of course, you could now also apply a class like this conditionally if you wanted to. If you had some condition here, and that's of course now just a dummy example, you could apply either this class or no class, depending on whether this condition here is met or not. So you can still apply this conditionally as you learned it before, and you can also inject it into strings with those here with this syntax here because of course in the end, the value stored under this paragraph property will be a string. It will be that unique class name that was generated by the build tool. So that all works. As you see, if I reload here, and therefore it works almost like Vanilla CSS but you got that extra scoping. Hence for CSS modules, the advantage of course still is that your CSS code is decoupled from your JSX code, that you write CSS code as you know and maybe love it, that it can be written by another person. So just as we had it for Vanilla CSS, but we now also got the additional advantage that CSS classes, so rules that are using CSS classes are scoped to the component file which imports the CSS file with those rules. Of course, we also still have some disadvantages though because you still need to know CSS because we're still writing Vanilla CSS in the end. And a new disadvantage when using CSS Modules is that you may end up with many relatively small CSS files in your project when using CSS Modules because as you can already see here, we got this header module CSS file, which contains some CSS code and we could now also add one for auth inputs. And if we had a bigger project with more components and every component has its own CSS file, you would end up with many CSS files where at least some of those CSS files likely won't contain too much CSS code. And that of course could be seen as a disadvantage.

39836018.title: Introducing "Styled Components" (Third-party Package)
39836018.transcript: So now that we had a very detailed look at solutions that all built up on vanilla CSS, in the end, even CSS modules, in the end, do as you saw, we'll now explore a totally different way of styling your React components. And that would be the styled components package, which you can use in React projects. And the idea behind this popular package is that you do not define your CSS rules and styles in separate CSS files, but also not as inline styles but instead in special components that are built with help of that package. And for that, to understand this package, we have to install it, which you can do locally by opening your terminal and then running npm install styled-components or encode sandbox by going to that dependencies area here and simply adding styled components like this as a dependency. This will install it in this project and make it available in this project. Now with it installed locally, you need to restart your development server. On CodeSandbox, this is not required. And now with that installed, let's say here in AuthInput, we now wanna style this div here, let's say with help of that styled-components package. For this, in AuthInputs.jsx, you now need to import something from styled-components and that something is called styled. So you need to import styled from styled-components and with that imported, here in that same file for now, you can then use styled, which turns out to be a JavaScript object to access different properties with the dot notation. And you'll see that these properties in the end map to HTML elements, for example, the h1, h2 and so on elements and also the div element. And what this will do is it will create a div as a separate component but a component that will have any styles you want applied to it. Now, for that, you now need to use a very strange syntax, which you maybe haven't seen before. You need to add two back ticks right after this element property here. So right after this div here. So again here, these are not single quotes, it's also not a double quote. Instead, these are two back ticks, a special character you can find on your keyboard. And this code here, in the end is a so-called tagged template, which even though you maybe never heard about it turns out to be a regular JavaScript feature. So not React specific and not specific to that package. Attached, you find a link to this MDN article where you can learn more about tagged templates if you want to. But in the end, a tagged template is like a function which receives this template literal as an input you could say. And it's now this template literal, which should now contain all the styles you wanna apply to this div here. And the great thing, of course, in JavaScript about template literals is that you can write multiline code like this without breaking the string. So now with that added, we can see that this div, which I wanna replace here, has a class name of controls. So therefore, we can go back to the index.css file and there look for all the styles that should be applied to that div. So to that class here. Of course, I then also have some styles that depend on that class being on a parent element, but we'll take care about those later. For now, let's focus on these styles here, which should directly be applied to that div and we can now copy those styles and paste them into this template literal here like this. And you don't need to use a camel case notation or anything like that here. Instead, you can write standard CSS code in here. And what this overall code will give you will be a component, a React component that automatically returns a div that has these styles applied to it. And it will be a div that internally also uses the special children prop so that it can be wrapped around other content. So with that, we can now store this component in a new constant or variable that here could be named ControlDiv or something like this, ControlContainer maybe. The name is up to you but it will be such a styled div now. And you can now use this custom component here to replace this div, here the opening tag, and then further down below, here the closing tag. Now, with that, I, of course, have a second component in this AuthInputs component file and we could outsource this into a separate file if we wanted to. But for now, since I really only use this component in this file here, I'll keep it in that file, If I were using that in other components as well, storing it in a separate file, of course, would make more sense. But with that, we can now save this. And if we now reload, we, of course, see that we lost some styles here because I did remove that controls CSS class that was applied here before. But what we can see if we inspect this and we take a look at this diff here is that in there, we got these styles being applied to it, these styles, which we did add here. And by inspecting this, we also see how this works. Under the hood, this styled-components package simply also creates unique CSS class names and defines the rules for these classes in the head section where this gets injected here. And then it also adds those created classes to your elements, here to that div, which it created because we used div here. If I would've used p here and I save this, this would become a paragraph instead of a div. But here I want a div. But this is how you can work with styled components. Otherwise, we could apply the text xl utility clause, which still sets up a large text but not as large as it is with text for xl. And therefore if I save this, it looks exactly as before, but if I shrink my screen, you see the text gets smaller and the margin here below the header also got smaller than it is on bigger screens. 39836118.title: Tailwind CSS: Pros & Cons
39836118.transcript: And that's it for this introduction to Tailwind CSS. Now, as you can tell, when using Tailwind CSS, you do end up with certain elements that have a lot of CSS classes on them, and that of course means that there is more code to add in your JSX code. And there certainly also are developers who don't like having long class name lists as we have it here. Though it's of course worth mentioning that Tailwind does actually work with React really well because you can create reusable utility Components like this input Component which then have all these CSS classes inside of them so that you can use them in your application without having to repeat these long class lists over and over again. Instead, as you can see here, these custom input Components can be used in a pretty lean and easy to understand way and all these CSS classes are simply hidden inside of the Component. Therefore, we don't have to repeat this all the time. So when embracing Components, as you should do when building React apps, embracing Tailwind CSS and this idea of building Components by combining multiple classes does actually work really well. Nonetheless, using Tailwind CSS is, of course, something that like all these approaches in the end comes down to personal preference. An advantage of Tailwind CSS is that you don't really have to know CSS to use it, and you can develop React apps or web apps in general to be precise pretty rapidly when using Tailwind CSS because as you'll see, it doesn't take you very long to memorize the most important utility classes, and especially with this nice extra extension, which even suggests classes and helps you find the right classes, styling elements, and Components is actually pretty easy and done quickly when using Tailwind CSS. You also avoid style clashes, of course, because you don't define any global CSS rules on your own. And Tailwind CSS gives you all these advantages whilst still being highly configurable and customizable and extensible so that you can really tweak it exactly to your likings and requirements. But you do, of course, end up with those relatively long class name values, at least for certain elements. And any style changes that should be applied, of course, do require the editing of JSX code, so you don't really have a strong separation between JSX and styling code. And you also tend to end up with a lot of relatively small wrapper Components like this input or button Component, which isn't too bad though. After all that's the idea behind React kind of. Creating and using them can still sometimes be cumbersome and annoying. Or if you don't do that, you would end up with a lot of copy and pasting because if you don't create that input component here, you will have to copy and paste that code to all the inputs and labels you want to use in your application. And therefore, as for all these solutions shown in this section, there are various advantages and disadvantages but now since you got an introduction to all these different approaches for your future projects, you can hopefully pick the solution you personally like most. For this course, I will mostly stick to Vanilla CSS simply because it allows me to focus on teaching you React and it avoids writing all those extra CSS class names. But in your projects, you should definitely use the approach you personally like.

39836178.title: Module Introduction
39836178.transcript: Like all developers, as a React developer, you will eventually write code that contains errors and bugs, because we all do. And therefore in this course section you will learn how to efficiently debug your React apps, so how to find and fix errors. And we're going to get started by taking a closer look at those error messages React sometimes shows you, so that you can make sense of these error messages, and so that they actually help you find and fix errors. But we're not going to stop there, because some errors don't actually produce error messages. Instead, unfortunately, as a developer, you sometimes also write code that leads to logical errors, and therefore in this section you will also learn how you can find and fix such errors with help of the browser developer tools and the browser debugger. In addition, in this section, you will also learn about React's Strict Mode, how you can enable that mode, and why you might want to do that. And last but not least, we'll also take a look at the React DevTools, and you will learn how to install these tools and what you can do with them.

39836194.title: Understanding React's "Strict Mode"
39836194.transcript: Now besides error messages, logical thinking and the browser dev tools, there also is another tool, you could say, another React feature that can help you catch and fix some kinds of errors. And to demonstrate this feature, I edited the Results.jsx file and I moved this results array here out of the component function right below the import statement, and attached you find the updated Results.jsx file which contains this change. Now I did this for a reason because with that, if you reload, everything seems to work. But as you make a change here you see that something seems to be wrong because now the table just gets longer and longer and we now also have an error message in the browser console, a couple of errors actually if you take a look, and all these errors are about two children having the same key. So they seem to be related to our list here because it's in that list where we are outputting elements that have a key prop. And of course it's pretty obvious here that the problem is that this list keeps on getting longer and longer instead of being cleared and replaced as we edit those inputs. Now, we could again use the debugger or take a closer look at our code to find out what's wrong here. But before we do that, it's worth noting that we only see this problem as soon as we start editing some values here, which probably arguably is something we would be doing when testing this application. But nonetheless, it's not an error we see immediately. And at least this is an error React can surface to us immediately right when the app starts. It can do that if we use StrictMode and therefore we're going to use StrictMode before I'll explain what's causing this error. Using StrictMode typically begins in Index.jsx though you can also enable it just for parts of your application if you wanted to, because StrictMode, in the end is simply a component you import from React, a built-in component called StrictMode. So StrictMode is a component provided by React and you therefore use it like a component. To be precise, it is a component that's meant to be wrapped around other components. For example, here around the App component, we can wrap the App component with the opening and closing tags of the StrictMode component. And that's what I meant when I said you could use it anywhere in your application. You don't have to wrap the root component. Instead, we could also import and use the StrictMode component somewhere deeper, nested inside of our component tree and for example only wrap the Results component or the UserInput component or whatever we want. But it's not uncommon to wrap the root App component with it because StrictMode does a couple of things behind the scenes that can help us catch certain problems in our app. For example, one of the most important things the StrictMode component does is that it will execute every component function twice instead of just once. Now, it only does that during development. So if you would prepare your application for production and you would upload it to some server, StrictMode would no longer execute every component twice as this of course does slightly impact the performance of your application. But it does that during development because when every component gets executed twice, it helps you catch errors like this because now if I reload, since every component gets executed twice including the App component, I start with twice the table here because I seem to have some error in my code that causes this table to grow and grow. So that of course, does not fix the problem, and it also doesn't necessarily tell me yet what's causing this problem but it immediately surfaces that there is a problem. I don't even have to edit the inputs to see that something's wrong. Instead, thanks to StrictMode being enabled and wrapped around my entire app, this error can be seen immediately. And the error here is stemming from the fact that in my Results component, I'm creating this Results array outside of the component function and therefore only once because whilst this component function will be re-executed by React whenever the state in apparent component changes, so whenever the UserInput changes in this case, the other code in this file, so the creation of this array, will not be re-executed, that's still only executed once. And therefore what's happening here is that an array gets created and then inside of the calculateInvestmentResults function, more and more items are being added to one and the same array in memory because the array is never reset or anything like this. That's why we should take this code and move it back into the component function so that a new array is recreated every time this component function is executed. And with that, we can see that if we reload this app, this error goes away and there is no error in the console. And we can now also know with confidence that this also won't change if we start editing these values because we're using StrictMode.

For example, the color mode, the display density, and then also for this components tree, also if the component tree should be expanded by default, and a couple of other things as well. Simply play around with these settings to get a feeling for what they're doing. But if you now click on one of those components, you also learn more about that component. 39836236.title: Module Introduction & Starting Project
39836236.transcript: This course section is about Refs and Portals. Two slightly more advanced React concepts which you definitely don't need in all React apps you are going to build. After all, we did not need those features in the demo apps we built up to this point. But two features which can simplify certain things and also help you solve certain problems which otherwise are almost impossible to solve. And therefore, in this course section here, you will learn what Refs are and how you can, for example, use this Refs feature to gain simplified direct access to DOM Elements and what you can do with that access. You will also learn how you can also use this Refs feature to manage values that shouldn't be State. And you'll see when exactly that could be the case. And you will also learn how you can build Components that expose an API. For example, callable functions to other Components with help of that Refs concept. And then, this section of course also is about Portals and you will learn what Portals are, and how you can use this Portals feature to detach the place where an element gets rendered in the actual DOM from the place where you use it in your JSX code. And obviously at this point, this definitely all sounds pretty abstract and is probably all pretty confusing. But that's why we're now going to dive into a new demo project, which will allow us to use all these features. And step by step along the way, you will therefore then learn how to use them. Now, in this section we are going to build this demo project which in the end is kind of a game where website visitors can start different timer challenges with the goal of estimating when time is up and stopping the timer early enough before it expires. And we're going to build that game with Refs and Portals and of course, also Components and State, and all these things about which you already learned. And as always, you'll find a starting project attached. Both a local project and a CodeSandbox project link, of course. And in that starting project, you'll find a pretty basic app component, and one custom component, a player component, which also is pretty basic. And if you start that development server locally after installing the dependencies locally as you always need to do, you don't need to do that on CodeSandbox, of course. After doing that, if you visit the website, you should see something like this. And please note that at the moment this input here won't really work. If you click Set Name, nothing happens. But that will now be the first thing on which will work. And that will be the first example for using Refs.
