As you learned, React will pass it to this handleChange function, when it executes this handleChange function, which we're passing to the onChange prop, and React will execute that function for every change that happens to the value in that input field, so for every character we type, for example, and then here, we can therefore then set the enteredPlayerName to event.target.value, like this, and then we can feed that state back into the input field so that the latest value is reflected there through the value prop, of course, and we can set value equal to enteredPlayerName, so to our state here, and with that, we got a component that should store that player name. Now, we can also add another function, handleClick, which should be connected to this button through the onClick prop of course, like this, and it's in this function where we wanna take that state and output it here. Therefore, we'll essentially need a second piece of state, which we could name "submitted," or something like this, and initially, that's false, and here in handleClick, we want to set "submitted" to true so that when this button is clicked, we change this state, and we can therefore now conditionally output this part after "Welcome" here by wrapping it with curly braces, and we can check this submitted state with a ternary expression, and if it's true, I wanna output the enteredPlayerName, if it's false, I wanna output "unknown entity" as a string, and with that, we should have everything we need in this component here so that if we save that and we go back and I enter "Max" here and click "Set Name," it says, "Welcome Max," here, and initially, when we reload, it does not update with every keystroke, but only set this when I click here. Thereafter though, it does update with every keystroke because thereafter, we got enteredPlayerName as a value being output here. If we would want to prevent that, we would have to call setSubmitted here in handleChange as well and set it to false here so that as soon as we start typing in that input field again, this state is set back to false and we output "unknown entity" again. So that with that, if I type "Max" here and I set this name and I continue typing, we go back to "unknown entity" until I set my name again. Now of course, with that, we always jump back to "unknown entity" as we type again and we only change that once we set the name again, so still not ideal, and we could work around that by adding more states or computed values to save that name, which we already set in the past, but as you can already tell, it's quite a lot of code we have to write here just to read the value that's entered into this input field at the point of time where this button here is pressed. Because we have to manage that value, that underlying enteredPlayerName state value, with every keystroke, with that onChange prop, to store it and to have it available for outputting it, and we have that second state just to know whether this button here was pressed or not, and this component here is now a great example for a component that can be simplified by using refs.

39836296.title: Introducing Refs: Connecting & Accessing HTML Elements via Refs
39836296.transcript: So what are refs and how can we simplify this component with refs? Well, a ref in React is a value, just as the state in the end is a value and just as a variable contains a value but it's a special kind of value or it's managed by React in a special way to be precise. Now you can create such a ref value in React by importing the use ref hook from React. So another built-in hook function, which like all hook functions, must be called inside of a Component function or inside of a custom hook but that's something we'll explore later. You then create a ref value like this and you can store it in a constant or variable and give it any name you want. Now here I will create a ref value to, in the end, get the value that will be entered into this input field. And therefore, I'll name it input or player name. The name here, of course, is up to you. And whilst you can do more with these ref values, and later in the section you will see what you can do more with them, one thing you can do with them and one thing you will probably do the most with those refs is that you can connect them to JSX elements. And you can do that with a special prop that's supported by all React Components automatically out of the box, the ref prop, which just like the key prop is a special prop. Now React takes such a ref value as an input, so in my case, my player name like this, and with that, this input component here is in the end connected to this ref. Now how does this connection help us though? Well, we can now use this ref value here, for example, in handle click, so when this button here is clicked, to access the underlying connected element, so through player name we can access this input element because player name is connected to it through the ref prop. Therefore, here in handle click, we can get access to the input element though it's worth noting that for all these ref values created with useRef, you first have to access a current property. So this ref value you are getting back from useRef will always, really always be a JavaScript object that will always have a current property and only a current property. And it's then in this current property that the actual ref value, so in this case, the connected input will be stored. Therefore now current, in this case, holds this input element as a value and therefore even though I'm not getting auto suggestion for it here, because my IDE doesn't fully understand this connection here I can now access all the methods and properties that are exposed by that input HTML element. And I really mean the built-in Native input element. On MDN, you can learn all about its methods that are exposed and all the properties that are exposed and you can access all these properties and methods through the ref here, which is connected to this element. And therefore here we can, for example, access the value property which holds the currently entered value. And we could now use that to update our entered player name state, so in handle click we could now call set entered player name to update that state and pass this value which we're getting directly from the input field as a value here. And therefore we don't have to log that value on every keystroke anymore. Instead, we can get rid of the handle change function, get rid of the submitted state and just keep the ref and the entered player name state and down there also get rid of the unchanged prop here and of the value prop here, we don't need all of that. The ref is enough instead. And up here we therefore now just want to check if entered player name is truthy, so if it's not an empty string, for example, in which case we want to output it. Otherwise we output unknown entity. And for return expressions like this, there is a shortcut in JavaScript. You can simply write two question marks and with that you are telling JavaScript that it should output this value if it's truthy or this value if it's falsey. So if entered player name is an empty string, for example. But with that we're now updating the state only on a click and we're getting the value directly from the input element with help of that ref. Therefore, now if we save that and we go back to this application and reload, if I enter Max, nothing happens on every keystroke but if I click set name, it's output here. And if I now continue typing, nothing happens which is great, but if it clicks that name again, it's updated. And that's one of the reasons why this ref feature exists. For use cases like this where you just wanna read a value from an input field, for example, it can really save you a lot of code and lead to leaner Components.

39836302.title: Manipulating the DOM via Refs
39836302.transcript: So as you could see in the previous lecture refs can be really useful. But what if you now want to clear this input whenever we set a name? What if you wanna reset it? Well, you could theoretically in 'handleClick' again use 'playerName.current', so this input element which is stored in that ref, and then set value equal to an empty string. That would clear it. If I save that and I now click 'Set Name', it gets cleared. And you can now argue whether that's good or not, because you must not forget that React is about writing declarative code. And it's about not directly manipulating the DOM yourself. Instead, you wanna let React do that. Now, here in line nine, we're already crossing that line a little bit, but here we are just reading a value. We're not changing anything in the DOM. But here in line 10, we definitely have imperative code. We're not in the declarative world anymore. Instead, we're clearly instructing the browser to set the value of that input to an empty string. And we're therefore basically violating that rule, or that idea, that React should handle all those DOM interactions. Nonetheless, for a use case like this, where you just clear an input, which is then not really connected to any other state or anything like that, you can definitely consider writing code like this. Because overall it allows you to save a lot of code. But you should definitely be careful that you don't start using refs to read and manipulate all kinds of values on your page, because that's really not the idea behind React. But if you can make your life easier as we're doing it here, using refs can be a great alternative to using state and all these other mechanisms you learn about.

39836310.title: Refs vs State Values
39836310.transcript: So refs can be pretty useful, and you will soon learn about another use case where refs can be useful, even if you don't connect them to HTML elements. But before we go there, it's really important to also understand the difference between refs and state because here you could ask yourself, why are we using this state at all? Why don't we get rid of that state here and of that state updating function call here, and we instead directly use playerName.current.value down there. Why don't we do that? And the answer is because it doesn't work If we try to do that, you see that here something seems to go wrong. And indeed here I have an error that it fails to read a property value of undefined. So the problem here is that I'm trying to access this value property on the value stored in playerName.current, and that playerName.current seems to be undefined here. And the reason for that is that I'm trying to output the value stored in playerName.current here when the component gets rendered for the first time. But when that happens, this connection here through this ref prop won't be established yet. So in that first component render cycle, when that component function is executed for the first time, playerName.current will simply be undefined. It's only in the next render cycle that it will have disconnection to this input field and that we will be able to reach the value. But we could, of course, work around that by checking if playerName.current is truthy. So if it's not undefined, and if that's the case, we try to output playerName.current.value because then we know that playerName.current is set so that this connection has been established. And otherwise we output that. If we tweak the code like this and we save that the error indeed goes away. But you will now also see that if I enter something here and I click Set Name, nothing happens. It's cleared, but unknown entity isn't replaced. And that is the key difference between refs and state. It's not just that this connection doesn't exist yet initially. More importantly, it's that whenever a ref changes, the component function does not re-execute. And for a state, that's of course different. Whenever you update state by calling that state updating function the component function will be re-executed. And for refs, that's simply not the case. So, therefore, both state and refs are important concepts and have their purposes in the React world. State values do cause a component to be re-executed when changed through that state updating function and therefore state should be used whenever you have values that should directly be reflected in the UI. On the other hand, you should not use state for values that are only used behind the scenes and have no direct UI impact. And you'll see an example for such a value in the next lectures in this section. Now, refs on the other hand, do not cause a component to be executed again. The component will not be reevaluated just because the ref value changed. But you can use refs, as I showed you before, to gain direct DOM element access. And you'll see another example where this can be useful later. But you, of course, already see an example here as well where we use refs to get the value that's currently entered in an input field. But now with that, I'll also bring back my state here because, as you now learned, you need that state to update the UI and to reflect the updated value on the website. And with that, we're back to that state where it works as it should, and we can now dive deeper into refs.

Here, on click, we want to execute handleStart like this. So timerStarted could be the name and setTimerStarted and we want to set this to true right before we set the timer, not inside of it once it expired, but right before it, setTimerStarted to true, or right after it. That also works because this line will execute immediately after setting the timer, not after it expired. It's only the code in here that will only execute once the timer expired. This code here will execute right after the timer was set and with this second state added here, we can now also update the UI to reflect this information that the timer is running. For example, here, instead of always saying "Start Challenge", we can check if timerStarted is truthy and if that's the case, we wanna show "Stop" instead of start. Like this. Here, I want to add the active CSS class conditionally. So we can check if timerStarted is truthy, in which case we add active as a class, otherwise, we add nothing. So we set the class name to undefined and then in here, we check if timerStarted is true and if that's the case, I wanna output "Time is running". Otherwise, I want to output "Timer inactive" like this so that the UI is updated based on whether the timer is running or not. And that's where a ref can help us.

39836346.title: Using Refs for More Than "DOM Element Connections"
39836346.transcript: So how can we stop this timer from inside this function? Well, JavaScript has a clear timeout function, which can be used to stop a timer but it needs a pointer at that timer, the ID of that timer you could say as an input. Now such a pointer is returned by setTimeout. So here we can store this timer in a constant, for example, and therefore, of course, we could add a variable here, timer outside of this function so that both functions can use it and set it equal to the timer here and then use it as a value in here. And now we just need to connect handleStop to this button if the timer is running. And therefore we don't always want to pass handleStart to onClick but we want to check if timerStarted is truthy. And if that's the case, we wanna point at handleStop so that this function is executed, if this button is clicked and the timer was started and otherwise it's handleStart. But now with that, handleStop should execute if the timer is running and we should clear this timer. So if this works, we should not see you lost after starting and stopping if we stop quick enough. So therefore, if I start this and stop this, I still get you lost and I did press stop immediately. So I start this, I stop this and still I get you lost. So somehow the timer was not stopped as it seems. And the problem is that we're using a variable. As you know, whenever state changes, this component function re-executes. And that, of course, also means that this variable is recreated. So the timer used in handleStop will be a different timer than in handleStart because in between, the component function re-executed because we updated the state in handleStart. That's why we can't use this variable. Now, we could use this variable if we define it outside of the component function because then it would not be recreated. And as a result, now if I reload this, if I start and stop, indeed we never see this you lost message. Of course, this part here isn't updating. We'll take care about that later but we don't see the you lost message but we'll now face a different problem. And to show you which problem we'll face, I'll start the five second challenge. Then immediately the one second challenge, and then I'll stop both challenges. So start five, start one, stop one, stop five. And you'll see as a result, I still get you lost here in the five second challenge even though I stopped it. I'll show it to you again. Start, start, stop, stop. I did click here on the five second challenge. Still, I get you lost and I don't get that if I just start and stop this challenge. Now here I stopped it and therefore, I never get you lost. So why do I get this strange behavior where the five second challenge is lost even though I stopped it if I also started and stopped the one second challenge? I'm getting that behavior because this timer variable is now a variable defined in this TimerChallenge file outside of this component function. And therefore, it's actually a variable that will be shared across all component instances that are based on this component function. And therefore, when I start the five seconds challenge, this timer will store the timer of the five seconds challenge but if I then after starting the five seconds challenge, also start the one second challenge, timer here will be overwritten with a pointer at that one second timer. And that pointer at the five second timer will basically be thrown away by React. Therefore, when I start this, start this, stop this and stop this, I'm not stopping anything here in the five second challenge because that pointer at the five second timer was thrown away when I started the one second challenge because this variable here is defined in this file and is therefore, shared across all instances of this component. And that's why a variable is not the solution. So if a variable is not the solution, what is a solution then? Well, refs can be a solution because you cannot just use refs to connect to HTML elements, even though that is a very common use case but you can also use refs to manage any kind of value. And therefore, here in this TimerChallenge component, we can create a new ref, let's say a timer ref, a timer constant, which stores this ref. And we can now use this timer and set timer.current because you always must target this current property as you learned and set this equal to the timer. And now we can clear the timeout by clearing it with timer.current because in this current property, we'll store this pointer at this timer. And now since this is defined inside of the component function, it will be component instance specific. Every component instance of this TimerChallenge component will get its own timer ref that works totally independent from the other refs that belong to the other instances of that component. But at the same time, unlike variables defined in component functions, this ref will not be reset or cleared when this component re-executes. Instead, just as with state values, React will store these timer values behind the scenes and make sure that they don't get lost as this component function re-executes. That's another special thing about refs. Like state values, the value here is not lost when this component function re-executes. But unlike state, setting this value also doesn't cause this component function to execute again and therefore, now with that, finally, we can save this and start and stop this and we don't get you lost. And I can now also start and stop both challenges and stop both and I never get you lost in any of the two. And that's therefore another use case for refs if you have a value that must be managed but that isn't really a state because that timer itself has no direct impact on the UI. We only care about whether a timer was started, but we, for example, don't wanna update the UI when we stop the timer here. Nothing changes here. I just clear the timer but I don't wanna trigger a UI update. So if you have cases like this where you have a value that doesn't really impact the UI, at least not directly, and you still need to manage it such that it's not reset when the component is re-executed, then you might have a great use case for a ref. And of course, you'll see more such example use cases throughout this section and also throughout this course.

Instead that's a standard browser feature here. If I click this, you'll see that after one second, I indeed do get that modal here. So passing this dialog graph here to ResultModal and extracting and using it there clearly works. However, this is only the case because this project here uses at least version React 19. Prior to that version, so in React projects that use older React versions, you would've gotten an error. Their React would've complained that you can't accept a ref like a regular prop. This simply didn't work in the past. And therefore, since my goal with this course, of course, is to teach you react even if you are using a slightly older version, I'll also show you how you would set up this component to receive a ref if you couldn't use this approach, so if you were using an older React version. So a function that must be imported from React. And that function was called forwardRef, or is called forwardRef because you can still use it, even if you are using a more modern version of React. And as a result of calling forwardRef, you'll get back an adjusted version of this component function, you could say. An adjusted version, which I'll therefore store in a constant called ResultModal. So that's now my adjusted component here, so to say. And you should then still export that adjusted component here to make sure that it's still available outside of this file. Now with forwardRef wrapped around this component function, this component function then receives an additional parameter. So a second parameter besides receiving that props parameter. And that second parameter then is this ref prop, so to say. So this special ref prop. So whichever value you are setting to the ref prop on your component, in this case this dialog ref, whatever ref you are setting to this ref prop here is received as a value through that special ref parameter when using forwardRef. And again, just to be very clear, here in this project, since we are using a recent version of React here, forwardRef is not required. Using it here is optional. I'm just showing you this syntax here so that you know about it because you will encounter it in many projects out there and you will need it if you are working on some project that might not be using the latest React version. 39836376.title: Exposing Component APIs via the useImperativeHandle Hook
39836376.transcript: Now this approach here where we forward this ref to this dialog has one small potential flaw, because the problem with that is that the timer challenge component, which uses the ResultModal component in the end, needs to know that this dialog ref here will be attached to this dialog element. And of course here, in this tiny demo application where we are the only developer working on it, that's absolutely no problem because we wrote this code, we know what it does. But keep in mind that you might eventually work on bigger React projects and that there, you might be writing some components and other developers write other components. And you might be working with some of those components written by other developers and vice versa. If you don't always have to dive into those components to understand how they work internally, so that you use them correctly and you for example, call the correct method on that ref here, that can be a problem. If that other developer, for example, switches from a dialog to a div, you are lost, because now calling showModal won't work anymore. Therefore, it might be preferable to build this ResultModal component such that it exposes its own function that can be called with help of a ref outside of that component. So from here for example, which will work independent of how this JSX code might change in the future, so that the person working on that ResultModal component can change it however they want, as long as they keep this one function which is exposed to you, which you call on this component, on this ResultModal component, instead of calling showModal on that internal dialog element. And this is something that can be achieved by using another special hook in the component that wants to expose such a callable function. It's the useImperativeHandle hook. You can call this hook in this component function here to define properties and methods that should be accessible on this component here from outside this component. And I will say right away that you won't use this hook too often because in most cases, you should prefer working with props and so on. But for use cases like this, this hook can be very helpful to make this component more stable and reusable and useImperativeHandle wants two arguments now and the first argument should be a ref object, as you for example receive it from your forwardRef function, so this second parameter I'm getting here. Now of course, if you are not using forwardRef, because you are in a React project that's using at least React version 19 or higher and you therefore don't need forwardRef, you would of course simply use the ref that you are getting and extracting as a regular prop and you would pass that to useImperativeHandle, because ultimately, useImperativeHandle simply wants a ref, a ref that's passed to this component. Now here, I'm getting it with help of forwardRef since I deliberately used forwardRef in this project to show you how this function would work because you might need it in older react projects. But useImperativeHandle of course also works with more recent react versions where you might not use forwardRef. Either way, the ref parameter, the ref argument here isn't the only argument that's wanted by useImperativeHandle. Instead, it also wants a second argument. And this second value then must be a function that then returns an object which groups all the properties and methods that should be exposed by this component to other components. For example, here we could expose a open method, though this name here is totally up to you, but it's this method that will soon be callable from outside this component. Now with that added here, we should also import the useRef hook here in this ResultModal component file and execute useRef in the ResultModal. Because now, we'll need a separate ref for reaching out to the dialog because the idea now is to detach this dialog element, which is used in this ResultModal component, from any other outer components. So from the timer challenge component for example. Therefore, we'll add a new ref here, which I'll name dialog like this. And it's now this dialog ref which is passed as a value to the ref prop to the dialog. And it's now in this open method in this object returned here by this function, which is passed as a second argument to useImperativeHandle. It's this open method where I now want to use this dialog ref and they're the current property to call showModal, so that when this open method here is called the showModal method on this dialog is called. And now with that setup here, we can go back to the timer challenge and there, this dialog ref is now connected to our ResultModal still, but under the hood it will basically, you could say refer to this object now. That's what useImperativeHandle does here. And therefore, we're now able to use dialog.current.open here. Because this open method, which I'm calling here is this open method I defined here. Because as I just mentioned, it's now this object that's in the end stored in this dialog ref here in the timer challenge component. Because we're passing dialog as a value for ref to ResultModal. And thanks to forwardRef and useImperativeHandle, this connection to this object here is established. So it's now this open method which is called from here and therefore now, if you save this as a result, if you start this challenge and you don't stop it, we got the same result as before, but now we detached the timer challenge component from the dialog element in this ResultModal component. And we could now change this here and everything would still work as long as we would also adjust the logic here in this open method. That now of course, would have to change. But now the person working on that ResultModal component is totally free to change it however it wants, as long as this component keeps on working, and as long as it keeps on exposing a method called open because it's now this open method which we're calling here. And again in this basic simple demo app, this of course doesn't matter, but in bigger projects, this is a pattern and a feature that can be helpful. And with that, I'll switch back to a dialog here, and you now know how you could use this feature to expose callable functions from inside your components.

39836418.title: Introducing & Understanding "Portals"
39836418.transcript: Now with that, we're almost done for this section. You learned a lot about refs and passing data between components and so on. But wait a second. This section also is about portals, and that's therefore what we'll tackle next to then conclude this section. So what's this portals feature all about, and how can you use it? To understand the idea behind portals, let's make sure that we see such a modal here, for example because we won. And let's then inspect this modal. If we do that, you see that this dialogue element here in my DOM is inserted anywhere in that DOM, nested in other HTML elements in the end. And that's of course the case because this ResultModal component is output as part of the JSX code returned by the TimerChallenge component. So therefore it's output in the same place where this section here is output, for example. That's why we see that dialogue next to this section. Here's the section, here's the dialogue. Now visually, this actually all works here. There's nothing wrong with it, but technically it would make more sense if this overlay element which visually sits on top of the entire page would be output directly inside of the body or maybe inside of this div which sits here with an ID of modal which I did not add to the starting project by accident. And it would make sense to have the dialogue on such a higher level because that would map its visual appearance to its location in the HTML structure which can be better for accessibility reasons and which can also help you avoid styling problems. Here we don't have any, but a deeply nested element could be hidden by other elements above it in certain circumstances. And therefore we might wanna control and kind of output this modal component in the TimerChallenge component because that's the component where we use it, but we'd like its JSX code to go somewhere else in our page. And that's exactly the kind of problem this portal feature solves. To use this feature, you have to import something, and you have to import that something from react-dom. So not from the React library itself, but from this React DOM library which of course is managed by the same team. The difference is basically that the React library only exposes functions and features that work in all kinds of environments. For example, also, if you would build a Native app with React Native, for which I have a separate course if you wanna learn more about that. Whereas the React DOM library includes a couple of features and functions that in the end allow React to then interact with the DOM. So with a website rendered in the browser. And one feature, one function exposed by React DOM which we need here is to createPortal function. As the name suggests, this is used to create such a portal and the idea behind a portal is really just to kind of teleport this HTML code that will be rendered by this component into a different place in the DOM. To achieve this, you simply have to wrap this JSX code that should be teleported away with createPortal like this. And then createPortal takes a second argument that comes after this JSX code. So the first argument is this JSX code. The second argument for createPortal then is an HTML element to which this code should be teleported. So where this code should be rendered in the end. And that should be an element that exists in your index HTML file. And that's why in that file, I added this special div with the ID modal in the starting project, because it's this div where I wanna attach this modal to. So that it's not deeply nested inside of my content here, but instead relatively close to the root of the body. Therefore, in the result modal in this createPortal function for this second argument, you should select that element with a default browser API by using document.getElementById. And then in my case here, the ID is modal. That's the ID I added here by which I'm selecting this div. And this code here, of course, is kind of similar to what you see in the main or index JSX file. There, we have this createRoot method which also needed an element in the HTML file where the React app would be rendered to. Now we're doing the same here with a part of the app with createPortal, so it's still the same React app just output in a different place. And that's now all. If we now save this and reload and we let such a timer expire, we have the same overlay as before. But if we inspect this with the developer tools, we can now see that this dialogue is inside of that modal div here and not inside of that content div. So it's not nested in there, but instead here in this modal div. And by the way, there, you see multiple dialogues because we have multiple timer challenges, but only this first dialogue is visible at the moment. And that's the whole idea behind this portal feature. And it's therefore commonly used for use cases like this. So for modals or similar scenarios where you have some JSX code that should actually not be rendered in the place where you are using it in your app, but somewhere else in your document.

39836474.transcript: So, to make sure that we can add a new project, I'll add another component, which I'll name NewProject, because that will be a component that should display all the inputs we need, to gather the data we need for a new project. So in here, we, of course, also export this NewProject component function as we always do. And then in here, in that component function, I wanna output a div. And then, in that div, I'll add a menu element here. And these of course, are all built-in elements, as you can tell by the fact that they're starting with a lowercase character, which should output two buttons, a Cancel button and a Save button. And below that menu, I, then, wanna have a div with the different inputs for this project, so, for these project details. We could, also, display a form here and, semantically, that would be a bit better. But, when it comes to handling forms, there's a bit more I wanna explore together with you, which is why this course has a separate section on forms and handling user input so that you can learn about all the important things you should know when dealing with forms in React apps. Therefore, here, I'll just use a div. But in that div, I then wanna have a paragraph which, then, has a label and an input inside of it. And I'll then repeat this three times, basically, because I'll need an input for the title of the project, for the description of the project, and, also, for the due date of the project. Though, for the description, I actually want a textarea so that we can enter longer text. Nonetheless, we have three pretty similar JSX structures here. And, therefore, I'll actually add yet another new component, the input component, which will wrap this structure, and which can, then, be reused. Also, so that we don't have to repeat the same tailwind CSS classes all the time. Therefore, here we'll export another new function called Input, in this Input.jsx file. And here, I then want to return my paragraph which includes that label, and then either an input or a textarea. Therefore, this input component should be configurable via props, so that we can, for example, set a textarea or isTextarea prop here when using the input component. And if that should be true, we wanna render a textarea, otherwise, we wanna render an input element. So, I'll just name it textarea here, and then below this label here, I'll check if textarea is truthy. And if that's the case, I wanna output a textarea here, otherwise, I'll output an input element. That's the idea. Now, the idea also is that this input component also gets a label prop which is used for setting the label like this. And, that we collect all other props that might be passed in so that we can spread those remaining props onto either this textarea or our input here. So, that textarea and input are fully configurable from outside this component. Now, back in NewProject, we can replace these three paragraphs here with this newly added Input component which, of course, therefore, must be imported from the Input.jsx file. And we just have to set different labels here. And then of course, also make sure that we extract the different input values correctly. And the label here for the first input is Title. For the second input here, it's Description, and for the third input, it's Due Date. Now, for the second input, I, also, wanna set textarea like this. We could also set it to true, but that's redundant. If you just add it like this, it will also be set to true. And, therefore here a textarea should be rendered. So with that, we have a NewProject component here and even though it's not really styled yet, I now want to use this component in my app component to display it next to the sidebar. Soon it will only be displayed, if this button was pressed, but for the moment, I wanna always show it. So here, we'll import the NewProject component from this file and then output it here, and to make sure that it's shown next to the sidebar and not below it. I'll now, also, add the flex class to this main element, to enable flexbox on that element and add a gap of eight, so that between these elements, we have a gap of 2rem here, as you can see. 39836522.title: Managing State to Switch Between Components
39836522.section: Practice Project: Project Management App (with Components, State, Refs & More)
39836522.transcript: So we wanna make sure that we either show this or this NewProject component depending on whether this add project button or this button here was pressed. And for that, we'll, of course, need state because state then allows us to render content conditionally. And we'll need to manage this state here in the App component because that's where we make this decision about which component to display. Hence, in here, we should use the useState hook, which, of course, must be imported from React. So this import must be added. And then here we have this state that controls which component is displayed. Now, there are definitely many valid ways of managing the state here, but I'll go for an approach where I'll manage an object as a state which has a selectedProject property, which I'll initially set to undefined, not to null, but to undefined. And I'll soon show you why I'm doing that. And I'll add a projects array here where we'll later add the projects that are created by the user. Now, I'll name that state projectsState because that sounds like a good name for this object and setProjectsState to update it. Now, my idea behind adding this selectedProject property and setting it to undefined initially is that this selectedProject property will be used to either store the ID of the project that was selected when we later have multiple projects that can be selected here or null if we want to add a new project or undefined if we're not adding a new project and we also did not select any project. Now that's, of course, just one way of managing this. You could also add another state, which you maybe name currentAction, and that could be something like adding or selectedProject or nothing selected, which you could then also use to control what's being displayed. Here I just wanna work with as little state as possible, which is why I'm reusing this one property and I'll rename it to selectedProjectId to be a bit clearer about its purpose. So initially, it's undefined, which simply means that we're neither adding a new project, nor have a project selected. But that should change if we click this add project button. Then it should change to null instead. So I'll add a new function here in the App component. handleStartAddProject could be a fitting name because this should be triggered once we start adding a project by clicking one of these two buttons here and in there, I wanna update my project's state. And of course, I wanna update it such that we don't lose the old state because that old state, for example, includes all our projects, which later, of course, will not be an empty array anymore. And therefore, we need that function form where we get the previous state and return the updated state, the updated state object here. And in here, I'll set my selectedProjectId to null because as I explained before, that should act as a signal for us that we're now adding a new project. Undefined means that we're doing nothing. Null means that we're adding a new project. And that's, of course, just my definition in this project. You can definitely also go for a different solution. I'll also make sure that we don't lose the old state by spreading the old state into this newly returned state object. Now we need to connect handleStartAddProject to this NoProjectSelected component so that in there, we can connect it to this button. Therefore, I'll add a prop, which I'll name onStartAddProject, for example, where I'll point at this handleStartAddProject function we just added. And I'll do the same on my sidebar because there we have this other button that should start this add project process. Now we, of course, must go to those components and accept and destructure this onStartAddProject prop there. So for example, in the ProjectSidebar component, I'm destructuring it here and we can now connect it to this button where we add the onClick prop and then point at the onStartAddProject prop so that in the end, this handleStartAddProject function pointer is forwarded to the onClick prop in this button. And keep in mind that in this button, the onClick prop is simply collected with all the other props and is spread onto the native built-in button element, so that this entire connection can be established. So with that, we should be able to start adding a new project from inside the sidebar in the NoProjectSelected component here. We of course also need to accept this OnStartAddProject prop and there connect it to this button by forwarding it to the onClick prop there. Now both components are connected and we therefore now can use the project's state here to either render this component or the NewProject component. For that, I'll add a new variable here, content, where I'll check if projectsState.selectedProjectId is equal to null, which as explained before, should mean that we wanna add a new project. So therefore, in that case, I'll set content equal to this NewProject component like this. Else if projectsState.selectedProjectId is equal to undefined, I wanna set content equal to my NoProjectSelected component. So in the end, to this component here. Therefore, I'll grab and use that and later, we'll also make sure that content could also hold a selectedProject. But for the moment, it's either of these two components and therefore, now we can simply output content down there next to the sidebar. And with that, by using state here like we're doing it, if we save that and reload, initially, we should see this fallback screen but if I click this button, we switch to this NewProject component. Or if I reload and click this button, the same happens. Now as a next step, we wanna gather these inputs and create a new project. And of course, also make these buttons here work.

39836524.title: Collecting User Input with Refs & Forwarded Refs
39836524.section: Practice Project: Project Management App (with Components, State, Refs & More)
39836524.transcript: Now that we can see this component here and enter values here, the next step is to collect these values when we click save, and to then validate them. And if invalid values were entered because at least one of these inputs was left empty, I want to show a little error dialogue. But if correct values have been entered in all inputs, I want to be able to click save and add a new project, which then should also show up here in the sidebar. And let's start with this success case. For this, in this NewProject component here, we need a way of collecting those values that were entered into the inputs. And one way of course would be to listen to the change event and update some state with every keystroke and store the entered values like this. That's one possible approach about which you learned earlier in the course. But here, since I really only wanna read those values when I click this save button here, I don't want to add and manage all those extra state. Instead I'll simply use refs, because as you learn, you can use refs to connect them to HTML elements and then interact with those elements, for example, to retrieve the value of an input. So for that, we of course must import useRef from React. And then here I'll have my title ref or just title, however you want to name it, then my description ref. And last but not least, the dueDate ref. So three refs since I have three inputs down here. Now it's important to keep in mind that this input here, of course, it's a custom component. It's not the built-in input, and therefore adding a ref prop like this might not work depending on the React version you are using. If you are using React version 19 or higher, this will work and you can simply use and extract this prop named ref which we added here in the input component like any other prop, just that it will hold a ref as a value. But if you are using an older version of React, React 18, let's say, then this won't work as you learned. Instead, you'll then have to go to that input component and there, instead of using this ref prop like a regular prop, you have to wrap this component with the forwardRef function that must be imported from React because that will then forward any incoming refs. And as you learned, wrap your component function with forwardRef, and then store it in a variable or constant, which then is exported as a default like this. But with that, we're wrapping this component function and this component function therefore now receives that extra parameter, this second parameter after the props, this ref parameter to be precise. And with that extra ref parameter, we can now go to the text area and to the input and there, add this special ref prop, which exists on all built-in elements and set it equal to this incoming ref here, like this. And we do the same on the input because of course either of the two is being rendered depending on the value of that text area prop. Now with that, we're able to set this ref prop on our custom component and it will get forwarded and can be used inside of that custom component as we're doing it here. And we can, and should now repeat that for the other inputs, here for the description input, and then here also for the dueDate input, so that we're setting all these refs here, and with those refs being set, we can add a function, handleSave could be the name, which should be connected to that save button. So here I'll split this across multiple lines to make it easier to read and add the onClick prop and point at handleSave here so that this handleSave function is executed when this button here is clicked. Now with that inside of handleSave, we can get the enteredTitle by reaching out to this title ref and there the current prop as you learned, you always have to do that when working with refs. And then since this will be referring to an input or a text area and both elements will have a value property, we can access value to get the entered value. And I'll then repeat that here for the entered description using my description ref, and also repeated of course for the enteredDueDate using this dueDate ref. Now we also want to add validation, but we'll do that later and show that modal if invalid values were entered. But for the moment, we'll just assume that valid data was entered, and we therefore now need to pass that data back to the app component because that's where I'm managing all these projects and where I want to add this new project because I will need to then pass it to the sidebar, for example, so we need that state lifted up to the app component since only the app component has access to the NewProject component where the data is generated and the state change will be initiated, and the project sidebar component where the state data will be needed thereafter. So therefore, here in the app component, we must add a new function, which I'll name handleAddProject, here in this function we start this adding process, here, we finish it. And in this function we now of course need to call setProjectsState again using that function form, since we're updating the state based on the old state so that we don't lose any old state. And we'll return a new object here where I'll start by spreading all the existing state so that the existing data is copied into this newly returned object. And we then update the project's Array. And I want to update this Array without losing any old projects. Therefore, I'll start by copying all the old projects with the spread operator into this new Array. And then as a last element, we'll add the new project that should be added. And for that here I'll add a newProject, which will be a new object, which in the end should contain a title and a description and then a dueDate property, but to keep things simple here, I'll simply expect to get some project data here as a parameter from the place where this function is invoked. And I expect project data to be an object with a title, description and dueDate property, hence, I'll just spread my project data into this new object and enrich it by also adding an id, which will, for example, later be important for selecting a project. Now this id should be generated here. And here I'll simply generate it by calling Math.random, a function built into JavaScript, which does not give us a perfect id because theoretically I could generate the same number twice, but it's unlikely, and good enough for this dummy project. So with that, I'm creating this newProject and it's now this newProject that should be added here to this project Array. Now, handleAddProject should be invoked from inside the newProject component, which is that component that has this handleSave method where we are collecting all these input values. And of course that works via props. Here we can expect to get a onAdd prop, for example. Name as always is up to you, which we can then call as a function here in handleSave, and to which I then want to pass an object as an argument, an object with a title property, which contains my enteredTitle, a description property, which contains of course, my enteredDescription and a dueDate property with my enteredDueDate, like this. So that's an object of that shape I'm expecting in the app component. And it will be this object, which I'm passing to onAdd that will be received here as an argument, as a parameter in handleAddProject, because we will now set handleAddProject as a value for this newly added onAdd prop on the newProject component. So here, I'll add this handleAddProject function, and with that, the connection should be established and this project's state should be updated. To see whether that works, I will for now add a console.log statement to my component function, so to the app component function here, where I simply log my project's state so that I can see whether that list of projects changes over time. Therefore, now, if we save that and I reload and open my log here, initially I see an empty Array, but now if I click create new project, the app component got updated, so I get another output here with an empty Array. But now if I enter Test, and, This is a test, and then a due date of today, though I'll soon make sure that this year is actually a date picker, and I click save. Now indeed, I get a state with a project Array that includes this data. So here's the data I just entered. So this connection works. By the way, in case you're wondering why I'm getting two outputs here every time the app component is executed, that happens because I have StrictMode enabled, and as you learned before in the course, StrictMode causes component functions to execute twice. But now with that, I wanna make sure that this here is actually a date picker so that we really get the right data, and hence back in the new project component, I'll set the type of this input to date, which is a built-in supported type. And on the first input for completeness sake, I'll set it to text. With that, if I save that, I got this nice date picker here, which I can now use to select the date. And if I save that, I can see that this project was also added and that there we got a proper date. So updating that state works, as a next step, the projects that are added should be shown in the sidebar. And I also wanna close this component here when I click save with valid data.

39836528.transcript: So let's now make sure we close this component. We remove it from the screen if we click save. Now, to achieve that, we of course must make sure that in the app component, where I will now also remove this console log statement, we set the selected project ID back to undefined. Because at the moment, it's set to null and it stays null, which is why this component here does not disappear because the new project component is shown for as long as the selected project ID is equal to null. 39836532.title: Validating User Input & Showing an Error Modal via useImperativeHandle
39836532.section: Practice Project: Project Management App (with Components, State, Refs & More)
39836532.transcript: [Maximilian Schwarzmüller] So let's now add validation for those inputs and make sure that we show an error modal if we click save with invalid values being entered here. For that, we of course need to go to the new project component where we have those inputs and where we are extracting those values, and the first step here is to validate those values. Now here, I'll keep it fairly simple. I'll just check if entered title once we trim it to remove excess white space at the beginning and end if that's equal to an empty string, which would mean that it's invalid because it's an empty value. So I wanna show the modal if this condition here is met. But that's not the only case where this error modal should be shown because it should be shown if the title is empty, or if the description's empty. So I do the same thing here, checking for emptiness. Or, of course if the entered date is empty, once we trim it. If that's empty, we also wanna show that modal. So in all three cases, I wanna show the error modal. Now for that, of course, we first of all need a modal and therefore I'll add a new component, which I'll name Modal.jsx. I'll not name it Error Modal because, at least in theory, that should be a reusable modal that could be used to show other kinds of information as well. Now therefore, here in this Modal.jsx file, I'll export a modal function, and in there I wanna return this built-in dialogue element. But now here I really wanna make this modal component as flexible as possible and therefore I'll extract the children prop, which is passed to every component so that I can use my modal in the end as a wrapper around any content I want, and that content will then be wrapped by that built-in dialogue component. But that's not all. In addition, I wanna use the portal feature provided by React to render this dialogue and the content that will be wrapped by that dialogue in a different place of the DOM. For that, in this starting project, in the index HTML file, we have this modal-root div here. Therefore I'll render this with that portal feature by importing createPortal from React-dom. And then as you learn, you simply have to wrap this JSX code with createPortal like this. And as a second value, you should pass that HTML element where this content should be rendered in. For that, we use a browser API, document.getElementByID to select this div, which has an idea of modal-root. So that's what I'll use here. And with that, I have a really flexible modal here, but now it should become even more flexible and reusable. I wanna make sure that we can open this dialogue by calling a function that should be exposed by my custom component that does not require the calling component, so another component that uses the modal to know that a dialogue is used internally. That should not be information that's needed by the component that uses my modal component. And you'll learn how you can build reusable, flexible components like this in the end by using two features provided by React, the forwardRef feature, and the useImperativeHandle hook. Though, of course, the forwardRef function is only required if you're using React version 18 or older. If you're working in a project that uses at least React version 19, you don't need forwardRef. You can simply accept refs as regular props in your components. ForwardRef is not needed then. But here I'll, again, show you the code that will work with React 19, but that would also work with older React versions and I will use forwardRef therefore. So we must wrap our component function with forwardRef like this. And we now must store it in a separate constant or variable, which is then exported here. Now with that, you learned that this modal component function will receive an extra parameter, which will be the ref that might be set from outside on that component. Now that's the first step. To now expose a function that can be called from outside this component function, we must use useImperativeHandle. To this hook, you first pass this ref which you are receiving, so this ref here is passed here to use imperativeHandle. And then as a second value, you must provide a function that will be called by React in the end where you return an object that then exposes any properties or functions you want to expose to our components. For example, open method. And in this open method, we now wanna reach out to that dialogue and call the show modal method that is provided by that built-in dialogue component. Therefore, here we can use the useRef hook, which of course also must be imported to get our dialogue ref here. And we connect that through the special ref prop to that dialogue element. And now in this open method here, we can call dialogue.current.showModal. And showModal is now a method provided by the built-in dialogue element. But open is now a method provided by our component to other components. Therefore, in this new project component, we can now show the error modal by using this modal component here. And for that I'll add a fragment which wraps this div. And then also this newly added modal component here, which of course must be imported from the Modal.jsx file. And now we can connect this modal to a ref. Hence, I'll add another new ref here to my new project component, my modalRef, which will be connected to my own modal component. And for that, we now simply add the ref prop to our custom component, which we can do since we're forwarding the refs in there, and I'll set this to modal, so to this ref I just added. And now this ref can be used here in this validation code to call modal.current, and the value stored in there will be our own component function, or to be precise, it will be that object here in the end with all the functions or properties that are exposed in that object, so in this case the open method. And therefore now we can call open here to open that modal. And then I'll call return so that the code thereafter, namely this onAdd call here, will not be executed if we made it into this if block so if we do have invalid input. And with that, we should be showing this modal if we got invalid input. Now it's just important to keep in mind that this modal out of the box doesn't show a lot, it's just a wrapper. Therefore, now we should actually wrap the content we do want to show in our new project component here. So here I'll add a h2 element where I'll say invalid input. And below that, a paragraph where I'll say, oops, looks like you forgot to enter a value. And then another paragraph where I'll say, please make sure you provide a valid value for every input field. And then I also want to be able to close the modal, and for that, back here in the modal component, I will actually add something below children because I will add a form here with the method set to dialogue because that can be used to close such a dialogue by adding a button in there, which will submit that form, and the browser will do the rest. And here I'll just say close. We could also make this model more reusable by also accepting a label for this button instead of hard coding this to close here, so that here on this modal component, we accept a button caption prop, for example, and we then use this button caption here as a value. And with that back in new project, we can set this button caption prop here on our modal and set this to close, for example, or to okay. And with that, we should then also have this form with this button, which closes this dialogue to which it belongs with the caption we chose here. Now if we save that all, if we go back here and try saving empty inputs, we therefore do get this modal here. But we, of course, also see that the styling is pretty off. This of course does not look good, but it works.

And give that border a color of that light gray color here, border stone 300. And then on this diff here, I'll add a class, which makes sure that tidal and button get positioned next to each other by adding flex here items center and justify between to add some spacing between the title and the button. And then here for the title, I'll add text 3XL as a value to make this text pretty big, add font bold to make it bold and give it a color of Text Stone 600 and add some margin to the bottom. For the button, I'll add a text color of text stone 600 and then a hover text color of text stone 950, so a darker color in the end. Now for the paragraphs here, for the first paragraph, I'll give this margin bottom four class and text-stone-400 for the text color. And then for the second paragraph here, I'll choose text stone 600, so a darker gray color. And also add white space pre wrap to make sure that line breaks that are added here in this description field are kept and are not removed. So that's what white space pre wrap will do. And with that, this shouldn't look too bad. Now to see it in action, we have to make sure that projects can be selected in that sidebar. And for that of course, we must make sure that this button here changes some state in the app component. And the value should be this newly added handle select project function. Inside of the project sidebar, we now have to extract this on select project prop From the incoming props here with destructuring and connect it to this button here. Now in addition here I also wanna highlight which project was selected And therefore I actually also want to get the selected project ID as a prop in addition to this onselect project prop we just added because we can now use this prop, which should contain the ID of the project that was selected to highlight a certain button. Now to do this, I'll change this code here in this map method to move away from this very short inline notation and instead add these opening and closing curly braces and return this list item here like that so that, We can conveniently add more code in this function that we pass to map. Because I now wanna derive These CSS classes that are set on this button here dynamically, depending on whether this element should be highlighted or not. For that, I'll set up my CSS classes string here, which should basically be this string. And then I wanna check If the idea of the project we are currently handling here is equal to the selected project ID we're getting here as a prop. To be precise in that case, I wanna add a background color of BG Stone 800. So that same background color I'm using when hovering over the item. And I want to change the text color to that text stone 200 color. Now to make sure that this works and does not clash with that default text-stone-400 color here, I'll actually add a block here. 39836542.title: Handling Project Deletion
39836542.section: Practice Project: Project Management App (with Components, State, Refs & More)
39836542.transcript: So to make this delete button here work, we, of course, need to do basically the same, we did a couple of times before already. We need to add a function to the app component, a function which edits this state and removes a project. And we need to pass that function to that selected project component in this case here. So let's do that. For that, I'll add a new function here in the app component a handleDeleteProject function and in there, we wanna update the state. So I'll copy one of those state update function calls here and paste it into this handleDeleteProject function. And now here for one, the selectedProjectID should indeed be set back to undefined so that we no longer select that project. But in addition, the project's array must be updated and it should be updated in an immutable way so that we don't edit the original array in memory. Now an elegant way of doing that is to use the previous state and the projects that are stored there and calling the filter method, which just like map and find, is a method built into vanilla JavaScript. And filter works such that it takes a function as an input just like map and find and this function will be executed for every item in this array. Again, just as it's the case for find and map. But this function should then return true if an element should be kept or false if it should be dropped. And the filter method will then return a brand new array that only contains the elements that were not dropped. So, for which this function yielded true. Now this function, just like find and map will receive every item here as a input. And then here we should return true if we want to keep the item and false if we want to drop it. Therefore, here I want to drop the item and I wanna return false if the idea of the project I'm looking at is not equal to the ID that was previously selected because it's this selected item that we're deleting. So, we can use the previous state and then there selectedProjectID. If the IDs here do not match, I know that I'm not looking at the item that should be deleted and therefore I'll return true to keep that item. If these IDs do match, I'm looking at the item that should be deleted and therefore thanks to this comparison here, I'm returning false and the item will be dropped. That's how this handleDeleteProject function here will work and it doesn't even need the ID of the item that should be deleted as input because we can get that from our previous state. So, therefore, now we should pass a pointer to this function to the selected project component, maybe through a prop named "onDelete" like this so that we can call this function through that prop from inside that component. So, in SelectedProject, we again should extract this newly added onDelete prop and then connect it to this delete button. And since we don't need to pass any data to this function, we can just set it as a value on the onClick prop like this so that on this delete button, we're in the end calling the function received on that onDelete prop. And with that, if I save that, and I open my dev tools here to check if I'm getting any errors, if I click delete, indeed the project is gone and I'm back to this screen. And if I quickly add two new projects just to check if the right project is being deleted, like this and I now select the first one and click delete, you see that the other one is still there. So that all seems to work and therefore, now we can add projects, we can select projects, and we can also delete projects.

39836556.title: Managing Tasks & Understanding Prop Drilling
