39836800.title: How NOT To Send HTTP Requests (And Why It's Wrong)
39836800.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836800.transcript: Now an HTTP request can be sent in a client site project in different ways. For example, with help of the built-in fetch function. This is a function that's not provided by React but instead directly by the browser. And fetch is a function that can be used to send an HTTP request to some other server. Now, unlike the name might imply you can actually not just use it to fetch data but also to send data, but we'll explore that later. In its simplest form, fetch wants the URL of the server you wanna send a request to. Now herein this project I provided to you that's http://localhost 3000 separated by a colon here. This URL will target my running backend server. And then there it's this /places path that we wanna target to get back the places data that's stored in a file on the backend. So therefore here we should append /places. Now out of the box, this will send a get request to this URL, which is indeed exactly what we need here. Now fetch then returns a promise which is a JavaScript value that will eventually resolve to another value. So it's basically a wrapper object around a value that's not there yet but that will eventually be there. In this case, it's a wrapper around an eventually received response object. So a promise is a standard JavaScript object that will yield different values depending on the state of that promise. And to access those different values you can chain methods on the result of calling fetch. You can add the then method, for example and pass a function to the then method to define a function that should be executed once this promise resolved and the response is there. And here in this function, you would then automatically receive that response object. But this function, which you're passing to then will be executed by the browser only once the response is there. So not instantly, but at some point in the future. Now in modern JavaScript, you can also use the await keyword to access this response like this and to have a more readable syntax. But this is only available if the function in which this code executes is decorated with async, which on the other hand is not allowed for component functions. That's a restriction implied by React. So we can't use async await here but we will soon use it in some other place. So here I'll go back to that other notation which does work where we got the response and where we can define which code should be executed once the response is there. Now, when using this fetch function this response object will have some other methods that can be used to access the data that's part of the response. For example, the JS method can be used to extract data that's attached in the JSON format. And JSON is simply a text-based data format that looks like this here in the places.json file this is how it looks like, basically like a JavaScript array and object you could say. But all the keys are wrapped with quotes. That's a special feature of JSON and JSON is the facto standard data format for exchanging data with backends. Now this JSON method returns another promise and therefore if I return JSON here, we can add another then method after the first then method to eventually get back our response data and work with that data. And that's how you can chain those different then calls. And here we then got the final response data, which in case of this dummy API here should be an object that has a places key where we then have the places data, so this array of available places. And therefore here we can access resData.places in this case for this dummy API to get access to this array of available places. And now we could call set available places and pass that data to it. But this code here has a huge flaw and problem. It would create an infinite loop because when calling fetch like this directly in the component function, this code of course will be executed every time the component function executes and therefore a new request would be sent whenever this component function executes. The problem is that in the second then block we then update the state, which of course causes this component function to execute again. So we would end up with a new request, a new state update, a new execution, a new request, a new state update, and so on. And therefore we can't send the request like this. But thankfully, if you went through the side effects section this problem might sound familiar to you. And if you went through that section, you are also aware of a solution. We can useEffect to wrap this code and to avoid this infinite loop.

39836928.title: Module Introduction
39836928.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836928.transcript: Up to this point in the course, we always worked with demo projects where all the data that was required for those projects was part of the React project. For example, this project where we could pick a place, there all the place dummy data and all the place images were part of the React project. And whilst you, of course, might be building projects like this, that's not always enough. It's indeed quite common that you need to build projects where the data is centrally managed on some server so that users from all over the world can operate on that data and can maybe also change that data. If you are trying to build something like Facebook or Amazon, you of course can't manage the data inside of the React project because that React project code in the end gets executed inside of the browsers of your visitors. So different users from different parts of the world each have their own copy of the React project running in their browser, but they can't interact with each other. They can't exchange data unless there is some central server, some central database in between them that can be reached from all those different locations on the world, so from all those different React apps running on different computers. And therefore, in this course section here, we'll take a look at data fetching and sending HTTP requests from inside React apps. We'll explore how your React apps can get and send data from and to such central servers so that you're not limited to building React apps that work with local dummy data, but that you instead can also build apps that need to communicate with such a central server. And therefore, in this course section here, you will learn how you can connect your React app to such a backend server, to a database that may need to store shared data created and accessed by different users, and we will explore how you can fetch data, so how you can download and use data in your React app, and how you can send data from your React app to such a server, how you can, for example, store user generated data.

39836932.title: How (Not) To Connect To A Database
39836932.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836932.transcript: Now, as I already mentioned in the previous lecture, there often are good reasons for having a central server, a central backend or database to which multiple instances of the React app connect in order to share data across users. If you are building an online store, you wanna make sure that different users from different parts of the world can access the same data and can of course also send data, can place orders, for example. And you as an owner of the shop, of course then also need access to those orders so that you can fulfill them. Therefore, you often need such a central backend or database. And therefore, an important question is, how do you connect your React app to such a database? How can you send data to such a database and get data out of such a central database? And the short answer is, you don't, at least not directly. You don't directly connect your React app to a database because if you were to try that, you would run into security issues. Because what's important to understand, as a web developer in general, is that when writing code that runs in the browser, which is exactly what you're doing with React, all your React application code runs in the browsers of your visitors. If you are doing that, your visitors can access that code, they can view that code. And if that code then, for example, contains the credentials to access a database, your database can get compromised because users can retrieve those credentials and access your database to destroy data, insert incorrect data, or do all kinds of bad things. In addition, you also have certain restrictions when writing front-end code. So code that runs in a browser. For example, you can't easily access a file system. Especially not a shared file system that lives on some central server or computer. You don't really have those capabilities in the browser. And therefore, instead of directly accessing a database or some file system that may hold shared data, you instead communicate with a backend server, which kind of acts as a middleman, you could say. So that your front-end React application, which runs on the browser, and where users can theoretically view the entire code, that application can reach out to a backend. So a separate project running on a separate server, a computer owned and operated by you, the developer. And it's then on that backend where you can interact with databases, et cetera because that backend code is inaccessible by the users of your website because only you, the owner and developer, only you have access to the code on such a backend server. And to connect the frontend and the backend, you use HTTP requests so that from inside your React application you, for example, send an HTTP request to the backend to request some data or to request a change to some data. But what's super important, and a safety feature here, is that you can only send HTTP requests that are allowed and accepted by this backend. And if a backend doesn't want a certain request, it's not possible. And therefore you can control what users can do and can't do. So to sum it up, if you have a React app that needs to talk to a database, for example, you have to understand that with React, you are writing client-side code. You are building a client-side web application that runs in the browser and where the code can be viewed by the visitors of your website. Therefore, you communicate with a backend through HTTP requests and it's that backend code that then may interact with a database because that code is not exposed to your users. Instead, you control which kind of requests should be possible by exposing so-called endpoints on your backend API. Simply, URLs that accept certain requests. You should never try to connect to a database directly from inside your React code because you would expose your database credentials. And therefore it's quite common to have separate frontend and backend projects where you also don't have to use the same programming language. When building a React app, you are of course using JavaScript but you could build your backend with any language that can be used for that like PHP, C#, or also JavaScript with NodeJS, for example. You can also build full-stack React apps where you kind of blend frontend and backend whilst still ensuring that the backend code can't be accessed with extra frameworks and libraries like NextJS or Remix. And later in this course, I do indeed have a thorough introduction to NextJS which gives you a slightly different approach for building such backend-powered web applications with React. But in this section, we'll take a look at how you can connect to a standalone backend from inside your React app.

For the local version, you need to run npm install once you downloaded and extracted the attachment, and then npm run dev. And, for example, when we pick a place, when we make a selection, that change will also be stored on a backend. And therefore, this starting project also comes with a backend folder that contains a separate backend web API, A backend project, not written in React, but instead, in this case with NodeJS. But as mentioned before, you could build such a backend with any programming language. Now, this here is a very simple dummy backend. And in the end, the entire backend logic lives in this app.js file. Here, I'm setting up a simple backend API, where I'm accepting certain kinds of requests, because remember, that's what I mentioned before, the backend controls which kind of requests are allowed and blocks other requests that are not allowed. Hence, ensuring that users can only do what you want them to do. And here in this dummy backend, in this starting project, I, for example, allow GET HTTP requests to my backend URL and then /places to fetch available places that could be picked by users. And then I have some user-places routes that can be used to get places stored by the users of this website and to change those stored places. Now, as mentioned, this is a simple backend. Instead, we'll just use this dummy backend to connect to it from inside this React application, which you'll also find in this starting project.

39836946.title: Sending HTTP Requests (GET Request) via useEffect
39836946.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836946.transcript: So let's use useEffect here to improve this code and fix this problem of this infinite loop. For that, we should import useEffect from React and then we can execute useEffect here, pass our effect function to it, and this dependencies array. And then here we move this code, this fetch code into this effect function. Now as you learned in the effects section, this effect function will always execute immediately after this component function executed but only if it's dependency is changed. Now here I got an empty array of dependencies and therefore those dependencies of course never change because what's not there can't change. And therefore indeed in this case, this component function will only execute once after this component function executed for the first time. But the effect function will never execute again thereafter and therefore we won't enter an infinite loop. And therefore with this code here, we should now indeed be fetching those places, setting those places as a state and using those places down there. So if you save that and you have both the front end the React server and this backend server with NodeJS up and running, which is important, and you go back and reload you should see some place data down there. So fetching that data works. But of course as you can tell all the images are missing here. And those images are missing here because in this Places component where we output the available places, I'm trying to output the images by simply accessing some source property on some image property of every place. Now if we take a look at the raw data that's fetched, we see that this image source property in the end just contains the name of the image file. But those image files are now actually stored in the backend project, there in this images folder. So we need to access those images from there. Now we can't send direct requests to that images folder because as I mentioned before, by default all that backend project code and file structure is inaccessible by our users. We can only send the kind of requests that are explicitly allowed by the backend code. Now here in this code I don't have some explicit images route, but I have some extra code that actually exposes all the files stored in the images folder directly on the root backend server, which simply means that on the front end in the Places.jsx file we can tweak this source here and replace this with a template literal so with backticks around it. And then construct a URL where we point at the backend with localhost:3000 and then the image file name. Like this injected into the URL. 39836950.title: Using async / await
39836950.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836950.transcript: We're now successfully fetching data here, but this data fetching code can be improved now. For one, using all those then blocks here works and is definitely readable, but many people prefer the async await syntax I briefly mentioned before, and you can use it here as well. Now, what you should not do when using useEffect is add the async keyword here and then try to use await here. As you see, I'm already getting a warning in this project that this is in the end not allowed or not supported by React. Instead, what you can do to use async await inside of such a effect function is you can create a new function. Because, of course, in JavaScript, you can create functions inside of functions which could be called fetchPlaces. And here you can add async because this is now simply a function defined by you, not a function that would be used in any special way by React. Now you can grab this code where you send the fetch request and await this, and as a result, you'll get back the response. Then as a next step, you can get the response data by awaiting response.json. And then as a last step, you can set the places as we did it before and now get rid of all that code here. And this code is, of course, now a bit more readable and structured. Now, of course, now you also must make sure that fetchPlaces gets executed though because at the moment, that's not happening, and therefore here still in the effect function. But outside of fetchPlaces, you should call fetchPlaces like this. So you define it and then you immediately call it. But by defining it and decorating it with async, you can use this await keyword. So that's a nice little and pretty common trick for using async await in an effect function. And with that, if you save that, we're, of course, still fetching the data as we did before.

39836954.title: Handling Loading States
39836954.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836954.transcript: So fetching the data works, but here's something you should try. Go to your developer tools, and then there to the network tab. And now, there enable throttling and turn on slow 3G. If you do that and you reload, you'll see that the page takes some time to load. But then once it did load, you also see that it takes some time to fetch the places, and I don't just mean the images. Instead, if I do that again, we can see that initially we see no places available until at some point of time the places data arrives. And that's of course not really a great user experience. It would be better if we would be showing some fallback here, some fallback text or some loading text whilst we are waiting for the data to arrive. Now, this places component indeed supports a fallback text prop here where we can set some text in case we have no places. But that's not really what I wanna show here, because we might have places, we just didn't fetch them yet. So I wanna show some separate loading text, let's say, where we could say fetching place data, dot, dot, dot or something like this. And at the moment, this places component does not support such a prop, but it would be nice if it would and if it would then also have a prop like isLoading, where we could pass true as a value as long as we're waiting for the places to arrive and falls once the data is there. So inside of places JSX, we should accept these two props isLoading and loadingText, and then use these props to show some special output. For example, here right below the title of this category box here, we could check if isLoading is true, and if it is, show a paragraph with a class name of fallback text for styling reasons, where inside of that paragraph here we output our loadingText. This could be our fallback if this isLoading prop is true. And on the other hand, I only want to show the other fallback text if we got no places or the places data if isLoading is not true. So here we could check if not isLoading and places length equals zero, and if not isLoading and places length greater zero. With that, if we set isLoading to true here, in available places, we see that fallback text here. But of course, this at the moment is not a great solution, because at the moment we always show that. And instead of always showing this, this should of course be controlled dynamically and it should depend on the progress of our HTTP request here. As long as it's on its way, this should be set to true. And once the data is there, this should be set to false. And therefore when sending HTTP requests like this, it's quite common that you don't just wanna manage the data that's going to arrive with state, but that you also want to manage some loading state here. So some extra state that could be called isFetching and setIsFetching, which initially is false, for example, but which is set to true inside of this effect function here. So that here we call setIsFetching and set this to true, because we're now starting to fetch places. We therefore could also do that inside of fetchPlaces. Doesn't really matter. And then once we're done, once we've got the data here, we set this to false again, because we're not fetching the data anymore. Now, it might look like there are just three lines of code between these two state updates, but keep in mind that this step here can take a couple of seconds. So there actually may be a couple of seconds between these two lines of code being executed. And now we can use this isFetching state as a value for isLoading, so that it will either be true or false depending on whether we just started the HTTP request or we already got a response. And therefore, if you save this and you reload with that network throttling still enabled, you will of course see that again fetching the React code takes some time. But that, once it's there, we see that fallback loading text, and then the actual place data eventually. And with that, I'll disable that throttling, but this is how you can display such a loading state based on your data fetching.

39836956.title: Handling HTTP Errors
39836956.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836956.transcript: So we're making good progress, and we're fetching data and using that fetched data, and we're also managing that loading state. Now, another situation you may frequently face when sending HTTP requests are errors because when sending HTTP requests, there are many reasons for why things may fail, because a user lost their network connection, because maybe something went wrong on the back end, the server might be temporarily offline, for example, or you have some bug in your code there. Whatever the reason may be, errors can occur, and therefore, in your front-end code, you should also be prepared for the scenario that fetching the data might fail, and there are two main ways of how fetching that data may fail. For one, you might fail to send that request in the first place, for example, because the network connection crashed. The other situation where sending the request may kind of fail is that it is actually sent successfully to the back end, but something goes wrong there, and the back end sends back an error response, and you typically wanna handle both cases. Now, let's start with the error response. When using the fetch function for sending HTTP requests, you can check if the response is an error response by checking the response.ok property. If this is true, it means that you got a success response with a 200 or 300-ish status code. If this is not true, so if it is false, you got a 400 or 500-ish status code, so here, you wanna check if response is not ok, and if it is not ok, you wanna handle this situation. For example, by throwing an error. Here, we could prepare an error object by instantiating the built-in Error class and set an error message that could be 'Failed to fetch places' or something like this, and then we could throw that error, or do that all in one step, of course. Now, when you throw an error, you will crash the application, though, which might not really be what you wanna do. Therefore, you should wrap code that could potentially throw an error with try-catch, which you can do when using async/await. You can wrap try around the code that might fail. In this case, that's this code here, and then you can catch any errors that might have been thrown and define code that should be executed if an error was encountered, and this allows you to stop the application from crashing and enables you to define which code should be executed instead. Now, of course, here, I could have simply avoided throwing an error, and we would then not have needed try-catch, but the fetch function itself can actually also fail and throw an error. For example, if there is no network connection. That's why I definitely wanna use try-catch here and throw an error if I decide that I'm not happy with the response, so that either way we end up in this catch block if anything goes wrong. Well, and here in the catch block, we can now handle the error, and, of course, handling the error in a React application typically means that we wanna update the UI and show an error message to the user, and that, of course, means that we need a third piece of state, an error state here, so I'll name it error and have a state updating function that's called setError, and when fetching data, it's super-common to have these three pieces of state work together. The data state, where you store the data that you're fetching, the loading state, which you use to tell the user that you are currently fetching data, and the error state, which you use to show potential errors on the screen, and now, with that error state added, we can set the error here in our catch block, and, for example, set this error object as a value for error. This will be an error object that contains an error message, for example, this error message which we're setting here. Now, before using this error state to update the UI, there also is one other thing we should do here when using try-catch because you might already see those red squiggly lines here. We're getting those because here I'm trying to update my available places based on data that's inside of that try block. Now, if we do face an error, in this step here, for example, we will never have response data, so trying to set it here won't work. Instead, we should move this code into this try block because here, we know that when we make it to the end of this block, all the data will be there. On the other hand, setIsFetching false should stay outside of the try-catch block, typically, because I wanna end this loading state no matter if we got an error or not because even if we failed to fetch places data, we're still not fetching it anymore. We're not loading the data anymore. We just got an error, but we're not fetching it anymore, so this should be set to false either way, but with that, we can now use this error state to update the UI, and for this, in this component here, I'll simply check if error is truthy, so if we have an error, and if that's the case, I wanna return a separate error component, which I prepared in advance and which you find attached. You should add this Error.jsx file, which you'll find attached in your components folder, and this is simply a component that will output a title and a message and that will also later allow us to dismiss it. Here, in AvailablePlaces, I'm not interested in dismissing it. Instead, I just want to render this Error component, and for that, of course, you must import it from Error.jsx, and then, with it imported and rendered down there, you must set a title, for example, "An error occurred!" and then a message, and the message here is my error.message, so the message on this Error object, and now, to check whether this works, I will provoke an error by sending this request to an invalid URL. Keep in mind when sending requests to a back end, you can only access resources and endpoints that are exposed by that back-end server, so if I try to send this to placesssss with a couple of extra s at the end, that would be an invalid URL, and therefore, an invalid HTTP request I'm trying to send to the back end, and it will therefore not be fulfilled by the back end, so if I save this and reload, you see I got this An error occurred! box here. Now, you'll also see that here a message is missing, so what we might wanna do here is that when we catch this error, we set this error here to an object that will always have a message prop where we then try to access error.message, but as a fallback, if that should be undefined, we define our own custom message, like 'Could not fetch places, 'please try again later.' Something like this so that we have this as a fallback error message, and with that, if you save that, you see that fallback message here, and that's how you can handle errors. Here, I'll get rid of all those extra s's so that this does work again if we save that and reload, but it is important to know how you could handle errors and that when fetching data, you typically have these three states working together.

39836960.transcript: So now that we're successfully fetching the places here, there's one additional thing I wanna do to that data before I set those places as my available places. And you can call this method to get the current position of the user. Now get current position, just like sending an HTTP request takes some time. The position is not available instantly and therefore getCurrentPosition works such that it takes a function which will be executed eventually by the browser in the future once the position has been fetched. So here we can't use Async await because getCurrentPosition does not yield a promise but here we're using this callback pattern to define the code that should be executed once the position is there. But now here's something important to watch out for. Since we're not using Async await here, but instead this callback function we have to change the place where we call setIsFetching and set it to false. And since we can't await this here since this won't work here, we can't call setIsFething here because this code would now execute too early because JavaScript will not wait with executing this code until this callback function here is done. And in addition, we should also call it after setting the error if we have one, so that we set isFetching to false if we're done fetching and sorting our places or if we have an error. And if I reload the app now that I granted the permission, this will happen quicker. But still it can take some time until my location has been fetched.

39836968.title: Extracting Code & Improving Code Structure
39836968.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836968.transcript: Now to clean up the code in this AvailablePlaces file a little bit and make this file a bit leaner, I will now add a separate helper file which I'll name http.js next to the loc.js file which is totally optional, but where I wanna add a separate function, an async function called fetchAvailablePlaces which should be exported. And my idea is to place this data fetching code, this code here in the end where we fetch the place data in this http.js file So I will grab this code here where I sent the request and check whether it's okay and cut it from AvailablePlaces and instead move it in here into fetchAvailablePlaces. 39836972.title: Sending Data with POST Requests
39836972.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836972.transcript: Now, fetching those available places here is the first step. Of course, I now also wanna make sure that we can click on those places and add them to our selection. So of course, then thereafter, I also wanna fetch my storage selection from the backend. And therefore, this dummy backend indeed does have two routes to endpoints that allow us to store the places we picked and to get our places. As mentioned earlier, here in this dummy app, the places would theoretically be shared across all users that use this web app, so they would all be managing the same selection. But for this demo, this is absolutely fine and no problem, of course. So let's now start by making sure that when we select a place, we actually send that updated selection of places to the backend. Now for this, we should go to the App.jsx file because it's here where I'm outputting the userPlaces, and it's here where I have my userPlaces state, which is this array of places that is updated whenever I select a place. And it's therefore now here, inside of handleSelectPlace, where I don't just wanna update this state but where I also wanna send my updated array of places to the backend. To do that, we need to add some code here after we updated the state. Or also here, before we update the state, and I'll get back to which position you might wanna choose for which reasons soon. So I'll start here by sending my request after updating the state. And to do that, we can again use the fetch function, because as I mentioned earlier, this function can also be used to send requests that want to change data, not just to get data, even though the name might imply that you can only fetch data, but that's not the case. Now I will actually not write my fetching code here, though, or my sending code, but instead, I'll use that http.js file and add a new function to this file. A new exported async function that should be responsible for updating the user places. Therefore I'll name it, updateUserPlaces. Like this. Now here, I expect to get an array of places that should be sent to the backend, because this dummy backend API here, this dummy backend server, expects to get an array of places that will then simply be stored in a file there. And therefore here, I must make sure that I send such an array of places. As mentioned, this can be done with the fetch function. And here, we, again, need to specify the URL to which the request should be sent. Here, to localhost:3000, and then user-places. That's the path that should be targeted for this dummy backend I'm providing to you. But now, it's actually not enough anymore to just send the request like this. Instead, here, to change those places, a put request is expected by the backend. So, we must change the http method that's used. Because the default is the get method, but that's the wrong method here. And you do this with help of a second argument that can be passed to fetch, which is a configuration object that allows you to configure the outgoing request. Here we can, for example, set the method property and define which http request method should be sent. In this case, for example, PUT. In addition, we also must add the body property to define which data should be attached as a request body to that outgoing request. And here, that should be my places array. Though, that data must actually be in a format that is attachable, and JavaScript arrays are not an attachable format. Instead, this should be converted into this JSON format which I mentioned before first. So here, we should call JSON stringify places. This will convert this places array into JSON format, and this can now be attached. Last but not least, some extra headers should be added here. So, some extra metadata that's attached to the request. And here, specifically, you should add the Content-Type header and set this to application/json to inform the backend that the data attached to this request will be in JSON format. This is required to make sure that the data is extracted successfully on the backend. Well, with that, we can then await the result of that and get back our response. And then just as before, we can take a look at that response, parse it, and so on. So here, I'll get my response data, for example, by calling response.json and awaiting that. And I also wanna check if response is not okay. So if we got an error status code, in which case I, again, wanna throw a new error where I say, "Failed to update user data." And then here at the end, I can return my response data, and then there, actually, the message property, because if we take a look at this backend API, and there, the put user-places route, we see that the response will contain an object with a message property. Though here in this application, I will actually not use that data, but nonetheless, we can return it here. So with that, we got our updateUserPlaces function here, and back in the app JSX file, we can now use this function after updating the state inside of this handleSelectPlace function here. So here, we can then call updateUserPlaces and import this function from the http.js file, of course. And then down here, where I am calling it, I wanna pass my updated places to it. Now you could think that we can therefore simply use our userPlaces state here because I have just updated the state here in the step right before this step. But as you learned before, the state update will not immediately be available in this next line of code. Instead, it will only be available after the component function executed the next time, which is scheduled by this state update here. But this code will still execute before that happened, because it's part of that old component function, so to say, before it was updated. So, just using our old state won't work here. Instead, what we can and should do here is we should, of course, use our old state and extract the old userPlaces into a new array here. And then just as we're updating the state here, we can add the newly selectedPlace in front of those old userPlaces. And this will send this updated array of data to our backend. Now of course, this operation will take some time, and therefore we might wanna await this before we continue with the next steps. But await only works and functions that are decorated with async. Now the great thing is that this function here can easily be decorated with async, because all we're doing with that function is that we're setting it up as an event listener function in the end that's triggered whenever we selected a place. And it's no problem to use an async function for that. It will still work as before. But now, we can also use await in here. And when using async await, we can also use try catch to wrap this code, which might fail to catch potential errors, so that we can then thereafter also define some code that should be executed if this should fail. But at the moment, I won't care about this, and instead just send a request like this. Please note that at the moment, I'm also not managing any loading state here. Instead, we should be able to save this code like this, go back to the application, and open the dev tools to see potential errors. And we see a lot of logs here because, of course, due to these different states we're going through when fetching the data, the loading state, for example. And because this application also uses StrictMode, the component function where we are fetching data does get executed quite a bit. But eventually, we end up with the data. And if I now pick a place here, you see two things. For one, you see that it's added immediately here, but you also see that I got an error here, a connection refused error. And if I open my terminal where the backend is running, I can see that this actually crashed because it looks like it was not happy with the data it received. So therefore, we should restart that process by, again, running node app.js on CodeSandbox. You can simply restart it right from inside CodeSandbox if it crashes or you simply reload the page there. That should all work. And to fix this error, we should go back to the https.js file, because there, I made a tiny mistake. The data that's sent along with that PUT request should not be the places array like this. But instead, my backend expects to get an object that contains a places key, which will then actually contain this array. So, we need a wrapping object around that array. And this is actually a good example, proving that the backend only accepts certain kinds of requests with exactly the data it wants. So therefore, now back in http.js, we should wrap this with curly braces and add a places key, and pass our places as a value for that key. And since here, the key has the same name as the variable that contains the value, we can also use this shortcut here. This shorter notation. 39836976.title: Using Optimistic Updating
39836976.section: Sending Http Requests (e.g. Connecting to a Database)
39836976.transcript: So we're now also able to send non-GET requests that may carry some data. But of course if we take a closer look at how we're sending that request here there are a couple of noteworthy things here. For one, I'm for example not managing a loading state here. And I did mention before that it's quite common to have these three pieces of state when sending HTTP requests. And that is absolutely true, but here in this App component, instead of managing some loading state and showing some loading text or loading spinner to the user I'm doing something which is called optimistic updating. I'm updating my local state before I'm sending this request and before I'm waiting for the response. So when I pick a place here, as you can tell the UI updates instantly and the request is basically sent at the same time behind the scenes. Now of course, sending that request could fail though. Therefore, here when catching this error what I want to do is I want to set my userPlaces again and I wanna reset them to the state they were before. So in the end here, simply to the old userPlaces state and I'm not using this function form for updating here because here I don't wanna have that state that includes the new place. But instead I wanna simply use the old userPlaces state that will not include that newly selected place so that if something goes wrong, we roll back that change and we update the UI again. And often optimistic updating can provide a better user experience than showing a loading spinner or some loading text. Though it of course depends on your preferences and on the exact situation you are facing. It's not always possible and especially when fetching data you of course simply have to wait for it until you can use it. But when performing updates as we're doing it here optimistic updating can be a good alternative. Alternatively, we could of course also move this code here where we update our places in front of this state update here. And now this state update would wait until this request is done. But if we're doing that, we should be showing some loading text or loading spinner because otherwise it might seem to the user as if the application is stuck. So I'm not doing that here. I'm going to comment out this code, but that would be an alternative way. And again, it simply is up to you which approach you prefer. Here I'm sticking to this optimistic updating. Nonetheless, if we are facing an error here we still might want to inform the user about it because of course rolling back the change is nice but it still looks rather strange to the user if a change made by them suddenly disappears without any explanation. So we still might want to manage some extra state in this component here, an extra errorUpdatingPlaces state, for example with a setErrorUpdatingPlaces state updating function, which initially holds no value, which we do update if updating our places goes wrong. So here in this catch block, besides rolling back the change I now all the wanna call setErrorUpdatingPlaces and then set my state to an object where we have a message property, which is equal to error message if this is available or as a fallback failed to update places, for example. And now we can use this errorUpdatingPlaces state here to show an error message on the screen. And of course, as always, there are lots of different ways of showing such an error message. 39837042.title: Revisiting the "Rules of Hooks" & Why To Use Hooks
39837042.transcript: Now before we'll explore the concept of custom Hooks, I wanna repeat the rules of Hooks. Two important rules you must be aware of when working with Hooks in React. And here the first rule is that you must only use React Hooks inside of React Component functions. And the second rule is that those Hooks must not be nested in if statements nested functions for loops or anything like that. But now we'll actually change that first rule a little bit because it turns out that this first rule is actually a bit more flexible because you are actually not limited to just using React Hooks inside of Component functions. Instead, you may also use them inside of other Hooks so inside of custom Hooks. So Hooks may be used inside of Component functions or inside of other Hooks. That's an important variation of this first rule and it's this variation that enables us to build custom Hooks, as you will see throughout this section. But why would you build custom Hooks and what should these custom Hooks do? Well, the idea behind building custom Hooks is always to wrap and reuse code that goes into your Component functions. For example, here in the App.jsx file in this project I provided to you. There, I got this useEffect Hook where I in the end send an HTTP request and where I manage some loading state and potentially an error state. And if you take a look at this code and you then take a look at the code in the AvailablePlaces.jsx file, you will notice that the code is quite similar. It's not exactly equal, we are sending different requests to different URLs and here in AvailablePlaces we are then also transforming the data we're getting back, but the main idea is the same. We're sending an HTTP request, we're managing some loading state, we're managing some error state, and we're using useEffect for all that. And this would be an example for some code that we might want to outsource and make more generic so that we can reuse it, so that we don't have to write this similar code twice in two different Components, but so that we maybe have one reusable configurable function that could be used in both Components. Because, of course, you might remember that the idea behind Components was to reuse Logic and JSX structure as we're doing it here with the modal, for example, we are reusing this Component with different data. And just as this was the idea behind Components we might want to reuse other code as well, code that does not return JSX code so that can't go into its own Component, for example. Code like this, this useEffect call with the logic inside of it, we might want to reuse that, but we can't put it into a separate Component because this is just a part of a Component. It does not return JSX code or anything like that and therefore this is the kind of problem we can solve with custom React Hooks. Now of course, we could also try to outsource and reuse code like this by grabbing it and putting it into a separate function, a function which we could call fetchData and which we could define in any file we want. And then we could try to call fetchData in every Component that needs that functionality because that is how you normally outsource logic in programming and JavaScript programming is, of course, no exception. If you have some logic that needs to be used in different places, you put it into a function and that function can then be used in different places. Just as we created this fetchUserPlaces function before which does contain that reusable code for fetching this userPlaces data. But this does not work here because the code which I wanna outsource and share actually uses Hooks inside of it and it also needs to manipulate state. And these are things which can only be done inside of Components, not in some random shared function. Sure, here I'm using this function in a Component, but there is no guarantee that I'll always use fetchData just in a Component and nowhere else. And if I would use it somewhere else, all this code here would fail because Hooks can only be used in Components in the end and state updates therefore can also only be made in Component functions. And that's why this will not be a valid solution and why I'll roll back this code and go back to the code I had before. And that's exactly why we need this custom Hooks feature I'll introduce you to in this section because custom Hooks will also be functions that we can then call from different places, but they will be functions that are guaranteed to be used in valid places, so that are guaranteed to be used in Components in the end.

39837048.title: Creating a Custom Hook
39837048.transcript: So how could we outsource this into a custom hook? Well, we can create a new folder for that in a src folder that could be named hooks but this is totally optional. You don't have to create that folder and you can name it whatever you want. So I'm just doing this to have some structure in this project and in there, we could then add a file that could be named useFetch.js, though this name is also up to you and this notation here might also look a bit weird. It's also up to you. You could also name it use-fetch like this, or just fetch. But I'm going for useFetch like this since this will also be the name of the hook I'm about to create. And I'll explain why it starts with use in just a second. Now, inside of this useFetch.js file, we can now create a custom hook by simply creating a function, a standard function. But now, and that's important, it should be a function that starts with use because that's a convention. And actually also, basically a rule in React projects that functions that start with use are treated as hooks and React projects typically look for functions that start with use and enforce certain rules on such functions. For example, in App.jsx, if I would try to use the built-in useState hook nested inside of some other code, you would see that here I'm getting an error. I'm getting red squiggly lines and the information that a React hook must be called in a React function or a custom hook function. So basically what I told you before but technically, this would be valid JavaScript code because useState is just a function and theoretically, you can call functions anywhere. This is a rule that's instead enforced by this project setup, and which can only be enforced because of the name of this function because this project, like most React projects is configured such that functions that start with use are treated as hooks and that those rules of hooks are enforced on such functions so that we, for example, get an error if we try to use them in the wrong place. That's why your custom hook function also must start with use so that those rules are also enforced on this function. And these rules are important because without them, if using hooks in the wrong place, your applications could break or behave incorrectly. But of course, use is just a part of the name. Here I wanna name my hook useFetch, though the name is, of course, up to you. You just should make sure that you don't clash with built-in hook names like useState or useEffect. But now this is recognized as a hook function and you can now use other hooks inside of this function. This allows you to, for example, outsource this useEffect code. We can copy that and paste that into the useFetch function. And now import useEffect from react and React won't complain. It will complain about some other things here which we have to fix but using useEffect here is fine. If I would name this function just fetch without use, you would see I'm getting some red squiggly lines here and I'm getting an error that this must only be used in a React component function or a custom hook function. But with use in front of that name, everything is fine. But why would we put this code into our custom hook? And how should this custom hook work? What's the idea behind it? Well, the idea is that in the end later, we'll be able to get rid of useEffect here in App.jsx and that instead, we can simply use our custom hook here and that will then internally perform all the work we stored in there. But our component function therefore got leaner. And we could use that same hook in other components that would then perform the same work. And that's, of course, in general in programming, the idea behind creating functions that you can create them once and that you can then use them in different places of your application to trigger them. And here we wanna do the same with the special condition that we want to use built-in hooks like useEffect in there and that's why we have to build a custom hook, which we simply do by adding use in front of it. But of course, this hook is not done. What else do we have to do in there to make this hook functional?

39837058.title: Custom Hook: Managing State & Returning State Values
39837058.transcript: Well, to make this useFetch hook functional, we also need to manage some state in there, because every component that later uses this custom hook should of course also get that state that's managed by that hook. And it should not have to register that state on its own, because that would of course reduce the reusability of the custom hook. So this custom hook should not just send some requests, instead, it should also manage all the related states. And therefore here, to make this hook useful and reusable, we should also import useState here so that we do, for example, manage the isLoading or isFetching state as we also named it before like this. In addition, of course, we might face an error. So that's another piece of state we should manage here, error and setError. And eventually, since we're sending an HTTP request here, we of course will end up with some data. Though since the idea behind this hook is to use it in different places with different pieces of data, I don't wanna name my data state something like userPlaces, but instead I wanna give it a more generic name like data or fetchedData and setFetchedData. And then here I have my state which initially also is undefined. And now here where I store the fetchedData, I would do that with help of setFetchedData. But of course this code here is still not very generic. We are still explicitly fetching the userPlaces, and it would be great if we would be a bit more flexible here. Well, keep in mind that, of course, a custom hook is just a function, and like all functions, it can receive parameters. So here we could expect to get a fetch function as a parameter. And of course this parameter name is always up to you. And then here, we could simply execute this fetch function, and now this hook would be usable with all kinds of fetch functions. And here we then get back our data, which we then set as the fetched data. And now this is fairly generic except for this error message. So we could either accept another parameter here which allows us to set this error message or we choose a more generic error message here like failed to fetch data. Another thing that should be changed Is the name of this function here. Instead of naming it fetchPlaces, I'll just name it fetchData so that we have this function name here. Now here we can also see that we now got some yellow squiggly lines down there because I'm now using this fetch function in here, as I also learn if I hover over this. And I therefore have to add fetch function as a dependency, because that's some external data which is not defined inside of useEffect and which theoretically could change. And if it would change, this effect should be executed again so that we're fetching data correctly based on the latest available fetch function. Therefore, fetchFn should be added as a dependency here. It should not be executed, instead just a function like this as a value should be added as a dependency. And with that, we got a fairly reusable useFetch hook, but of course at this point, we're not doing anything with all those states. We're just setting them, but then they're basically getting lost inside of this hook here. So what could we do here to make those values available to the component that will later use this custom hook? Well, what can you do in a normal JavaScript function? You can return values so that in the place where you use this hook, you will get these values returned. And therefore here, at the end of this hook, outside of the effect function, we can return these values by grouping them into an array or into an object. That's of course up to you. And keep in mind that useState, for example, also the returns a value, it returns an array with two elements, the state snapshot and a state updating function. And we can do the same with our custom hook. Here, we can return an array or an object. And here I'll go for an object. We could also return just a number, but here, since I wanna return free pieces of data, we need to group them together and we can do that with arrays and objects. So here I'll go for an object and I'll simply add those different states as key value pairs so that we got isFetching, data, and error. And data is named fetchedData here. So I'm returning these three state values grouped together into an object in my custom hook. And now last but not least, to make this hook usable in Components, we must export this hook function. And now we can go to the app JSX file, for example, and get rid of the state we managed there. And I already deleted the useEffect hook there. And instead we import useFetch from hooks and then useFetch.js. And now we just have to pass the fetch function to useFetch, which in my case here is fetchUserPlaces. So a pointer at this function which contains the actual code for sending the HTTP request. And then, as a result of calling useFetch, we will get those state snapshots that we are returning there grouped into an object. So therefore here we can use object destructuring to get isFetching, error, and of course the fetchedData. And the great thing is that if you use a custom hook in a component, any state that is managed by that custom hook will also belong to the component in which you are using your custom hook. So therefore, if the state gets updated in the custom hook, for example, here when we setIsFetching to true, when that happens, the component where your custom hook gets used also gets executed again. So that still works as if you would be managing the state directly inside of the component. That still works in exactly the same way even when using a custom hook as an extra layer in between so to say. So the behavior of this component won't change, but now we can use the state returned by useFetch in this component. Now here, this of course also means that we face some problems here because we also previously worked with that fetched data to update it and to, for example, change it when a place gets selected. Now, we'll take care of that later. And for the moment, I'll just comment out these different functions here, and I'll also comment out those props where I pointed at those functions, also here. And I'll just replace userPlaces with fetchedPlaces now or fetchedData was the name. So I'll use that down here. Alternatively, if you wanna stick to user places, you can also assign an alias up here by adding a colon after the destructured property name and then assigning any alias of your choice, like userPlaces. And with that, you'll notice that if you save this, we get an error here. And we get an error because initially it looks like we're expecting an array in someplace as we're trying to access the length property, but instead we get undefined. And that's happening because previously, I initialized my userPlaces here with an empty array. Now in the more generic useFetch function, this instead is undefined initially, because of course we could be using this hook in all kind of situations where we don't necessarily always expect to get an array back from the server. Therefore, we could simply accept another parameter here, let's say an initial value, which we could then set as an initial value to make this a bit more configurable. And with that back and app JSX, we could now pass our wanted initial value for the userPlaces to useFetch as a second argument, and in this case here it would be an empty array. So that at the point of time where we don't have any data yet, we are falling back to this empty array. With that, if you save that and reload, you'll see that fetching those places works again, but now this works with help of our own custom hook. And that's the idea behind using such a custom hook. The logic for managing those different built-in hooks like useState and useEffect, and for orchestrating this HTTP request, all that logic lifts in a custom hook. And in our Component, we can simply use that custom hook and we get all that behavior, all that functionality in this component without adding all that code here.

39837062.title: Exposing Nested Functions From The Custom Hook
39837062.transcript: So we're using the custom hook to successfully fetch our places here. But one thing's missing or one thing had to be removed, and that was the editing capability of this component. Of course, in this component, we have the handle select place function and the handle remove place function. And these two functions should be able to edit the state that is created with help of useFetch. But at the moment, those components don't really work as intended because I'm trying to update the state in there. But of course I can't update the state from in here because the state is now managed in useFetch, at least this userPlaces state, which I wanna update here. So what could be the solution here? Well, the solution is actually pretty straightforward. In our custom hook, we are not limited to just exposing state values. We can of course also expose state updating functions or custom functions that wrap those state updating functions. So here to keep things simple, we could simply expose setFetchedData, so that here where we return values we also return a pointer at setFetchedData. And in App JSX, we can then of course also extract setFetchedData here and also assign an alias to that function if you want to. For example, setUserPlaces, which is the name I also used before. And with that, we'll be able to edit this state that's managed by our custom hook. If you wanna restrict the editing capabilities, you could wrap this state updating function in a custom function that maybe adds some extra validation before the update is performed. But here we don't need that. But you might wonder what happens if you use this useFetch hook in other components as well. If we use it in another component and we then update the state from inside this app component, will that affect those other components might be a valid question. And the answer is no, because just as with components, whenever you use them, whenever you use components and it's the same for custom hooks, whenever you use them, a brand new independent copy is created. So when I use useFetch here in the app component, the state that's created and managed in useFetch will be connected to the app component. And if I then also use useFetch in another component, that same state will be recreated as independent state snapshots for that other component. So just as you can use useState in as many components as you want and you get totally independent state snapshots. Just as you can do that, can you use custom hooks that might be using state? Every component that uses this hook gets its own independent state snapshots. So changing the state in one component will not affect the state of other components even if they use the same custom hook. And with that, we can of course also re add those props where we trigger handleRemovePlace and handleSelectPlace. And I got a warning here that setUserPlaces should now be added as a dependency here in this useCallback call. So to this dependencies array of this useCallback call. Now, we did not have to do that before using a custom hook because those state updating functions normally don't need to be added to dependency arrays of useCallback or useEffect because React guarantees for all those state updating functions that they will never change. But here in this case now the project simply doesn't understand that setUserPlaces in the end refers to a state updating function because all it sees here is that it's some property we're pulling out of some object. Therefore, for completeness sake, and to get rid of that warning, we can simply add setUserPlaces here. It technically won't make a difference though because it does refer to a state updating function and those are guaranteed by React to never change. But to get rid of the warning, I'll add it here. And with that, we're now using this custom hook here and we should now also be able to update those places. I can therefore now remove the useEffect import here from App JSX and save all files. And with that, if we reload and I add a new place, that should work. Also if I reload, it's still there and we got no error here in the console. And if I delete a place like this one here, that also works and it was deleted without any errors and it stays deleted if I reload. So that all works and that all works now powered by our custom hook.

39837070.title: Using A Custom Hook in Multiple Components
39837070.transcript: Now as explained before, the idea behind creating custom hooks like this is of course to get leaner components because you can outsource some state and logic into those custom hooks. But another reason for creating and using custom hooks is of course also that you can share them across components if multiple components have fairly similar logic that can be outsourced. For example, in this application here in the available places component we also have this code where we fetch some data, manage a loading and an error state and then store the data once it's there. And we do all that with useEffect and useState. And therefore here we in the end have logic that is fairly similar to what we're doing in useFetch. There's just one important difference because here I'm then also taking that data that I'm getting and I am combining it with the position of the user to sort these places, which I am fetching by distance. But let's worry about that later because since the rest of the logic is fairly similar, we can of course also import useFetch here in available places and import that from the hooks folder and there from the useFetch JS file. And we can call useFetch here in this component. Now, useFetch, as you saw in the previous lectures needs a fetch function and an initial value as an input then. 39837948.title: What Are Forms & What's Tricky About Them?
39837948.transcript: So what's so difficult about forms? Why do we need this special course section? Well, let's first of all define what exactly a form is. That's what a form is. But what's now so difficult about forms? Well, in the end, forms are about two main things you wanna do with them. You wanna handle their submission and extract the values entered by the user, and you typically also want to validate the data that's provided by the user and show some validation errors to the user if incorrect data has been provided. Now, thankfully, handling the form submission is relatively easy and so is the extraction of entered data. If you set the type to button on a button in a form, that button will not submit the form, because the default type is the submit type, which you could also set, but which you don't have to set because, well, as mentioned, it is the default. 39837968.title: Getting Values via FormData & Native Browser APIs
39837968.transcript: So let's explore another solution for extracting user input values which can be a bit more convenient to work with when dealing with more complex forms. It helps you by allowing you to create a special kind of object based on a special kind of constructor function that's built into the browser, the FormData constructor function. This is built into the browser, not provided by me or React, and FormData is an object that makes it easy to get hold of the different values entered into a form. For that you just need to pass that form as an input to FormData, and you can do that by simply passing event.target to FormData because the target of this submit event is that form, because it is that form that was submitted. So therefore as a result you'll get back a FormData object that will in the end give you access to the data that was added to all the inputs in that form. Now for this to work all those inputs for which you wanna extract values must have that name prop set on them though. So all your input fields, even if they're not input fields, but select fields for example, must have that name attribute, that name prop. You'll find that on all the input fields and you need that for FormData to work and to give you access to the entered values because now you can use this FormData object to call one of the built-in methods, for example the get method to extract the value for a specific name. So for example, here I could get the value for the input field that has the name email which would be this input field here. So calling get on this form data object would give me the entered email address and I could then extract and use it. Now we could repeat that for all the input fields, so for example also for the password to get the entered password, but we therefore of course would end up with quite a lot of code here. Therefore, a common pattern or trick that's often used to quickly get hold of all the entered values and group them together into an object is to use the built-in Object class, which is also provided by the browser, and call the fromEntries static method on that class and pass the FormData object to it. To be precise, pass the result of calling the entries method on that FormData object to object fromEntries. This might look a bit weird, but in the end, calling entries on the FormData object will give us kind of an array of all the input fields and their values and calling Object from entries on that array then will simply give us an object where we have key value pairs for all our input fields. So if I console log data here and I submit this form by clicking Sign up, you'll see I get an object where I have different key value pairs one for every input I have in this form. So for my first name, last name, email, password and so on. Though once taking a closer look here you might notice that one input field is missing here in this object, and that would be this How did you find us input, which is actually a group of inputs. This how did you find us area in the end is a group of check boxes, which all have the same name because in the end here I want to get an array of all the checked values, so all the values of the boxes that have been checked by the user, and such multi value input fields are lost when using entries and fromEntries. But you can thankfully easily get them back by manually extracting and storing them. So here I can get my acquisitionChannel by using FormData getAll, which is the method you want to use if you want to get multiple values from one input field, which you, for example need if you have multiple check boxes that all have the same name and you want to get the values of all check boxes then you can use getAll with the name that's set up on the checkbox elements, so in this case acquisition, and you pass that to getAll and that's it. Now we also have the acquisition channel and we could merge it into this data object by adding a new property to it which could be called acquisition, and which is equal to acquisitionChannel, which will be an array of all the values selected by this acquisition input.
