And indeed, if I enter some value into my form here into any input fields of my choice and I then click on this Reset button. The form indeed is reset and those inputs are cleared. And that's happening because in that form on that reset button, the type attribute is set to reset. And that's one of the built-in types we can use. We got button, submit and reset, submit is the default. And of course, well submits the form, button is a value we explored before. It allows this button to act as a button that does not submit the form, and that also doesn't do anything else. And the last value reset, therefore is the value you wanna choose. If you have a button in a form that should reset that form. So that's one way of resetting it. Another way is to programmatically reset it. Now for that, if you are managing the input with state. You can of course simply reset those state values. For example, here in that state login component we worked on before, we could set the entered values state to that same object we used as an initial state value. Where email and password both have empty strings as values. And since those values are then set as values on those inputs, those state values would be reflected there. So that's how you can reset input values when managing those values via state. Now, when getting the entered values with refs you can reset the inputs by manually setting the value property of the connected input elements to empty strings. But this is a solution you should use with care because typically you should leave it up to React to update the DOM. And you should not imperatively perform updates or changes as we're doing it here. It might be just a small update but it is an update to the DOM and therefore not really recommended. Instead here when working with refs it's typically better to reset the form as you also can do it when using that foreign data approach for extracting the values. Because in that handleSubmit function we're getting this event object. And as I already mentioned before the target of that event is the underlying form element. And it turns out that this form element has a reset method which you can call. Which is essentially the same thing this button does if you set the type to reset as I'm doing it here. So calling reset on event target is another way of clearing that form. And yes, if we're honest this is also some imperative code again, but at least it's less code to write then resetting all those ref values manually by setting them back to empty strings step by step. That's why you should prefer this event target reset code over manually resetting all the ref values. And with that, we now, for example can make sure that if we submit the form here we also reset that when clicking Sign up. Thanks to calling reset here. Now here, I actually don't wanna reset the form, but of course it is important to know how you could do it and now you know and therefore now we're ready to dive into validation.

39837980.title: Validating Input on Every Keystroke via State
39837980.transcript: [Maximilian So now that we have a good idea of how we can extract data when a form is submitted, let's explore form validation and input validation next. And when it comes to input validation, as mentioned before in this section, you get a couple of different options. For example, you can validate on every keystroke so whenever the user types into an input field. To show you how this could work I'll go back to the StateLogin form here. So to the Login form that uses state, because if we wanna react to every keystroke and validate the value on every keystroke, we of course need to listen to all changes that are made to an input, and that is what we're doing here when using state and the handleInputChange function. When using refs or when using the built-in FormData approach for extracting data, we of course only have the data once the form is submitted and not on every keystroke. So if you want to have keystroke validation we need that stateful approach here. Therefore, I'll go to App.jsx and get rid of the Signup form import and instead import the Login form again from the components folder and there from the StateLogin.jsx file, because I want to have that stateful version of that login form. And then of course the login form should also be output here in the JSX code. Now, back in StateLogin.jsx, we can now validate the email input on every keystroke, for example, by adding a computed value to this component. So that whenever the component function is executed again, for example, because the email input changed, we calculate a new value. That value could be called emailIsInvalid, for example, and we might wanna store true or false in here, which we can then use down here in our form to update the look of this form or to output an error message. And we can set this emailIsInvalid constant here based on this email state value which we're managing. So here we could say that emailIsInvalid, should be true if the entered email does not include an at symbol. For this we can use the includes method, which is supported on every string, and check if email does not include an at symbol by adding an exclamation mark in front of this expression. So with that, we're checking if the entered email does not include the at symbol, in that case, emailIsInvalid, would be true. Now that we got this information, we can update the UI and therefore below the email input field I'll add a new div, which I'll give a class name of control-error, which is a CSS class I prepared in advance in that index.css file which is already part of the starting project. And here I wanna output an error message if the email is invalid, so if this is true. Therefore down here we can output emailIsInvalid and then output a paragraph if that is true where I output some error message of my choice, like for example Please enter a valid email address, like this. If you now save that, you'll see that if you reload this page, we got this error message here and this error message goes away as soon as I add an at symbol, so as soon as I have a valid email address again. But if I then invalidate this by removing the at symbol, the error message appears again. So that's how we can validate on every keystroke. But of course this approach isn't perfect at the moment because this error message is now showing up right from the start, and that might not be the behavior you want. Because you might want to at least give the user a chance of entering a correct value before you start showing error messages like this one. Therefore, we might wanna make sure that emailIsInvalid is only set to true if the user did start entering a value. And to make sure that this is the case, we could check if enteredValues.email is not equal to an empty string and it includes an at symbol. So if it is equal to an empty string, this will be false. It will not be marked as invalid because it's still that's starting value that we're seeing and therefore we don't wanna set this to true yet. Therefore, with this change made, if we reload the error message is gone, but it appears as soon as I start typing until I do enter a valid value. But this solution also has two problems, for example, one problem is that if I did enter a valid email address, but I then erase it I'm not showing an error message here because we are checking whether the email field is an empty string in which case we will never set email is invalid to true. Another problem which we'll face here is of course that yes, we don't see an error message initially but we do see one as soon as the user starts typing. And of course, at this point of time, the user also didn't really have a chance of entering a valid value. So as mentioned before on the slide we're showing the error message too early and that's why we'll next explore validating on lost focus and how that could be a valid approach.

39837988.title: Validating Input Upon Lost Focus (Blur)
39837988.transcript: So how can we validate inputs on lost focus? Well, for that, we need to react to an input field losing focus. For example, the email input field again. And you can react to this input losing focus by adding another event listening Prop to it, the onBlur Prop because the blur event is a built-in default browser event that will be fired whenever this input loses focus. Therefore, we can add a new event handling function here, handleEmailBlur or of course, set up a more generic handleInputBlur event handling function. And then here, we could again accept an identifier, like this, and manage a new piece of state. Because now, we don't just wanna keep track of the value that has been entered into an input, but we also wanna keep track of whether the user touched this value or not. Because when the blur event is fired and this input field therefore loses focus, we of course, also know that it seemed to have focus in the first place. So that the user did touch it, that the user worked with it. Hence, we need that second piece of state or we could of course, also merge it into this existing state by managing some nested object here for the email where we have the value and the didBlur or didEdit property which initially is false and then might be set to true. But here, I'll simply go for an extra piece of state with useState. And then again, I'll manage an object here with an email property which initially is false. And the password property, which initially is false because this here will be my didEdit state and set didEdit where I keep track of whether these inputs have been edited. Which in my case here, simply means that they lost focus. So the user interacted with them and then they lost focus. Therefore, now with that state added here, here in handleInputBlur, I wanna update this didEdit state by again, using the function form where I return a new object, where I spread in my old state so that we don't lose any data and information. And then just as with the handleInputChange function will dynamically target a property with this syntax. And then here, simply set it to true because this will be fired when an input is blurred. So therefore, then I wanna set this state to true. Keep in mind that initially, it is false. And the offer now it's handleInputBlur, which I wanna trigger here. And again, we need to wrap this with a function so that we can control how it will be called so that we can pass this identifier to handleInputBlur. In this case, email as an identifier. And now, we can use this new didEdit state to perform validation. And we can check whether emailIsInvalid by not checking for whether it still has the initial value but by instead keeping this check and combining this check with didEdit.email. If the user did edit the email address and the email then does not include an @ symbol, I want to set emailIsInvalid as true. And with that, we make sure that we give the user a chance of editing before we run this check. And therefore, before we show an error message. So with that, if you save this all and you go back and reload, you see there's no error message here initially. And if I start typing, there still is no error message. But if I then type out of there and I lose focus, there is an error message. And I also get that if I just click in there, don't enter anything, and click out of here. As soon as I start typing, the error goes away once I have a valid value. And that's now arguably a good user experience, therefore. Now on the slide I mentioned that when validating on lost focus, errors may be shown too long though. And what do I mean by that? Well, here, you could argue that the error is shown a little bit too long because if I have that error message and I start typing, it only disappears once we did enter a valid value. Which you could argue is the experience you might wanna provide. But you could also argue that this error message should disappear as soon as the user starts typing again. Because once the user starts typing again, they have another chance of entering a valid value and therefore, we might wanna remove this error message until they're done typing and the input loses focus again, at which point of time, we might wanna run this validation again. And therefore, the solution we have at the moment could be improved by also updating didEdit on every keystroke. And by resetting this to false whenever the user starts typing again. And we can do this by going to handleInputChange and also updating setDidEdit here. And again here, we would use the function form for updating this and copy in the old state and then target and input dynamically via its identifier to reset it to false here. So that whenever the user starts typing again, we reset this didEdit state for the input in which the user types again. And with that, you'll see there's no error initially but we do get one if I lose focus and have an invalid value in there. But if I then start typing again, the error is removed again and it reappears if I, again, lose focus without a valid value. And therefore, this might then be the best of both worlds. And indeed, combining validation on every keystroke with validation on lost focus and resetting that focus state whenever the user starts typing again can be a good pattern for validating user input. But it's not the only way of validating input. You can also validate upon form submission.

39837990.title: Validating Input Upon Form Submission
39837990.transcript: So here in this example, we added some validation to validate on keystroke combined with focus-based validation. So when we lose focus, and we are providing a decent user experience here I'd argue. Still, this is not the only way of adding validation. If we switch back from the StateLogin version of the login form to the ref-based login form, we can also explore another way of validating user input. For that, I'll tweak this import in the App component to import the login form from the Login.jsx file. And in that file, I'm using refs. Now, since I'm using refs, I can't really validate on every keystroke. I would have to set up separate event listeners for that. And if we would do that, we could also just start managing the input values via state. So if I wanna stick to refs, I can basically only validate the input when the user submits the form. And indeed, that is a valid and also quite popular strategy as well. You don't have to validate whenever the user types. Instead here in the Login component function, in the handleSubmit function here, we could simply determine whether the email is invalid, for example, by adding the emailIsInvalid constant and then here when the form is submitted, performing our check, which here again is that we check if we don't have an @ symbol in that email with the includes method like this and the exclamation mark in the beginning. Now we, of course, want to reflect this error here in our form, and we can do this with some extra state because we need to update the UI if we find out that an invalid form has been submitted. And we do that with state as you learned. So we must import the useState hook. And then with that, we can manage some state here. And that could be the formIsInvalid state if we wanna show some general error message that affects the entire form. Or of course, the emailIsInvalid state and setEmailIsInvalid. And initially, that's false, but we might wanna update this once we determined that the emailIsInvalid. Now here I have a name clash because I'm using emailIsInvalid twice and therefore, to work around that, I'll simply rename this here to emailIsValid and remove this exclamation mark to perform the opposite check. And then here I can check if not emailIsValid, which of course means that it's invalid. And in that case, I can call setEmailIsInvalid and update this state by setting this to true. We also might want to return here in this if statement because we might want to make sure that no other code thereafter gets executed. For example, if we would be sending an HTTP request with the entered data, we might wanna prevent that from happening if invalid data has been entered. And by returning here, we make sure that this code won't execute. So with that, I'm now validating the input when the form is submitted and we can now use this state, which is only updated in that case if invalid data has been submitted. We can use this state to again show an error message here. And for that, I'll again add my div here with a class name of control-error and then check if emailIsInvalid. And if that's the case, I wanna output my error message. Please enter a valid email address like this. With that, if we go back, I don't get any error no matter what I'm doing with that email field here. But if I click log in, I now get my error message. And this error message now also doesn't disappear until I submit the form again and I do so with a valid email address. And even then here at the moment, it doesn't disappear, though you can tell by this console log here that we made it past this if check and we did have a valid email address. If I wanted to update the UI in that case, we could, of course, do that by simply setting emailIsInvalid to false here after we make it past this check and we know that we have a valid email address. By doing this, we make sure that if we submit a valid form, the error message here goes away. And that's how we can check the validity of the provided values when the form is submitted. And in the end, it's up to you to decide which approach you wanna use and which user experience you wanna provide to your users. Validating on submission definitely takes a bit less code than validating on every keystroke and combining that with the focus state of the input element. But you, of course, are able to give the user a more direct feedback if you are using this keystroke focus-based approach. Nonetheless, validating upon submission is, of course, also totally fine and can be the experience you wanna provide to your users. And it's also worth noting that you often might want to add submission-based validation even if you are already validating on keystroke and blur. Because otherwise, if we switch back to this StateLogin form for a second where I am performing my validation on keystroke and upon blurring the inputs, if you only rely on that validation, you got a nice user experience as the user is interacting with those inputs. But you have a problem if the user simply clicks the submit button because in that case, as you see here in the console, the invalid data is handled here inside of handleSubmit. So if the user simply ignores your hints and submits the invalid form, you might have a problem. That's why it's always a good idea to also add validation here in this handleSubmit function. I'm not going to do it here to save you some time because we did already do it here in the Login.jsx file. And I'm also going to switch back to that file. But it is definitely something you should keep in mind. Adding submission-based validation is always a good idea even if you are already validating the user input on every keystroke. And in the next lecture, I'll show you an even easier way of validating inputs upon form submission.

39837996.title: Validating Input via Built-in Validation Props
39837996.transcript: So you can validate inputs upon form submission by adding your own code like this but you can make your life even easier. But we of course would have to write a lot of code and we could also handle the form upon submission as we're doing it here in this login form in the last lecture. But we can also make our life even easier. For that, I'll first start by importing this signup component from Components signup JSX here. And then I'll use it here in the JSX code. And now to validate all these inputs, when the form is submitted, we can take an even easier route and not write any custom code at all for the moment, and instead use some built-in attributes, some built-in Props provided by the browser, not by React but by the browser, which you can add to those input HTML elements. For example, the required Prop. And the required Prop is just one of multiple built-in Props and attributes, which you can set on input elements to let the browser validate the user's input. You can learn more about this if you, for example, search for HTML form validation because you'll then find an article on MDN where you can learn more about form validation and where you can also learn about these built-in attributes and Props, which you can add to your form control elements to control which kind of validation will be performed by the browser. For example, if you add required, the browser will not allow this input field to be empty if it's submitted. In addition, it will then also take the type of the input field into account. And for example here, when the type is email, not allow values that are not email addresses. So if I save this with a required add to this email and I reload this form, if I try to sign up like this I get this error message that this must be filled out. If I do enter some value here, which is now not a valid email address I also get an error also provided by the browser, not by me, not by React where it also tells me that this is an invalid email address that I should add and @ here. And if I do that, now I can submit this. And that's of course, therefore an even easier way of adding validation because we don't have to write any code on our own, we just have to add these attributes. And I can also add that here to the password. And on the password field we could now of course also add the minLength Prop to set this minLength validation attribute which just like required is a built-in one. And this allows us to define a minimum expected length of the entered value, for example to make sure that the entered password must be at least six characters long. And of course we can add that to all these inputs here. And also add required here, and also add that to a select. You are not restricted to just input elements. Instead, all form control elements, including selects support these special Props and attributes. So we can do this here as well. I don't wanna do it on these inputs because these check boxes are indeed optional. So I won't add required here, but I will add it on my terms of use checkbox here like this. And with that all done, now if I submit this I get an error message for every input that's invalid. So I also get it for the password now and if I enter a too short password I also get an error regarding this, as you can tell. 39837998.title: Mixing Custom & Built-in Validation Logic
39837998.transcript: So using the browser and the built-in validation attributes can be a great way of adding validation, and indeed would be my recommendation for many use cases also, because it doesn't require any extra React or JavaScript logic from your side. For example, here in the signup form I might wanna make sure that the value entered in this confirm-password field matches the value entered into this input field. But of course, just because we're using these built-in attributes here does not mean that we can't add our own logic and combine that with that built-in logic here. For example, here, we could add some extra state to this component to output an error if the passwords are not equal and have a setPasswordsAreNotEqual state updating function. And initially this could be false. And then here, of course, when the form is submitted before console logging the data, we could check if data.password, since I'm using password as a name here on this input, is not equal to data. And then here it's the confirm-password prop. That's why I'm using the square bracket notation here. And I'm accessing the property confirmed-password here because that's the name I assigned here to this confirm password input. So now with that, I'm checking for the quality of these two entered values and if they're not equal I can return to not execute any further code and make sure that this code doesn't get executed, therefore. And I can use this newly added state and set PasswordsAreNotEqual to true. So with that, I am blending my own validation logic into this built-in logic. And now we can use PasswordsAreNotEqual to show an error message if that's the case. For that, below this input here, this confirm-password input, I can add another div with that class name of control-error and then here check if passwords are not equal, in which case I'll output an error message where I'll say passwords must match. With that, if I save this and I reload, I still get the standard checks performed by the browser. But either way, if I enter a totally different value here and I then fill out the rest of the form, you will, of course, see that now if I click sign up, we still don't get the log here but instead we get this error because of the custom logic which we now also added to this form. And that is, of course, something you can do and that's why I'm showing this here. You are not limited to the built-in validation features just because you're also using those, but you can instead also add your own custom validation logic to this form.

Now, I wanna start exploring these important topics by answering one of the most important questions, which is how does React update the DOM? How does React update what the user sees on the screen? And related to that, how are component functions executed? Because of course, it's the content of the components that ends up on the screen, but having a good understanding of how React actually checks your components and derives the actual DOM from those components, having that understanding is crucial as a React developer because it will allow you to write better code and better applications. So let's dive into this demo application which I prepared for you. And I got my development server up and running here in this local project on CodeSandbox, you, of course, don't need to install or start anything there, everything works. And in this project here, we get a bunch of components, a bunch of components working together as always. And definitely feel free to explore this project code and this component structure. But in the end, we just have a bunch of components that are responsible for rendering these buttons, these icons on the buttons, the counter values, the header here, and so on. Now, of course, all these components that we have here, for example, the app component, are being rendered to the screen, otherwise, we wouldn't be able to see them. And as you already learned in this course, rendering a component to the screen in the end simply means that React goes ahead and executes this component function. So the app component function, for example, and it starts with the app component function in this project because the app component is the first component and the only component that's being referenced here in the main JSX file, which is the first code file to be executed when the website loads. And here, we're creating this root object and we're rendering the app component to the screen. And it's because of this code here that this app component function gets executed by React. Now, as I explained before in this course, this then means that all this code in there gets executed step-by-step. And in case of the app component function, that, for example, means that two pieces of state are registered for this component and then all the other code gets executed, so these functions here get created, for example. Important, they get created, not executed yet, just created. And then this JSX code here gets executed so that, in the end, it can be returned by this component. And as you learned, every component function must return something that can be rendered, typically JSX code, sometimes also a portal or something like this, but here, it's some JSX code. And therefore, of course, this JSX code is, in the end, translated to JavaScript code and translated to actual elements that can be rendered on the screen, that's what React is doing. Now, in this JSX code here, we see a bunch of built-in components, some native HTML elements like main, section, h2, and so on. So all these blue components that start with the lowercase characters, as you learned. And then we also have some custom components in there that start with uppercase characters, Header and Counter. And whenever such custom components are encountered in JSX code that's being executed, like here, this code that's returned, React goes ahead and also executes those component functions from top to bottom, so starting with the header component function. Before executing the rest of this code, React will reach out to this header component function and execute this function and all the code that's in there. Now, in this case, it's a very simple function which then just returns some JSX code that consists only of built-in elements and therefore, at this point, React is done with this part of the component tree because as you also learned, React constructs a component tree. And for example, for this application, this means that, at the top, we have the App component, and then as one child, we have this Header component. But since this Header component then does not contain any other custom components, this branch of the tree ends here. But it's not the tree overall that ends because in the App component, we also have this Counter component. So therefore, React, of course, also goes ahead and executes this component, and since this component also receives a prop, React also forwards this value here, chosenCount, which is some state I'm managing here in the App component. React passes this value through this prop to this component when executing this Counter component function. So it's this Counter component function that's being executed, this function here, and again, it's executed from top to bottom. For example, this function here, which determines whether this initialCount is a prime number or not, is being executed. Some state is registered, some functions are created but not executed yet. And then this JSX code here is executed. So the Counter component was added to the component tree, but of course, this Counter component in its JSX code then also contains more components which, of course, also are executed by React and which are added to the component tree. So these two buttons here, these two IconButton components, are added to the tree below the Counter component, and the CounterOutput component is added. Now, these IconButtons actually receive another component through a prop as an input. And then inside of these IconButton components or in this component file, this then gets rendered as a custom component, so this icon that's received gets rendered as a custom component here. And therefore, of course, this Icon component also gets added to the component tree. But then the tree ends here, and if we take a look at the CounterOutput there, the tree also ends because in there, we also got no more custom components. But as you see, we got a bunch of components being executed, and I added some logging functionality to all these components here. 40270518.title: Analyzing Component Function Executions via React's DevTools Profiler

You can also log stuff to the console, as I'm doing it here, but you can also use the React Developer Tools. I did show you these tools earlier in the course already, in the debugging section. The React Developer Tools are simply an extension which you can, for example, install in Chrome. And if you've got them installed you can open up your developer tools and there if it's not an incognito tab, you should find this profiler tab. And this profiler tab can also be used to understand which components are updating and are being re-rendered. You could say, under which circumstances. For this you can start profiling by clicking this button here and then you can interact with the page and for example, increment the counter. And if you then stop profiling you will see a graphical representation of which component functions were executed for which reason. And you can switch between different representations depending on what you prefer. So let me walk you through what this means. If you're in this flame graph chart mode here, you in the end get a representation that also shows you the order in which component functions were executed. And you also get a relation between component functions. For example, here you see that the app component is the root component, since it's at the top, and that it has this header and the counter components as children. Now you also see if you hover over 'App', or 'Header', that for one, they are highlighted on the left, but that they also did not render. So that clicking this increment button here did not cause the app or header component functions to be executed again. Instead, I clicked this button below, therefore the change that occurred happened in the counter component, because it's in there where I clicked this increment button, which led to this counter state being changed. Therefore, it was the counter component that was re-executed. But as you also learned earlier in the course already, such component reevaluations then don't propagate up. So React executing this counter component function again has no impact on the parent component, the app component. It does have an impact on the child components though, because as I explained before, of course all these custom component functions are executed again, and they do receive those prop values again, and so on. And you also see this here, in this flame graph chart, because here you can see that we got four components that were executed again. Basically all components that are colored here were executed again. So in this case, the icon button and the icon that's displayed by this button, this icon button and the counter output. And actually it's not just the minus icon that was executed again, but also the plus icon. And we can actually see that here, if we switch to the ranked chart mode, where we only see the components that were re-rendered. And where we then see the component that in the end cost the re-render cycle, the counter component in this case because it's state changed, and then other nested components below it, like the minus icon, the counter output, the icon button, the other icon button and the plus icon. So we see that here. But of course the idea is the same, that we can see which components were executed again and how they're related. You can also go to the settings here and under profiler, enable 'Record why each component rendered'. And if you do enable this and you then clear this output and you start another recording session, which sometimes doesn't work, so I'll just reload here, and you then increment again and stop this. And if you click on it, you can also see some details here to the right. You see that this rendered again because one hook changed. In this case, this simply means that the underlying state changed. So these dev tools can be another nice tool to understand how components are executed and why they were executed. And it is really crucial to understand this fundamental concept because this also allows you to optimize your application in various aspects.

40270524.title: Avoiding Component Function Executions with memo()
40270524.section: A Look Behind The Scenes Of React & Optimization Techniques
40270524.transcript: So how can we optimize a React application with that knowledge of how different components are related and are being executed? Well in this demo app here which I prepared for you, we have one problem, you could say, one use case that could be optimized if you understand how components are executed and are related. And for that, I'll open this console again where we see which components are rendered and how they're related. And now I'll clear this and I'll enter a new number here in this input field. Clicking Set here won't do anything yet, but if I enter a new number, if I type one character here, you see that a bunch of component functions were executed again. Basically all components that make up this app. And why is that happening here? Well, that's happening because this input field here lives directly in the app component. It's this input field here. And I have an on change handler on that input field. And therefore on every keystroke I update some state. And of course, as you learned, when the state changes this component function gets executed again. And as you now also learned over the last lectures this then means that all child component functions get executed again. And that's why on every keystroke all those component functions get executed again. Now this does not necessarily have an impact on the actual DOM that's rendered, so it's not that bad as you will learn later in this section, but of course it's still not optimal because it is a bunch of code that's executed. And of course we can now understand why that is happening. But how can we fix the problem? Well, to fix the problem we got two main possible solutions we can go for. And I'll show you the first solution here at the example of the counter component because React gives you a built-in function that you can wrap around your component functions so to say, that will prevent unnecessary component function executions. And that function is the memo function which must be imported from React. Once you did import this function, you can wrap it around your component function by simply executing it and passing your component function as a value as an argument to memo. Now here I'm getting a warning that some development mode features won't work correctly if we do it like this. Therefore, it is actually recommended that you first store the result in a separate variable or constant which I'll give the same name as my component function name and that you then simply export this variable or constant. But that's just required to get the best developer experience. Memo works either way. And what memo will do is that it will take a look at the props of your component function and whenever the component function would normally execute again for example, because the app component function executes, memo will take a look at the old prop value and at the new prop value that would be received now if that component function would execute and if those prop values are exactly the same, which for arrays and objects means that they really have to be exactly the same array or object in memory. But if they are the same this component function execution here will be prevented by memo. So that this component function, this counter or component function would only be re executed if initialCount changed or of course if its internal state changed that's not affected by memo. Memo only prevents function executions that are triggered by the parent component, so the app component. And it makes sense that memo takes a look at the props because of course if this initial count prop value in this case here did not change, there is no reason for the counter component to be executed again just because the parent component executed again. Of course, if that internal state changes that should definitely trigger this component function. And as mentioned, memo will not prevent this. It does not care about internal changes. But external changes of course only makes sense for this component here to be executed if the prop value changed. Otherwise executing this function again will of course yield the same result as before and therefore is unnecessary. And therefore, of course, if this counter component function is prevented from being executed again these nested component functions in there also won't be executed again because this code is not executed at all because this overall counter function code is not being executed. And therefore as a result, if you do wrap memo around this counter function and you reload and you then change this value, you see that now it's only the header component that's being called again, in addition to the app component, because all I did here is that I changed the value entered here. I did not click set or anything like that. And therefore of course in the app component the state was updated, but it was this enteredNumber state which does not matter for the counter component which does not change the initialCount and which therefore shouldn't trigger this component function to execute. And thanks to memo it won't. So therefore of course you might be intrigued to wrap memo around all your components because it can be useful, right? Yes it can, but you should not wrap it around all your components. Instead, it makes sense like in this example to wrap it around a component that's as high up in the component tree as possible. Like it's the case here for the counter component because if that component is then prevented from executing again, all the nested components also won't be executed again. And if you would wrap memo around all your components that would simply mean that React always has to check the props before it executes the component function. And of course, checking the prop values for equality also costs some performance. And that's especially a problem if you wrap a function that almost always gets changed props. So where this comparison almost always yields the result that the component function should be executed because then you paid the performance price for this check just to get the same result as you would get without memo that the component function is executed. So you should use it with care. And in simple apps like this one, to be honest, you don't need to use it at all because it won't have any measurable performance impact. But if you do wanna use it and if it can have an impact you should use it with care only on components where you can actually prevent re-renders and as high up in the component tree as possible.

40270540.title: Avoiding Component Function Executions with Clever Structuring
40270540.section: A Look Behind The Scenes Of React & Optimization Techniques
40270540.transcript: So 'memo', if used with care, can be useful, but it's not the only way of preventing unnecessary renders here. Another technique that is often even more powerful than memo is a clever component composition in your application. For example here, I have this section with that input, where we register every keystroke and change the state with every keystroke, in this app component. Now of course, we could put this into a separate component, and then this state that changes on every keystroke would also live in that separate component and would not affect this app component. So here we could add a separate component, which could be named 'ConfigureCounter', something like this, where I export my 'ConfigureCounter' component function and we can then grab this section code here from the app component, cut it, and return it here in 'ConfigureCounter' and also grab this code here and copy that and add it here. Though we don't need the chosen count state here, just the entered number. And now we just need to lift some state up, so to say. So that of course when that number that was entered is confirmed by clicking this set button. We propagate this information from 'ConfigureCounter' to the app component because that state will be needed here, so that it can be passed down to the counter component where it will in the end then be needed. And therefore in the app component we can get rid of these functions and of the 'EnteredNumber' state. But we must keep the 'ChosenCount' state and we should now add a new function here, 'handleSetCount' could be the name, where we expect to get the new count as an input, as a parameter. And where we then call 'setChosenCount' and set 'newCount' as a value. And we should use 'ConfigureCounter' here, and of course therefore also import it here. And then on 'ConfigureCounter', we must set a prop, through which we can pass this function. So that this function here can be executed from inside 'ConfigureCounter', a pattern we used many times before in this course. So we could name this prop 'onSet' and point at 'handleSetCount'. And then in 'ConfigureCounter' we can destructure this 'onSet' prop or whichever name you chose. And we must import 'useState' of course from React. And now here in 'handleSetClick' instead of calling set chosen count, we can call 'onSet' here, and pass the entered number as a value to that function. So in the end, two 'handleSetCount'. And by doing that we now make sure that this state that does change on every keystroke lives in this separate component. And therefore causes this component function to be executed again. But the app component function will not execute on every keystroke in this 'ConfigureCounter' component because, as I mentioned before, state changes and re-executions of child components don't trigger parent component executions. To visualize this, I'll add this log function here, which must be imported from the log JS file, to output 'ConfigureCounter'. And this will be on level one. Because it's one level below the app component here. And if I save that all and I reload here, you see that 'ConfigureCounter' is executed initially. But if I now clear this and I change this value it's just 'ConfigureCounter' that's being executed. The app component is not executed. The header which was executed before is not executed. And of course the counter also is not executed. And now here we could and should actually also remove memo from the counter because now memo is useless here. Because the app component now isn't executed anymore when we enter a new value here, but it will be executed when 'ChosenCount' gets updated. So when we click this set button. But when that happens we also will always re-execute the counter function because 'ChosenCount' is received as a prop here and therefore the memo check will find a different value, most likely, unless we entered the same counter value as before. Which would be one scenario where memo still could be useful, but in most cases it won't be useful because we'll have a different value. And therefore here we could argue that memo should be removed because it's not going to prevent many counter function executions. But it will of course always perform this check, which also costs performance. Here, I'll leave it around, so that you still see how it could be used. But be aware of the fact that here you could consider removing memo because it doesn't make too much sense here.

40270548.title: Understanding the useCallback() Hook
40270548.section: A Look Behind The Scenes Of React & Optimization Techniques
40270548.transcript: Now even though memo couldn't to be honest should be removed here, I wanna take a closer look at using memo, and I'll use it on another component, a component down here in the counter component. Because of course at the moment in this app if I increment the counter, we get a bunch of component functions being re executed again. To be precise we got these icon buttons and icons executing again and of course the counter output. Now the counter output makes a lot of sense because of course that output does change whenever I click a button. So this of course needs to be executed again. But for these buttons it's different. Of course, those button components and the values they output of course technically don't change. It's always exactly this and this button that gets output when I press a button. So re-rendering these components, re-executing these component functions doesn't make a lot of sense. Now, as mentioned before it also isn't a huge problem in this app though because these components contain so little code that executing them is no problem. But still, we could wrap icon button with memo to make sure it's not getting re‐executed unnecessarily. And since it's in this icon button where the actual icon is being rendered the icon component therefore also won't be executed again. So we'll prevent the execution of two components by just wrapping one of them with memo. And therefore, of course, we can import memo here from React in the icon button component file. And then just as before, create a new constant and wrap this component function with memo and then export this newly created constant like this. If we do that, you'll notice something strange though. If you reload, clear this and click this button. Hmm, it still got executed. So looks like memo didn't work here or it did work and some prop value changed. But for that, let's take a look at the props accepted by icon button. It's the children prop, which is output here and that in the end is simply the text of the button. So the button is wrapped around this text and that's therefore the value that's fed in as a value for children. Now obviously those texts here don't change. So the prop value for children shouldn't have changed. Now what about icon? The icon prop in the end receives a pointer to a component, the name of a component, but not the component as JSX code. That's important just the name of the component, and it then translates it to JSX code inside of this component function. But clearly these pointers here these names of these components are also not dynamic and also shouldn't change when the counter component function is being executed. So it must be related to the remaining props which we got here, which I'm just forwarding to the button. And here I am just setting one other prop and that's onClick, which I set to a function. And this indeed is the problem here because these functions look as if they maybe don't change but keep in mind that they are created inside of the counter component function. These are nested functions, and therefore, indeed they will technically be recreated every time this counter component function executes. So every time this state changes, and it does not matter that the code in there is the same and does not change, the component function as a value, as an object in JavaScript is recreated. And it will be a different object in memory than before for the last execution of the counter component function and therefore it technically is a new prop value. Now this recreation here can be prevented with help of a special hook provided by React, which we also already used before in conjunction with effects and the useEffect hook. It's the useCallback hook. This hook can be used to avoid the recreation of a function and it is sometimes needed if you have a function as a dependency of useEffect. And it's also needed in conjunction with memo to avoid unnecessary re‐executions. Because you use useCallback by wrapping it around a function in your component function. And then by also as a second argument by passing an array of dependencies to useCallback. And here you would list all props or state or context values you would be using inside of this function because if one of those values changed, a new function would have to be created so that the latest value is used in that function. But here I'm only using setCounter which is a state updating function. And those state updating functions are guaranteed to never change by React, and therefore you don't need to add them to this dependencies array. Then useCallback returns a value in the end the same function, but in this unchanging way, so to say. And you can store this in a variable or constant which I am doing here, using that same name as before. And therefore you can still use these values as functions because they are functions but they will be functions that are managed by React that will not be recreated unnecessarily. So therefore, here for handleIncrement, I'll of course also wrap this with useCallback and also set this dependencies array. And with that done, if we now save everything and we reload and clear this, you'll see that now clicking increment does not cause those icon buttons and the nested icon components to be executed again, instead now it's just the counter output which makes sense because this, as mentioned did change and does need to update.

40270550.title: Understanding the useMemo() Hook
40270550.section: A Look Behind The Scenes Of React & Optimization Techniques
40270550.transcript: Now in this output from the last lecture, you also see that this calculating if is prime number code is output every time we click this button. So this clearly is code that is executed, again, if the counter changes. And that makes sense because it's this isPrime function, that is called directly inside of the counter component function, that is being executed whenever this component function changes. And it's this function where this log statement is coming from. Now, this function is just there for demo purposes, to be honest. But this function does, in the end, use this initial account value as an input. And this value technically only changes if we enter a new value here and click set. Though at the moment this doesn't work and we'll fix it later. But still it's only at this point of time where a new initial count value will be received and isPrime will yield a new result. Other than that, if initial count does not change and if the counter component function is only re-executed because the counter state changed, isPrime will always produce the same result. And that can be a problem if you have a function that performs a rather complex and performance intensive calculation. Which is not necessarily the case here but it's also not a super trivial calculation, especially if you enter a bigger number in here, like a million, or a trillion, or something like this. Re-executing all this code all the time, even though it will produce the same result as before is then not very efficient. And therefore just as you might want to prevent component function executions with memo, you also might want to prevent the execution of normal functions that are called inside of component functions, unless their input changed. And React gives us a hook that can be used for this scenario and problem, the useMemo hook. Which is not to be confused with memo, memo is wrapped around component functions, as explained a couple of lectures ago. useMemo is wrapped around normal functions that are executed in component functions to prevent their execution. And useMemo should really only be used if you have a complex calculation that you want to prevent. For example, here if we wanna prevent isPrime from executing we can call useMemo here. And then through useMemo, we pass a function, for example, an anonymous arrow function. Which then returns the result of the function that we want to prevent executing. So it's in this anonymous function where we now call isPrime. useMemo then also wants a dependencies array, just as useCallback wanted it. And the idea behind useMemo is now that react will now execute this function, which you pass to useMemo. So this anonymous arrow function here. And it will then store the result of this execution, so the result of calling isPrime then in the end since that's returned in this anonymous function here. And it will only re-execute this function if one of those dependencies here changed. So if you have an empty dependencies array this will never re-execute, because there are no dependencies that could change. But of course here you can see that I'm already getting a warning, because here I should add initial count as a dependency because that is used as a input by isPrime. And indeed, if initialCount changes isPrime will yield a different result, and therefore you should add initialCount as a value here in this dependencies array. And with that useMemo, we'll now make sure that in the end isPrime only gets re-executed if initial count changed. But it will not get executed if this counter state changed, for example. So if we save this and we go back and reload, clear this, you'll see that if I click increment we no longer get this output from inside the isPrime function. On the other hand, if I set a new counter value we do get that output here. Now I wanna emphasize though that you really should not overuse useMemo. You should not start wrapping it around all your functions, because just like Memo, of course it does need to perform this extra dependency value comparison. And if you have a function that for example simply needs to be executed on basically every component function, re-execution. Adding this extra check doesn't make any sense and instead just cost extra performance. On the other hand here where indeed a lot of executions can be avoided, and where we potentially execute code that could take some time to finish using useMemo makes more sense. So just as with memo, you should use it with care. But if used in the right situations it can help improve the performance of your react application.

40270566.title: React Uses A Virtual DOM - Time To Explore It!
40270566.section: A Look Behind The Scenes Of React & Optimization Techniques
40270566.transcript: So at this point, in this section, we spent a lot of time understanding how component functions are executed and how you can prevent that under certain circumstances. Now, I want to take a closer look at what actually happens if a component function is executed and how this JSX code here ends up on the screen. Because of course, the first thing you might think is that when a component function gets executed and this JSX code gets returned and this component tree gets built, in the end, you end up with a bunch of native HTML elements. If this entire component tree is in the end resolved. And that then this HTML code is inserted in the DOM. You could think that this is what's going on, that the HTML elements returned by all those components are inserted in the DOM. And indeed, that is what happens if you reload the page or if you visit it for the first time. Because if you do that, you essentially start with an empty page. Keep in mind that it's technically this index HTML file that is being loaded when you visit the website and this is a pretty empty page in the end. It just has one div and this script import. And indeed, it's in this div where this entire React app. So this component tree and the HTML code that's generated by that tree will be rendered. That's why if you view the page source code, you see nothing. And if you instead go to the elements tab, you see the, well, full DOM tree here. So yes, this was all inserted by React, but if you then click a button here, this is not all recreated and reinserted because that would be super inefficient. So just because a component function is executed again, like this counter component function when we click one of those buttons here, just because that happens does not mean that all the JSX code that's produced by that component function is reinserted in the DOM. The old code is not thrown away and replaced by new HTML code. And you can see that in the DevTools. For example, here, if I go to the counter section and I expand this paragraph, which does include the counter value. If you watch this HTML code on the right here, as I click this button here, you see that it's just this span here that's flashing. And in the Chrome DevTools in this elements tab, these elements flash if they are changed, so if the DOM changed in that place. So put in other words, only elements that flash here were touched by React. So when I increment this value, it looks as if React is only updating this span, is only working with that span element. All the other DOM elements are not touched by React. Even though technically, this counter component of course, also returns this paragraph and all of these buttons. Nonetheless, none of that flashes here, which means it's not changed in any way. And that's the case because React works with a so-called virtual DOM for finding out which parts of the actual DOM should be updated. And it's using this virtual DOM because working with such a virtual DOM, which lives only in memory is faster than working with that real DOM here. So how does this virtual DOM work then? Well, if I reload the page and the overall React app starts therefore and is rendered for the first time, React creates that component tree and then in the end derives the actual HTML code that should be rendered from that component tree and it then creates a virtual DOM snapshot. So it's not reaching out to the real DOM yet. Instead, it just creates a virtual representation of how that real DOM should look like. As a next step, React then compares that to that last virtual DOM snapshot it created. Now, if the app just started, there is no lost snapshot and therefore, React of course, sees that everything changed and it goes ahead to the real DOM and makes those changes, which means in this case, the entire virtual DOM is inserted into the real DOM, into this div with the idea root in this case. Now, when I click a button here, React in the end repeats that process. It again creates that component tree, and of course, it can quickly find out that only a part of that tree changed, that only some of those component functions were executed. In this case counter, and depending on if you are using memo or not, also those buttons. And React derives the updated HTML code. And it then compares that code with the old code, you could say. Still only virtually, it does not compare anything with the real DOM, it just compares this new virtual DOM snapshot with the old virtual DOM. And of course, it then quickly finds out which parts changed. And here in this case, this is only this span or this text inside of the span to be precise. And therefore then, as a next step, React goes ahead and applies those changes to the real DOM and only those changes. So in this case, it only goes to that span in the real DOM and replaces its old text with the new text and it does nothing else. And that is super important to understand. Just because a component function executes and it's JSX code therefore is evaluated, does not mean that this code is inserted or updated in the real DOM, because all those real DOM operations are quite performance intensive and therefore, React does not change the real DOM all the time. Instead, it just creates these snapshots, compares them and only makes the necessary changes to get to the target result.

40270602.title: State Scheduling & Batching
40270602.section: A Look Behind The Scenes Of React & Optimization Techniques
40270602.transcript: So now at this point, we're almost done for this section but there's one last state related concept here which I also want to explore together with you. And that's how state updates are in the end scheduled and executed by React. Now, I did mention earlier in the course that when you call a state updating function, like setChosenCount the state update will be scheduled by React. It will not be executed instantly. And that's why if you console log the state right after updating it, you will not get the new state here but instead the old state. And it's a common misconception that people think that they update the state in one line and then in the next line they can use the updated state. This won't work. If I open the console here and clear this and I set a new state. You see here I'm getting the old state because this state update is scheduled by React. And in the end it will of course be executed pretty much instantly, but it will not be executed right away and it will not magically change this value which belongs to the old component function execution, so to say. Instead, this will trigger a new component function execution and the new state will be available the next time this executes. But because those state updates are scheduled it is considered a best practice to perform state updates that depend on the old state value using this function form which I also showed you before in this course where you pass a function to the state updating function and that function then receives the old state snapshot and should return the new state snapshot. And you can use the old state snapshot to then derive the new snapshot. Because when using this approach here, React guarantees you that here you will always get the latest state snapshot available and if multiple state updates of the same time should be scheduled, they will be executed in the order in which they were scheduled. And you will always get the right value here. For example, here, if for some reason I decided that I then wanna update my chosenCount here by adding one to it, we would get a strange result if we try to do it like this. If we set the chosenCount to the newCount and we then add one to it. Since I'm using my old state like this here I will not get newCount plus one as a result of this, but instead my old state plus one. So if I do that and I click 10 here I get one as a new initial count value here because the one here was added to my old chosenCount, which was zero. If instead we used this function form here where we get the previous chosenCount passed in automatically by React, and we then add one to it we would get the newCount plus one instead because now both state updates are scheduled and they're guaranteed to be executed in the order in which they are scheduled. But now the second state update will then get the latest state value at the point of time where this scheduled update is executed and therefore it will get that newCount value that was set by this state update. So now if we change the code like this and save this, reload and enter 10 here, for example, we get 11 here. And this is an important mechanism to be aware of and you should especially keep in mind that you can't use the new state value right after setting it like this. This won't work, at least not as you might expect it to work. Now what's all important to know about this state scheduling is that if you have multiple state updates that are in the end triggered simultaneously so in the same function here, you will not end up with multiple component function executions because that would of course be pretty inefficient. But technically this is of course what could happen if React wouldn't take care for it not to happen because technically we're calling two state updating functions here. And therefore, technically since you learned that executing such a state updating function triggers the component function to run again, you could expect the component function to run twice because we have two state updates. But this is not what happens here. If we open the console and clear this and enter a value here and click Set. You see the app component function is only executed once. It's only called once and not a second time. And that's the case because React also performs state batching, which simply means that multiple state updates that are triggered from the same function, for example, are batched together and will only lead to one component function execution.

What is that pattern all about? What are Compound Components? If I type something more complex, I don't see anything. If I type two, I just see item two. If I type item, I see both. Now, for those places, I prepared a special component, which you find attached, a Place.jsx file, which contains a place component and you can use that component for outputting an individual place. 41699670.title: Working with Debouncing
41699670.transcript: So now that we learned about Compound Components and that we learned about render props, let's explore another pattern which you'll sometimes need actually, not just in React apps, but possibly in front-end applications in general, debouncing. And what is that? What is debouncing? Well, here in that SearchableList component, we are searching those items in the end upon every keystroke because whenever we type something into the input field, so on every keystroke, this function here gets triggered and this searchTerm state gets updated, which in turn leads to this component being rendered again, which leads to new search results being produced. Now for this demo app where we're only dealing with very simple local dummy data, that's not really a big problem, but it could become problematic if with every update, we, for example, needed to send a new HTTP request, or if this filtering logic here would be more complex and more performance intensive. In such scenarios, executing this entire component function and producing a new derived state on every keystroke in the end, can quickly become inefficient. And debouncing simply is a technique where we don't update the state on every keystroke, but where we instead define some timing threshold where we only update the state if the user stopped typing for a couple of milliseconds, for example. So that if the user types an entire word, we're not updating the search results for every character, but only once the word is done, for example. That's what debouncing is, and that's what we'll implement next. So to implement debouncing here in that SearchableList component, we should start here in handleChange because I now don't always wanna update my state. Instead, I only wanna update the state if the user stopped typing for a certain period of time. Therefore, we can achieve this by using the built-in setTimeout function here. And then by defining any threshold of our choice, for example, 500 milliseconds. You could of course also use a higher value. And then I'll move this state updating function into this callback function of setTimeout. so that the state update is delayed by 500 milliseconds. Now that's of course not all because this would still update the state on every keystroke, just delayed by 500 milliseconds, which isn't too useful. Instead, we can now also add a ref here to this component with the built-in useRef hook, and we could name it lastChange. Now with that added, I want to store the timer identifier that's returned by setTimeout in lastChange. So in that ref, by accessing the current prop on that ref object so that I store the timer ID in my ref. And now we can go to the start of handleChange. So before we execute this timeout code and check if lastChange.current is a truthy value, which it for example will be if we did store a timer inside of it. In that case, I want to clear the currently running timer with the built-in clearTimeout function. So I pass lastChange.current to clearTimeout. Now, why am I doing that here? Well, because with that code, with all that code here added with that if statement, I am making sure that if we have a currently ongoing timer, I clear it, and then we start a new one. So that last state update, which was part of that timer that started in the past was now basically canceled. And instead a new timer with the new state update for the new keystroke, for example, is scheduled instead. But that timer will also be canceled if the user types again and this handleChange function executes again, and that therefore we'll make sure that we don't perform all these state updates, but instead only the latest state update, at least if we keep on typing before that timer expired. Now we're not done though. Instead inside of that setTimeout callback, we should also set lastChange.current to null to clear the stored timer identifier so that if the timer does expire, the timer ID is removed from the ref and this check here, this if statement, won't become active for the next keystroke. Otherwise, if we don't add this line here, the timer id in the ref will never be removed even if the timer did expire, because the ref is not linked to the timer, it just contains the timer id. But we have to manually remove that ID if the timer did expire. And that's indeed all, that's how we can add debouncing so that not every event, not every keystroke will lead to a state update, but instead only keystrokes that happened outside of this threshold. And therefore back here in the application, you can see that as I type, as long as I keep on typing, nothing happens. But once I stop typing, the filter is applied. So if I type african, it only starts filtering after I then waited for a 500 milliseconds, which means I can of course also keep on typing and not get updates for every keystroke. And as mentioned, that can be more efficient than performing the update for every keystroke, especially if this filtering logic here would be more complex or if the filtered results would trigger an extra HTTP request or something like this. So it's not really needed for this demo app, but knowing about this pattern and knowing about this concept of debouncing in general, is definitely important.

41699756.title: Navigating Between Pages
41699756.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41699756.transcript: Okay. Now that we got this second page here, it would of course also be great if we could get to this page in any other way than manually updating the URL, because no one does that. It would be nice to have a link to that second page. And for that, on that starting page, we could add a new paragraph here, and then maybe add an anchor element where we say About Us. And here the address then could be /about, to go to that page. With that done, indeed, we have that link here, and if I click it, I'm taken to this About Us page. But this approach now has a flaw. It's not ideal, and you can see that there is a flaw if you take a look at this refresh icon up here, as I click About Us, you will see that it briefly turns to a cross, which always is a sign that a brand new page was downloaded from the backend. So, we left the current page and got a brand new page. The disadvantage with that is that we now no longer have a single-page application as we normally do when working with React. Now, of course, to some extent that makes sense, because I mentioned that with NextJS, you would build full-stack applications where the content is rendered on the backend, and therefore it makes sense that it's no longer happening in the front-end on the client side. But actually the great thing about NextJS is that we get both. When we visit a page for the first time by, for example, manually entering the URL, then indeed it's rendered on the server, we get the finished page, and we're here. But if we are on a page and we then navigate around by clicking links, for example, NextJS allows us to actually stay in a single-page application and update the UI with help of client-side JavaScript code. Now, technically the content of the Next page will still be pre-rendered on the server, but it will then be updated on the client side with client-side JavaScript code, you could say. And therefore you can get the best of both worlds, a highly interactive, reactive client-side application once it's active, but a finished page being served if you are visiting the page for the first time, so, if you did not navigate around on it yet. However, at the moment, we're not getting this benefit. With that link here, the page is always reloading, and we're not in that single-page application. And the reason for that is that we're using the regular anchor element. That works, but it does get us out of that single-page application. In order to stay in there, we have to use a special component called Link, which we can import from next/link. This is a component provided by the NextJS framework, which you should use instead of the anchor element if you have some internal link as we have it here. So, if you have a link that leads to another page of your website. It then still takes the ref prop and you could still also add other props like className and so on. But now it is a component that will actually ensure that we stay in that single-page application. So, with that link component being used here, if you now take a look at that refresh icon, as I click this link, you see it never changes to a cross. It always stays the same, which proves that we're not leaving the page, we're not loading a brand new page. Instead, behind the scenes, the content of the next page will still be rendered on the server, but it'll then be sent to the client, and there it'll be handled by client-side JavaScript code to update what we see on the screen.
