We can create a ref with the useRef hook, which is imported from React, so which has nothing to do with NextJS, but which instead works the way you know it from React, which gives you a ref, like a imageInput ref that you can then connect to an HTML element. And I'll actually just name it imageInput. But now we can use that ref and set it as a value for the ref prop on this imageInput field here like this. And that now allows us to use this ref to trigger the click method. However, of course, we have to access .current first because that then gives us access to the actual connected element and object. But that then is the code we can use to trigger a click on this input, which is connected to this ref. With that, if I now click my own button, this image picker opens up again. But now as a final step, I wanna make sure that as soon as I pick an image, I also show a preview of that here.

41718166.title: Adding an Image Preview to the Picker
41718166.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41718166.transcript: Now, to show a preview of the picked image, we need to handle the event that an image was picked and then store some state so that we can update this UI and show a preview as soon as we have an image. So we need some state here in this component, and therefore, we need to use state hook. This would require us to turn this component into a client component, but we already did this here, so no changes are needed. Now here with this state, I then wanna manage the picked image, so I'll name this picked image and have a set picked image state updating function. And now we need a second event handler function here that could be called handle image change, which should be triggered whenever this input here has a new value, so whenever the change event on that input is emitted. And therefore, we should add the on change prop here and set handle image change as a value. Now, here in handle image change, we'll then automatically get an event object, as you always do for those event handling functions in React, and we can use this event object to get hold of that picked image. We can get hold of that picked file by using event.target.files and then accessing the first file. This file property will exist because the target of this event is this input, and this file input object, under the hood, will have such a files property. That will be an array of all the files that have been picked, but here, it'll only be one file that can be picked. and therefore. I'll just access that first file. You could allow the user to pick multiple files by adding the multiple prop here to this file input, but since we don't have that here and don't want it here, it'll only be one file that we can access. Now, it's possible that the user actually did not pick a file for some reason, so I'll check if the file is undefined, if we got no file, and in that case, I'll just return and not continue. (paper crumpling) But if we make it past this if check, we know that some file has been selected. Now, in order to show it as a preview here, I now want to convert it into a so-called data URL, which is simply a value that can be used as an input for an image element, so that can be used as a source for an image element. And we can generate such a data URL with help of a class built into JavaScript, the file reader class. We can construct such a file reader and then do what its name implies. We can read our file, and we can simply call file reader read as data URL and pass that file to that method. Now, this method works in a bit of a strange way, because it doesn't actually return anything, not a promise, not the read file, and it also doesn't take a callback. Instead, we get hold of that data URL that's being generated by assigning a value to the on load property of this file reader object. So we store a function as a value in on load, and this function will then be triggered by the file reader once this method here, this read as data URL method, is done, But here, we then won't get the generated URL as an input. Instead, we can access it by accessing fileReader.result, and that will then be that generated URL. And that's what I then wanna store in my state. So I will set my picked image to the result of accessing fileReader.result inside of this on load function. Well, and with that, we can then use this picked image state to show a preview. So down here, in this controls div, I'll add another div with a class name of preview, and in that div, I now wanna check if we don't have a picked image, in which case I'll simply output a paragraph where I say "No image picked yet" or something like that. But if we do have a picked image, I wanna show that image. And for that, I'll use the image component provided by next, so you should import that from next image, and then here on that image, I'll set the source to my picked image, so to that data URL, and the alt text to "The image selected by the user," and I'll add that fill prop because I don't know the dimensions of that image in advance. And that should then be all. With that, we should have that finished image picker component, and we can now take a look at it. Here in this application, I now got this preview area, and if I now click this button, the file picker opens up, and if I pick an image, I can see that preview here. So that is working. And with that, we're now finally ready to also work on the submission of this form.

41718170.title: Introducing & Using Server Actions for Handling Form Submissions
41718170.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41718170.transcript: So, now that we've got this form here and we're able to pick images, it's time to make sure that this form can also be submitted, and that we then create and store a new meal when that happens. Now when it comes to handling form submissions, we could do that as we do it in most React projects. We can go to the component that contains the form and then we could add the onSubmit prop and define a function that should be executed when the form is submitted. There, we could then prevent the browser default, manually collect all the data, and send that data to a backend. But again, here we already are on the backend, at least kind of. We have a full stack application that has both backend and frontend. And that's why Next.js gives us a more powerful and convenient pattern than manually handling the form submission and collecting the data and sending it to a server. Instead, we can create a function in the component that holds the form, for example, which we could call shareMeal here, though the name is up to you. And we can then add a special directive in this function, so inside of this function body. And that's the 'use server' directive. Now, we have seen the 'use client' directive before, which however was not added inside of functions, but inside of files. And then when added there, 'use client' made sure that the components created in that file would be client components. 'use server' inside of a function is different because this creates a so-called Server Action, which is a function that's guaranteed to execute on the server, and only there. So just as components by default are server components which only execute on the server, this is now a function that only executes on a server. But in case of functions, you must explicitly state that it belongs to the server by adding this directive inside of them if you wanna create such a Server Action. In addition, to really turn this into a so-called Server Action, you also must add the "async" keyword in front of it. And now this is a Server Action. But what's now the special thing about this Server Action? Why does this feature exist? Well, this feature exists in React, by the way, not just in Next.js, but like server components, it doesn't really work in Vanilla React apps. Instead, you need a framework like Next to unlock this feature and use it. And this feature then exists because you can now take such a Server Action and assign this Server Action function as a value for the action prop on a function. So here, I'm setting shareMeal as a value on that action prop of that form here. And that's of course something you might have never seen before, because normally, the action prop is set to the path to which the request should be sent if you are relying on the browser's built-in form handling capabilities. Now that's not what we're doing here. Instead, we're setting it to an action, to such a Server Action function. And that's a pattern that's supported by Next and React here that will ensure that when this form is submitted, Next.js will, behind the scenes, create a request and send it to this Next.js server that's serving the website so that this function gets triggered, and you can then handle the form submission there, but on the server. So, that function will then execute on the server, not in the client. And this function will then automatically receive that formData that was submitted. So, the data that was gathered by the inputs in the form collected in a formData object, using that formData class that's available in JavaScript. So we'll get such a formData object here and we can then use that to handle the submitted data. For example, here, we could create a meal by extracting meal data from that form data. So we could add a title property to this meal and as a value store formData.get 'title'. This formData object that we're getting will have a get method that allows us to get the value that was entered into a certain input field, and the input field is identified by its name. So, if I get the value of the input field with the name 'title', I am getting the value of this input field here because this input field has that name, 'title'. And of course we can now do that for all the data that makes up a meal. So, all the data we're expecting in our database, like the image, the summary, the instructions, creator, creator_email, and so on. So for that here, we can also store the summary by getting hold of formData.get 'summary', extracting the data from the input field with the name "summary", get the instructions from formData.get 'instructions', and get the image from formData.get 'image'. Now, for this to work, we have to make sure that we pass the appropriate name to our ImagePicker component. And at the moment, I'm not doing that. Indeed I'm using the ImagePicker without setting either a label or a name, and I now wanna change this. So here I'll set the label to "Your image" and the name to "image", let's say. And with that, we make sure that we can extract that image with help of formData.get. Now, that will then be the uploaded file and we'll handle the storage of that file a little bit later. Now, besides that, I'll also get the creator of that meal by accessing formData.get 'name', because I'm getting that name of the person who created that meal from that name input field, so with a name of "name", so that's why I'm extracting it like this. And we got the creator_email with help of formData.get 'email', like this, and therefore that will give us that email. So with that, we create such a form object. And the next step then would be to store that in a database, though the image should actually be stored on the file system and then a path to that image should be stored in a database. But either way, the storage of this data is now the next step. But what's important to understand here is that you can use this Server Actions feature to create such a function that will be triggered when a form is submitted. And in order to see this in action, we'll not store that data yet, but instead simply log it to the console, like this. And with that, we can give this a try. If we now reload this, "Share your email page", and I then enter some data here, like this, and I then also pick an image, If I click Share Meal, and I open the developer tools before doing that, you see no log here, but you also see that the page didn't reload. So it looks like that default browser behavior, which normally would be to send the request automatically and therefore reload the page was prevented. And instead, you'll see some output here on the server side in your terminal, in that terminal where you started the development server. There you see that meal data. You see all the data I entered and that file. And again, we'll store that file soon. But with that, that's how you can handle form submissions with help of Server Actions.

41718172.title: Storing Server Actions in Separate Files
41718172.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41718172.transcript: So this is how we can add a Server action. There also is another way of adding it though. You can add it in a component like this, but this will only work if the component in which you are adding it is not a client component. If you had use client here at the top because somewhere else in the component you might be using some client-only feature, you would get an error that you are not allowed to have server actions in a client component file. So that would be a problem then. Of course, here we don't need use client, though actually later we will need it, but at the moment we would be fine like this, but nonetheless this would be a potential problem. In addition, you also might not want to have your server-side form submission handling logic in the same file as your JSX code. You might want to separate that. And for those reasons, you can also store server actions in separate files. Therefore, here, I'll actually go back to that root lip folder, and add a actions.js file in there, though the file name does not matter. It doesn't have to be named actions.js, it could be any name. What's important in that file though is that it has that Use Server directive at the top. So now not inside of a function, but instead at the top of a file. When adding it at the top of a file like this, all the functions you might define in that file will be treated as Server Actions. So that simply now allows us to go back to that Share page and grab that share meal function here, and cut it from there, and instead move it into this Actions file. There it now must be exported so that it can be used in other files, and you can and should now remove use server in here because we already got that at the top of the file. And with that server action outsourced here, we can now go back to the share meal page and still use it as an action here on the forum by simply importing it. So by adding this import. And now you would be able to convert this to a client component if you needed to. Now, you would not get an error when doing that, and that might sound strange, but the problem before was simply that you were defining client and server-side code in the same file, and the build process that's used by NextJS is essentially not able to separate that in a clean way, and therefore server-side code could accidentally end up on the client side, which could pose security issues and cause other problems as well. That's why for technical reasons, you can't mix both in the same file, but you can absolutely import a server action from another file and then use it in such a client component. Again, here we don't need use client, but it is important to understand that you can blend these two worlds and mix these two worlds, as I just described it. But with that, it's now finally time to make sure that we're not just logging the meal, but that we're actually storing it in a database, and that we store the image on the file system.

41718174.title: Creating a Slug & Sanitizing User Input for XSS Protection
41718174.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41718174.transcript: So let's now work on storing that data and to do that, I'll start in the meals.js file. Here we have functions for getting "meals" and getting a single "meal." Now I want to export another function here, which is there to save a "meal" and I expect a "meal" object as an input. A "meal" object that should have this shape here. Now, inside of "save meal" we therefore have to do a couple of things. For example, we'll need to generate a slug because in my database, I wanna store a slug for every "meal" and we don't get that from the form. Instead, I want to generate it based on the title. Now to do that, I'll stop the development server and install a extra package, a package called slugify that will do what its name implies, but that's not the only package I'll install. Instead, I'll also install a package called xss, which will help us protect against cross-site scripting attacks. Because you must not forget that we're storing user-generated content and I'm then outputting these instructions that are generated by the user as HTML, here in that "meal" detail page, I'm outputting those instructions as HTML. So we are vulnerable to cross-site scripting attacks and that's why we should protect against them and why we should sanitize the content that's sent by the user, which is exactly what we can do with help of this xss package. So therefore, now with that installed, we can restart the development server and back here in meals.js, I now want to import these two packages. So slugify from slugify and xss from xss. Now let's start with the slugify function. Here, I'll create a new slug by calling slugify and passing meal.title to it. In addition, I'll pass a configuration object to slug, where I set lower to true so that it forces all characters to be lowercase. That's the first step. As a next step, I wanna remove any harmful content from those instructions and for that we can use this xss thing which we imported, which turns out to be a function to call it on meal.instructions, so that we sanitize and clean those instructions and actually, instead of using these extra constants or variables, we can of course also add a slug property to "meal" on the fly like this, and overwrite instructions with the sanitized instructions like this. Now with that done, we prepared all the data except for the image.

41737266.title: Storing Uploaded Images & Storing Data in the Database
41737266.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737266.transcript: Now the image should be stored on the file system, not in the database because storing files in databases is a bad idea. It's bad for performance because databases simply aren't built for that. Instead, I wanna store the uploaded files in that public folder because any images stored there in that images folder will be publicly available so they can be rendered on the screen without problems. And therefore we'll now need to perform a couple of steps. First, I'll start by getting the extension of the uploaded image because it could be a JPEG or PNG file. So I'll dig into the meal image. Keep in mind that we're storing that image, which we got from the form under that image key. So I'm getting access to that here. And then on that I'll call split to split it on the dot and pop the last element, which will be the file extension. Next, I also want to generate a unique file name and not use the file name of the user. So therefore here what I'll do is I'll generate a string with those tactics using dot string template literal notation, which we can use in JavaScript to create a file name that uses .slug here. So meal.slug actually, and then a dot, and then the extension. So now with that we got the file name. Now we need to write that to a file in that public folder. And we can do that with help of an API provided by node JS. To be precise, the file system API. And for that I'll import fs from node:fs. This allows us to work with the file system. We can then use this fs module to call createWriteStream, which will create a stream that allows us to write data to a certain file. Now createWriteStream then needs a path to default, which you wanna write. And it'll then return a stream object which you can then use to write to that path. So regarding that path, I'll create a dynamic string here and I'll target public/images. So this images folder here in this public folder, / and then the fileName to which you wanna write must be included in that path. So we can inject that fileName here, this fileName which we constructed earlier. With that, we then get a stream which we can store in a constant or variable in which we can use to write to that path. So to that image in that path. Now we can write to that stream by calling the write method on it. And this write method then wants a chunk. Now what's that chunk? In case of our image here, it means that we should convert the image to a so-called buffer to a bufferedImage you could say, which might sound fancy, but which thankfully isn't too difficult to do. You can use that image object, which we have here, which we're getting from our form after all. So that image that's being stored under that image key. And that object has an arrayBuffer method you can call, which will give you such a buffer, which we need here for the write method. The only tricky thing here is that arrayBuffer actually will give you a promise that eventually resolves to that buffer and therefore we must await this here. And in order to use await here, we should add async in front of the saveMeal function here. And with that, we then got this bufferedImage. Now this is of type arrayBuffer and write actually wants a regular buffer. So therefore here we have to call Buffer from and past this arrayBuffer so this bufferedImage to it, like this. By the way, one other little error I just spotted is here where I split my image name to get the extension. Here, we have to access meal.image.name.split because meal.image is simply that image which we get from the form so that image object that's automatically generated by the browser, and that object will indeed have a couple of helpful properties. One of them is the name property, which carries the name of the image file that was uploaded. So that's something we should change here. But with that back to the stream, now we're writing this buffer to this path, including this file and this write method now also takes a second argument. The first argument is the thing you wanna write. The second argument is a function that will be executed once it's done writing. And here we get an error argument, which is null if everything worked, but which will hold some error information if something went wrong. So for here, we should check if error is truthy. So if we have an error, and in that case I wanna throw a new Error where I say, Saving image failed, or something like this. And with that we wrote the image to this file. Now we just need to store the overall data in the database. And for that I'll start by overriding the image that's stored in my meal object with a path to the image where we stored it. Because I don't wanna store the image file itself in a database, databases are not built for that, instead I just wanna store the path. Hence I'll overwride the image object in my meal with that path here, though there you should actually remove this public segment because all requests for images will be sent to the public folder automatically anyways. Or put in other words, the content of the public folder will be served as if it were served on the root level of your Server anyways and therefore public shouldn't be included here so that it won't be included later when requests for that image will be sent. But with that, we now finished the preparations for this meal object and we can now save it in a database. And to save it, I'll use my db object and then prepare another statement. And here I'll prepare a statement and I'll use this template literal notation to split it across multiple lines where I wanna insert some data into the meals table and I wanna insert data into the following fields, into the title, into the summary field, into the instructions field, into the creator field and the creator email field and into the image and slug fields. So basically into all fields that we configured when we created the table except for the id, because that will be populated automatically. Now we must specify the values that should be inserted into those fields. And here you could directly inject those values, but this is not recommended because that approach would be vulnerable to SQL injection attacks. Instead, you should use those placeholders one for every field though to make this a bit easier, you can also use another syntax here supported by better-sqlite, which I also used in the initdb.js file, this syntax here. You can target specific fields by their name, like this. And then later just pass an object to the run function, which we will call on this prepared statement and better-sqlite. The package we're using to execute this command here will then look at those property names in that object you're passing to it to extract the values stored under those property names and it will then use those values to store them in those fields. However, you must make sure that the order you got here is the same as you have it here and therefore I'll reorder that and put title first, then the summary, then the instructions, then the creator, then the creator email, and then the image and the slug. I also will remove that trailing comma and instead add one here. Getting those commas right is really important. You will get errors if you don't do it. Well, and then we can call run on that statement and pass our meal object to it. And thanks to this syntax we're using here, the data for those fields will automatically be extracted. And that's it then. This is a saveMeal function that will store both a file and the data in the database. With that done, we can go back to our Server Action and there, instead of console logging that meal, we can call saveMeal. So we can call this function on which we just worked. Now saveMeal will return a promise, so we can also add async here and then use a wait here. Though at the moment, I then don't have any follow-up action that should be executed here, but that will come later. With that though, we're making sure to call saVeMeal here in the Server Action. Therefore, now we should pass the meal to saveMeal. And if we now save everything and you got that development server up and running, if you go to that share meal page, you should be able to enter some data here, like this and then pick an image and click Share Meal. And with that, nothing happens here, which is a good sign. At least we got no error. But of course we also don't know whether it worked. Now we'll take care of that in a second. For the moment, we can take a look at the public folder, and there indeed, I find my uploaded image. So that's a good hint that it seemed to have worked. I also got no error here in my terminal. And if we go back to the page and browse our meals again, we should also find our meal here. And that therefore proves that it did work. Now to provide a better user experience, we might want to redirect the user once the meal data has been submitted. And for that we can go back to our actions and hereafter a saveMeal. We can call redirect and import that from next navigation. This redirect function will do what its name implies. It will redirect the user to a different page. For that, you have to pass a path to redirect, and that of course should be the path of the page you wanna redirect the user to. So for example, to /meals. With that added, if we reload our page here maybe and browse all those meals that we got there, and I then add a new meal. And here I'll just quickly enter some dummy data to save some time. I'll use the same image though because we'll use the title as a slug for the image name on the Server anyways, so we won't override the old image. But if I do all that and I then click Share Meal, initially, still nothing happens. We'll take care of that soon. But after a short while, we are redirected and I see my new meal here, so that all works.

41737268.title: Managing the Form Submission Status with useFormStatus
41737268.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737268.transcript: So now that we're able to save meals, it's time to further enhance the user experience here. And for example, as you saw before, it takes quite some time until we are redirected after adding our data. Now that will typically be a bit faster in production, but still, it would be nice to get some feedback as a user whilst the data is being submitted to see that everything's fine and a request is on its way. So it would be nice to, for example, update this button once we clicked it to tell us that the request is on its way. And to do that, we can use another hook provided by React. Yes, React, but again, a feature that really only works when using Next. For that, I'll go back to my share page here where I have that form. And here I want to conditionally update this button and for example, show the text submitting when the request is on its way. And we can achieve this by using another special hook called useFormStatus. And this hook must be imported, useFormStatus from react-dom, so not from React, not from NextJS, but from react-dom. And this then gives you a status object, which, for example, has a pending property, which is true if there is an ongoing request and false otherwise. However, if you add that hook, you'll see that you'll get an error because this hook requires a client component in order to work, which makes sense because it is about updating the client side UI based on any ongoing requests. Now therefore, we would have to add use client here and we could do that, wouldn't be a problem, everything would work. But we also might not want to turn this entire page into a client component just because we want to conditionally update this button. In addition, this hook here will actually only give us the status of a form if it's inside of that form for which it should give us the status. So it must be inside of this form here if we want to get the submission status of that form, and with inside this forum, I mean inside of some component that's inside of that forum. So it wouldn't work here anyways. And therefore, I will not add it here, and therefore remove it, and instead add a new component, a new component here in the meals folder in the components folder, let's say that could be named meals-form-submit, or anything like this. And I'll export a component function here, which I'll name MealsFormSubmit. In this file, I'll then import this useFormStatus hook, and I'll add that client directive, and then we can get our status here by calling useFormStatus like this. Now as mentioned, this status thing here is an object with various properties, and therefore, we can actually use object destructuring here to pull out that one property I am interested in here. And that's the pending property. Well and then meals-form-submit should return a button which says Share Meal. So what I had on this button here before as well, but actually it shouldn't always say that. Instead, I wanna output some conditional content here and check if pending is truthy, in which case I want to output the text Submitting, and otherwise I'll output the text Share Meal. In addition, I want to disable the button if we are submitting. So I'll set the disabled prop to pending, so that we do disable the button if the surrounding form is being submitted and we enable it, if that's not the case. And with that, we can go back to our form and replace this vanilla button here with our meals-form-submit button, this component we just worked on. With that done, if I reload this page, I can again enter some data here. Like this and pick the same image as before. And if I click Share Meal, you now see this button changes, and therefore we get a better feedback, and then after a while, we're redirected, and we see our new meal here. So that's better.

41737272.title: Adding Server-Side Input Validation
41737272.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737272.transcript: So, we're already providing better user feedback, but that's not all we should do here. One other thing you should typically do when handling user data and form submissions is validate the values you are getting. And at the moment, we're not performing any validation at all in our server action here. Now, we do have some validation, because in that form, I'm using those built-in special attributes. Those required props here, to be precise, to make sure that we can't submit an empty form. So if I press submit here, I'm getting some errors automatically by the browser. And that's a useful behavior, but it's not enough, because I can disable that by going through the DevTools and removing that. Now of course, many users won't know about that, but some do, and in that case, those users could submit invalid values to my backend, because now I would not get an error for this field anymore. And that's why client-side validation isn't enough. Instead, you should also validate on the server side. And here you can now, of course, set up some very complex validation rules. You can use a third-party package that helps you with that. I'll keep it fairly simple to not waste your time, but it's important to know that you should do that in your applications. So, here we could, for example, check if meal.title, once we trim it to remove excess whitespace on the left and right, we could check if that's then equal to an empty string, which would mean that it's an invalid value. We could also check if meal.title maybe even doesn't exist. So if it's false, so if it wasn't part of the submitted data at all. And therefore here, we would now make it into this if block, if we either don't have a title, or if we have one, but it's empty. And you should now probably do that for all those fields. And then over here I'll actually add a helper function, isInvalidText, could be a fitting name, where I expect to get some text and where I return the result of this check so that I return true if the text we got is either false, or it's an empty string after trimming it. And now I can use isInvalidText down here and check if meal.title is an invalid text or if meal.summary is an invalid text, and so on. We can now relatively easily do that for all those fields. So, also do it for the instructions, for the creator, and also for the creator email. And of course that should be meal.instructions, meal.creator, and meal.creator_email. And we also might wanna check whether that maybe exists, but is an invalid email address, so, I'll also check if meal.creator_email does not include an @ symbol by adding an exclamation mark here at the beginning. So, if the email does not include an @ symbol, we also make it into this if block. And last but not least, I want to check the image that we got. I wanna check if maybe we don't have an image. So, if it's undefined, or if that image here has a size that's equal to zero, which means it's some kind of invalid file. And in all those cases I wanna make it into this if block, and then do what? Well, then we could throw an error. So, we could throw an error where we say invalid input. And by doing that, if you save that, you ensure that if you reload here and you then enter values into all those fields except the email address, and we then get rid of the required attribute on that email field so that we can submit it, if we do all that, UCI gets this error page. Now, the error message here is a bit misleading, because it's that main error page which we set up earlier. We should probably set up a specific error page for sharing a meal. So, I will copy that here, paste it here, and say, "Failed to create meal." That sounds better. But the important thing is that we got some error at all. However, that's just one way of handling this. Throwing an error as we do it here works, but it also means that we destroy the entire input of the user. So, we throw away everything they entered. And that's not necessarily a great user experience. It would be better if we would stay on this page and just output some error message somewhere on this page, above the form or below the form, for example. And that's therefore what we'll implement next.

41737274.title: Working with Server Action Responses & useFormState
41737274.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737274.transcript: So how can we handle validation errors like this in a more elegant way? Well, in Server Actions, as we have it here, you are not limited to redirecting or throwing errors. Instead, you can also return values. You can return response objects to be precise and I'll show you how to access those objects in your components in just a second. So here we could return an object which maybe has a message field which holds a value of invalid input. Though the shape of this object is totally up to you, it's just important that it's a serializable object, which means it, for example, shouldn't include any methods because those would get lost whilst being sent to the client. But any simple values like strings, numbers, nested objects or nested arrays, those values all work. So that's how we can return a response. How can we then use that response in that share page where we trigger that Server Action? Well, we can use it with help of another hook, another hook provided by React dom, the useFormState hook. So not useFormStatus, which we had before, but useFormState. Sounds similar but is a different hook. This hook also must be imported from React dom like this. And then this hook here works a little bit like the useState hook that's built into React because this hook is responsible for managing the state of this page or of this component, which uses a forum that will be submitted with help of Server Actions. And that's precisely the use case where useFormState can help you because useFormState needs two arguments. And the first argument is the actual Server Action that should be triggered when the form is submitted, in this case shareMeal. The second argument you pass to useFormState is the initial state of this component, and that simply means the initial value that should be returned by useFormState before this action has been triggered and yielded a response. So the initial value that should be used if we haven't received a response from this action yet, and that could be anything you want, for example, null or an object where you have a message field that holds a value of null. To reassemble the shape of that response, we will eventually send back. Eventually I'll send back an object with this shape where the message holds a string. So our initial value here could be the same shape but null as a value for the message. Then useFormState will give you an array with exactly two elements, which might sound familiar because the default useState hook provided by React also gives you two elements. And indeed here the two elements we get here are kind of similar to the two elements we get from useState because we get the current state, the current response you could say, of this page here, of this component here. So the latest response returned by this Server Action in the end or this initial state if no response has been received yet. And we get another formAction here, which we should actually set as a value for this action prop on the form now. So instead of setting share meal as a value for action down here, I'm now setting this formAction, which I'm getting back from useFormState as a value for the action down there. And this must be done so that useFormState can basically step in and manage that state for this component. And that state depends on the execution of that Server Action and its response. And that's why useFormState kind of needs to act as a man in the middle, you could say. With that, we can then use this state here, which will essentially be either this year or any response we got back from ShareMeal to output data in this component. Now, in this case, I know that state will therefore either be an object with a message that's a string or it'll be an object with a message that's null. So we can use it down here at the end of the form right before the submit button, let's say, to output an error message. For that, we can check if state.message is truthy, which means we have a message. And in that case I wanna output it here between my paragraph tags. Now with that, we also need to tweak the shareMeal action though, because when passing it as a value to useFormState, it must have a different shape than it did before. It should no longer just accept that form data. Instead, it should now actually accept two parameters because useFormState will pass two parameters to shareMeal when it executes it when the form gets submitted. The second parameter will still be that submitted data, but the first parameter will be the previous state. So either that initial state that we set up here or any other previous responses that might have been generated, I don't care about it here, but still we need to accept it because form data is now the second argument we get, not the first. With that though, we got everything set up that needs to be set up, but we'll now get an error if we try to load the page because useFormState, since it in the end again deals with updating the client, needs to be executed in a client component and therefore we should add use client here in our shareMeal page component file. We could again try to outsource this into some nested component, but here I'll stick to this component, add use client and save that. And with that, this is looking good. We got no error and therefore now we can try submitting an invalid form again. So here, I'll enter some data like this and if I now inspect my email field and I remove the required prop here and I click share meal, I now get this error message. Now of course we could make it pop more. We could make sure that it stands out, that it's more descriptive and you can do all these things. You can, for example, tweak that message here, which in the end is the message that's showing up on the screen. But the main takeaway is that this is how you can show such an error message, that you can return responses in your Server Actions and that you can get and handle them with help of the useFormState hook.

41737276.title: Building For Production & Understanding NextJS Caching
41737276.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737276.transcript: So sharing meals works now, but there actually is one problem with our application. Everything works fine here during development when using that development server, but things will change once we move to production. Now, to move from development to production, we should quit that development server here and then run another script in the terminal, navigate it into our project folder still. And that's the NPM Run build script. This will build and prepare our NextJS application for production. It will essentially give us a project that we could then deploy onto a server. Now you can run that production server and that optimized code by then running NPM start. So first NPM run build, and then thereafter NPM start. This will start a server, but now not some development server, but instead the production server, which here is still served under the same address, but now it's serving that optimized code and so on. Now therefore you can revisit local host 3000 and you should, for example, still see your meals there, but you'll now notice something strange when you add a new meal. For that, I'll again, going to enter some data and I'll name my meal, tasty Bruschetta, Very delicious. Add my instructions like this and pick the same image as always. If I now click share meal, I'm redirected. And this is now quicker, as I mentioned before. That's great, it's quicker, better user experience, but the meal is missing here. I see the three meals I submitted before, but this new meal is missing. Keep in mind that I added a text of very delicious to it and I don't see that text anywhere here. And there's one other thing that's pretty strange here on this site. If I go back to the starting page for example, and I reload and I then go to browse meals, I'm there pretty much instantly. If I'm on this page and I reload, it also reloads pretty much instantly. You might recall that before, this took five seconds because I added this extra delay here in the meals.js file, this delay. Why is this missing here in production and why is this meal we added missing? Well, because as mentioned earlier in this section, NextJS performs some pretty aggressive caching and there is one extra step it performs and goes through when preparing the app for production that we didn't see when testing it with the development server. Because when you run this NPM run build command to prepare the app for production, NextJS goes ahead and actually generates, pre-renders all the pages of your app that can be pre-generated, so basically all non-dynamic pages. So it, for example, pre-generated the meals page. So this page.js file in the meals folder, this page here, which outputs some meals, it was pre-generated, so all that data was fetched and rendered there during the build process. And NextJS is doing that because by pre-rendering all these pages, it of course has those pages available right from the start after being deployed. And so the very first visitor of your website is able to instantly see the finished page without having to wait for anything to render, which of course can be a great user experience. And NextJS then caches those pre-rendered pages so that it's able to serve them to all visitors. But the downside of this approach, of course, is that it never re-fetches the meals because this code here never executes again. Instead, it just uses those pre-generated pages. And I can prove that we can simply console log, fetching meals here in that meals component that's being used by the meals page. And if I do that and I then rebuild my project by running NPM run build again, you will see that it generates all those pages and then once it's done, if I start that production server, if I reload the meals page, I don't see fetching meals here. I get some warning that we might want to install some extra package when using that image component, but we can ignore that here. The main thing is that we don't see this message being logged, so that is not executing anymore because those pages were pre-generated and cached. But that's of course a problem if the data that's visible on that page changes as is the case for me here. Now you can see that latest meal I added earlier now because I regenerated all the pages, but if I would add another new meal here, it would again be missing. And that's of course absolutely not the behavior we want here.

41737280.title: Triggering Cache Revalidations
41737280.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737280.transcript: So how can we fix this problem of NextJS caching too aggressively? Well, we need to tell NextJS to throw away its cache or parts of its cache whenever we add a new meal. And of course, this is such a common requirement that there's a built-in method for that, a built-in function provided by NextJS, a function which I wanna execute in my server action right after saving a meal before I redirect. And that function is called revalidatePath. This function tells NextJS to revalidate the cache that belongs to a certain route path. So for example, if I know that I want to visit the meals page and that the meals page depends on data that changed now, I can tell NextJS to revalidate the /meals path. And with that, that path would be revalidated. Now what's important is that, by default, only that path will be revalidated, no nested paths. So if I had some nested path there in my meals folder, that also depends on all the meals data, I would have to revalidate that separately. Alternatively, you can pass a second argument to revalidate path and set this to layout. The default is page, which means that simply this one page for this one path will be revalidated. If you set it to layout, it's the layout that will be revalidated, which as you learned, also wraps nested pages, and therefore, with this, all nested pages would be revalidated as well. And revalidate simply means that NextJS throws away the cache that is associated with those pages. So, for example, the cached pages themselves. Now here, I don't care about any nested pages because the dynamic page here isn't pre-generated anyways in our current setup and the share page doesn't depend on the meals data. So I'm fine with just revalidating /meals. If you would want to revalidate all the pages of your entire website, you could, by the way, do that by targeting slash and then setting the mode to layout. But here, I'm fine with just revalidating the meals path. Well, and with that done, if we now run npm run build again to build all those pages again, they will still be pre-generated and cached, but now that cache should be revalidated and partially cleared once we added a new meal. So if I now run npm start again to start that development server, and I then add another new meal, like this, picking the same image as always, if I submit that, it takes a bit longer here, which is a good sign because it's taking longer because it loaded all those meals again. And here we are, now the image is missing, we'll take care of that in just a second. But the meal is there and that's of course a good sign. In addition, we got these Fetching meals logs back here, which also proves that this is now working as intended.

41737282.title: Don't Store Files Locally On The Filesystem!
41737282.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737282.transcript: So now that we understood how caching worked and we fixed that problem related to it, let's focus on that missing image. Why was that image missing when we added a new meal whilst running this production server? Well, the image was missing because we store those images in this public folder and they're in the images folder. The problem with this approach just is that this folder is available during development, but for production, it's actually copied into this .next folder and it's this .next folder, which also contains all those cached pages and so on, hat will be used by the running NextJS production server. If you then thereafter add new images into the public folder, those will be ignored because the production server doesn't care. So when you deploy your next project, you therefore technically don't even have to deploy the public folder. NextJS is not interested in it. Now, that's, of course, a problem for our app here because we do store images in that public folder. Well, this behavior is described in the official NextJS documentation and there they therefore recommend that you should store any files that are generated at run time, essentially, using extra file storage services like AWS S3. Now, since working with a provider like S3 is now really a bit beyond the scope of this course, we'll ignore this problem here. However, after this lecture, in the next lecture, you'll find a text lecture where I do describe how you could use AWS S3 for storing and serving your user-generated images. You don't have to implement that. You can ignore it for this demo, but if you're interested in learning how that would work, that next lecture is for you.

41737290.title: Adding Static Metadata
41737290.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737290.transcript: All that, we are now finally pretty much done with this application here. Now I will quickly fix a styling error, which I only just detected, which has been fixed in the attachments I provided to you though, so you already got the right styles earlier, but here, I'll fix it by moving position relative in the meal-item.module CSS file from this meal header selector to the image selector. Again, I did already fix that in the attachments I provided to you, so you don't have to do anything and you got the right styles all the time already. But now here for me, those meals also look a bit better, but that's just one thing. The more important last step I want to go through together with you is the page metadata that you can add to your pages. And you can see that in the root layout.JS file, there we have a metadata constant that's being exported and that's not some random constant, but instead, NextJS is looking for exported variables or constants called metadata in all your page or layout files. And then in this metadata object, you can set some metadata fields, which is also described in the official documentation. There, you can find details about all the fields you can set on this metadata object and attached you find a link to this documentation so that you can explore in depth which fields can be set there. In short, this metadata constant allows you to add a bunch of metadata, which is then, for example, exposed to search engine crawlers or which shows up when you are sharing a link to a page on X or Facebook. Now here, I'll keep it fairly simple, but I wanna add a title and description to every page. Now, if you add this metadata to a layout, it will automatically be added to all the pages that are wrapped by that layout unless a page specifies its own metadata. In that case, the page metadata wins. Any nested layout metadata would also win over that root layout metadata, but by setting this metadata here, every page will at least have this basic metadata. But now of course, I wanna set more specific metadata for sum of my pages. For example, for that all meals page. There, it would be nice to have some metadata and we can therefore add it by simply exporting this metadata constant there as well. And then here we could set the title to all meals, and then maybe add a description of browse the delicious meals shared by our vibrant community, something like this. If you do that and you go to that meals page, you'll see that if you reload, you have this all meals title here. And if you take a look at the page source, you will also find this description meta tag in there, which contains your description text as content. Here it is. So that's how you can add metadata, but how can you add metadata to dynamic pages where the title and the description depends on the concrete data that has been loaded? Adding static metadata like this typically doesn't do the trick there.

41737292.title: Adding Dynamic Metadata
41737292.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737292.transcript: For dynamic pages, you can add metadata by not exporting a constant or variable named metadata, but by instead exporting an async function called generateMetadata. And it must be called like this because NextJS is also looking for functions like this. If it doesn't find any other metadata, it's checking whether there is such a function, and if there is such a function, NextJS will execute it for you and then it's your job to return such a metadata object in that function. Now how does that help us with dynamic data? Well, this function receives the same data our page component receives as props. So here we also get an object with a params key, and that of course allows us to get the meal for which the metadata should be generated by simply accessing mealSlug and passing that to getMeal. Well, and with that, we can then return a title that's set equal to meal title and a description that's set to meal.summary, for example, and that's how we can generate the metadata for a dynamic page. With that, if we now visit a meal, we can see its title, its name represented here in the tab title. Now if we enter some invalid slug here, we now get an error though instead of the not found page because the metadata is actually generated first and this now simply fails. When I try to access title on meal, that fails because meal is undefined. So therefore we should actually add this if check here, where we check whether the meal is maybe not defined, and called the notFound function if it isn't, inside of generate metadata to ensure that we show the notFound page if generating the metadata fails, which it does if we don't find that meal. With that, you see that now we get that meal not found page again, but that's how we can handle that metadata.

41737296.title: Module Summary
41737296.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js
41737296.transcript: And that's now it for this section. In this section, we had a in-depth look into NextJS using this App Router and you learned how you can set up routes by using the file system instead of app and by using these special file names like page.js and layout.js. You also learned about other special files like error.js for handling errors, or not-found.js for handling not found errors, or the loading js file, which I'm not using here, but which we used temporarily to show some loading state on the screen. Though you learned that you have more granular control by using suspense, which is what we then did. Now you also learned how to configure dynamic routes where some path segments aren't known in advance, like here, where we load pages for individual meals, where we don't know in advance how many meals we'll have and most importantly, you learned that all those pages in the end just export standard React components, but that those components are kind of special when using NextJS. That they are server components that are executed on the server, that are rendered on the server, therefore. And it's not just those page components, but instead all components unless you explicitly opt out of this behavior by adding this use client directive to convert a file and its components into a client component, which then is rendered on the client and which then therefore may use client side functionality like useState or handling user events. You therefore also learned that you can take advantage of those Server components by fetching data directly inside of those components. There is no need to use user effect and send requests to separate backends. Instead, you can fetch the data from right inside your Server components. And you can just fetch the data, you also learned about Server Actions, async functions that either have to use Server directive inside of them or that are stored in a file with used Server at the top. And the special thing about those Server Actions is that you can assign them as values to the action prop on form elements, either directly or as we're doing it here with help of useFormState, which is a hook provided by react-dom to handle responses returned by the Server action function and then potentially update the UI based on those responses. Like here where we are conditionally showing an error message. Speaking of updating the UI based on Server Actions, you also learned about the useFormStatus hook, which can be used to find out whether a form is currently being submitted or not, which can then be used to update the UI accordingly. Now, related to all of that, you also learned that NextJS does some pretty aggressive caching and that therefore you should call revalidatePath whenever you change some data to make sure that the latest data is fetched and represented on your pages. And you also saw that it can be dangerous if you never test your app in production mode because everything worked in development mode in our app here, but then suddenly the data was missing in production mode. So this is an important function and an important concept to understand in general. Now, last but not least, we also talked about metadata that can be added to pages, static metadata as we have it here or dynamically generated metadata. And therefore at this point you have a super solid NextJS foundation and you are ready to use NextJS with its App Router in your future projects.

41999870.title: Creating a NextJS Project
41999870.section: A (Pretty Deep Dive) Introduction to Next.js

And we do that in a slightly different way than we did it for standard React projects because we need a project that comes with NextJS pre-installed and that has a certain structure and setup that's needed by NextJS. And there, you should somewhere see a command like this, npx create-next-app, which can be executed to create a new Next project. And it's therefore this command which I will execute. It is enough if you replace your app folder and your public folder since I also added an image in there. As well as your eslintrc.json fall since I added one rule in there with mine. The other files are the same. And with that, you can then go into your terminal in that project. 44245110.title: Module Introduction
44245110.transcript: In the previous course section, you learned in detail how you can handle form submissions and user input in React apps. In this section, we're kind of building up on what you learned there, and we'll explore a different way of handling user input. Because in this section, we'll explore form actions, a feature that's actually built into React, at least if you're using React 19 or higher. So it's a relatively new feature. It's not part of older React versions, but if you are working in a project that uses at least version 19 or higher, you can use this form actions feature, about which you will learn in this section here, instead of handling form submissions manually as you learned it in the previous section. Though, as a React developer, you should know about both approaches because you will encounter both approaches out there in the wild. Now, in this section, as mentioned, I'll introduce you to this form actions feature. I'll explain how you can use it and what it's all about, and you will learn how you can use this React feature to extract values and manage form state. You will learn how to set up and deal with synchronous and asynchronous actions, and you will learn how you can implement optimistic updating into your user interface with help of this feature and with help of React. Now in this section, we'll use a brand new starting project, which you'll find attached, of course. And this starting project is very similar to the project you saw in the previous section. It's just a slightly simplified version if you want to call it like this, because here I just have this signup component with that signup form you already know though. But I got no login form because you already know how you can handle forms in general. But in this section you'll now learn about an alternative because you'll learn about form actions. Now in this starting project, if you're using the local version, you of course should download it and then run npm install to install all dependencies, and then run npm run dev to start that development server. And if you then visit local host 5173, you should see a page like this on the screen.

44245850.title: What are Form Actions?
44245850.transcript: So what's this Form Actions feature all about? Well, as mentioned, it's available in React version 19 or higher. So if you're working in a React project that uses an older version of React, you can't use this feature. So you should check your package.json file to verify that you're at least using version 19. And if you are using such a version, you can simply use a new feature kind of built into React that helps you with handling form submissions. Because in the previous section you learned that you can, of course, handle form submissions by adding this onSubmit prop to the form element. And then you can point at a function that should be executed when that form is submitted. So here we could add a new function handleSubmit, and then use that as a value for onSubmit. And in that function, as you learned, you would get an event object automatically, and you would have to call event.preventDefault() to prevent that browser default, which otherwise would be to send an HTTP request, and we don't want that here. Now, just to be very clear, you can absolutely still use that approach. There is nothing wrong with it. And indeed you'll see this approach being used in many React projects out there, no matter which version they use. You can use this approach, but if you are using React version 19 or higher, you can also set this function handleSubmit as a value for the action prop on the form element, and that's new. Now, this action prop is not really a new prop. Form elements support the action prop or the action attribute no matter if you are using React or not. Well, the prop is supported if you're using React because props are a React feature. But even in non-React projects, you could set a action attribute on the form element. That's what I mean. And normally when not using React, this action attribute here would be used to define the path or the URL to which the browser sends the form data when the form is submitted. So that would be the default behavior which we're preventing by calling event.preventDefault(). Now, in React, when adding the action prop to a form element, this is not what will happen. Instead, React kind of overrides this attribute, this prop, and React will make sure that this function, which you pass as a value to action, will be executed when the form is submitted. So React will do that for you and React will behind the scenes call preventDefault for you so that that browser default is suppressed and is not happening, and that instead this function is executed. Now, this function then, however, will no longer get an event object. Instead, that function when used as a value for the action prop will get a formData object. The same kind of formData object you could create manually based on a form as we did it in the previous section. There I showed you that you can construct a formData object based on the event target, which was the submitted form. Now, when using this form actions feature, this formData object is created automatically for you by React. And this formData object then will contain all the submitted data, so all the submitted input values in that form. For that it's just important that you add the name prop to all your input elements because that name prop sets the key, which is then used in the formData to extract the values for the different input fields. So you must add the name prop to your inputs for that inputs value to be included in that formData object. But we're already doing that here in this form. And then here in handleSubmit we can, for example, get the entered email by accessing formData and calling the get method that's built into this formData object. And then we extract the value for the key email. And I'm using the key email here because I used email as a name down there. So that's how this is related. So that then gives me the entered email.
