44245874.title: Managing Form-dependent State with useActionState()
44245874.transcript: So how can we now use this errors array here to show error messages to the user when they submit an invalid form when using this React form action's feature? Well, in this action function we could check if our errors array has a length that is greater than zero. So if we have at least one error. And if that's the case, we could return an object, let's say, though you can return anything in such a action function. You don't have to return anything at all actually, but if you do want to return something, it can be anything. And here it's an object, an object where I'll set the errors key, let's say, to my errors array. But that key name is totally up to you because you can, as mentioned, return any value of your choice, or nothing at all. And therefore, if you return an object, the shape of that object is also totally up to you. But here I wanna return an object that has an errors key and the value for that errors key is my errors array. And since I have the same names here, I can use this JavaScript shortcut to create this object with this key value pair like this. Now, if we make it past this if check, so if we have no errors, I therefore wanna return an object where errors is set to null so that I have some indicator whether we have errors or not. If it's null, we have no errors. If it's an array, we have errors at least one. But how can we use this return value when using this form action's feature? Because this action function is passed as a value to this action prop, how do we now get hold of that value that's returned by that action function? Well, for that, React gives us a special hook that we can use, a special hook that must be imported from React, a special hook that's called useActionState. And that's a new hook that was added with React version 19. So if you're using an older version, you can't use this hook, but that shouldn't be a problem because you also can't set a function as a value for the action prop there. So I have this useActionState hook, how do we now use it? Well, like all hooks, you must use it in your component or inside of some other hook. And here I'll use it after this signupAction function, which I collapsed here so that it takes up less space. So useActionState is called here after this action function, and I'm calling it after this action function because this action function now actually must be passed as a first argument to this useActionState hook. So this useActionState hook wants a action function as a first input value, but that's not all useActionState wants. Instead, as its name implies, this hook aims to manage some form-related state for you or some action-related state. But actions of course are related to forms, so in the end, it's some form-related state that's being managed here. And therefore this useActionState hook actually also wants a second value where you provide an initial state value. Because this action function may then return an updated state, this object which we return here, but if the form has never been submitted, of course this action function will have never executed, and therefore no value will have been returned at any point of time. That's why you also must provide an initial state for this hook, which will be active if the action has not been executed yet. And in my case, that is an object where I'll set the errors key to null because initially if the form hasn't been submitted, I don't want to show any errors to the user because they didn't even have a chance of inserting any values. So that's the initial state here. Now, useActionState does not just take input values, but it also returns something, and it does return an array and therefore we can use array destructuring here to store the different array elements in different variables or constants. So just like the useState hook, which you already know, useActionState also returns an array and it does return an array with three elements. And the first element it returns is the current formState. So initially, this initial state here. But then once the action has been executed, the state that was returned by that action. So those return values here, that's then what will be stored in this formState constant or variable. And that's how we can get access to these return values because formState will eventually contain them. But that's not the only thing we get in this array that's returned by useActionState. Instead as a second element in that array, we get an updated formAction. Now we pass an action as a first argument to useActionState and internally React then kind of creates a new function that's wrapped around our action. And it does that so that it can listen to the invocation of this action, you could say. That's why we get back and updated formAction, which in the end is our formAction, but enhanced by React, you could say. So this formAction which we get back is our formAction with extra features or with React being aware of it if you want to call it like this. And it's there for now. This formAction which we get back from useActionState, which should be set as a value for this action prop here. So this action is set as a value here for the action prop. Well, and the last element we get back here is a pending element, which is true or false depending on whether the form is currently being submitted or not. Now in this case, this doesn't make much sense because this action executes pretty much instantly anyways, so it won't be pending for long. But later in this section when we explore async actions, that will change. But for the moment, I don't need that pending value. But I do want to work with that formState now because as mentioned, eventually it may contain the error messages which we pass back here if we have errors. And therefore we can use it here in our form, maybe at the bottom of that form, let's say right above these buttons to conditionally output some error messages. So here I'll check if formState.errors is truthy, so if it's not null, which it is initially and which it is if no errors were found. But if it's not null, so if it contains this errors array, then I wanna output something down there. I wanna output an unordered list with a class name of errors for styling purposes. And in that unordered list, I'll go through my errors and map every error object to a list item where the key can be the error message, and where I then also wanna output that error or message. And that should make sure that error messages are displayed if we got some. Now with that, if we save that, if we go back to our form here and I click sign up without entering anything, I get an error that formData.get is not a function. And I get this error now because when using this useActionState hook, this formAction function, which we pass to useActionState, so this signupAction function in our case is called in a different way than it was called before. Previously without using useActionState, our action function was called and the formData was passed as the first and only input value to this action function by React. But when passing signupAction to the useActionState, this action function will be called differently. And formData will actually be the second input value, the second parameter, because the first parameter will be the previous form state, so the old form state, because of course it's possible that this action is invoked multiple times. And in that case, React gives you the last form state it was aware of as an input value in case you wanted to base your new state on that old state. And the first time this is being executed, the previous form state will simply be that initial state that's being passed to it. Now here, I don't really need that previous form state, but nonetheless we need to accept it and extract formData as a second parameter since we'll get an error otherwise. But with that done, if we now save this and go back, if I now click sign up here, you see now I get these errors down there. And the styling is a bit off because in my form, the class here should be error, not errors. But with that done, this now looks better. So that works. But we currently have a problem. If I enter a valid email address, for example, and I then click sign up, the overall form of course is invalid. And that's why I'm getting some errors though I'm not getting the email error message anymore because that was a valid value. But as you can see, the field here was cleared because every time you submit a form, when using this React form action's feature, React resets the form for you in the end. And that's of course not what I want here. If I have some invalid values, I definitely don't wanna throw the valid values away. Or in general, I don't want to throw the values the user entered away. That's not a good user experience. So that's the next thing we'll have to fix here.

44245880.title: Using User Input
44245880.transcript: So at the moment, all entered values are thrown away if an invalid form is submitted. And that's of course not a good user experience. Now, to make sure that this is not happening, we have to adjust our signup action a little bit. And there we should make sure that the state that we return does not just consist of this errors information, but that it actually also contains some information about the entered values. So the values that have been entered by the user, because we can then use that information that's returned in that state to pre-populate these input fields again, to make sure that the entered values aren't lost there. And therefore here I will actually add an entered values property to my returned state object. Though of course this doesn't have to be named entered values, the name is up to you instead. But here I will name it enteredValues, and the value will be a nested object where I want to have all the values I extracted up here. So I will simply pass email, password, confirm password, first name, last name, role, acquisition channel, and terms to this entered values nested object. And with that done, we can go down to our form and then for example, go to that email field and set the default value prop on that input element. Not the value prop, but the default value prop because that in the end will define the value to which this input will be set when the form is reset. And as mentioned, React does reset the form whenever the action here is executed. And that's why I want to change the value to which this input here is reset. And I wanna reset it to formState.enteredValues.email so that the email address that has been entered by the user, which I do return in my updated state here, is repopulated into this input field. Now, it is possible that enteredValues is not defined because for example, it's not part of my initial state. There I only have the errors key, no entered values key. And the same is the case if I did submit a valid form. So therefore, we should check if entered values exists by adding a question mark here so that JavaScript only tries to access this email property if this is a defined object. Otherwise undefined will be set as a default value and that will basically set this input to an empty input field. Now I'll migrate the other inputs soon. First, let's check whether this works as intended for this email input field here. So with that added, if we save this, if I reload and I enter some email here like this, and I click sign up, you see I get these error messages, but now my email input field did not lose its value. So that's working the way it should. And therefore we can now use the same approach for all the other inputs. Here for the password input, I want to set it to enteredValues?.password} For confirm password, it's enteredValues?.confirmPassword} For the first name, unsurprisingly, it's .firstName} And for the last name it is .lastName} Now for the role, we must set the default value on that select element because that will then select the right option. And here it's .role} For the acquisition channels, here it's a little bit different. There on every input we should add defaultChecked, and then set this to {formState.enteredValues? acquisitionChannel.includes('google')} So if the acquisition channel array includes the word Google, this Google checkbox should be checked. And here for friend, I want to check for the value friend. And for the other input I want to check for other here, like this. And here for the terms check box, it's also the defaultChecked prop that should be added. And I want to check if {formState.enteredValues?.} if that's set, if terms is set to true. So this will be checked if terms is true or a truthy value in general. And that should make sure that all the entered values are kept and aren't lost just because some invalid value has been entered. With that, if you reload, if I enter, let's say a password and a first name and I check this Referred by a friend box and I click Sign up, you see these values are all kept, but I'm also getting some error messages because well, some fields are invalid. Now let me switch to a different role here and all also check the Terms check box and Sign up. And we got less error messages. Now, one thing you might notice is that this dropdown did actually not keep its value. And to me that seems to be a bug with React here because I am setting the default value on that select element correctly here. But nonetheless, it looks like it always is reset back to its first option instead of the default value that's set up here. So that at the moment, to me, seems to be a problem with the select element default value and how use action state works. Because at the moment there also is no way of disabling this resetting that's performed by form actions automatically. So at the moment, you have to then manage the form state with use action state and set the values of the inputs manually if you want to keep them. And therefore at the moment, I am not aware of a way that would allow us to keep the value for this select element here, but for all the other inputs, it works as expected. I also want to highlight that this reset button now also works differently. If you press it nothing seems to change, the form isn't reset. But actually it is, as you can tell if you uncheck this Terms box, for example, and you then click Reset, you see it's checked again. And the reason for this behavior is that reset resets all these inputs to their default values, but now I changed those default values. I changed those default values to make sure that the entered values are kept around. So that's worth noting. If you wanted to empty all those input fields when the reset button is clicked, you would have to add some custom logic that does so. So that does target all these input fields and sets them to some empty values. So now with that, we're able to validate these inputs and we're able to show errors and keep the user input. Now, of course, if the user enters valid values into all these fields, so if I quickly do that here. If I select valid values and I submit this, then the form is reset again. Because then in that case, we of course make it through all these checks and we don't have any errors. So we make it past this safe check, and we then return this state object, which doesn't contain the entered values key anymore. And therefore all the input fields are set back to empty values because there are no values defined for the entered values key. And that's indeed the intended behavior. If it weren't, you simply would have to add entered values here and set your desired input values. But here I wanna reset them all to being empty because when we submit the form without errors, we are good to go, so to say. We would then typically take the entered values, for example, send them to a backend and either navigate away from the page or clear the input fields as we're doing it here. And that's therefore now how we can use form actions also in conjunction with this use action state hook to manage form submissions, input validation, and the values that have been entered by a user.

44245896.title: Moving the Action Function out of the Component
44245896.transcript: So now at this point you learned the basics of form actions. You learned how you can set up and use a synchronous form action. So a form action that does not use async await or return a promise, and you learned how to work with the useActionState hook. Now there is more, and we will move on to a more advanced example in just a second, but before we do that, there's one other important thing you should know about form actions. You don't have to create these action functions inside of a component function. You can, as you see here, but you don't have to. If in this action function you are not using any component specific data, so if you're not using props or some state in here, you can absolutely take this function and move it out of this component function. So that's what I did here now. I grabbed this signupAction function and I moved it right below my imports outside of the component function so that the component function is leaner. And of course, you could also store this action function in a totally different file if you wanted to do that. It doesn't have to be in the same file as the component. And especially moving it out of the component function can make sense from a performance perspective because if the action function is inside of the component, the action function will of course be recreated every time the component function executes. Now, this is not necessarily a big problem, but it could become a problem depending on the complexity of your application and how often this component function is being re-executed. And therefore moving the action function out of it if you don't need it to be inside of the component function can make sense. Of course, if you were using props or state in that action function, you would have to move it back into the component function so that you have access to those props or state. But here, that's not the case. So moving it out of there makes sense. And with that, we can now move on to the more advanced features.

44245908.title: Working with Asynchronous Form Actions
44245908.transcript: So now that we got our form action connected to the form with help of useActionState and we're handling errors, it's time to handle the success case, so to say. So it's time to submit the data to the backend if valid data has been entered. Now in this starting project, I already created a context object and provider for you. And here in this ContextProvider component, I am managing some opinions, opinions that are loaded from the backend. But I also have a addOpinion function, for example, where I send a request to the backend to store a NewOpinion, a post request. A post request, that in the end will be sent to this backend endpoint here. As you can see, there is an artificial delay here in that backend code so that we can also simulate a slow backend because that will become important later. But for the moment, it doesn't matter too much. I have this backend here and in there I have some code that will store the submitted data. So we have that addOpinion function in the context, and this function is made available to any component that's interested through this context value. Here, addOpinion is added as a method. So therefore in NewOpinion, in order to send that request, we have to get hold of that for context here. And we can do that in that component with help of the useContext() hook of course, or since this is a project that uses at least React version 19, we can use just the use() hook which has been added with React 19. This use() hook can also be used after being imported from React to access some context. In this case, my OpinionsContext, which also must be imported of course. So then I got this context value here, and here I'll use object destructuring to get hold of that addOpinion function that's provided through that context. And it's that function that should now be called here in this form action. So here, after checking for errors, if we have none, I'll call addOpinion. And to addOpinion, you should pass an object that contains a title property, a body property, and a username property. And conveniently, these are the properties or the values I'm getting from my form here. So we can simply pass this object here to addOpinion. Now, addOpinion, if you take a look at it here in the context, in the OpinionsContext, addOpinion is an async function. Because I'm using a wait here to wait for this Promise that's returned by fetch to resolve to its response. Therefore, what we might wanna do in our form action here as well is also await here so that we only clear the form after the data has been submitted to the backend. This will also become important later once we update the UI based on the current submission status. So I wanna pause this form action. I wanna wait for the result of calling a opinion before I mark this form action as completed by executing the last bit of code, because again, that will become important later. Now, in order to use await here, we of course have to add async in front of this function keyword. And therefore this now becomes an async form action. And the great thing is that this is something that's supported by React. Your form actions can be both synchronous or asynchronous functions. Both works and React doesn't care too much, though if it is an asynchronous function, React will actually wait for the Promise that is returned by this action function to resolve before it internally marks this form as submitted. And again, this will matter later. For now, we can just save it like this. And if you now go back and you enter your name and you add a title and your text, if you click submit, then you see after a short pause, the form is cleared and the UI is also updated. And my opinion is showing up here due to how this app works. And if I reload, my opinion is still there because it really was stored on the backend. And it does show up here on the frontend after being submitted, because in my context in addOpinion, once I got a response, I actually update the opinions that are shown in this app and add this newly added opinion at the front of this array, after I got back this response from the backend if it wasn't an error response. So that's working the way it should and that's of course great, but now it would be nice if when we submit the form, it wouldn't be stuck in this strange mode where it isn't really clear whether submitting worked or not. But if instead, we, for example, would disable this submit button whilst the form is being submitted also to make sure that it's not being submitted twice. And that's therefore what we'll do next.

44245918.title: Updating the UI with useFormStatus()
44245918.transcript: So we can submit forms, but at the moment, we are stuck for one second until the response from the backend is back when we click Submit, at least if we have a valid form. And as mentioned, it would be great if this Submit button, for example, would be disabled, and maybe the text on it would change whilst the form is being submitted. And we have two main ways of adding such a feature to this application. One way would be to use this pending value here, which we got back from useActionState as a third element in this array because that will be true if the form is currently being submitted and it will be false otherwise. And therefore we could use this information to update the UI. And that's also why it's important to await here because pending then will be true until this promise is resolved. React will wait for that. But I'll not use that approach here because I wanna show you another hook that's offered by React that helps us with situations like this. A new hook that's meant to be used in conjunction with formActions. And that new hook is a hook that's actually imported from react-dom, the useFormStatus hook. And this hook actually can't be used in the component that contains the form and the formAction. So we can't use it in this component, but instead it must be used in some nested component. So some component, and that's why I'm removing this import, some component that is used inside of this form. Therefore here, I'll add a new component in this component's folder and I'll name it Submit.jsx, or that's the file name I guess. The component function then is named Submit. So I'm exporting a new default function called Submit. And in this Submit.jsx file, I will now import this useFormStatus hook from react-dom. Now back in NewOpinion.jsx, I'll grab this paragraph here which contains my Submit button, and I'll cut it and remove it from this component therefore, and instead return that here in the Submit.jsx file in this Submit component because now here in this Submit component, we can and should now call this useFormStatus hook. And as a result, we'll get back an object that contains various pieces of information about the current form status, the current status of the form in which this component is being used. For example, we could get hold of the data that has been submitted. We could get hold of some other information as well. The official documentation lists all the information you can get out of that object. But probably the most common piece of data, the most common information you want to get access to is the pending property, which is true or false depending on whether the surrounding form is currently being submitted or not. And we can use this pending property to conditionally disable this button by setting the disabled prop to pending. So now if pending is true, if the form is being submitted, the button will be disabled. And we can also replace this text here and set it dynamically and check if pending is truthy. And if that's the case, I'll set the text to, "Submitting..." Otherwise, I'll set it to Submit. And now we got this reusable component that we could use in any form that uses formActions to output a button that adjusts to the current submission status. So now back in the NewOpinion component, we can use this Submit component like this, and of course, import it like always. And if you then use it here inside of this form element and you save this, you'll see that now if you reload, if I enter my name and a title and some text, if I click Submit, you see the button is disabled, the text changes. And then once the form is submitted, everything is reset. And I see my opinion here, and that's therefore now working the way it should. And that's how you can update the UI based on the current form status.

Instead, I just want these functions to be triggered. So therefore, upvoteAction is now set as a value for this first button for its formAction prompt. And then for the second button, which is responsible for downvoting for this button here, I'll set formAction to downvoteAction. With that done, if you save this and if you reload, if you open your developer tools and you open the JavaScript console there, if you click the upvote button, you see upvote being logged, and hopefully unsurprisingly for the downvote button, it's downvote. Well actually, for technical reasons, it would be a good idea to also wrap this request with try catch since we could run into some technical issue. And now back in the opinion component file, instead of logging upvote or downvote, we of course should make sure that these context functions are invoked. So again, as before, you should import use or useContext, one of these two hooks from React, so that you get hold of the context. So I'll call useOpinionContext, OpinionsContext to be precise. And then I'll destructure this context object to pull out the downvote and upvote opinion functions on which we just worked. So upvoteOpinion and downvoteOpinion are pulled out of the context. And then here in upvoteAction, I want to call upvoteOpinion. And in downvoteAction, it's downvoteOpinion. Now of course these functions yield promises. These are async functions. 44246076.title: Using the "pending" State from useActionState()
44246076.transcript: So how could we improve the user experience here? Well, for example, by again, disabling these buttons after they have been clicked. And I wanna disable both buttons if one of them has been clicked so that we have to wait for the vote to be registered before we can vote again. But here I'm now mostly interested in the actions and the pending states. So it's now the upvoteFormAction that's set as a value for the first button here. So this upvoteFormAction is set as a value for this first button. And downvoteFormAction is set as a value for the second button, like this. Well, and then we can use these pending states to disable these buttons. So the first button is disabled if upvote is pending, or if downvote is pending.
