44246084.title: Adding Optimistic Updating
44246084.transcript: This year is a great example for a place in the user interface where optimistic updating might be a good idea. Because when I press this button, we have to wait for a second until this vote number goes up. Now that's not horrible of course, but we can do better. And we can do better with help of another hook that's offered by React and that's related to this form Actions feature. We can use the use optimistic hook here. And use optimistic, as its name already kind of implies, is a hook that aims to help us with optimistic updating. Now how does it work? Well you call it here in this case, in this component where I have the piece of UI that should be updated optimistically. So the number of votes in my case. I call it here in the opinion component use optimistic. And then as a first input value, it needs the value that should eventually be updated optimistically. In my case here, the votes, which is a prop value in the end. So that's the first input value. It's the initial state of this optimistically managed state, you could say. Now use optimistic also takes a second parameter and that second parameter is a function that will in the end be invoked by React, but at a point of time defined by you. As you will soon see. Now this function will then receive a parameter automatically passed in by React. And the value it will receive will be the old state that's managed by use optimistic. So the state that was active before this function was invoked or that is still active at the point of time where it's invoked. In my case here, the previous votes. Because this function then has the job of returning a new state, a new value, so in my case, a new number of votes. That's why here I also expect a second argument in this function, which I'll name mode. And this is now a argument that is in the unset by me, as you will soon see. And here I expect mode to be a string that's either up if we're up-voting, in which case the new state I wanna return in this function is my previous number of votes plus one. Else if it's down or anything but up, I wanna return previous votes minus one. Now how does this mode parameter end up in this function though? It's definitely not managed by React because React doesn't know about which kind of values I care here. Well, use optimistic, like most hooks, returns a value. And the value it returns is an array. That's why I'm again using array destructuring here. And it's a little bit like they use state hook. Because the first value is my optimistic votes state, you could say. So it's my number of votes that's in the end this votes value initially and that's then managed with help of this function. The second element returned by use optimistic or the second element in this array I should say, is a function that we can call if we wanna invoke this optimistic updating function. So I'll name it, set votes optimistically, though the names of course are up to you. Now this function, set votes optimistically, can be called in any form action of your choice. That's the idea behind it. It should be called inside of a form action because use optimistic is meant to be used in conjunction with form actions. Because the state produced by use optimistic, my optimistic votes in this case, will be a temporary state, you could say, that's only shown on the UI whilst the form that invoked this optimistic function is being submitted. And thereafter this state will be thrown away and the actual UI state that is applied by some other code in your application will become active again. So it's just about having a temporary value whilst the form is being submitted. That's the idea behind this use Optimistic hook. But it's now this function which you can call to invoke this function, which you passed as a second argument to use optimistic. And any values, any arguments you pass to set votes optimistically will be passed as parameters to this state updating function here after this first parameter. So this first parameter here will always be passed in by React and it will always be the previous state, but any other parameters thereafter are simply the arguments you pass to this function. So if you pass no arguments to this function, you will get no extra arguments here. If you pass 10 arguments to this function, you will get 10 arguments here. So that's how this works. In my case, I plan on passing one argument to this function and that will be that mode, which should be a string that's either up or down. So where should this function be called then? Well in these form actions when the form is submitted, but before we call up mode or download opinion. So before we send that request and wait for its response. So it's here where I'll call set votes optimistically and pass up to it, and then download action I'll pass down to it. And that's all. This function doesn't return anything. Instead this optimistic votes here, this state will be updated automatically when we call this function. Because this function here will then execute and produce a new optimistic votes value. And then this component function will re-execute just as it does with use state. And therefore it's now the optimistic votes which we can and should use here in our JSX code in this span. Like this, and the idea now simply is that these votes will change instantly when this function is called. And then we sent the request thereafter. And the great thing about use optimistic is that it will understand in which action it's being invoked in the end. So that is meant to be called inside of form actions. Just to be clear, you should invoke this function from inside of form action. And use optimistic then registers that and waits for that action to complete. And once the action completed, it will get rid of this optimistic state and instead apply the regular UI state that would've been applied anyways. So the value that's produced by use optimistic is only shown whilst the form is being submitted. That's the idea behind use optimistic. It gives us a temporary value that's shown as long as the form is being submitted. But therefore with that logic added, if you save everything, if you reload and you up upload, you see this changes to a one instantly. And that's therefore now of course a way better user experience because this ensures that we now update the UI instantly, but behind the scenes the process takes a bit longer. Now, if the update were to fail because for example, in the backend code we had an error and to simulate that, I'll go here in my upvote endpoint in the backend code and I'll return a response here where I set the status code to 500 like this. So that I always return an error response here and I then stop that backend server and restart it so that this updated code is picked up. If I do that, you'll see that now when you upload it still updates instantly optimistically, but then it rolls back to the old value. Because again, use optimistic only gives you this temporary value. That's the idea. Now, with that, I'll get rid of this error in the backend because I don't want it, it was just a demo. And I'll restart my backend server so that everything works again. But that's now use optimistic in action. Another hook that can be quite useful when working with form actions.

44246086.title: Module Summary
44246086.transcript: Maximilian And that's now it for form actions. These are the core form actions related features React offers. And the key takeaway they offer is that you can define functions which you can pass as values for the action prompt to form elements, or as you learned to buttons, and then there the formAction prompt, and React will then make sure that these action functions are invoked and it will give you the form data automatically. So it will automatically collect all the input values and give you such a form data object. It will also automatically reset the form, which can be a problem. And that's why you also might want to use useActionState so that your form action can return a value, any value of your choice. Can be a object, as is the case here, but doesn't have to be. And then you can use that form state to update the UI to show some error messages, for example, but also to pre-populate, or repopulate those input fields with the values entered by the user, for example. And you can do anything you want in your form actions. You can store the code in local storage if you want to do that. But you can also, of course, use context or directly send a request from inside the form action if you want to, to send the data to a backend, because form actions can be async. They don't have to be, as you also saw in this section, but they can be. And if they are async and if they potentially take a bit longer, you got additional hooks like you useFormStatus, which you can use to update the UI whilst the form is being submitted. And you got useOptimistic to perform Optimistic updates. So to set some temporary state, some temporary value, which will automatically be thrown away once the form submission is over, so once the action function is done. You can use all these features thanks to this form actions feature that's built into React. Now when it comes to handling form submissions, it's therefore, of course, is up to you whether you want to use form actions or whether you want to handle submissions manually with help of the on submit prompt and by preventing the default and by using all these things you learned in the previous course section, because that's also absolutely valid. You don't have to use form actions, but of course, you can.

44246126.title: Module Introduction
44246126.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246126.transcript: Now, in the previous course section you learned a lot about next JS. And there you also learned a lot about features like React server components. In this section, we'll now revisit those features and kind of summarize these core concepts, which are actually part of React, but which are not usable in all React projects. Therefore, you can also take this section, if you did not complete the previous section, though, I would recommend going through that Next JS section first, but it's not a hard requirement. Now, in this section here, as mentioned, we'll take a look at some key React features that are part of React, but that are not usable in every React project. And I'll explain why that's the case. And we'll specifically take a closer look at React server components and client components, what these are and how you use them. We'll explore the idea behind server actions and compare them to form actions, about which you learned in a separate course section already. And we'll revisit React's Suspense component and how it relates to the use hook, which is a hook that was added with React 19. It's a hook I already introduced in the React context section, because you can use it to get access to context, but in this section you will learn that you can also use it to get access to data that's resolved by promises, at least in certain projects. So let's dive in.

44246130.title: Why We Need A Special Project Setup
44246130.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246130.transcript: So React has a lot of features and you learned about a lot of features throughout this course, of course. But React also has some features that are part of React, but that you can't use in every React project, as strange as that might sound. And specifically I'm talking about React Server Components, Server Actions, and the use hook when used in conjunction with Promises. These are features which can't just be used in every React project. Instead, these features require special project setups or special libraries that must be installed. They're not supported by standard Vanilla React projects like the ones you can create with Vite, though these are very popular projects, so therefore, indeed most React projects can't use these features. But you can thanks to this section in this course, and thanks to projects like Next.js, which give you a setup that do unlock these features. But why are they so hard to use in the first place? Certain React features like React server components and server actions can't be used in standard vanilla React projects because they actually involve code that needs to run on the server side, not just in the browser. And with standard React projects, you have just the browser side. So a project setup is needed that actually splits the code automatically kind of into two main packages, you could say. One package that's meant to never execute in the browser, and one package that is meant to run there. And then the project also must provide a server site environment for that code to run that shouldn't run in the browser. That's why a special setup is needed, because the code needs to be split because not all features work in the browser. Now, one project setup that does support these features would be a next.js project, like the one we used in the previous course section. Because next.js is a framework that builds up on React and that adds many features to React. But one feature it adds is that it does set up a project where the code is split, as I just described it, and where there is an environment for the server side code to execute. That's why attached to this lecture you find a new next.js project, a very simple one, which we'll use to dive into these core concepts here and to explore them. And that's also why it helps if you at least started with that next.js course section so that you understand the structure of such a next.js project. Though it isn't too difficult in the end, in next.js projects, you can set up page.js files to define different pages for your multi-page server rendered React project, because next.js is a full stack react framework and you can then use components in these pages to compose your user interface. You learn all the details about that in that previous section. In this section here, we'll actually only work on one page anyways. So you don't need to know about any advanced next.js features or anything like that. Once you downloaded this attached project, you simply have to run npm install to install all the dependencies, and you can then start the development server by running npm run dev. And with that out of the way, we're now ready to dive into these special React features if you want to call them like this.

44246140.title: Understanding React Server Components
44246140.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246140.transcript: So React has some features that are part of React and that are marked as stable that can't be used in every React project, and therefore you need special project setups like the one you get from Next.js, which does support running some code on the server and running some code on the client side. And, therefore, let's start by taking a closer look at this React Server Components feature, because that's a big one. That's an important React feature even though you might not be able to use it in all projects. But understanding React Server Components is important. And it's also important to understand the difference compared to Client Components, which is kind of the opposite kind of components you can build. And React Server Components, as the name kind of implies, are components that are never executed on the client side. So those component functions never execute on the client side. Indeed, the code of these components never makes it to the client side. Instead, these component functions are executed on a server or actually during the build process if they are pre-rendered, so to say, but they're never executed on the client side. Now, client components on the other hand are rendered on the client side, but not just there. They are instead also rendered on the server. They are pre-rendered there when a request hits the server, so to say, so that the finished HTML page is sent back as part of the response, but then thereafter they run again on the client side, and you get a single page application thereafter, so to say, where users can then interact with a client side React app and where you can therefore use client side features like the useState hook or something like that, which would not be possible in server components. But let's simply take a look at that in practice in this demo project, which I shared in the last lecture. Here in this project, I have a very simple Next.js application, which doesn't contain any components yet. Basically, it just has this page.js file, which is pretty empty, but now we'll add some components. And, therefore, I'll add a components folder here to the main project folder, and attached you then find two component files. You find an RSCDemo.js file attached, which you should store in your components folder. And you find a ClientDemo.js file attached, which you should also store in your components folder. Now, both component files contain regular React components. There's nothing special about them. And if you now go to this page.js file, which in the Next.js application in this app folder here is responsible for rendering a page. If you go there, you can use these components in there because the page.js file also includes just a regular component, just a component, which due to this special reserved file name will be picked up by Next.js and will be rendered to the page automatically. So in here we could now output the RSCDemo component, which is imported from that components folder. And this @ symbol here is a special import alias, which is supported in Next.js projects, which simply refers to the root project folder so that we import this from the RSCDemo file, which is in the components folder, which is in the root project folder. And then I output this RSCDemo component here. And if you do that and you reload the page that's served by that development server on localhost:3000, you should see this component on the screen. And what's interesting now is that if you open the developer tools here in your browser and you reload maybe, you never see this log message there in the console, so that's not logged in the browser. Instead, you can see it if you open the terminal or command prompt, which you used to start that development server because there it's being logged. So this log message here is showing up in this terminal here in this log. And messages that show up here are printed by the server. So they are output on the server side, so to say, and that proves that this component is rendered on the server. Now, you might say there's nothing special about this component. It looks like any other component from one of the many other sections you saw in this course, and you would be right. This is a regular React component. So what turns this component into a React Server Component then? And the answer is, as I mentioned before, the special project setup that is provided by Next.js. Next.js projects are set up such that all React components by default are treated as server components, which means they are never rendered on the client side. They are always rendered on the server side. And that's, for example, not the case when using a Vite-based project. There you get a client-side React application as a default instead. So that's why this is a server component. And, similarly, if you take a look at this client demo, this also actually at the moment still is a React Server Component because it's also just a regular React component. So if we output that here, maybe below the RSCDemo component, and you reload, you can see that component here, but you see no log here in the browser. And instead you see this message here in the server side log. So this is no client component yet. It's a server component because by default in Next projects, and actually in all projects that support server components, components are server components out of the box. And if you wanna change them to client components, you have to tell React and this project setup. And that's what we'll do next.

44246156.title: Changing Server to Client Components
44246156.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246156.transcript: So how can we now change this component from a server component to a client component? Well, by adding a special directive at the top of the file, and this directive is simply a string with opening and closing quotes where between the quotes you type use client. And it doesn't matter if it's double or single quotes, and you also don't need the semicolon. This directive, when added at the top of the file, will tell the project setup, you could say, that this code, this component, should be treated differently, that it should not be executed on the server, but that instead here we have some code that should be sent to the client and that should be executed on the client. And actually this wasn't even entirely correct, this will still be executed on the server, but not just there, it will also be executed on the client instead. So therefore, with this change made, if you now reload, you see ClientDemo being rendered here and it's rendered twice due to strict mode in this demo project. But you see that being rendered here and you also still see it here on the server side because again, it is rendered there and on the client side. So that's how client components work. But the question of course is why would you convert a component to a client component? Why would you not leave it as a server component? So what's the advantage of having a client component? Well, first let's take a look at the advantage of having a server component. And here are the advantage is that of course, less code has to be sent to the client because it's rendered on the server, hence, the client doesn't need to download all the code that's required to render this component. So that can improve the performance of our web application. In addition, as you will see later, we can even fetch data on the server and therefore make sure that this doesn't happen on the client side, which allows us to send finished pages that even include data that has to be fetched to the client on the initial request instead of performing data fetching in the browser, which adds an extra delay between the page load and when the data arrives. So that's another advantage we'll see a little bit later in this section. But why would you then convert a component to a client component? Well, the answer is relatively straightforward because you might need to use some client component feature in your component. Like for example, the useState() hook. If you need to manage state in one of your components, let's say a counter state, then you need to convert this component to a client component because state can only be managed in client components. And most React hooks can only be used in client components. Like for example, context, would be another client component feature. So if you, let's say, have a button in your component, which is used to increase this counter. So in there you call setCount and you update the count based on the previous count as you learned earlier in the course. And if you then maybe also have a span where the count is output. If you have code like this, and most importantly if you are managing state, therefore, then you must mark this component as a client component. And then with that you have this working button. So that would be a reason for a converting a component to a client component. Now here, I'll remove this code again, but I'll leave the state in here so that it's very clear why we need a client component. So that's why we have two kinds of components in modern React if you are in a project that supports it. We have server components which never execute on the client, but which therefore return the finished page to the client. And where you can fetch data and use async await as you will see later, and these components are entirely rendered on the server and the finished HTML code is then sent back to the client. But as mentioned, a special project setup is required in order to have them. Client components on the other hand, are executed on the server and the client so they can run on the client. And therefore, client side features like useState can be used. Now most React projects, like for example, projects created with Vite only support client components, at least at the point of time where I'm recording this. So you need a special project set up that supports server components. Client components on the other hand, are the kind of component you've always worked with over all these years because they are the default component type in most React projects. Now, if a project does support React server components, then client components are not the default anymore. And instead to convert a component to a client component, you have to market as such with the use client directive. That's what you should know about React server components and client components.

44246160.title: Combining Server and Client Components
44246160.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246160.transcript: Now when it comes to working with React server components and client components, there are certain rules you should be aware of when it comes to combining these two types of components. Because it's important to understand and keep in mind that React server components can directly include client components in their JSX code, but the other way around, it won't work. So client components can't directly include server components in their JSX code. With one exception, they can accept server components as children. So this here would be allowed. Here I'm rendering a server component, which then uses a client component inside of it. This, on the other hand, would not be allowed, rendering a client component which tries to render a server component. That won't work unless it's done with help of children. So if the client component tags are wrapped around the server component, this will work again. Now translated to our project here, this means that in the React server component, we can use this client demo component. We can import this here and output this component as part of the JSX code of this server component. If you do that, you see that the client component is rendered inside of the server component. This works without any issues. Now let me comment this out and also comment out this import and let's try something else. Let's try including the server component instead of this children placeholder here in the client component. So now I'm importing my React server component and I'm trying to output it as part of the JSX code of this client component. If you do that, you might expect an error, but actually this also works as you see the server component is included here in the client component. So why is this working if I just told you that this does not work? Well, because by default, next JS or this project setup here, which supports server components simply converts a component that's used inside of a client component to a client component as well. So this RSC demo component is converted to a client component because it's being used in a component that's marked as a client component. That's why it works. But now this isn't a server component anymore. Instead, now with that, you will see that if you open your log on the client side, RSC demo is also rendered here. Now, we could force this RSC demo component to be a server component by adding the async keyword in front of the function keyword. Because server components can be async functions, something which normally isn't allowed in React, but it is allowed for React server components. But since this is not allowed for client components, now if you save this, if you reload, you will get an error here in your client side browser log. You'll get a warning that async await is not yet supported in client components. Only for server components. Now of course here at the moment, we have no reason to mark this as a async function, but that will change once we add data fetching, which we'll do soon. But it is a way of forcing a component to be a server component in the end. And it proves that this here, using a server component in a client component does not work unless that server component is also converted to a client component, which it is automatically if it's possible. But if it's not possible, like here where I have async in front of the component function, which is not allowed in client components, if that's the case, we're getting an error. Now, what would be possible however, is to not use the server component in the JSX code of the client component, but to instead use the special children prop and remove this RSC demo import here and pass the server component to the client component kind of by moving it between the tags of this client component. Because by doing this, this server component here is technically rendered in this home component, which is a server component, and it's then just the rendered code that's sent to the client component. That's why this works. So with this setup, if I reload, the error message is gone, but we don't see the RSC demo rendered output here on the client side. So this proves that it's not being rendered there, that it's not a client component, but we see it on the screen. Because again, it was rendered on the server and then just injected into this client component, you could say. And that's therefore how you can combine server components and client components in React.

44246172.title: Fetching Data with RSCs
44246172.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246172.transcript: So, now we had an in-depth look at server components, client components, and how we can combine them. Next, I want to explore data fetching with you, because when using server components, you have some new options when it comes to fetching data in your components. And therefore, attached you'll find two new files. The first one is a file named dummy-db.json, which you should add in your root project folder. That's a file that contains some dummy data and kind of simulates a database here, you could say. And then attached you'll find a DataFetchingDemo.js file, which you should also paste into your components folder. And this DataFetchingDemo.js file contains a server component as you can tell by the fact that there is no use client directive at the top and that we have the async keyword here. And I did add the async keyword here, because this component now actually also contains some code that does fetch some data. It contains some Node.js code for opening a file and reading the file content, and for then loading data from that file. And then that data, these users, are output in the JSX code of this component. And in normal components, in client components, and components as you would add them to Vite-based projects, for example, this would not work. There, you can't put Node.js code into your components and you can't use async await because they're executed in the browser. And whilst the browser does indeed support async await, React doesn't support it for client components. But when we have server components, this entire code here as you learned, executes on the server. So in a Node.js environment for example, and if that's the case, we can write Node.js code in here, and this code will never execute on the client. And therefore, React server components vastly simplify the process of fetching data in components because instead of using useEffect to wrap code that fetches data from a separate backend, the code for loading the data is included right in the component. And this is then executed on the server and the finished HTML code that includes the fetch data is then sent back to the client and is displayed there. And therefore here what we can do with that file added, we can go to the page.js file and get rid of the ClientDemo and RSCDemo components here, and instead output this DataFetchingDemo component and remove these unused imports. And if you do that and you then go back to your website to this development server preview here, you see this component being rendered here and you see the loaded data being rendered here. And that now was all rendered on the server, or not on the client, to put it like this. And that's therefore another awesome feature that's unlocked by server components. You can use them for data fetching.

44246184.title: Submitting Data with Server Actions
44246184.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246184.transcript: So you can use React server components for executing server site code and for fetching data on the server. You can also use them for submitting data to the server. And for that, attached, you find another new component file, which you should add into your components folder. And that's the ServerActionsDemo.js file. And this file includes another server component, React component decorated with async. But this is now a component that also includes a function that is actually used as a form action. So this saveUserAction function is passed to the action prop as you learned it in the form actions section earlier in the course. But there is something special about this function. And the special thing here is this use server directive. This is something that can be added to a form action function to turn it into a server action. And it then must be a async function too, that's also important. But if you have an async form action and you then add this use server directive inside of it, you convert it to a server action. And a server action is, in the end, a form action, which is a default React feature that can be used in all React projects. But it's a form action that's actually executed on the server side, not on the client side. And it's created by adding this use server directive. But because of that, it's now a feature that's not available in all React projects. So whilst form actions can be used in any React project out there and require no special setup, server actions do require a special setup, a special project, so to say. Because this code inside of this function here will now be executed on the server, not on the client. If you add a console log statement here and maybe a log executed, you will not find this log in the browser developer tools. So if you save this and you go to the page.js file and there you output this ServerActionsDemo component, and if you save that file as well, then you'll see this component here on the screen. And if you then enter some data here and you open your browser developer tools and the console there and you click Save User, you won't see anything here. But if you go to your backend terminal, you'll see executed here. And this proves that this code was executed on the server side. And that's the idea behind server actions, which is another feature that's part of React, but that's only available in projects that support it, like this Next.js project. Now when it comes to working with server actions, there are a couple of important things to understand. For example, you can only define such a server action here inside of a component if it's not a client side component. So if I had use client here at the top of this file, we would get an error. We would get the error that it's not allowed to use this use server directive in a client component. Which I guess makes sense because we're now essentially telling the bundler, the build process, that this file should contain code that runs on the client, but then we tell it that we have code that should run on the server, not on the client. And this is kind of contradicting. So that's why you can't have both in the same file. However, you can use server actions in client components. You just can't define them there. What you could do is grab that server action and then add a new actions folder, for example, though the name doesn't matter, and then add a file with any name of your choice and put your server action in there and export it. And, that's all important, remove the use server directive from the function and instead added at the top of this file. This is allowed by React in projects that support this feature and this simply tells React and next JS here that this file only contains server actions in the end. That's what putting use server at the top does. So this is now also a server action and you can indeed now go to your component and remove this import here and actually put that into the actions file, because it's needed there. And then here in the server actions demo file you can simply import this saveUserAction from this actions folder and the user's file. And then still use that as a value for the action prop. So importing it and using it is allowed. Defining it in the same file, if it's a client component file, is not allowed. Because when you import that server action, this underlying build process is again able to split the code accordingly and put this code on the client side, but put the imported code on the server side, so to say. But it's not able to do that if you have both in the same file. Of course now since I added use client here, you also have to remove the async keyword here on this component though, since client components can't be asynchronous. But with that done, if you reload, any errors here should be gone. And you can again submit that form. That again works and all that data is being saved. if you take a look at this dummy-db.json file. The data was stored there indeed. So that's what you should know about server actions. Another feature built into React that doesn't work in all React projects, but it does work in Next.js projects, for example.

44246190.title: Using the use() Hook for Promises & Data Fetching
44246190.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246190.transcript: Now to conclude this theory part of this section, there's one last crucial React feature that you also should understand, and that's the use() hook. Now, you already saw the use() hook in action earlier in the course when we used it to connect to some context value, when we used it to access context, you could say. But it can also be used to await Promises, you could say, in client components without using Async/await, which isn't supported there, which might sound strange, but it is a feature developed by the React team that allows you to read values from Promises or to wait for Promises to resolve, you could say, in client components without using async/await. And it does work together with Suspense, a component offered by React that helps you with showing a loading fallback whilst data is being fetched, and you'll see it in action soon as well. But it actually only works with special kinds of Promises, you could say, not with any random Promise you might create in your code. Instead, this use() hook only wants to work with Promises that are in the end created by libraries that integrate with React's Suspense feature. So Promises ++, you could say, Promises that behind the scenes are built with React's Suspense feature in mind, and that send certain pieces of information to React, you could say. And again, thankfully, Next.js actually gives you a way of creating such Promises. Now for that attached, you'll find another new file, this UsePromisesDemo.js file, which contains a relatively straightforward React component, a React server component, since there is no use client directive in it, and a component that wants some users, which are then output down here. So we could go to the page.js file and use this, UsePromiseDemo component here and remove this unused import. And what you could do now is go to that data fetching demo and copy that code here, for example, and move that into your page.js file. This is also a server component, so we can use async here and we can use it to fetch data from that file. That will work. We should just also bring in that fs.import from Node.js so that we have access to this file system module. While we're in that we're fetching users here, and we can then set that users prop here on this UsePromiseDemo component and pass our users to it. And if you do that, if you reload your page, you see all these users, including the ones I added over the last lectures here on the screen. So that works. But this approach here has one potential downside. Let's say we have a slow server where reading that data and fetching that data takes a while. And to simulate this, I'll await a new Promise here which will actually resolve after a timer expired, so let's say after two seconds. Now if I go back here and I click this reload button, it takes two seconds until this page reloads. And this is even easier to see if I open a new tab. It takes two seconds until anything appears on the page. That's of course not necessarily a great user experience. And the reason for that is that I'm fetching the data here in the page.js file, and therefore this entire page content is only rendered once the data is there. Now, one way around that would be to grab that code and remove it from this home component, from this page.js file and put it into this UsePromisesDemo.js file. For that, we need to add Async here, which we can, since it's a server component, and we have to bring over that fs import. Now the users are fetched here, therefore we no longer need that prop. And in the page.js file, we no longer need to pass anything to UsePromiseDemo. Now with that alone, nothing would change though. If I copy that and open a new tab, it still takes two seconds until we see anything. But we could now use a feature built into React that allows us to wrap a part of our JSX code that might take a bit longer to load and show a fallback without blocking any other JSX code or any other content from loading. And that would be the Suspense component that's built into React and that you already saw in the context of lazy loading earlier in the course. Suspense is a component that's provided by React, that can be wrapped around any content that is loading something. That something could be code if you're using it for lazy loading, but it can also be data if that data fetching is done in a way that works with Suspense, which is the case in Next.js projects when working with React server components. So to put it in other words, React server components that fetch data do integrate with Suspense and can be wrapped by it. It won't work with all components, but it will work with React server components. Suspense then takes a fallback prop, and this can be used to define some fallback markup that should be shown whilst the data is being fetched here. As a result of using Suspense, you will see that now the page loads instantly, and we see that fallback until the data appears, and that's of course a better user experience than what we had before where the entire page took two seconds to load. But how is this now related to the use() hook? Well, that's what we'll explore next.

44246194.title: The use() Hook In Action
44246194.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246194.transcript: [Maximilian Schwarzmüller] So at this point, we're still not using this use hook. Instead, we have a server component with async await and that works with Suspense, which is great. That's good to know. But when would we need this use hook that's built into React? Well, let's say UsePromiseDemo for some reason, must be a client component. Because we also want to useState in here, let's say. Let's say we have a counter here. Of course, this is kind of a made up example, but it is just an example to get my point across. Let's say we have a counter here and we therefore have a button, which says Increment. And there upon a click, just as we did it earlier in the section, I want to update my count and I do that by using my previous count and adding one to it. And then I also have a span here where I output that count. So we have this code here. And therefore, we need this to be a client component. And therefore, this won't work anymore. Because now we're using server-side code in a client component. And we're trying to use async here, which also doesn't work for client components. So now we actually have to remove async and we have to grab that code and bring that back into our page.js file. And also bring back that import here, like this. Now of course, with that, we have the users in the page.js file and we could again pass them down to the UsePromiseDemo component by passing them as props, so that just as before, we accept the users props here. And it looks like I accidentally removed useState here. Of course, we do need that here. We need count and setCount like this here in our UsePromisesDemo.js file and actually remove that from the homepage. I don't want that there. Here, I just wanna have to code for having that delay and fetching that data. But with that, if you save that, this works again. But if you copy this and open a new page, we're back to the old state where the entire page takes two seconds. Because even though we're using Suspense, the slow part is in this home component, not in the component that's wrapped by Suspense. So Suspense doesn't really help us here. And that's now where the use hook can come in handy. Because what we can do now is create a promise here in the server component. And pass that promise as a prop to the client component. Yes, this is something you can do. And then use the use hook there. Now for that to work, I'll just need to restructure this a bit and I'll store this promise here in a constant. And when the timeout expires, I'll actually set up a function in which I then fetch the data from that file and where I then call resolve(users), so that I have one promise that has a delay, but that then after that delay expires, also fetches some data. And now we can pass this fetchUsersPromise to this users prop. And in the UsePromisesDemo, users now gets a promise as a value. And therefore to get our users that we want an output, we have to use this use hook, which is imported from React. And I'll name my prop here, usersPromise. And therefore, also change that name here in the page.js file to avoid confusion. And my users are then the result of calling use on that usersPromise. And this will then wait for that promise to resolve on the client side. And it will integrate with Suspense, so that the fallback is shown whilst that data is resolved on the client side. So with that, if I copy this and again, open a new page, the page loads instantly, we see the fallback and then this component appears. But this now is a client component, but a client component that waits for data with that use hook. And that's also something you can do with that use hook. It is however a feature that as mentioned, does not work in all React projects. Instead, this use hook only works when you use promises that are created by libraries that integrate with React's Suspense feature, or if you use it on promises that are created in a server component and that are then passed to that component that users use as it's the case here.

44246198.title: Handling Errors
44246198.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44246198.transcript: So now with the use hook added and used here, we can also take this to the next level by adding another new component, which you also find attached. And that would be the ErrorBoundary component. Now that's a type of component I covered earlier in the course in the class-based components section. Because this component is built in a different way than all those other components. It is built as a class that extends the component class, which is provided by React. And normally you wouldn't build components like this anymore, but this component, this ErrorBoundary component must be built like this because it uses some special error related React features that in the end allow you to catch errors that are thrown by wrapped components and handle them in any way you want. You then have a render method here where you can define what should be rendered on the screen if an error did occur. And I do cover this in greater detail in that earlier course section where I do explore class-based components. Here I'm providing this component to you and you can use this ErrorBoundary to wrap it around this code that also uses suspense. So ErrorBoundary here is wrapped around suspense and therefore also around UsePromiseDemo. And if this component now would generate an error because fetching the data fails, for example, this ErrorBoundary would become active and would show this output here. An output where the error message is displayed and some fallback that can be passed as props to this ErrorBoundary component. So this ErrorBoundary component is built such that you can add a fallback prop and the there we could say, something went wrong. And if you do that and you then go to this promise that does the data fetching, and there, you accept this second parameter, this reject parameter here so that you can reject the promise and therefore throw an error. If you do that and instead of resolving, you reject with a new error where I say, error, and you save that. If you reload, you will see that error output here and that's coming from that ErrorBoundary. And I'm showing you this here, not because ErrorBoundary would be a feature that needs Next.js or some special project set up, that's not the case. ErrorBoundary components can be created in any React project, but I'm showing you this feature here because it is quite a nice combination if you have suspense and ErrorBoundary working together. Because with that setup here, you have a component that handles the data fetching, then you have a component that handles the loading state, and then you have a component that shows an error if loading goes wrong. Now, when working with Next.js, you also have other ways of handling errors as you learned in the previous section, but this is an interesting pattern that you should be aware of when using features like suspense and the use hook or React server components and data fetching.

44248576.title: Theory Wrap Up
44248576.section: React Server Components (RSC) & Server Actions - A Closer Look
44248576.transcript: And that's now it for this theory part here. You did now learn about these crucial React features, which are part of React, but which can't be used in all React projects and I hopefully could explain why that's the case, because these features, at least to some extent, need a server side environment and the code therefore must be split. And I hopefully also was able to explain these different features, what they do, how you can use them in a Next.js project here because at this point there aren't too many projects that support them. That's why I used Next.js as an example. And I also showed you how these different features can work together. And therefore, if you are working in a React project that does have these features enabled, like a Next.js project for example, you should now also be able to use them.

44248580.title: Migrating To Form Actions
44248580.section: Practice Project: Building a Food Order App
44248580.transcript: So in this section here, in the Checkout component, we actually handled the submission of this checkout form manually with the onSubmit prop. And we then extracted those entered values in that function, in that handleSubmit function by constructing a FormData object by then getting hold of the event.target, which is the form, and by then collecting that FormData like this. And that, of course, all works, but you did, of course, also learn about form actions earlier in this course. And, therefore, we could, of course, also handled this form submission with help of form actions. And that's the other of what I wanna do now. Now, migrating actually isn't too difficult. We can start by giving this handleSubmit function a different name, which is not mandatory but which we can do to make it clear that it is a form action. And I'll name it checkoutAction. And then I'll use this function. And down there, I'll actually not set this onSubmit prop on my form anymore. But instead, I'll set the action prop to this renamed function, so to the checkoutAction function. Now, as you learned earlier in the course, when triggering this as a form action, you will get a FormData object as an input. And I'm naming it FD, since this was also the name I used down here when I created that form data manually. So by switching to a form action, I can actually get rid of some code here. Now, I'm still extracting my customerData like this then, and I'm still sending a request thereafter. And sendRequest is still that function that's provided by my custom HTTP hook here. It's this function here, which in the end is an async function, so a function that returns a promise. 44248586.section: Practice Project: Building a Food Order App
44248586.transcript: Now, of course, now that I'm using this formActions feature here, I also, again, wanna make sure that we show this Sending order data text here whilst the order is on its way, and since we're using formActions here, we actually no longer have to manage that state manually, so I don't care about this isLoading state that's returned by my useHttp hook anymore. I don't need it anymore, so I won't extract it here anymore. It was nice to have it, but now we don't need that extra information from here because I am awaiting my sendRquest call here in my formAction, and as you learned before, earlier in the course in this section about formActions, you can now use another hook offered by React, the useActionState hook, to get information about the current form status, so whether it's pending or not. Alternatively, in some nested component, you could use the form status hook, but here, I'll use the useActionState hook instead. Now, therefore, all I have to do is go below my checkoutAction, and then, here call useActionState and pass the checkoutAction to it as a first argument and the second argument is my initial formState, and here, for the moment, I'll set this to null. Now, as a result, I'll get back my updated formState about which I don't care here, but I then also get back my updated formAction and this pending state information, so this pending value, which is true or false. It's now this formAction that should be set as a value for this action prop on the form, like this, and it's the pending value here which I wanna use to show this Sending order data text. Now, here I'm checking for a variable named isSending, so either we replace this with pending or I use isSending here as a name when destructuring this, and I'll do the latter. With that done, again, if I add items to the cart, and I go to the Checkout page, and I enter some information here, like this, if I click Submit Order, I saw that state briefly, but then I got an error, and, of course, that is that error, kind of, which we saw before earlier in the course already because when using useActionState, your action function will no longer get this form data as a first parameter. Instead, it will now get the previous state as a first parameter, so this action function must be changed. With that, again, if we pick some items, go to checkout, and enter our data here, now you see Sending order appeared there briefly, and we would see it a bit longer if we added a delay to the back end again, so if we go to the back end code and there to the post orders route, we could add code like this to await a promise that will resolve after one second, and if you do that, and you restart that back end code, and we go through that process again, you'll see that text a bit longer, so that works the way it should. Now, of course, we could do more here. We could, for example, also use this action state to manage some formState and to show some validation errors in case the user does submit invalid values. Though, of course, we already have some basic browser-based validation in place here where I am getting some warnings already if I do omit values, so we're relying on the browser here, and, of course, we can still do that when using formActions. There's nothing wrong with that. That's why here I also won't add any extra logic to use formAction-based validation in addition to the browser-based validation, but you could, of course, do that in your projects, but with that, this is how we could migrate this application to use formActions instead of manually handling form submissions, and, as mentioned before, it simply is up to you which approach you prefer.
